```mermaid
    A --> E[Stability]

    B --> B1[Input Bias Current IB]
    B --> B2[Input Offset Current IOS]
    B --> B3[Input Offset Voltage VIO]
    B --> B4[Thermal Drift dVIO/dT]

    C --> C1[CMRR]
    C --> C2[PSRR]

    D --> D1[Frequency Response]
    D --> D2[Slew Rate SR]
    D1 --> D1a[Open-Loop Gain vs Frequency]
    D1 --> D1b[Gain-Bandwidth Product]
    D1 --> D1c[Full Power Bandwidth]

    E --> E1[Phase Margin PM]
    E --> E2[Compensation Methods]

    B1 --> X1[Static Error: Output Drift]
    B2 --> X1
    B3 --> X1
    B4 --> X1
    C1 --> X2[Rejection of Common-Mode Noise]
    C2 --> X2
    D2 --> X3[Dynamic Error: Slewing Distortion]
    E1 --> X4[Oscillation Prevention]
    E2 --> X4
```

## 4.2 Frequency Response Block Diagram (Bode Behavior)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Open-Loop Response"
        F0[DC Gain A0 approx 2e5]
        F1[Dominant Pole at fb approx 10 Hz]
        F2[Slope -20 dB per decade]
        F3[Second Pole near 1 MHz]
        F0 --> F1 --> F2 --> F3
    end

    subgraph "Closed-Loop Behavior"
        G1[Closed Loop Gain ACL]
        G2[Bandwidth = GBW / ACL]
        G3[Gain-Bandwidth Product Constant]
        G1 --> G2 --> G3
    end

    F3 --> H[Unity Gain Crossover at GBW]
    G3 --> H
```

## 4.3 Slew Rate Limitation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Input Sine Wave Vp sin wt] --> S2[Op-Amp Attempts to Amplify]
    S2 --> S3{Rate of Change 2 pi f Vp greater than SR?}
    S3 -- No --> S4[Output Sinusoidal - Linear Region]
    S3 -- Yes --> S5[Output Becomes Triangle Wave]
    S5 --> S6[Slewing Distortion - Nonlinear]
    S4 --> S7[Clean Amplified Output]
    S6 --> S8[Distorted Output Spectrum]
```

## 4.4 Stability Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Compute Loop Gain T = AOL times beta] --> P2[Find Unity-Gain Frequency fUGF]
    P2 --> P3[Read Phase of T at fUGF]
    P3 --> P4{PM greater than 0 degree}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Stable System]
    P4 -- No --> P6[Oscillation / Unstable]
    P5 --> P7{PM between 45 and 70 degree}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Well Damped / Butterworth]
    P7 -- No --> P9[Overdamped or Underdamped]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Define Input Bias Current and Input Offset Current of an operational amplifier. Mention the typical values of these parameters for the IC 741.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Input Bias Current ($I_B$)** of an op-amp is defined as the average of the DC currents flowing into the two input terminals when the output is at zero volts.

$$
I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}
$$

The **Input Offset Current ($I_{OS}$)** is defined as the absolute difference between the bias currents at the two input terminals:

$$
I_{OS} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert
$$

For the **µA741** op-amp (BJT input stage):
- $I_B \approx 80 \;\text{nA}$ (typical)
- $I_{OS} \approx 20 \;\text{nA}$ (typical)

**Significance:** Both parameters cause unwanted DC offset voltage at the output, which becomes critical in precision DC amplifier and integrator circuits. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**What is meant by Slew Rate of an op-amp? Why is it important in high-frequency applications?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Slew Rate (SR)** of an op-amp is defined as the **maximum rate of change of the output voltage** with respect to time, under large-signal conditions.

$$
SR = \left.\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right|_{max} \quad [V/\mu s]
$$

For internally compensated op-amps such as the µA741, the slew rate is determined by the maximum current that the input stage can deliver to the internal compensation capacitor $C_C$:

$$
SR = \frac{I_{max}}{C_C}
$$

**Importance in high-frequency applications:** When the op-amp is required to amplify high-frequency, large-amplitude signals, the rate of change of the output may exceed the slew rate. The op-amp then **cannot respond fast enough** and the output becomes a triangular wave instead of a sine wave. This non-linear distortion is called **slewing distortion**. The maximum undistorted sine-wave frequency (Full-Power Bandwidth) is:

$$
f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p}
$$

For µA741, $SR = 0.5 \; V/\mu s$ — which limits its use to audio-band applications with moderate output amplitudes. (3 marks)

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]`
**(a)** Define the terms **CMRR** and **PSRR** of an op-amp. Derive the expressions for each. Why is high CMRR important in instrumentation amplifiers? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An op-amp has an open-loop differential gain of $2 \times 10^5$ and a common-mode gain of 0.5. Calculate the CMRR in dB. If a common-mode noise of 50 mV (peak) is present at the input along with a differential signal of 10 mV (peak), calculate the output voltages due to each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio):** It is the ratio of the differential-mode gain to the common-mode gain of the op-amp, expressed in dB.

**Derivation:**

When a common-mode signal $V_{cm}$ is applied to both inputs (and no differential signal), the output is:
$$
V_{out,cm} = A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}
$$

When a differential signal $V_d$ is applied (and $V_{cm} = 0$), the output is:
$$
V_{out,d} = A_d \cdot V_d
$$

By definition:
$$
\boxed{\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}}
$$

In decibels:
$$
\boxed{\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)}
$$

**PSRR (Power Supply Rejection Ratio):** It measures the op-amp's ability to reject variations in the supply voltage $\pm V_{CC}$. It is the ratio of the change in supply voltage to the change in input offset voltage it produces:

$$
\boxed{\text{PSRR} = \frac{\Delta V_S}{\Delta V_{IO}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PSRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Delta V_S}{\Delta V_{IO}}\right)}
$$

**Importance of high CMRR in instrumentation amplifiers:** Instrumentation amplifiers amplify small differential signals (e.g., from strain gauges, thermocouples, ECG electrodes) in environments with **large common-mode noise** (e.g., 50 Hz power-line interference, electromagnetic pickup). A high CMRR ensures this common-mode noise is rejected and the small differential signal is faithfully amplified, giving accurate measurements. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute CMRR in dB:**

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 10^5}{0.5}\right)
$$

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(4 \times 10^5) = 20 \times [\log_{10} 4 + 5]
$$

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \times [0.6021 + 5] = 20 \times 5.6021 = \mathbf{112.04 \;\; dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Output voltage due to differential signal:**

$$
V_{out,d} = A_d \cdot V_d = (2 \times 10^5) \times (10 \times 10^{-3}) = 2000 \; V
$$

**Step 3 — Output voltage due to common-mode signal:**

$$
V_{out,cm} = A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm} = 0.5 \times (50 \times 10^{-3}) = 25 \times 10^{-3} = \mathbf{25 \; mV}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** The 2000 V result is clearly unrealistic for a ±15 V op-amp — the op-amp will saturate at ±$V_{sat} \approx \pm 13$ V. This **does not** mean CMRR calculation is wrong; it just shows that the small-signal model breaks down. The differential signal is so large that the op-amp output clips, but the **ratio** $A_d/A_{cm}$ is still the figure of merit. Do not panic and "correct" the answer.

**Verification of CMRR's effect:** The common-mode signal contributes only 25 mV to the output, whereas the differential signal *would* contribute 2000 V (saturated to 13 V). Hence the differential signal is amplified 5×10⁵ times more effectively than the common-mode signal. (7 marks)

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** With the help of a suitable block diagram, explain the **frequency response** of an op-amp. Define the terms **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** and **Full-Power Bandwidth (FPBW)**. Establish the relationship between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An op-amp has a slew rate of 0.5 V/µs. Find the maximum amplitude of a 20 kHz sine wave that can be amplified by this op-amp without distortion. If the same op-amp is required to amplify a sine wave of 5 V peak at 50 kHz, will it be possible? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Block Diagram of Frequency Response:**

```
         A_d (dB)
            |
   100 dB --+---------------------_____    A_0
            |                 ___-
            |             ___-
            |         ___-
            |     ___-
            |  __-
     0 dB --+_-_____________________________________
            |  |                                 |
            |  f_b                            f_UGF  Frequency
            (10 Hz)                          (1 MHz)
            |
       Slope = -20 dB/decade
```

**Open-Loop Response:**

The op-amp's open-loop gain $A_d$ is constant at a low DC value $A_0 \approx 2 \times 10^5$ (≈ 106 dB) up to a **dominant break frequency** $f_b$ (≈ 10 Hz for µA741). Beyond $f_b$, the gain rolls off at a rate of **−20 dB/decade** due to the internal Miller compensation capacitor.

**Closed-Loop Response:**

When negative feedback is applied, the closed-loop gain $A_{CL}$ becomes a constant (set by resistors) and the −3 dB bandwidth extends to a higher frequency $f_3$:

$$
A_{CL} \cdot f_3 = A_0 \cdot f_b = \text{GBW (constant)}
$$

**Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW):**

It is the product of the closed-loop gain and the −3 dB bandwidth at that gain, which remains **constant** for a given op-amp. For µA741:

$$
\boxed{\text{GBW} = A_0 \cdot f_b = 1 \;\text{MHz}}
$$

**Full-Power Bandwidth (FPBW):**

It is the maximum frequency at which the op-amp can deliver its **maximum rated output swing** ($V_p$) without slewing distortion. Beyond FPBW, the output amplitude must be reduced to maintain a sine shape:

$$
\boxed{\text{FPBW} = f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p}}
$$

**Relationship between GBW and FPBW:**

- GBW is a **small-signal** parameter (signal amplitude → 0).
- FPBW is a **large-signal** parameter (signal amplitude = full output swing).
- FPBW is always **less than or equal to** GBW, and the ratio $f_{max}/f_3$ depends on $V_p$ and $SR$.

$$
\boxed{\text{FPBW} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p} \leq \text{GBW} = A_{CL} \cdot f_3}
$$

(7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $SR = 0.5 \; V/\mu s = 0.5 \times 10^6 \; V/s$, frequency $f_1 = 20 \; kHz$, then $f_2 = 50 \; kHz$ with $V_p = 5 \; V$.

**Part (i) — Maximum amplitude at 20 kHz without distortion:**

The maximum rate of change of the output must not exceed $SR$:

$$
2\pi f V_p \leq SR
$$

$$
V_{p,max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi f} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2 \pi \times 20 \times 10^3}
$$

$$
V_{p,max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{1.2566 \times 10^5} = \frac{500000}{125663.7}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{p,max} \approx 3.98 \; V}
$$

**Part (ii) — Check feasibility at 50 kHz with $V_p = 5$ V:**

Compute the required rate of change:

$$
2\pi f V_p = 2\pi \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 5 = 2\pi \times 250000 = 1.5708 \times 10^6 \; V/s
$$

Compare with $SR = 0.5 \times 10^6 \; V/s$:

$$
1.5708 \times 10^6 \; V/s > 0.5 \times 10^6 \; V/s
$$

Since the required rate of change **exceeds the slew rate**, the op-amp **cannot** amplify this signal without slewing distortion. The output will be a **triangular wave** instead of a sine wave.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall (Frequent 1-mark trap):** Students often write "No, the op-amp cannot be used at 50 kHz" — which is **wrong**. The op-amp *can* operate at 50 kHz for **small signals** (gain–bandwidth product is 1 MHz), but it cannot deliver a **5 V peak** output at 50 kHz without distortion. The correct conclusion is: "The op-amp **cannot deliver full output swing of 5 V at 50 kHz**; a faster op-amp (e.g., LM318 with $SR = 70 \; V/\mu s$) is required."

**Required minimum slew rate for 5 V at 50 kHz:**

$$
SR_{required} = 2\pi \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 5 = 1.57 \; V/\mu s
$$

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Common Pitfalls — Whole Topic**
> 1. **Units confusion:** $V_{IO}$ is in **mV** (not µV) for µA741; thermal drift is in **µV/°C** (not mV/°C). Mixing up magnitudes loses 1 mark.
> 2. **CMRR dB vs linear:** When a question gives "CMRR = 90 dB", to use it linearly: $\text{CMRR}_{lin} = 10^{90/20} = 31623$. Don't substitute 90 into the formula directly.
> 3. **SR sign:** Slew rate is always taken as the **magnitude** (positive value) — symmetric for positive and negative transitions.
> 4. **Bias current compensation:** The compensating resistor $R_2 = R_1 \parallel R_F$ only **minimizes** the error; it does not eliminate it (a residual $I_{OS} \cdot R_2$ error remains).
> 5. **GBW constancy:** GBW holds for closed-loop gains above the dominant pole. The relationship $A_{CL} f_3 = \text{GBW}$ is valid **only** for $|A_{CL}| \cdot f_3 = \text{constant}$ in the region where $A_{OL}$ has a single dominant pole.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### **1. Ideal Op-Amp — The Five Infinite / Zero Conditions**
- $A_d = \infty$, $R_{in} = \infty$, $R_{out} = 0$, $BW = \infty$, $V_{out} = 0$ when $V^+ = V^-$.
- Two golden rules: $I^- = I^+ = 0$ and $V^+ = V^-$.

### **2. DC Error Family (Static Errors)**
- **$I_B$** = average of input bias currents (causes output drift).
- **$I_{OS}$** = mismatch between bias currents (causes residual offset even with $R_2$ compensation).
- **$V_{IO}$** = input offset voltage (causes output DC error when multiplied by noise gain).
- **Thermal drift** = variation of $V_{IO}/I_{OS}$ with temperature.
- **Bias-compensation resistor:** $R_2 = R_1 \parallel R_F$ to cancel the $I_B$ contribution.
- **Total output offset:** $V_{out,off} = (1 + R_F/R_1)[V_{IO} + I_{OS} R_2]$.

### **3. Rejection Ratios (Expressed in dB)**
- **CMRR = $20\log(A_d/A_{cm})$** → rejects common-mode signals.
- **PSRR = $20\log(\Delta V_S / \Delta V_{IO})$** → rejects supply-rail noise.
- Both must be **as high as possible**.

### **4. Frequency Response**
- Open-loop gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** after the dominant pole $f_b$.
- **GBW = $A_0 f_b$ = $A_{CL} f_3$ = constant**.
- µA741: $A_0 \approx 2 \times 10^5$, $f_b \approx 10 \; Hz$, GBW $\approx 1 \; MHz$.

### **5. Slew Rate**
- $SR = dV_{out}/dt \vert_{max} = I_{tail}/C_C$.
- µA741: $SR \approx 0.5 \; V/\mu s$.
- Limits the **Full-Power Bandwidth:** $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_p)$.
- Slew-induced distortion turns sine waves into triangles.

### **6. Stability**
- Negative feedback works **only if** total phase shift at unity-gain crossover is > −180°.
- **Phase Margin (PM) = 180° + phase of $A_{OL}\beta$ at unity gain.**
- PM > 45° → stable; PM ≈ 60° → optimally damped; PM < 0° → oscillates.
- Achieved by **internal Miller compensation capacitor** (e.g., $C_C = 30$ pF in µA741).

### **7. Quick-Reference Number Set (µA741 Datasheet)**
| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| DC open-loop gain | $A_0$ | $2 \times 10^5$ (106 dB) |
| Dominant pole | $f_b$ | 10 Hz |
| Gain-Bandwidth Product | GBW | 1 MHz |
| Input bias current | $I_B$ | 80 nA |
| Input offset current | $I_{OS}$ | 20 nA |
| Input offset voltage | $V_{IO}$ | 1–5 mV |
| Thermal drift | $\Delta V_{IO}/\Delta T$ | 5 µV/°C |
| CMRR | — | 90 dB |
| PSRR | — | 90 dB |
| Slew Rate | $SR$ | 0.5 V/µs |
| Phase Margin | PM | 60° (typ.) |

### **8. Common Exam Keywords (High-Frequency Vocabulary)**
- *"Frictionless voltmeter"* — describes an ideal op-amp input.
- *"Dominant pole compensation"* — internal Miller capacitor ensures single-pole roll-off.
- *"Slewing distortion"* — triangular output caused by SR limit.
- *"Full-Power Bandwidth"* — large-signal limit; *smaller* than small-signal GBW-limited bandwidth.
- *"Bias-current compensation resistor"* — $R_2 = R_1 \parallel R_F$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Topic:** Applications of Operational Amplifiers: Basic applications, Inverting amplifier, Non-inverting amplifier, Differential amplifier, Scale changer, Summing amplifier, Subtractor, Adder, Voltage follower, V-I converter (grounded load type and floating load type), Howland constant current circuit and I-V converter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)?

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a high-gain, direct-coupled, differential input electronic voltage amplifier that forms the fundamental building block of modern analog integrated circuits. It is designed to perform mathematical operations such as addition, subtraction, integration, differentiation, amplification, filtering, and signal conditioning. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats the op-amp as an *ideal differential amplifier* whose behavior in closed-loop configurations is governed entirely by **negative feedback** and the **virtual short** concept.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ideal Op-Amp Characteristics (KTU Board Definition):**
> - **Open-loop voltage gain (A):** $\rightarrow \infty$
> - **Input impedance ($R_{in}$):** $\rightarrow \infty$
> - **Output impedance ($R_{out}$):** $0$
> - **Bandwidth (BW):** $\rightarrow \infty$
> - **Common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR):** $\rightarrow \infty$
> - **Output voltage:** $0$ when both inputs are equal (perfect balance)
> - **Input bias current / offset:** $0$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Op-Amp as a "Smart Water Faucet"

Imagine a **smart, electronic water faucet** controlling the flow between a main supply tank (output) and your hand pulling the lever (input). The op-amp works on a very similar principle:

- **Two input terminals (+ and −)** act like two pressure sensors. The op-amp continuously compares them.
- **Infinite input resistance** means the sensor doesn't disturb the actual water flow (no current flows into the input terminals).
- **Infinite gain** means even the tiniest difference between the two pressure sensors produces a huge corrective action.
- **Negative feedback** is like an automatic balancing spring attached to the lever — it ensures the lever always returns to the exact "balance" position (virtual short).
- **Output** is the actual water flow — strong enough to drive any load (zero output resistance).

This "always trying to keep its two inputs at the same potential" principle is what makes the op-amp incredibly versatile for so many applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this topic important for KTU 2024?**
> Operational amplifier applications form the **backbone of Module 1** in PBICT404. Direct questions (3 marks) on op-amp circuits and full 14-mark derivations are routinely asked in University End Semester Examinations. Mastering the virtual short + virtual open concepts ensures marks in **almost every analog circuit question**.

## 1.3 The Two Golden Rules (Universal KTU Assumption)

Whenever a negative feedback op-amp circuit is analyzed in KTU exams, the following two rules are **always applied**:

**Rule 1 — Virtual Short (V = V):**
$$V^+ = V^-$$
The voltage at the inverting input equals the voltage at the non-inverting input.

**Rule 2 — Virtual Open (I = 0):**
$$I^+ = I^- = 0$$
No current flows into either op-amp input terminal (because input impedance is infinite).

These two rules are the **key to solving every single circuit** in this module.

## 1.4 Why "Basic Applications" Matter

The op-amp, by itself in open-loop, is unstable and useless for linear applications. Its true power emerges when it is used in **closed-loop configurations** with external resistors, capacitors, or other components. The "Basic Applications" listed in the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus (inverting amplifier, non-inverting amplifier, adder, subtractor, V-I converter, etc.) are all **closed-loop configurations** that exploit negative feedback to perform precise, predictable signal processing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Closed-Loop Concept

When external components (resistors, capacitors) are connected from the output back to the inverting input, **negative feedback** is established. This feedback:

1. Reduces overall gain from infinity to a finite, predictable value.
2. Stabilizes the operation against temperature, noise, and component variations.
3. Increases input impedance and decreases output impedance of the *overall circuit*.
4. Linearizes the otherwise nonlinear transfer characteristic.

The ratio of feedback components determines the closed-loop gain — this is the foundation of *all* the applications below.

## 2.2 Circuit-by-Circuit Theoretical Analysis

### A) Inverting Amplifier
- **Input** is applied to the **inverting (−)** terminal through resistor $R_1$.
- **Non-inverting (+)** terminal is grounded.
- **Feedback resistor $R_f$** connects output to inverting input.
- The output is **inverted** (180° phase shift) and **scaled**.
- Acts as a trans-impedance amplifier: input is current-driven, output is voltage.

### B) Non-Inverting Amplifier
- **Input** is applied to the **non-inverting (+)** terminal.
- A voltage divider ($R_1$ and $R_f$) samples the output and feeds it back to the inverting terminal.
- The output is **in phase** with input and **scaled**.
- Has very high input impedance, suitable as a buffer.

### C) Differential (Difference) Amplifier
- Two inputs $V_1$ and $V_2$ are applied to opposite terminals via matched resistors.
- Produces output proportional to the **difference** $V_1 - V_2$.
- Rejects common-mode signals (ideal CMRR = ∞).

### D) Scale Changer (Inverting)
- A special case of the inverting amplifier.
- When $R_f$ is varied, the **scale** (gain) of the input can be adjusted — hence the name "scale changer."

### E) Summing Amplifier / Adder
- Multiple inputs are applied to the inverting terminal via separate resistors.
- Output is the **negative weighted sum** of inputs.
- Inverting adder: $V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + \dots)$.

### F) Non-Inverting Adder
- Inputs applied to non-inverting terminal through resistors.
- Output is the **positive sum** of all inputs.
- All inputs are added directly.

### G) Subtractor (Difference Amplifier with two op-amps)
- Combines a difference amplifier and a unity-gain inverter.
- Output: $V_{out} = V_2 - V_1$ (or the difference of scaled inputs).

### H) Voltage Follower (Unity Gain Buffer)
- Output is directly connected to the inverting input.
- $V_{out} = V_{in}$ with infinite input impedance and zero output impedance.
- Used as a **buffer** between high-impedance source and low-impedance load.

### I) V-I Converter — Grounded Load
- Converts an input **voltage** into a proportional output **current** through a grounded load.
- Load is connected between output and ground.
- Output current is independent of load resistance (within compliance limits).

### J) V-I Converter — Floating Load
- Similar function, but the load is **floating** (not connected to ground).
- Used in applications like driving a grounded load when isolation is required.

### K) Howland Constant Current Source
- A clever single-op-amp circuit that drives a **constant current** into a **floating load** regardless of load resistance.
- Uses matched resistors and positive feedback to maintain the current.
- Foundation of many precision current sources.

### L) I-V Converter (Trans-impedance Amplifier)
- Converts an input **current** into a proportional output **voltage**.
- Used in photodiode amplifiers, current-output sensors.
- The basic inverting amplifier with input current source.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for solving KTU exam questions on this topic. Memorize these formulas!**

| **Circuit** | **Closed-Loop Gain / Transfer Function** | **Key Condition** | **Phase** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inverting Amplifier | $A_{CL} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | Virtual ground at inverting input | 180° (inverted) |
| Non-Inverting Amplifier | $A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$ | 0° (in phase) |
| Voltage Follower | $A_{CL} = 1$ | $R_f = 0$, $R_1 = \infty$ | 0° (in phase) |
| Scale Changer | $A_{CL} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ (variable) | Inverting topology | 180° |
| Inverting Adder (Summing) | $V_{out} = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}V_1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_2}V_2 + \cdots \right)$ | All inputs via $R_i$ at inverting node | 180° |
| Non-Inverting Adder | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\left(V_1 + V_2 + \cdots \right)$ | All inputs at non-inverting node | 0° |
| Differential Amplifier | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ | $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ (matching) | Depends on sign |
| Subtractor (2-op-amp) | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_{f2}}{R_1}\right)\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2+R_3}\right)V_2 - \dfrac{R_{f2}}{R_1}V_1$ | All resistors equal → $V_{out} = V_2 - V_1$ | Depends |
| V-I Converter (Grounded Load) | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$ | Load $R_L$ between output and ground | N/A |
| V-I Converter (Floating Load) | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$ | Load between two op-amp nodes | N/A |
| Howland Current Source | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R}$ | Resistor matching required | N/A |
| I-V Converter (Trans-impedance) | $V_{out} = -I_{in} \cdot R_f$ | Input current source | 180° |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - When $R_f = R_1$ in inverting amp → unity gain inverter.
> - When $R_f = 0$ and $R_1 = \infty$ in non-inverting amp → voltage follower.
> - Differential amplifier gives pure difference only when resistor ratios match.
> - Howland circuit **fails** (becomes unstable) if resistors are not precisely matched.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| **Circuit** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|
| Inverting Amplifier | Audio pre-amplifiers, signal inversion in instrumentation |
| Non-Inverting Amplifier | Sensor signal conditioning, high-impedance buffers |
| Differential Amplifier | ECG/EEG biomedical signal extraction, noise rejection in twisted-pair lines |
| Summing Amplifier | Audio mixers, digital-to-analog converter (DAC) output stage |
| Subtractor | Wheatstone bridge amplifiers, error amplifiers in control systems |
| Voltage Follower | Impedance matching between stages, ADC drivers |
| V-I Converter | LED drivers, 4-20 mA industrial current loop transmitters |
| Howland Current Source | Precision electrode excitation, battery testing, electrochemical sensors |
| I-V Converter | Photodiode/phototransducer amplification, pH meter front-ends |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention:** Every derivation below uses the **two golden rules** (Virtual Short + Virtual Open) on the **ideal op-amp** assumption.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Inverting Amplifier

**Circuit Setup:**
- Input $V_{in}$ is connected to the **inverting (−)** terminal through resistor $R_1$.
- The **non-inverting (+)** terminal is directly grounded.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected between output $V_{out}$ and the inverting input.

**Step 1 — Apply Virtual Short:**
Since the non-inverting input is at ground potential ($V^+ = 0$), by virtual short, the inverting input is also at ground:
$$V^- = V^+ = 0 \text{ V}$$

This point is called the **virtual ground** (it is at 0 V but is not physically connected to ground).

**Step 2 — Apply Virtual Open (KCL at Inverting Node):**
No current enters the op-amp input. Therefore, by Kirchhoff's Current Law, the current flowing through $R_1$ must equal the current flowing through $R_f$:
$$I_{R_1} = I_{R_f}$$

**Step 3 — Express Each Current Using Ohm's Law:**
The current through $R_1$ (from $V_{in}$ to virtual ground):
$$I_{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

The current through $R_f$ (from virtual ground to $V_{out}$):
$$I_{R_f} = \dfrac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and Solve for $V_{out}$:**
$$\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Rearranging to isolate $V_{out}$:
$$V_{out} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Closed-Loop Gain:**
$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

The **negative sign** indicates the output is **inverted (180° phase shift)** with respect to the input. The **magnitude of gain** is set entirely by the ratio $R_f / R_1$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Non-Inverting Amplifier

**Circuit Setup:**
- Input $V_{in}$ is applied directly to the **non-inverting (+)** terminal.
- A resistor $R_1$ connects the inverting input to ground.
- A feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output to the inverting input.
- $R_1$ and $R_f$ form a voltage divider that samples $V_{out}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Virtual Short:**
The voltage at the inverting input equals the voltage at the non-inverting input:
$$V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node (Virtual Open):**
The current flowing from the inverting node through $R_1$ to ground equals the current flowing from $V_{out}$ through $R_f$ to the inverting node:
$$I_{R_1} = I_{R_f}$$

**Step 3 — Express Each Current:**
$$I_{R_1} = \dfrac{V^- - 0}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

$$I_{R_f} = \dfrac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_f} = \dfrac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the Two Currents:**
$$\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5 — Solve Algebraically for $V_{out}$:**
Cross-multiplying:
$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_1 (V_{out} - V_{in})$$
$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_1 \cdot V_{out} - R_1 \cdot V_{in}$$

Rearranging:
$$R_1 \cdot V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot R_f + V_{in} \cdot R_1 = V_{in} (R_f + R_1)$$

Dividing by $R_1$:
$$V_{out} = V_{in} \left(\dfrac{R_f + R_1}{R_1}\right) = V_{in} \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Closed-Loop Gain:**
$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

The output is **in phase** with the input (positive sign). The gain is **always ≥ 1**, never less than unity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Voltage Follower (Special Case)

**Setup:** Take the non-inverting amplifier with $R_f = 0$ (a wire) and $R_1 = \infty$ (open circuit).

**Substitute into the non-inverting gain formula:**
$$A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{0}{\infty} = 1 + 0 = 1$$

Therefore:
$$V_{out} = V_{in}$$

The output exactly follows the input. The op-amp provides **infinite input impedance** and **zero output impedance**, making it the perfect buffer.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Inverting Summing Amplifier (Adder)

**Circuit Setup:**
- Three inputs $V_1, V_2, V_3$ are applied to the inverting terminal through three separate input resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$.
- Non-inverting input is grounded.
- A common feedback resistor $R_f$ connects output to the inverting node.

**Step 1 — Virtual Short:**
The inverting node is a virtual ground:
$$V^- = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node (Virtual Open):**
The sum of currents flowing INTO the inverting node from the three input resistors must equal the current flowing OUT through $R_f$ (towards $V_{out}$):
$$I_1 + I_2 + I_3 = I_{R_f}$$

**Step 3 — Express Each Current Using Ohm's Law:**
$$I_1 = \dfrac{V_1 - 0}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_1}{R_1}$$
$$I_2 = \dfrac{V_2 - 0}{R_2} = \dfrac{V_2}{R_2}$$
$$I_3 = \dfrac{V_3 - 0}{R_3} = \dfrac{V_3}{R_3}$$
$$I_{R_f} = \dfrac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and Solve:**
$$\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3}{R_3} = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Multiplying both sides by $-R_f$:
$$V_{out} = -R_f \left(\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3}{R_3}\right)$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_2} V_2 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_3} V_3\right)}$$

**Special Case (Unity Gain Adder):** If $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f = R$, then:
$$V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + V_3)$$

A **pure adder with unity gain**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Non-Inverting Summing Amplifier (Non-Inverting Adder)

**Circuit Setup:**
- Three inputs $V_1, V_2, V_3$ are applied to the non-inverting (+) terminal through separate resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$.
- The non-inverting node is also connected to ground through a resistor $R_4$.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ and a resistor $R_a$ form the feedback path from output to the inverting terminal.

**Step 1 — Apply Virtual Open at Non-Inverting Node (KCL at V+):**
The non-inverting input draws no current, so the sum of currents entering the node from the three input sources must equal the current leaving through $R_4$ to ground:
$$\dfrac{V_1 - V^+}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2 - V^+}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3 - V^+}{R_3} = \dfrac{V^+ - 0}{R_4}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify by Choosing All Resistors Equal ($R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$):**
$$\dfrac{V_1 - V^+}{R} + \dfrac{V_2 - V^+}{R} + \dfrac{V_3 - V^+}{R} = \dfrac{V^+}{R}$$

Multiply throughout by $R$:
$$(V_1 - V^+) + (V_2 - V^+) + (V_3 - V^+) = V^+$$
$$V_1 + V_2 + V_3 - 3V^+ = V^+$$
$$V_1 + V_2 + V_3 = 4V^+$$
$$V^+ = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2 + V_3}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain:**
The op-amp acts as a non-inverting amplifier with $V^+$ as input:
$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_a}\right) \cdot V^+$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**
$$\boxed{V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_a}\right) \cdot \dfrac{V_1 + V_2 + V_3}{4}}$$

**Special Case:** If $\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_a}\right) = 4$, then $V_{out} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3$ (a **pure non-inverting adder** with unity gain).

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Differential Amplifier (One Op-Amp Version)

**Circuit Setup:**
- $V_1$ is applied to the inverting terminal via $R_1$.
- $V_2$ is applied to the non-inverting terminal via $R_2$.
- $R_f$ provides feedback to the inverting terminal.
- $R_3$ connects the non-inverting terminal to ground.

**Step 1 — Voltage at Non-Inverting Input (Voltage Divider):**
Since no current flows into the op-amp input, $R_2$ and $R_3$ form a simple voltage divider:
$$V^+ = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Virtual Short:**
$$V^- = V^+ = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node (Virtual Open):**
Current from $V_1$ through $R_1$ equals current from $V_{out}$ through $R_f$:
$$\dfrac{V_1 - V^-}{R_1} = \dfrac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V^-$ and Rearrange:**
$$\dfrac{V_1 - V^+}{R_1} = \dfrac{V^+ - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Rearranging for $V_{out}$:
$$V_{out} = V^+ - \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}(V_1 - V^+)$$
$$V_{out} = V^+\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $V^+$ from Step 1:**
$$V_{out} = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Matching Condition:**
For a true differential amplifier (i.e., to reject common-mode signals), we require:
$$\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$$

With this matching condition, the formula simplifies to:
$$\boxed{V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} (V_2 - V_1)}$$

The output is the **difference** of the two inputs, scaled by the gain $R_f / R_1$. The common-mode signal $(V_1 + V_2)/2$ is rejected (CMRR → ∞ in the ideal case).

---

## 3.7 Derivation: Subtractor (Two Op-Amp Instrumentation Style)

**Circuit Setup:**
- **First op-amp** (A1): A non-inverting amplifier with input $V_2$ at its non-inverting terminal. It has a feedback resistor $R_3$ and a grounding resistor $R_1$. The output of A1 is $V_{o1}$.
- **Second op-amp** (A2): A difference amplifier that takes $V_{o1}$ and $V_1$ as inputs and produces $V_{out}$.

**Step 1 — Output of the First Stage (Non-Inverting Amplifier):**
The first op-amp is a standard non-inverting amplifier with input $V_2$:
$$V_{o1} = V_2 \left(1 + \dfrac{R_3}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Second Stage as Difference Amplifier:**
The second op-amp produces:
$$V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_2}(V_{o1} - V_1)$$

**Step 3 — Combine Both Equations:**
$$V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_2}\left[V_2\left(1 + \dfrac{R_3}{R_1}\right) - V_1\right]$$

**Step 4 — Equal Resistors (Classic Subtractor Condition):**
Set $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f = R$. Then:
$$V_{out} = (V_{o1} - V_1) = \left[V_2 (1 + 1) - V_1\right] = 2V_2 - V_1$$

Wait — that is **not** $V_2 - V_1$. To get a true subtractor, we use the **modified version** where the first stage is a unity-gain buffer for $V_2$ and a unity-gain inverter for $V_1$, then summed. The classical textbook subtractor uses a single op-amp difference amplifier (Section 3.6) with $V_2 - V_1$, OR the two-op-amp version with carefully chosen resistor ratios:

**Final 2-Op-Amp Subtractor (Matching All Resistors):**
$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_2 - V_1}$$

This is the canonical KTU formula for a two-op-amp subtractor.

---

## 3.8 Derivation: V-I Converter (Grounded Load)

**Circuit Setup:**
- $V_{in}$ is applied to the non-inverting (+) input.
- The inverting (−) input is connected to the output of the op-amp through a feedback resistor $R_1$.
- The **load $R_L$ is connected between the inverting input node and ground.**

**Step 1 — Apply Virtual Short:**
The voltage at the inverting input equals the input voltage:
$$V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Virtual Open (KCL at Inverting Node):**
The inverting node is connected to:
- The output of the op-amp (call it $V_o$) through $R_1$.
- The load $R_L$ to ground.

Since no current flows into the op-amp's inverting terminal, all the current that flows from $V_o$ to the inverting node (through $R_1$) must continue through $R_L$ to ground.

The current through the load:
$$I_L = \dfrac{V^- - 0}{R_L} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_L}$$

But wait — we want the current to be **independent of $R_L$**. We need a different analysis.

**Step 3 — Reconsider the Topology (Standard V-I with Grounded Load):**
In the standard V-I converter, $V_{in}$ is applied through $R_1$ to the inverting input, and the load $R_L$ is connected between the output and ground (not at the inverting node).

**Corrected Circuit Analysis:**
- $V_{in}$ at inverting input via $R_1$.
- Non-inverting input grounded (virtual ground at inverting input = 0).
- Load $R_L$ between output and ground.

**Step 1 — Virtual Ground:** $V^- = 0$

**Step 2 — Current through $R_1$:**
$$I_{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 3 — Current cannot enter op-amp, so it must flow through $R_L$:**
$$I_L = I_{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result:**
$$\boxed{I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}}$$

The load current is **directly proportional to $V_{in}$** and **independent of $R_L$** (as long as the op-amp stays in linear region, i.e., $V_{out}$ remains within saturation limits: $V_{out} = I_L \cdot R_L$ must satisfy $-V_{sat} \leq V_{out} \leq +V_{sat}$).

---

## 3.9 Derivation: V-I Converter (Floating Load)

**Circuit Setup:**
- $V_{in}$ is applied to the non-inverting (+) input.
- The load $R_L$ is connected between the output $V_{out}$ and the inverting input (floating — neither terminal is grounded).
- A feedback resistor $R_1$ is connected from a fixed reference (or from the inverting input to the non-inverting input through $R_1$).

**Standard Topology:**
- The op-amp is in a non-inverting configuration.
- $V^+$ is the inverting input is grounded via $R_1$, with $R_f$ from output to inverting input.
- The load $R_L$ is connected from $V_{out}$ back to the inverting input (a feedback path).

**Step 1 — Let the inverting node voltage be $V^-$.**
Since the non-inverting input is at $V_{in}$:
$$V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Current Through Load:**
The current through the floating load $R_L$ flows from output to inverting input:
$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_L} = \dfrac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_L}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Feedback Condition:**
By the design of the V-I converter (with $R_1$ and $R_f$ chosen such that $R_f / R_1 = 0$ in some configurations or matched in others), the relationship simplifies to:
$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result:**
$$\boxed{I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}}$$

Same as the grounded load case! The key difference is that the **floating load** is not referenced to ground, which is useful for applications like **isolation amplifiers**, **plasma generation**, and **bio-electrode stimulation**.

---

## 3.10 Derivation: Howland Constant Current Source

**Circuit Setup:**
- One op-amp with **both positive and negative feedback**.
- $V_{in}$ is applied to the non-inverting (+) terminal through a resistor $R_3$.
- The non-inverting terminal is connected to ground through $R_4$ (so $R_3$ and $R_4$ form a voltage divider).
- The inverting terminal is connected to the **top of the load** through $R_1$.
- The output of the op-amp is connected to the inverting terminal through $R_2$ (negative feedback) **and** to the **top of the load** through $R_3$ (this feedback path doubles up, see standard diagram).

**Standard Howland Circuit:**
- Load $R_L$ is connected between the output node and ground.
- The inverting input is connected to the **top of the load** (i.e., to the output node) via $R_1$.
- The non-inverting input is connected to a voltage divider ($R_3, R_4$) from output to ground, and $V_{in}$ is injected via the divider.

**Step 1 — Voltage at Non-Inverting Input (Voltage Divider):**
$$V^+ = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} V_{out} + \text{contribution from } V_{in}$$

For a clean derivation, assume the standard Howland where $V_{in}$ is applied directly to the non-inverting input through $R_3$ and $R_4$ is to ground, with $R_1$ and $R_2$ being the input and feedback resistors of the inverting path.

**Step 1 — Standard Howland Formulation:**
$$V^+ = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_4 \parallel (\text{parallel branch})}{...}$$

Let's use the **canonical Howland equations** with matching condition $R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4$:

**Step 2 — Apply Virtual Short and KCL:**
The current through the load is:
$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_1} - \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_3}$$

Wait, more simply: in the matching condition, the current through the load is independent of $R_L$:
$$\boxed{I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}}$$

provided that the matching condition is met:
$$R_2 \cdot R_3 = R_1 \cdot R_4$$

**Detailed KCL Derivation:**
At the inverting node, by virtual open:
$$\dfrac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_2} = \dfrac{V^- - V_L}{R_1}$$

At the non-inverting node:
$$V^+ = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} + V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}$$

(Note: $V^+$ depends on both $V_{in}$ and $V_{out}$ in this circuit.)

**Step 3 — Apply Virtual Short ($V^+ = V^-$):**
After substitution and the matching condition $R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4$:
$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_1}$$

And using the constraint on $V_{out}$ from the circuit equation:
$$V_{out} = V_{in}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right) - I_L \cdot R_L \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$$

Solving for $I_L$ with the matching condition:
$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Final Result (KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula):**
$$\boxed{I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} \quad \text{subject to} \quad R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4}$$

The matching condition is **critical** — without it, $I_L$ will depend on $R_L$ and the circuit will not be a true constant current source.

---

## 3.11 Derivation: I-V Converter (Trans-Impedance Amplifier)

**Circuit Setup:**
- An input current source $I_{in}$ is connected to the inverting (−) input.
- The non-inverting (+) input is grounded.
- A feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output to the inverting input.

**Step 1 — Virtual Ground:**
$$V^- = V^+ = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at Inverting Node:**
The current entering the node from the source must equal the current leaving through $R_f$ (since no current enters the op-amp):
$$I_{in} = I_{R_f}$$

**Step 3 — Express $I_{R_f}$ in Terms of $V_{out}$:**
$$I_{R_f} = \dfrac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = \dfrac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**
$$I_{in} = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$
$$V_{out} = -I_{in} \cdot R_f$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{V_{out} = -I_{in} \cdot R_f}$$

The output voltage is **proportional to the input current**, and the proportionality constant is $-R_f$ (the **trans-impedance gain**). The negative sign indicates inversion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important?**
> Many sensors (photodiodes, phototransistors, pH electrodes) produce tiny currents (μA or nA range). The I-V converter converts these tiny currents into measurable voltages. This is the foundational circuit of every optical communication receiver and biomedical instrumentation front-end.

---

## 3.12 Summary of Key Derivations (KTU 2024 Quick Reference)

| **Configuration** | **Final Formula** | **Critical Condition** |
|---|---|---|
| Inverting Amp | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_{in}$ | Virtual ground at inverting node |
| Non-Inverting Amp | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$ | $V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$ |
| Voltage Follower | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | $R_f = 0$, $R_1 = \infty$ |
| Inverting Adder | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_i}\sum V_i$ | All input resistors $R_i$ |
| Non-Inverting Adder | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_a}\right) \cdot \dfrac{\sum V_i}{n}$ | Matched input resistors |
| Differential Amp | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ | $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ |
| Subtractor | $V_{out} = V_2 - V_1$ | All resistors equal |
| V-I (Grounded) | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$ | $R_L$ between output and ground |
| V-I (Floating) | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$ | $R_L$ between output and inverting node |
| Howland Source | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$ | $R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4$ (matching) |
| I-V Converter | $V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f$ | Current source at inverting node |

**Key Insight:** The voltage follower has **infinite input impedance** (no loading of source) and **zero output impedance** (drives any load), but provides **unity voltage gain**.
