# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an ideal operational amplifier. List any four ideal characteristics. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**
An ideal operational amplifier is a differential amplifier with infinite voltage gain, infinite input impedance, zero output impedance, infinite bandwidth, infinite CMRR, and zero output for zero differential input.

**Four Ideal Characteristics:**
1. **Open-loop voltage gain ($A$):** $A \rightarrow \infty$
2. **Input impedance ($R_{in}$):** $R_{in} \rightarrow \infty$ (draws no current)
3. **Output impedance ($R_{out}$):** $R_{out} = 0$ (drives any load)
4. **Common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR):** $\text{CMRR} \rightarrow \infty$ (rejects common-mode signals)

---

### Question 2: State the two golden rules used in the analysis of ideal op-amp circuits. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**
The two golden rules used in the analysis of ideal op-amp circuits with negative feedback are:

**Rule 1 — Virtual Short:**
$$V^+ = V^-$$
The voltage at the inverting input is forced to be equal to the voltage at the non-inverting input. This is because the open-loop gain is infinite and the output is finite.

**Rule 2 — Virtual Open:**
$$I^+ = I^- = 0$$
No current flows into either input terminal of the op-amp. This is because the input impedance is infinite.

These two rules allow every closed-loop op-amp circuit to be analyzed using simple Kirchhoff's laws.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Inverting and Non-Inverting Amplifier Analysis

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Part (a)** [7 Marks]: With a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the closed-loop voltage gain of an **inverting amplifier** using an ideal op-amp. Mention the conditions assumed.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Diagram (as described in Section 4.2):**
- Input $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting (−) terminal via resistor $R_1$.
- The non-inverting (+) terminal is connected to ground.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output $V_{out}$ back to the inverting terminal.

**Step 2 — Assumed Conditions:**
- The op-amp is **ideal** (infinite gain, infinite input impedance, zero output impedance).
- The circuit operates in the **linear region** (negative feedback is present).

**Step 3 — Apply Golden Rule 1 (Virtual Short):**
Since $V^+ = 0$ (grounded), by virtual short:
$$V^- = V^+ = 0 \text{ V}$$
The inverting node is a **virtual ground**.

**Step 4 — Apply Golden Rule 2 (Virtual Open) + KCL at Inverting Node:**
No current flows into the op-amp's inverting input, so the current through $R_1$ equals the current through $R_f$:
$$I_1 = I_f$$

**Step 5 — Compute Each Current:**
$$I_1 = \dfrac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$
$$I_f = \dfrac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 6 — Equate and Solve:**
$$\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$
$$V_{out} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_{in}$$

**Step 7 — Closed-Loop Gain:**
$$A_{CL} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$$

The **negative sign** indicates phase inversion (180°). The gain is determined entirely by the **ratio** of the two resistors.

---

**Part (b)** [7 Marks]: For the same op-amp, derive the closed-loop gain of a **non-inverting amplifier**. Compare the two configurations.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Diagram (as described in Section 4.3):**
- Input $V_{in}$ is applied directly to the non-inverting (+) terminal.
- A resistor $R_1$ connects the inverting terminal to ground.
- A feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output $V_{out}$ back to the inverting terminal.

**Step 2 — Apply Golden Rule 1 (Virtual Short):**
$$V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at Inverting Node (Virtual Open):**
The current through $R_1$ (from inverting node to ground) equals the current through $R_f$ (from output to inverting node):
$$\dfrac{V^-}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V^- = V_{in}$:**
$$\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-Multiply and Solve:**
$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_1 \cdot V_{out} - R_1 \cdot V_{in}$$
$$R_1 \cdot V_{out} = V_{in}(R_f + R_1)$$
$$V_{out} = V_{in}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Closed-Loop Gain:**
$$A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$$

The output is **in phase** with the input. Gain is always ≥ 1.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Parameter** | **Inverting Amplifier** | **Non-Inverting Amplifier** |
|---|---|---|
| Input Impedance | $R_1$ (low) | Very high (≈ $R_{in} \cdot A_{CL}$) |
| Output Impedance | Low | Very low |
| Phase | 180° (inverted) | 0° (in phase) |
| Gain Formula | $-R_f / R_1$ | $1 + R_f / R_1$ |
| Gain Range | Can be < 1, = 1, or > 1 | Always ≥ 1 |
| Virtual Ground | Yes (at inverting node) | No (inverting node follows $V_{in}$) |

---

### Question B: Differential Amplifier and V-I Converter Analysis

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Part (a)** [7 Marks]: With a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the output of a **differential amplifier** (one op-amp version). What is the matching condition required for common-mode rejection?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Diagram (as described in Section 4.4):**
- $V_1$ is applied to the inverting (−) terminal via $R_1$.
- $V_2$ is applied to the non-inverting (+) terminal via $R_2$.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ from output to inverting terminal.
- Resistor $R_3$ from non-inverting terminal to ground.

**Step 2 — Voltage at Non-Inverting Terminal (Voltage Divider):**
Since no current flows into the op-amp's non-inverting input:
$$V^+ = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Virtual Short:**
$$V^- = V^+ = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 4 — Apply KCL at Inverting Node (Virtual Open):**
$$\dfrac{V_1 - V^-}{R_1} = \dfrac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**
$$V_{out} = V^-\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $V^+$ from Step 2:**
$$V_{out} = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Matching Condition:**
For a **true differential amplifier** that rejects common-mode signals, we require:
$$\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$$

Under this condition, the output simplifies to:
$$\boxed{V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)}$$

**Step 8 — CMRR Discussion:**
- If $V_1 = V_2 = V_{cm}$ (common-mode signal), the ideal output is $V_{out} = 0$ — full common-mode rejection.
- In real op-amps, finite CMRR results in a small common-mode output.
- The matching condition is **essential** to achieve the highest possible CMRR.

---

**Part (b)** [7 Marks]: Explain the operation of a **V-I converter with grounded load**. Derive the expression for the load current. Also write a note on the **Howland constant current source** and its matching condition.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — V-I Converter (Grounded Load):**

**Circuit Setup:**
- $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting (−) terminal through $R_1$.
- Non-inverting (+) terminal is grounded.
- Load $R_L$ is connected between the output and ground.

**Step 1 — Virtual Ground at Inverting Node:**
$$V^- = V^+ = 0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Current Through $R_1$:**
$$I_1 = \dfrac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 3 — KCL at Inverting Node:**
Since the op-amp draws no input current, all the current from $R_1$ must flow through the load $R_L$:
$$I_L = I_1 = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result:**
$$\boxed{I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}}$$

**Key Observation:** $I_L$ is **independent of $R_L$**, as long as the op-amp is in the linear region. The op-amp automatically adjusts its output voltage to satisfy this. The compliance voltage is:
$$V_{out} = I_L \cdot R_L$$
and must satisfy $-V_{sat} \leq V_{out} \leq +V_{sat}$.

**Part (ii) — Howland Constant Current Source:**

The **Howland current source** is an improvement over the basic V-I converter in that it can drive a **floating load** with constant current using a single op-amp.

**Circuit Description:**
- One op-amp with both **positive and negative feedback**.
- The inverting input is connected to the top of the load via $R_1$.
- The output is connected to the inverting input via $R_2$ (negative feedback).
- The non-inverting input receives $V_{in}$ via a voltage divider formed by $R_3$ and $R_4$.

**Key Result:**
$$\boxed{I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}}$$

**Critical Matching Condition:**
$$R_2 \cdot R_3 = R_1 \cdot R_4$$

If this condition is **not satisfied**, the load current becomes a function of $R_L$, defeating the purpose of the constant current source. This is the most common cause of failure in Howland circuits and a frequent source of KTU exam questions.

**Advantages over Basic V-I:**
- Can drive floating loads.
- Better stability and linearity.
- Single op-amp solution.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in inverting amplifier gain ($V_{out} = -R_f/R_1 \cdot V_{in}$, not $V_{out} = R_f/R_1 \cdot V_{in}$). KTU examiners **always** deduct 1-2 marks for this.
> 2. **Confusing inverting adder and non-inverting adder** — the formulas are structurally different.
> 3. **Writing the differential amplifier formula without the matching condition** $\frac{R_f}{R_1} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}$. Always state this explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply the virtual open rule** in KCL at the inverting node.
> 5. **Forgetting the load current's compliance voltage limit** when discussing V-I converters.
> 6. **In Howland circuits, omitting the matching condition** $R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4$ is a **direct 2-mark deduction**.
> 7. **Sign confusion in subtractor** — clearly define which input is at the inverting and which at the non-inverting terminal.
> 8. **Forgetting to draw the circuit diagram** — for a 7-mark derivation sub-part, the circuit diagram carries **at least 1-2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam!):**

### 🔑 Universal Concepts
- ☐ **Golden Rule 1:** $V^+ = V^-$ (Virtual Short, only with negative feedback)
- ☐ **Golden Rule 2:** $I^+ = I^- = 0$ (Virtual Open)
- ☐ **Op-amp needs negative feedback** to operate in linear region
- ☐ **Open-loop op-amp** is a comparator (saturates to $\pm V_{sat}$)

### 🔑 Inverting Amplifier
- ☐ Gain: $A_{CL} = -R_f / R_1$
- ☐ Inverting input is at **virtual ground**
- ☐ Input impedance = $R_1$ (low!)
- ☐ Output is 180° out of phase
- ☐ Acts as a current-to-voltage converter (trans-impedance)

### 🔑 Non-Inverting Amplifier
- ☐ Gain: $A_{CL} = 1 + R_f / R_1$
- ☐ Both inputs are at $V_{in}$
- ☐ Input impedance is **very high** (excellent buffer)
- ☐ Output is **in phase** with input
- ☐ Gain is always ≥ 1 (cannot attenuate)

### 🔑 Voltage Follower
- ☐ Gain = 1, $V_{out} = V_{in}$
- ☐ $R_f = 0$, $R_1 = \infty$
- ☐ Used as a **buffer** (impedance transformer)
- ☐ Highest input impedance, lowest output impedance

### 🔑 Summing Amplifier (Inverting Adder)
- ☐ $V_{out} = -R_f \sum (V_i / R_i)$
- ☐ Inputs to inverting node
- ☐ With equal resistors: $V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + \dots)$
- ☐ Used in audio mixers, DACs

### 🔑 Non-Inverting Adder
- ☐ $V_{out} = (1 + R_f/R_a) \cdot \frac{\sum V_i}{n}$ (for matched input resistors)
- ☐ Inputs to non-inverting node
- ☐ Output is **positive** sum
- ☐ Less common than inverting adder in practice

### 🔑 Differential Amplifier
- ☐ $V_{out} = (R_f / R_1) \cdot (V_2 - V_1)$
- ☐ Matching: $R_f / R_1 = R_3 / R_2$
- ☐ Rejects common-mode signals
- ☐ Foundation of instrumentation amplifiers

### 🔑 Subtractor
- ☐ Single op-amp version: $V_{out} = (R_f/R_1)(V_2 - V_1)$
- ☐ Two op-amp version: $V_{out} = V_2 - V_1$ (with all resistors equal)
- ☐ Used in error amplifiers, control systems

### 🔑 V-I Converter
- ☐ Grounded load: $I_L = V_{in} / R_1$
- ☐ Floating load: $I_L = V_{in} / R_1$ (different topology)
- ☐ Compliance: $|V_{out}| \leq V_{sat}$
- ☐ Used in 4-20 mA loops, LED drivers

### 🔑 Howland Current Source
- ☐ $I_L = V_{in} / R_1$
- ☐ **Critical:** $R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4$ (matching)
- ☐ Drives floating loads with constant current
- ☐ Mismatch → circuit oscillates or fails to regulate

### 🔑 I-V Converter
- ☐ $V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f$
- ☐ Used for photodiodes, sensors
- ☐ Inverting input is at virtual ground
- ☐ Trans-impedance gain = $-R_f$

### 🔑 Master Memorization List (One-Line Formulas)
- ☐ Inverting: $A = -R_f/R_1$
- ☐ Non-Inverting: $A = 1 + R_f/R_1$
- ☐ Follower: $A = 1$
- ☐ Inverting Adder: $V_o = -R_f \sum (V_i/R_i)$
- ☐ Difference Amp: $V_o = (R_f/R_1)(V_2 - V_1)$
- ☐ V-I (both): $I_L = V_{in}/R_1$
- ☐ Howland: $I_L = V_{in}/R_1$ with $R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4$
- ☐ I-V: $V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:**
> When solving a 14-mark question on this topic, **always start with the circuit diagram** (1-2 marks), state the **two golden rules** (1 mark), apply KCL at the **inverting or non-inverting node** (2-3 marks), and derive the **final formula** (2-3 marks). Substitute any numerical values if given, and **box the final answer** (1 mark). Allocate the remaining marks for proper justification of the matching conditions, if applicable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrumentation amplifier (3 op amp design), Op amp integrator, Op amp differentiator, Op amp circuit using diodes, Rectifier (half and full wave), Peak detector, Sample and hold circuit, Log and antilog amplifier, Multiplier and divider
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT404 - INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Instrumentation amplifier (3 op amp design), Op amp integrator, Op amp differentiator, Op amp circuit using diodes, Rectifier (half and full wave), Peak detector, Sample and hold circuit, Log and antilog amplifier, Multiplier and divider

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Instrumentation Amplifier (3 Op-Amp Design)

**Formal Definition:** An instrumentation amplifier (IA) is a precision, closed-loop gain block whose output is a highly accurate, amplified version of the difference between two input signals, with **infinite input impedance**, **zero output impedance**, and exceptional **Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**. The 3 op-amp topology is the industry-standard architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 3 op-amp design is the most frequently asked topology in KTU ESE papers. Master the expression for output voltage and CMRR derivation.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine two microphones (left & right ears of a stethoscope) picking up a faint heartbeat amid loud background noise. A normal amplifier would amplify the noise along with the heartbeat. An instrumentation amplifier is like a *selective hearing device* — it amplifies only the **difference** between the two microphones and *rejects* the common noise both pick up.

---

### 1.2 Op-Amp Integrator

**Formal Definition:** A circuit that produces an output voltage waveform which is the **time integral** of the input voltage waveform, implemented using an op-amp with a **capacitor in the feedback path** and a **resistor at the input**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a water tank being filled — the water level (output) is the **accumulation (integral)** of the flow rate (input) over time. The integrator "accumulates" the input signal.

> [!NOTE]
> An ideal integrator behaves as a **low-pass filter** with a roll-off of **-20 dB/decade**.

---

### 1.3 Op-Amp Differentiator

**Formal Definition:** A circuit whose output is proportional to the **time derivative** of the input voltage, implemented using a **capacitor at the input** and a **resistor in the feedback path**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** If the integrator is like a water tank filling up (accumulating), the differentiator is like a *speedometer* — it measures the **rate of change** of distance (position) with respect to time.

> [!WARNING]
> A practical differentiator suffers from **high-frequency noise amplification** and **instability**. KTU often asks for a comparison between ideal and practical (lossy) differentiator.

---

### 1.4 Op-Amp Circuits Using Diodes — Precision Rectifiers

**Formal Definition:** Conventional diode rectifiers cannot rectify signals below **≈0.7 V** due to the diode's forward voltage drop. By placing the diode inside the op-amp's feedback loop, the forward drop is **effectively divided by the open-loop gain**, making rectification of signals as small as a few millivolts possible.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A normal diode is a "high-jump bar" — signals below 0.7 V cannot cross. The op-amp feedback acts as a *powerful helper* that lifts weak signals over the bar, so even tiny signals get rectified perfectly.

---

### 1.5 Peak Detector

**Formal Definition:** A circuit that detects and **holds the peak (maximum) value** of an input signal. It typically uses an op-amp, a diode, and a hold capacitor.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Like a *flood-mark on a wall* — the highest water level ever reached remains visible even after the water recedes.

---

### 1.6 Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit

**Formal Definition:** A circuit that samples an analog input signal at a specific instant and **holds (freezes)** that value for a finite duration, using an electronically controlled switch (MOSFET) and a storage capacitor.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A *camera taking a snapshot* — at the precise moment the shutter is pressed (sample), the scene is captured and frozen in memory (hold) for later viewing.

---

### 1.7 Log and Antilog Amplifier

**Formal Definition:** 
- **Log Amplifier:** Output is proportional to the **logarithm** of the input. Uses a diode/transistor in the feedback path of an inverting op-amp.
- **Antilog Amplifier:** Output is proportional to the **antilogarithm (exponential)** of the input. Uses a diode/transistor at the input of an inverting op-amp.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A log amplifier is like a *compression lens* — it squashes large numbers into a manageable range. An antilog amplifier is the *expansion lens* — it restores the original wide range.

---

### 1.8 Multiplier and Divider

**Formal Definition:**
- **Multiplier:** Output voltage $V_o = k \cdot V_x \cdot V_y$
- **Divider:** Output voltage $V_o = k \cdot \dfrac{V_z}{V_x}$

Implemented using the **log-antilog principle** or the **Gilbert cell** (transconductance multiplier).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU's focus is on the **log-antilog multiplier/divider topology**, which directly tests Module-2 understanding of log/antilog amplifiers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Instrumentation Amplifier — Operational Analysis

**Block-level Architecture:**
1. **Input Buffer Stage (Op-Amps A1 & A2):** Provide high input impedance. Both non-inverting inputs receive $V_1$ and $V_2$. The gain-setting resistor $R_G$ is between their inverting terminals.
2. **Difference Amplifier Stage (Op-Amp A3):** Rejects common-mode signal and provides single-ended output.

**Key Operating Principles:**
- Due to virtual short, the voltage across $R_G$ is $V_1 - V_2$.
- The same current flows through $R_G$ and the two feedback resistors $R_1$.
- The output of stage 1 is the difference $(V_2 - V_1)$ amplified by $\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$.
- Stage 2 is a standard difference amplifier with gain $\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$.

**Real-world Utility:** Used in **ECG/EEG machines**, **strain gauge bridges**, **thermocouple amplifiers**, and any application extracting small differential signals from a noisy environment.

---

### 2.2 Op-Amp Integrator — Theory

**Working Principle:**
- The inverting input is at **virtual ground** ($V^- = 0$).
- Input current $I_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_i}$ flows entirely into the feedback capacitor $C_f$.
- Voltage across $C_f$ is $V_c = \dfrac{1}{C_f}\int I_{in}\,dt = \dfrac{1}{R_i C_f}\int V_{in}\,dt$.
- Since $V_o = -V_c$, we get the integration action.

**Practical Integrator:** Adds $R_f$ in parallel with $C_f$ to limit DC gain and prevent saturation. Functions as an integrator only for $f \gg \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$.

---

### 2.3 Op-Amp Differentiator — Theory

**Working Principle:**
- Current through input capacitor: $I_{in} = C_i \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$.
- This current flows through feedback resistor $R_f$.
- Output: $V_o = -I_{in} R_f = -R_f C_i \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$.

**Practical Differentiator:** Adds $R_i$ in series with $C_i$ and $C_f$ in parallel with $R_f$ for stability and noise suppression.

---

### 2.4 Precision Rectifiers — Theory

**Half-Wave Precision Rectifier:**
- During **positive half-cycle**: Diode $D_1$ is OFF, $D_2$ is ON, output is grounded ⇒ $V_o = 0$.
- During **negative half-cycle**: $D_1$ is ON, $D_2$ is OFF, output follows input ⇒ $V_o = -V_{in}$.
- Net effect: Negative half-wave is passed, positive is blocked. Output is **inverted** with respect to standard negative half-wave rectifier.

**Full-Wave Precision Rectifier (Absolute Value Circuit):**
- Combines a precision half-wave rectifier with a summing amplifier.
- Output: $V_o = \vert V_{in} \vert$

---

### 2.5 Peak Detector — Theory

**Operation:**
- During positive peak of input, diode conducts, charging the hold capacitor $C$ to $V_{peak}$.
- When input falls below the capacitor voltage, diode becomes reverse-biased, isolating the capacitor.
- A buffer (voltage follower) provides the output without loading the capacitor.
- A reset transistor (MOSFET) is used to discharge the capacitor for the next cycle.

**Real-world Utility:** Demodulators in AM receivers, test equipment, signal monitoring.

---

### 2.6 Sample-and-Hold Circuit — Theory

**Two Phases:**
- **Sample (Track) Mode:** Control signal closes the MOSFET switch; capacitor $C$ charges to track the input.
- **Hold Mode:** Control signal opens the switch; capacitor retains its last voltage.

**Key Parameters (KTU focus):**
- **Acquisition Time:** Time to charge the capacitor to the new input value within a specified error.
- **Aperture Time:** Delay between the hold command and actual opening of the switch.
- **Droop Rate:** $\dfrac{dV_c}{dt} = \dfrac{I_L}{C}$ (rate of voltage decay due to leakage).

**Real-world Utility:** A/D converters, DSP systems, multiplexed data acquisition.

---

### 2.7 Log and Antilog Amplifier — Theory

**Log Amplifier (using BJT):**
- $V_{BE} = V_T \ln\left(\dfrac{I_C}{I_S}\right)$ where $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 26\,\text{mV}$ at 300 K.
- Input current $I_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R}$ flows through the BJT (used as diode-connected) in feedback.
- $V_o = -V_{BE} = -V_T \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{I_S R}\right)$

**Antilog Amplifier:**
- BJT at the input, feedback resistor $R_f$.
- $V_o = -R_f I_S\, e^{V_{in}/V_T}$

---

### 2.8 Multiplier and Divider (Log-Antilog Method)

**Multiplier:** Uses two log amplifiers (one for $V_x$, one for $V_y$), a summing amplifier (adds the logs), and one antilog amplifier:
$$V_o = K \cdot V_x \cdot V_y$$
where $K$ is the scale factor (set by $V_{ref}$).

**Divider:** Uses two log amplifiers (one for $V_x$, one for $V_z$), a difference amplifier (subtracts the logs), and one antilog amplifier:
$$V_o = K \cdot \dfrac{V_z}{V_x}$$

---

### 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Circuit | Key Equation | Critical Parameters |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **3 Op-Amp IA** | $V_o = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_1 - V_2)$ | CMRR depends on resistor matching |
| 2 | **Ideal Integrator** | $V_o(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R_i C_f} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(t)\,dt$ | Frequency: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_i C_f}$ |
| 3 | **Ideal Differentiator** | $V_o(t) = -R_f C_i \dfrac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$ | Frequency: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_i}$ |
| 4 | **Practical Differentiator** | $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_i},\ f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_i C_f}$ | Differentiates for $f_a < f < f_b$ |
| 5 | **Precision HW Rectifier** | $V_o = 0$ for $V_{in}>0$; $V_o = -V_{in}$ for $V_{in}<0$ | Dead zone eliminated |
| 6 | **Precision FW Rectifier** | $V_o = \vert V_{in} \vert$ | Uses summing amplifier |
| 7 | **Peak Detector** | $V_o = V_{peak}$ (held) | Droop $= \dfrac{I_{leak}}{C}$ |
| 8 | **S/H Acquisition Time** | $t_{acq} \approx 7\,\tau = 7 R_{on} C$ | $\tau = R_{on} C$ |
| 9 | **Log Amplifier** | $V_o = -V_T \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{I_S R}\right)$ | $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$ at 300 K |
| 10 | **Antilog Amplifier** | $V_o = -R_f I_S e^{V_{in}/V_T}$ | Temperature sensitive |
| 11 | **Log-Antilog Multiplier** | $V_o = \dfrac{V_x V_y}{V_{ref}}$ | $V_{ref}$ is scale voltage |
| 12 | **Log-Antilog Divider** | $V_o = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{V_z}{V_x}$ | $V_x$ must be $> 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application Spotlight:** The instrumentation amplifier is the *backbone* of biomedical instrumentation. The precision rectifier enables AC voltmeters with **mV-level accuracy**. The log/antilog family forms the building block of analog computational circuits performing **multiplication, division, roots, and powers**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: 3 Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Output

Let the two input signals be $V_1$ (applied to non-inverting input of A1) and $V_2$ (applied to non-inverting input of A2).

**Step 1 — Apply virtual short to A1 and A2:**

$$V^-_{A1} = V^+_{A1} = V_1$$
$$V^-_{A2} = V^+_{A2} = V_2$$

**Step 2 — Voltage across gain-setting resistor $R_G$:**

The resistor $R_G$ is connected between the inverting inputs of A1 and A2. Therefore:

$$V_{R_G} = V^-_{A1} - V^-_{A2} = V_1 - V_2$$

**Step 3 — Current through $R_G$:**

Since no current enters the op-amp inputs, the same current $I$ flows through $R_G$ and through both $R_1$ feedback resistors:

$$I = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$

**Step 4 — Output of A1 (left op-amp):**

The current $I$ flows from $V^-_{A1}$ through $R_1$ to the output of A1. The voltage across the upper $R_1$ is $I R_1$:

$$V_{o1} = V_1 + I R_1 = V_1 + \left(\dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}\right) R_1$$

$$\Rightarrow V_{o1} = V_1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 5 — Output of A2 (right op-amp):**

The current $I$ flows from the output of A2 through its $R_1$ to $V^-_{A2}$. The voltage at $V_{o2}$ is *below* $V_2$ by $I R_1$:

$$V_{o2} = V_2 - I R_1 = V_2 - \left(\dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}\right) R_1$$

$$\Rightarrow V_{o2} = V_2 - \dfrac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 6 — Difference between the two intermediate outputs:**

$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2) + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)$$

$$\Rightarrow V_{o1} - V_{o2} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 7 — Apply standard difference amplifier to A3:**

The second stage is a difference amplifier with $V_{o1}$ and $V_{o2}$ as inputs, using matched resistors ($R_2$ at both inputs and $R_3$ in feedback). Standard derivation gives:

$$V_o = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o2} - V_{o1})$$

**Step 8 — Final combined output voltage:**

$$V_o = -\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$

The negative sign depends on the conventional ordering of inputs. In many textbooks, the assignment of $V_1$ and $V_2$ to inputs is reversed, yielding the more common form:

$$\boxed{V_o = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_1 - V_2)}$$

**Conclusion:** The gain is set by a *single* resistor $R_G$, which is the primary reason this architecture dominates instrumentation design — gain can be varied with one component without disturbing the matched resistor pairs in the second stage.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Op-Amp Integrator Output

The inverting input is at virtual ground ($V^- = 0$). The current through the input resistor $R_i$ is:

$$I_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in} - 0}{R_i} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_i}$$

**Step 2 — This current charges the feedback capacitor:**

The same current flows into $C_f$ (no current enters the op-amp input). The voltage across $C_f$ is:

$$V_{C_f} = \dfrac{1}{C_f}\int_{0}^{t} I_{in}\,dt = \dfrac{1}{R_i C_f}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply KVL at the inverting node:**

The output voltage is the negative of the voltage across the capacitor (since the op-amp output sits at the other plate of $C_f$, and the inverting input is grounded):

$$V_o = -V_{C_f} = -\dfrac{1}{R_i C_f}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}\,dt$$

$$\boxed{V_o(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R_i C_f}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(t)\,dt}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Op-Amp Differentiator Output

**Step 1 — Current through the input capacitor:**

The voltage across the capacitor is $V_{in}$ (since the inverting input is at virtual ground). The current through the capacitor is:

$$I_{in} = C_i \dfrac{d(V_{in} - 0)}{dt} = C_i \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — This current flows through feedback resistor $R_f$:**

$$V_o = -I_{in} R_f = -R_f C_i \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

$$\boxed{V_o(t) = -R_f C_i \dfrac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Precision Half-Wave Rectifier

Consider the standard precision HW rectifier with diodes $D_1$ (in feedback) and $D_2$ (from inverting input to ground), input resistor $R_i$, and feedback resistor $R_f$ (sometimes replaced by direct connection for unity-gain).

**Case 1: $V_{in} > 0$**
- Inverting input tends to go positive.
- Op-amp output swings negative, reverse-biasing $D_1$ and forward-biasing $D_2$.
- $D_2$ clamps the inverting input to ~0 V (virtual ground).
- $D_1$ is OFF, so the output is **isolated** from the op-amp and held at **0 V** through the load (if any).
$$V_o = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad V_{in} > 0$$

**Case 2: $V_{in} < 0$**
- Inverting input tends to go negative.
- Op-amp output swings positive, forward-biasing $D_1$ and reverse-biasing $D_2$.
- $D_1$ is ON, closing the feedback loop.
- The op-amp with $D_1$ acts as a normal inverting amplifier (assuming $R_f = R_i$).
$$V_o = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_i}V_{in} \quad \text{for} \quad V_{in} < 0$$

For unity gain, $R_f = R_i$:

$$\boxed{V_o = \begin{cases} 0, & V_{in} > 0 \\ -V_{in}, & V_{in} < 0 \end{cases}}$$

This is an **inverted half-wave rectified output** with a precision of a few microvolts (instead of 0.7 V).

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Precision Full-Wave Rectifier (Absolute Value)

A precision FW rectifier is constructed by combining a **precision HW rectifier** with a **summing amplifier**. The architecture has three op-amps:

- **A1**: Precision HW rectifier producing $V_{a} = -V_{in}$ when $V_{in} < 0$, else $V_a = 0$.
- **A2**: A unity-gain inverter producing $V_{b} = -V_{in}$ always.
- **A3**: Summing amplifier that combines $V_a$ and $V_b$ with proper weights.

When $V_{in} > 0$: $V_a = 0$, $V_b = -V_{in}$ ⇒ $V_o = -V_{in}$ (but sign corrected by choosing weights).
When $V_{in} < 0$: $V_a = -V_{in}$ (positive), $V_b = -V_{in}$ (negative) ⇒ $V_o = V_{in} - V_{in}$ adjustment yields $|V_{in}|$.

With proper resistor ratios (commonly $R_f/R = 2$ for the half-wave path and unity for the inverting path):

$$\boxed{V_o = \vert V_{in} \vert}$$

---

### 3.6 Log Amplifier Derivation (using BJT in feedback)

**Step 1 — Apply virtual short:**

The inverting input is at 0 V, so the BJT's emitter is at virtual ground.

**Step 2 — Input current equals BJT collector current:**

$$I_C = I_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R}$$

**Step 3 — BJT equation in forward active region:**

$$I_C = I_S \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{BE}$:**

$$V_{BE} = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_C}{I_S}\right) = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{I_S R}\right)$$

**Step 5 — The output is the negative of $V_{BE}$:**

$$\boxed{V_o = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{I_S R}\right)}$$

For an ideal diode, replace $I_S e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$ with the diode equation, giving:

$$V_o = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{I_S R}\right) \approx -(60\,\text{mV})\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{I_S R}\right)$$

---

### 3.7 Multiplier Derivation (Log-Antilog Method)

**Step 1 — Take log of $V_x$:**

$$V_{L1} = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_x}{I_S R_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Take log of $V_y$:**

$$V_{L2} = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_y}{I_S R_2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Sum the two log outputs:**

$$V_S = V_{L1} + V_{L2} = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_x V_y}{I_S^2 R_1 R_2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply antilog (using the antilog amplifier with reference $V_{ref}$):**

$$V_o = -I_S R_3 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_S}{-V_T}\right) = -I_S R_3 \cdot \dfrac{V_x V_y}{I_S^2 R_1 R_2}$$

Simplifying:

$$\boxed{V_o = -\dfrac{R_3}{I_S R_1 R_2}\, V_x V_y}$$

By choosing $I_S$ and the resistors such that the scale factor equals $\dfrac{1}{V_{ref}}$:

$$V_o = \dfrac{V_x V_y}{V_{ref}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 3 Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeV1["V1 INPUT"] --> nodeA1["OPAMP A1\nNON INVERTING BUFFER"]
    nodeV2["V2 INPUT"] --> nodeA2["OPAMP A2\nNON INVERTING BUFFER"]
    nodeRG["GAIN RESISTOR RG"] -.-> nodeA1
    nodeRG -.-> nodeA2
    nodeA1 --> nodeR1A["FEEDBACK R1"]
    nodeR1A --> nodeA1
    nodeA1 --> nodeVo1["VO1 INTERMEDIATE"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeVo2["VO2 INTERMEDIATE"]
    nodeVo1 --> nodeA3["OPAMP A3\nDIFFERENCE AMP"]
    nodeVo2 --> nodeA3
    nodeA3 --> nodeR3["FEEDBACK R3 R2"]
    nodeR3 --> nodeA3
    nodeA3 --> nodeOut["V OUTPUT\nSINGLE ENDED"]
    nodeCMR["COMMON MODE NOISE\nREJECTED HERE"] -.-> nodeOut
```

> [!NOTE]
> The differential signal path is: **$V_1$, $V_2$ → input buffers (A1, A2) → gain stage controlled by $R_G$ → difference amplifier (A3) → single-ended $V_o$**. Common-mode signals appear equally at both $V_{o1}$ and $V_{o2}$ and cancel at the output of A3.

---

### 4.2 Op-Amp Integrator — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVin["V INPUT"] --> nodeRi["INPUT RESISTOR Ri"]
    nodeRi --> nodeVM["VIRTUAL GROUND\nINVERTING NODE"]
    nodeVM --> nodeCf["FEEDBACK CAPACITOR Cf"]
    nodeCf --> nodeVout["V OUTPUT\nNEGATIVE INTEGRAL"]
    nodeOpa["OPAMP"] --> nodeVout
    nodeVM --> nodeOpa
    nodeSat["OPAMP SATURATION\nPREVENTED BY Rf"] -.-> nodeCf
```

**Key Signal Behavior:**
- Input: Sine wave $\rightarrow$ Output: Cosine wave (with phase shift of $-90°$ and amplitude scaled by $\dfrac{1}{R_i C_f \omega}$)
- Input: Square wave $\rightarrow$ Output: Triangular wave

---

### 4.3 Op-Amp Differentiator — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVin["V INPUT"] --> nodeCi["INPUT CAPACITOR Ci"]
    nodeCi --> nodeVM["VIRTUAL GROUND\nINVERTING NODE"]
    nodeVM --> nodeRf["FEEDBACK RESISTOR Rf"]
    nodeRf --> nodeVout["V OUTPUT\nNEGATIVE DERIVATIVE"]
    nodeOpa["OPAMP"] --> nodeVout
    nodeVM --> nodeOpa
    nodeCmp["NOISE AND HF\nPROBLEMS HERE"] -.-> nodeVout
```

**Key Signal Behavior:**
- Input: Sine wave $\rightarrow$ Output: Cosine wave (with phase shift of $+90°$ and amplitude scaled by $R_f C_i \omega$)
- Input: Triangular wave $\rightarrow$ Output: Square wave

---

### 4.4 Precision Half-Wave Rectifier — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["V INPUT"] --> nodeRi["Ri INPUT RESISTOR"]
    nodeRi --> nodeVM["VIRTUAL GROUND NODE"]
    nodeVM --> nodeD2["DIODE D2\nTO GROUND"]
    nodeVM --> nodeOpa["OPAMP"]
    nodeOpa --> nodeD1["DIODE D1\nIN FEEDBACK"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeOut["V OUTPUT"]
    nodeRf["FEEDBACK RESISTOR Rf"] -.-> nodeD1
    nodePos["POSITIVE HALF CYCLE\nD1 OFF D2 ON\nVout EQUALS 0"] -.-> nodeOut
    nodeNeg["NEGATIVE HALF CYCLE\nD1 ON D2 OFF\nVout EQUALS NEGATIVE Vin"] -.-> nodeOut
```

---

### 4.5 Precision Full-Wave Rectifier (Absolute Value Circuit)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["V INPUT"] --> nodeA1["OPAMP A1\nPRECISION HW RECTIFIER"]
    nodeIn --> nodeA2["OPAMP A2\nUNITY INVERTER"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA3["OPAMP A3\nSUMMING AMPLIFIER"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3
    nodeA3 --> nodeOut["V OUTPUT\nEQUALS ABSOLUTE VALUE OF Vin"]
    nodeW1["WEIGHTED INPUT FROM A1"] -.-> nodeA3
    nodeW2["WEIGHTED INPUT FROM A2"] -.-> nodeA3
```

---

### 4.6 Peak Detector — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["V INPUT"] --> nodeOpa["OPAMP\nBUFFER CHARGER"]
    nodeOpa --> nodeD1["DIODE D1\nUNIDIRECTIONAL"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeCh["HOLD CAPACITOR C\nCHARGES TO V PEAK"]
    nodeCh --> nodeBuf["UNITY GAIN BUFFER\nOPAMP FOLLOWER"]
    nodeBuf --> nodeOut["V OUTPUT\nEQUALS V PEAK"]
    nodeReset["RESET MOSFET\nFOR DISCHARGE"] -.-> nodeCh
    nodeDroop["DROOP CAUSED BY\nLEAKAGE CURRENT"] -.-> nodeCh
```

---

### 4.7 Sample-and-Hold Circuit — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["V INPUT"] --> nodeSw["ANALOG SWITCH\nMOSFET"]
    nodeSw --> nodeCap["HOLD CAPACITOR C\nSTORES VOLTAGE"]
    nodeCap --> nodeBuf["HIGH INPUT IMPEDANCE BUFFER\nOPAMP FOLLOWER"]
    nodeBuf --> nodeOut["V OUTPUT"]
    nodeCtrl["CONTROL SIGNAL\nLOGIC COMMAND"] --> nodeSw
    nodeS["SAMPLE MODE\nSWITCH CLOSED\nCAPACITOR TRACKS Vin"] -.-> nodeSw
    nodeH["HOLD MODE\nSWITCH OPEN\nCAPACITOR FREEZES LAST VALUE"] -.-> nodeSw
```

**Operational State Table:**

| Control Signal | Switch State | Capacitor | Output |
|:--------------:|:------------:|:---------:|:------:|
| HIGH (Sample) | CLOSED | Charging | $V_o = V_{in}$ |
| LOW (Hold) | OPEN | Frozen | $V_o = V_{in}$ (at instant of hold) |

---

### 4.8 Log and Antilog Amplifier — Log-Antilog Multiplier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVx["Vx INPUT"] --> nodeLA1["LOG AMPLIFIER 1"]
    nodeVy["Vy INPUT"] --> nodeLA2["LOG AMPLIFIER 2"]
    nodeLA1 --> nodeSum["SUMMING AMPLIFIER\nADDS TWO LOGS"]
    nodeLA2 --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeAA["ANTILOG AMPLIFIER\nWITH Vref SCALING"]
    nodeAA --> nodeOut["V OUTPUT\nEQUALS Vx Vy DIVIDED BY Vref"]
    nodeVref["REFERENCE Vref\nSETS SCALE FACTOR"] -.-> nodeAA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The same architecture, with a **subtractor** replacing the summer, implements a **divider**. Both inputs to the log amplifiers must be **unipolar positive** for proper operation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
Define an instrumentation amplifier. List **any four** of its ideal characteristics.

**Model Answer:**

An **instrumentation amplifier (IA)** is a precision differential amplifier with high input impedance, high CMRR, and low output impedance, designed to amplify small differential signals in noisy environments.

**Four ideal characteristics:**
1. **Infinite input impedance** (so it does not load the source).
2. **Zero output impedance** (drives any load without loss).
3. **Infinite CMRR** (rejects common-mode noise completely).
4. **Infinite gain for differential signal, zero gain for common-mode signal** (ideally $A_d \to \infty$, $A_c = 0$).
5. *Additional:* Low offset voltage, low noise, high slew rate.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **peak detector** circuit using an op-amp.

**Model Answer:**

A peak detector detects and holds the **peak (maximum) value** of an input signal. The circuit consists of:
- An op-amp (charging buffer)
- A diode $D$ in series with the op-amp output
- A hold capacitor $C$ connected from the diode cathode to ground
- A unity-gain buffer (voltage follower) at the output to prevent loading

**Working:**
- When $V_{in}$ exceeds the capacitor voltage $V_c$, the op-amp output goes positive, forward-biasing $D$ and charging $C$ to the new peak.
- When $V_{in}$ falls below $V_c$, the diode becomes reverse-biased, isolating $C$.
- The output buffer provides $V_o = V_{peak}$ without discharging $C$.
- A reset MOSFET (controlled by a clock) discharges $C$ to prepare for the next peak detection.

**Application:** AM demodulators, signal monitoring, test & measurement instruments.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the output voltage expression of a **3 op-amp instrumentation amplifier**. Explain how the gain can be varied using a single resistor without affecting CMRR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a circuit diagram, explain the working of a **precision full-wave rectifier**. How does it overcome the limitations of a conventional diode rectifier? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Step 1 — State the topology:** The 3 op-amp IA has two input buffers (A1, A2) and one difference amplifier (A3). The gain-setting resistor $R_G$ is connected between the inverting inputs of A1 and A2. Feedback resistors $R_1$ are in the feedback paths of A1 and A2. Resistors $R_2$ and $R_3$ form the difference amplifier A3.

**Step 2 — Apply virtual short to A1 and A2:**

$$V_{A1}^- = V_1, \quad V_{A2}^- = V_2$$

**Step 3 — Current through $R_G$:**

$$I = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$

**Step 4 — Output of the first stage:**

$$V_{o1} = V_1 + I R_1, \quad V_{o2} = V_2 - I R_1$$

$$\Rightarrow V_{o2} - V_{o1} = (V_2 - V_1) - 2 I R_1 = -(V_1 - V_2)\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the difference amplifier formula for A3:**

$$V_o = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o2} - V_{o1}) = -\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}(V_1 - V_2)$$

By choosing the sign convention appropriately:

$$\boxed{V_o = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_1 - V_2)}$$

**Step 6 — Explanation of single-resistor gain control:**
- The differential gain $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ depends on $R_G$ (single variable resistor).
- The CMRR of the IA depends on the **matching ratio** of $R_3/R_2$ in the second stage, *not* on $R_G$.
- Therefore, varying $R_G$ changes the gain **without disturbing the matched resistor pairs** in the difference amplifier, preserving CMRR.
- *This is the primary advantage of the 3 op-amp IA topology over a single op-amp difference amplifier, where changing gain would alter resistor matching and degrade CMRR.*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Conventional Rectifier Limitation:**
- A silicon diode has a forward voltage drop of **≈ 0.7 V** (≈ 0.3 V for germanium).
- Signals below this threshold **cannot be rectified**.
- This makes conventional rectifiers unsuitable for **millivolt-level signals** (e.g., sensor outputs).

**Step 2 — Precision FW Rectifier (Absolute Value Circuit) Block Diagram:**

```
V_in → A1 (precision HW rectifier) → V_a
V_in → A2 (unity inverter) → V_b
V_a, V_b → A3 (summing amplifier) → V_o = |V_in|
```

**Step 3 — Working in two phases:**

*Phase 1: $V_{in} > 0$*
- A1's output $V_a = 0$ (precision HW rectifier blocks positive half)
- A2's output $V_b = -V_{in}$ (unity inverter)
- A3 sums with weights: $V_o = -V_b = V_{in}$

*Phase 2: $V_{in} < 0$*
- A1's output $V_a = -V_{in}$ (positive value)
- A2's output $V_b = -V_{in}$
- A3 sums: $V_o = V_a + V_b$ combination yields $V_o = -V_{in} = \vert V_{in} \vert$

**Step 4 — How it overcomes limitations:**
- The op-amp's high open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ (typically $10^5$ to $10^6$) effectively **divides the diode's forward drop** by $A_{OL}$.
- Effective diode drop: $V_{F,\text{eff}} = \dfrac{V_F}{1 + A_{OL}} \approx \text{few } \mu\text{V}$.
- Hence, **millivolt-level signals** are rectified accurately — useful in precision AC voltmeters and instrumentation.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the output expression for an **op-amp integrator** and an **op-amp differentiator**. Draw the input and output waveforms when a **square wave** is applied to both. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the operation of **log and antilog amplifiers** using a BJT. Show how the **log-antilog principle** implements an analog **multiplier**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Integrator Derivation:**

Input current: $I = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_i}$ flows into the feedback capacitor $C_f$. Since the inverting input is at virtual ground:

$$V_o = -\dfrac{1}{C_f}\int I\,dt = -\dfrac{1}{R_i C_f}\int V_{in}\,dt$$

**Differentiator Derivation:**

Current through the input capacitor: $I = C_i \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$. This flows through $R_f$:

$$V_o = -I R_f = -R_f C_i \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

**Waveforms (Square Wave Input):**

| Circuit | Input | Output |
|---------|-------|--------|
| Integrator | Square wave (0 to +V) | Triangular wave (rises linearly during HIGH, falls during LOW) |
| Differentiator | Triangular wave | Square wave (spikes at transitions) |

> For a true square wave into a differentiator, the output is a series of **impulses (spikes)** at each transition. In practice, with a non-ideal op-amp (finite slew rate and bandwidth), the output appears as sharp narrow pulses.

**Key Point:** The integrator produces a **lagging** output (phase shift of $-90°$), while the differentiator produces a **leading** output (phase shift of $+90°$).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(b)

**Log Amplifier Operation (BJT in feedback):**

- The BJT is diode-connected (collector shorted to base), placed in the feedback path of an inverting op-amp.
- Input current $I_C = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R}$ flows through the BJT.
- BJT equation: $I_C = I_S \exp(V_{BE}/V_T)$
- Solving: $V_{BE} = V_T \ln(I_C/I_S) = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{I_S R}\right)$
- Output: $V_o = -V_{BE} = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{I_S R}\right)$

**Antilog Amplifier Operation (BJT at input):**

- The BJT is diode-connected at the input, with a feedback resistor $R_f$.
- Voltage across BJT: $V_{BE} = V_{in}$ (input)
- Current: $I_C = I_S \exp(V_{in}/V_T)$
- Output: $V_o = -I_C R_f = -R_f I_S \exp(V_{in}/V_T)$

**Multiplier using Log-Antilog Principle (Block Diagram):**

```
V_x → Log Amp 1 → V_{L1}
V_y → Log Amp 2 → V_{L2}
V_{L1}, V_{L2} → Summing Amp → V_S = V_{L1} + V_{L2}
V_S → Antilog Amp (with V_{ref}) → V_o = V_x V_y / V_{ref}
```

**Step-by-step:**

$$V_{L1} = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_x}{I_S R_1}\right), \quad V_{L2} = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_y}{I_S R_2}\right)$$

$$V_S = V_{L1} + V_{L2} = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_x V_y}{I_S^2 R_1 R_2}\right)$$

Applying the antilog amplifier with reference $V_{ref}$:

$$V_o = -I_S R_3 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_S}{-V_T}\right) = \dfrac{R_3 I_S \cdot V_x V_y}{I_S^2 R_1 R_2 \cdot V_{ref}} \cdot V_{ref}$$

Choosing component values to make the scale factor unity:

$$\boxed{V_o = \dfrac{V_x \cdot V_y}{V_{ref}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Students often write the final expression of the IA with a wrong sign. Always check the assignment of $V_1$ and $V_2$ to A1 and A2. The sign depends on this.
> 2. **Forgetting the Single-Resistor Gain Control Logic:** When asked *why* the 3 op-amp IA is preferred, students forget to mention that **CMRR is preserved** when $R_G$ is varied — this is the most important answer the examiner looks for.
> 3. **Log Amplifier — Temperature Dependence:** The expression contains $V_T = kT/q$ which is **temperature-dependent**. Examiners may deduct marks if you don't state the practical need for temperature compensation circuits.
> 4. **Multiplier — Sign Restriction:** The log-antilog multiplier works only for **single-quadrant** operation (both inputs positive). Mentioning this limitation is essential.
> 5. **Differentiator — Unstable:** Failing to mention that the ideal differentiator is **unstable at high frequencies** and that the practical differentiator adds $R_i$ and $C_f$ for stability loses easy marks.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **3 Op-Amp IA** is a two-stage circuit: input buffers (high $Z_{in}$) + difference amplifier (high CMRR). Output: $V_o = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_1 - V_2)$.
- [x] **Single-resistor gain control** via $R_G$ is the key advantage — does not affect CMRR.
- [x] **Ideal Integrator:** $V_o = -\dfrac{1}{R_i C_f}\int V_{in}\,dt$ — capacitor in feedback, resistor at input.
- [x] **Ideal Differentiator:** $V_o = -R_f C_i \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ — capacitor at input, resistor in feedback.
- [x] **Practical Integrator:** Adds $R_f$ in parallel with $C_f$ to limit DC gain (avoid saturation).
- [x] **Practical Differentiator:** Adds $R_i$ in series with $C_i$ and $C_f$ in parallel with $R_f$ to suppress high-frequency noise and ensure stability.
- [x] **Precision Rectifiers:** Place diodes **inside the feedback loop** to divide the forward voltage by the op-amp's open-loop gain — rectifies millivolt-level signals.
- [x] **Precision HW Rectifier** output: $V_o = 0$ for $V_{in} > 0$, $V_o = -V_{in}$ for $V_{in} < 0$ (unity gain, $R_f = R_i$).
- [x] **Precision FW Rectifier** output: $V_o = \vert V_{in} \vert$ (uses HW rectifier + unity inverter + summer).
- [x] **Peak Detector** uses op-amp + diode + hold capacitor + unity buffer. Output = held peak value. Reset via MOSFET.
- [x] **Sample-and-Hold** uses analog switch (MOSFET) + hold capacitor + buffer. Two modes: Sample (switch closed, cap charges) and Hold (switch open, cap freezes).
- [x] **Acquisition Time** for S/H: $t_{acq} \approx 7 R_{on} C$ (time to settle within 1 LSB).
- [x] **Log Amplifier** uses BJT in feedback: $V_o = -V_T \ln(V_{in}/(I_S R))$.
- [x] **Antilog Amplifier** uses BJT at input: $V_o = -R_f I_S \exp(V_{in}/V_T)$.
- [x] **Log-Antilog Multiplier:** $V_o = V_x V_y / V_{ref}$.
- [x] **Log-Antilog Divider:** Replace summer with subtractor; $V_o = V_{ref} \cdot V_z / V_x$.
- [x] **Temperature Compensation** is critical in log/antilog circuits due to $V_T$ and $I_S$ dependence on temperature.
- [x] All log-antilog multipliers/divider circuits are **single-quadrant** (inputs must be positive).
- [x] **Gilbert cell** is the modern alternative for four-quadrant multiplier (used in IC multipliers like AD633, AD534, MPY634).
- [x] **Real-world applications:** ECG/EEG machines (IA), digital multimeters (precision rectifiers), AM demodulators (peak detector), data acquisition systems (S/H), analog computers (multiplier/divider).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparator: Inverting and non-inverting type, Applications of comparator, Zero crossing detector, Window detector, Time marker generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT404 - INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Comparator: Inverting and non-inverting type, Applications of comparator, Zero crossing detector, Window detector, Time marker generator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Comparator?
A **comparator** is an electronic circuit (built using an op-amp in open-loop configuration) that compares two input voltages and produces a binary digital output indicating which input is **larger**. The op-amp operates in **open-loop** (no feedback) so that it functions as a high-gain differential amplifier. The output saturates to one of two levels, $+\mathrm{V_{sat}}$ or $-\mathrm{V_{sat}}$, representing the digital **HIGH** and **LOW** states respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBICT404 – Module 2):** The comparator is the *fundamental building block* of all signal-conditioning interfaces between the analog world and digital systems. Mastering its two basic forms (inverting and non-inverting) is mandatory before studying its derived applications.

### 1.2 Inverting Comparator
In an **inverting comparator**, the input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting terminal (−)** of the op-amp, and a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied to the **non-inverting terminal (+)**.

- If $V_{in} > V_{ref}$, then output $V_o = -V_{sat}$
- If $V_{in} < V_{ref}$, then output $V_o = +V_{sat}$

### 1.3 Non-Inverting Comparator
In a **non-inverting comparator**, the input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting terminal (+)** of the op-amp, and a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied to the **inverting terminal (−)**.

- If $V_{in} > V_{ref}$, then output $V_o = +V_{sat}$
- If $V_{in} < V_{ref}$, then output $V_o = -V_{sat}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU notation:** Saturation voltage magnitude is $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 2\,\mathrm{V}$ (for op-amp $\mu$A741 with $\pm 15\,\mathrm{V}$ supply, $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13\,\mathrm{V}$). Most KTU problems assume $V_{sat} = \pm 13\,\mathrm{V}$ unless stated otherwise.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Think of a comparator as an **automatic referee in a boxing match** 🥊. Two contestants walk in: one is $V_{in}$ and the other is $V_{ref}$. The referee does not judge who is "good" or "bad"—he simply raises a **red flag** (e.g., $+V_{sat}$, HIGH) or a **green flag** (e.g., $-V_{sat}$, LOW) depending on which contestant is *taller* (has higher voltage). The decision is instant, binary, and absolute—there is no in-between verdict, just like an open-loop op-amp has no linear region.

Now imagine:
- If the contestant carrying the **blue ribbon** ($V_{in}$) stands on the **left** of the judge, it is an *inverting* comparator: the red flag (HIGH) is raised when the *other* contestant ($V_{ref}$) wins.
- If the contestant with the **blue ribbon** ($V_{in}$) stands on the **right** of the judge, it is a *non-inverting* comparator: the red flag (HIGH) is raised when $V_{in}$ itself wins.

This simple analogy eliminates confusion between the two topologies for life.

### 1.5 Ideal Assumptions Used in KTU 2024 Analysis
- **Open-loop gain** $A_{OL} \to \infty$
- **Input impedance** $Z_{in} \to \infty$
- **Output impedance** $Z_{out} \to 0$
- **Offset voltage and currents** are neglected unless specified
- **Slew rate** is assumed infinite (no transition time)
- Saturation levels are $\pm V_{sat} = \pm 13\,\mathrm{V}$ (or as given)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of the Open-Loop Op-Amp
The op-amp output is governed by:
$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$$

Because $A_{OL} \to \infty$, even an infinitesimally small differential input $(V_+ - V_-)$ drives the output to saturation. The op-amp therefore behaves as a **decision-making switch**.

### 2.2 Inverting Comparator – Logic Analysis
Let $V_{in}$ be applied to the inverting terminal and $V_{ref}$ to the non-inverting terminal.

- Case 1: $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ → $(V_+ - V_-) = (V_{ref} - V_{in}) < 0$ → $V_o = -V_{sat}$ (LOW)
- Case 2: $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ → $(V_+ - V_-) = (V_{ref} - V_{in}) > 0$ → $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (HIGH)
- Case 3: $V_{in} = V_{ref}$ → undefined (transition instant)

### 2.3 Non-Inverting Comparator – Logic Analysis
Let $V_{in}$ be applied to the non-inverting terminal and $V_{ref}$ to the inverting terminal.

- Case 1: $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ → $(V_+ - V_-) > 0$ → $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (HIGH)
- Case 2: $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ → $(V_+ - V_-) < 0$ → $V_o = -V_{sat}$ (LOW)

### 2.4 Transfer Characteristics
The **transfer characteristic** of a comparator is a plot of $V_o$ vs $V_{in}$. It is a **step function** with the step occurring at $V_{in} = V_{ref}$.

- **Inverting comparator:** Step **falls** from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$ as $V_{in}$ crosses $V_{ref}$ from below to above.
- **Non-inverting comparator:** Step **rises** from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$ as $V_{in}$ crosses $V_{ref}$ from below to above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **slope of the linear region** (when considered ideal with finite $A_{OL}$) is equal to $A_{OL}$, but in KTU problems, this region is treated as a vertical step at the threshold.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Comparator Type | Condition for $V_o = +V_{sat}$ | Condition for $V_o = -V_{sat}$ | Threshold Voltage $V_{th}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inverting Comparator | $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ | $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ | $V_{th} = V_{ref}$ |
| 2 | Non-Inverting Comparator | $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ | $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ | $V_{th} = V_{ref}$ |
| 3 | Zero Crossing Detector (ZCD) | $V_{in} > 0$ | $V_{in} < 0$ | $V_{th} = 0\,\mathrm{V}$ |
| 4 | Window Detector – Upper Limit | $V_{in} > V_{UTH}$ (LOW) | $V_{in} < V_{UTH}$ (HIGH) | $V_{UTH} = V_{ref1}$ |
| 5 | Window Detector – Lower Limit | $V_{in} > V_{LTH}$ (HIGH) | $V_{in} < V_{LTH}$ (LOW) | $V_{LTH} = V_{ref2}$ |
| 6 | Time Marker Generator (Peak) | At peak: $dV_{in}/dt = 0$ | All other instants | $V_{th} = 0$ after $RC$ differentiation |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility
- **Industrial process control:** Comparators monitor process variables (temperature, pressure) against reference setpoints to trigger alarms or actuators.
- **Oscilloscope triggering:** A comparator detects the exact instant a waveform crosses a threshold, used for stable oscilloscope triggering.
- **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs):** Flash ADCs are essentially arrays of comparators operating in parallel.
- **Over-voltage and under-voltage protection circuits** in SMPS and battery management systems.
- **Signal regeneration** in communication receivers (Schmitt trigger is a comparator with positive feedback, an advanced form).

### 2.7 Important Symbols Used
- $V_{sat}$: **Saturation voltage** (typically $\pm 13\,\mathrm{V}$ for $\mu$A741)
- $V_{ref}$: **Reference voltage** (constant DC level)
- $V_{UTH}$: **Upper Threshold Voltage** (window detector)
- $V_{LTH}$: **Lower Threshold Voltage** (window detector)
- $RC$: **Time constant** of the differentiator (time marker generator)

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Application 1: Zero Crossing Detector (ZCD)

A **Zero Crossing Detector** is essentially a comparator whose reference voltage is **grounded** ($V_{ref} = 0\,\mathrm{V}$). Its sole purpose is to detect the precise instant at which the input waveform crosses the **zero-volt axis**.

#### 3.1.1 Circuit Topology
A non-inverting ZCD applies $V_{in}$ to the non-inverting terminal and grounds the inverting terminal.

#### 3.1.2 Logic Conditions
$$V_o = \begin{cases} +V_{sat}, & V_{in} > 0 \\ -V_{sat}, & V_{in} < 0 \end{cases}$$

#### 3.1.3 Threshold Voltage
Setting $V_+ = V_-$ at the transition:
$$V_{th} = V_{ref} = 0\,\mathrm{V}$$

#### 3.1.4 Output Waveform Derivation
Consider an input $V_{in}(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)$.
- The output switches from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$ every time $\sin(\omega t) = 0$ with positive slope, i.e., at $\omega t = 0, 2\pi, 4\pi, \ldots$
- The output switches from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$ at $\omega t = \pi, 3\pi, 5\pi, \ldots$

Therefore the output is a **square wave** of the same frequency as the input.

**Real-world utility:** ZCDs are used in **phase-locked loops (PLLs)**, **frequency counters**, **function generators**, and **AC line voltage zero-crossing switching circuits** (used in triac-based lamp dimmers to minimize EMI).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently-asked extension:** If the ZCD is built as an *inverting* type (i.e., $V_{in}$ at the inverting terminal), the output is **phase-inverted**—i.e., it gives $+V_{sat}$ during the negative half-cycle. This is sometimes called a "sign inverter" or "polarity detector".

### 3.2 Application 2: Window Detector

A **Window Detector** checks whether an unknown input voltage $V_{in}$ lies within a specified **range** $[V_{LTH}, V_{UTH}]$, where $V_{LTH}$ is the lower threshold and $V_{UTH}$ is the upper threshold. The "window" width is $W = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH}$.

#### 3.2.1 Circuit Topology
The window detector uses **two comparators** in parallel and a single **AND gate**:
- **Comparator 1 (C1):** Non-inverting input gets $V_{in}$, inverting input gets $V_{UTH}$.
- **Comparator 2 (C2):** Inverting input gets $V_{in}$, non-inverting input gets $V_{LTH}$.
- The two comparator outputs are fed into a 2-input AND gate. The final output $V_{out}$ is HIGH **only** when $V_{in}$ is inside the window.

#### 3.2.2 Derivation of Logic Conditions
For Comparator C1 (non-inverting type, $V_{ref} = V_{UTH}$):

$$V_{C1} = \begin{cases} +V_{sat} & \text{if } V_{in} > V_{UTH} \\ -V_{sat} & \text{if } V_{in} < V_{UTH} \end{cases}$$

For Comparator C2 (inverting type, $V_{ref} = V_{LTH}$):

$$V_{C2} = \begin{cases} -V_{sat} & \text{if } V_{in} > V_{LTH} \\ +V_{sat} & \text{if } V_{in} < V_{LTH} \end{cases}$$

The AND gate treats voltages greater than $V_{sat}/2$ as logic HIGH (assume digital threshold $\approx V_{sat}/2$).

#### 3.2.3 Truth Table Derivation

| Condition on $V_{in}$ | $V_{C1}$ | $V_{C2}$ | $V_{out}$ (AND output) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{in} < V_{LTH}$ | $-V_{sat}$ (LOW) | $+V_{sat}$ (HIGH) | LOW |
| $V_{LTH} < V_{in} < V_{UTH}$ | $-V_{sat}$ (LOW) | $-V_{sat}$ (LOW) | LOW |
| $V_{in} > V_{UTH}$ | $+V_{sat}$ (HIGH) | $-V_{sat}$ (LOW) | LOW |

**Wait—this is an important correction based on standard KTU convention:**

The standard KTU window detector configuration actually uses the outputs through an OR gate or with an inverted logic. Let me re-derive using the most common KTU textbook configuration:

- **Comparator 1:** $V_{in}$ → inverting terminal, $V_{UTH}$ → non-inverting terminal.
- **Comparator 2:** $V_{in}$ → non-inverting terminal, $V_{LTH}$ → inverting terminal.

Re-derivation:

For Comparator C1 (inverting type, $V_{ref} = V_{UTH}$):

$$V_{C1} = \begin{cases} +V_{sat} & \text{if } V_{in} < V_{UTH} \\ -V_{sat} & \text{if } V_{in} > V_{UTH} \end{cases}$$

For Comparator C2 (non-inverting type, $V_{ref} = V_{LTH}$):

$$V_{C2} = \begin{cases} +V_{sat} & \text{if } V_{in} > V_{LTH} \\ -V_{sat} & \text{if } V_{in} < V_{LTH} \end{cases}$$

When these two outputs are fed to an **AND gate**, the result is HIGH only when both are HIGH, which occurs when $V_{LTH} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{UTH}$.

#### 3.2.4 Final Window Detector Truth Table

| $V_{in}$ Range | $V_{C1}$ | $V_{C2}$ | $V_{out}$ (AND gate) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{in} < V_{LTH}$ | $+V_{sat}$ | $-V_{sat}$ | LOW (0) |
| $V_{LTH} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{UTH}$ | $+V_{sat}$ | $+V_{sat}$ | HIGH (1) |
| $V_{in} > V_{UTH}$ | $-V_{sat}$ | $+V_{sat}$ | LOW (0) |

The output is HIGH **only** when the input is inside the window—a clear binary "in-band" or "out-of-band" indicator.

#### 3.2.5 Design Equation for Threshold Voltages
Using a voltage divider with resistors $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$ across a single supply $V_{CC}$:

$$V_{UTH} = \frac{(R_2 + R_3)}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} \cdot V_{CC}$$

$$V_{LTH} = \frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} \cdot V_{CC}$$

**Window width:** $W = V_{UTH} - V_{LTH} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3} \cdot V_{CC}$

**Real-world utility:** Window detectors are used in **battery voltage monitoring** (e.g., Li-ion cells must stay between 3.0 V and 4.2 V), **over/under-speed alarms** in motor control, **signal level testers** in instrumentation, and **band-pass signal quality testers**.

### 3.3 Application 3: Time Marker Generator

A **Time Marker Generator** produces a **narrow pulse** (a "marker") at a specific instant of a periodic input waveform—typically at the **peak** (positive or negative maximum) of the input.

#### 3.3.1 Block Architecture
The time marker generator is a **two-stage circuit**:

$$\text{Input} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{[Stage 1: Differentiator using Op-Amp]} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{[Stage 2: Zero Crossing Detector]} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Marker Pulse Output}$$

#### 3.3.2 Stage 1 – Op-Amp Differentiator Analysis
The op-amp differentiator has input $V_{in}$ at the inverting terminal through a capacitor $C$, with a feedback resistor $R$. Using the virtual ground concept at the inverting terminal ($V_- = 0$):

The current through the capacitor:

$$i_C = C \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

The same current flows through the feedback resistor $R$ (no current into the op-amp):

$$i_R = \frac{0 - V_{o1}}{R} = -\frac{V_{o1}}{R}$$

Equating $i_C = i_R$:

$$C \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{o1}}{R}$$

Solving for the output of the differentiator:

$$V_{o1}(t) = -RC \cdot \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

This is the **fundamental differentiator output equation** used in KTU problems.

#### 3.3.3 Stage 2 – Zero Crossing Detection of $V_{o1}$
Let $V_{in}(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)$ be a sinusoid. Its derivative is:

$$\frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = V_p \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

So the differentiator output is:

$$V_{o1}(t) = -RC \cdot V_p \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

Setting $V_{o1}(t) = 0$ (the zero crossing condition):

$$-RC \cdot V_p \omega \cos(\omega t) = 0$$

$$\cos(\omega t) = 0$$

$$\omega t = \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}, \frac{5\pi}{2}, \ldots$$

These instants correspond to the **peaks** of the original sinusoid:
- $\omega t = \pi/2$ → Positive peak of $V_{in}$
- $\omega t = 3\pi/2$ → Negative peak of $V_{in}$

#### 3.3.4 Output Pulse Generation
The ZCD connected to $V_{o1}$ produces **sharp voltage transitions** at the instants when $V_{o1}$ crosses zero. The width of each transition (i.e., the "marker pulse") is governed by the slew rate of the op-amp. A typical time marker is therefore a **narrow positive/negative pulse** aligned with the peaks of the input waveform.

#### 3.3.5 Design Equation Summary

$$V_{o1}(t) = -RC \cdot \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

For sinusoidal input $V_p \sin(\omega t)$:

$$V_{o1}(t) = -RC V_p \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Peak amplitude of differentiator output:**

$$V_{o1,\text{peak}} = RC V_p \omega$$

**Real-world utility:** Time marker generators are used in **CRO time-base calibration**, **radar pulse timing**, **television synchronization pulse generation**, **digital frequency synthesizers**, and **medical instrumentation** (e.g., marking R-wave peaks in ECG signals).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The time marker is generated at the **peak** of the input signal because that is precisely the instant when $dV_{in}/dt = 0$, which makes the differentiator output cross zero. This is a beautiful application of calculus and electronics combined.

### 3.4 Consolidated Analytical Matrix

| Application | Reference Voltage | Threshold Condition | Output Type | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inverting Comparator | $V_{ref}$ at non-inv. terminal | $V_{in}$ vs $V_{ref}$ | Step (inverted) | Threshold alarm |
| Non-Inverting Comparator | $V_{ref}$ at inv. terminal | $V_{in}$ vs $V_{ref}$ | Step (non-inverted) | Threshold alarm |
| Zero Crossing Detector | $0\,\mathrm{V}$ (ground) | $V_{in}$ vs $0$ | Square wave | Phase detection |
| Window Detector | $V_{UTH}$ and $V_{LTH}$ | $V_{LTH} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{UTH}$ | HIGH inside window | Voltage range check |
| Time Marker Generator | $0\,\mathrm{V}$ after differentiation | $dV_{in}/dt = 0$ | Narrow pulse at peaks | Timing reference |

### 4.6 Timing Relationship (Textual Schematic)
For a sinusoidal input of frequency $f$, the time markers appear at:

- **Positive peak:** $t = \dfrac{1}{4f}, \dfrac{5}{4f}, \dfrac{9}{4f}, \ldots$
- **Negative peak:** $t = \dfrac{3}{4f}, \dfrac{7}{4f}, \dfrac{11}{4f}, \ldots$

The time interval between two successive markers of the same type is **one full period** $T = 1/f$.
