## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define a comparator using an op-amp. With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an inverting comparator and write its truth table.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A comparator is a circuit that compares two voltages and gives a binary output indicating which is larger. In an **inverting comparator**, the input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting (−)** terminal, and the reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied to the **non-inverting (+)** terminal of the op-amp. The op-amp operates in open-loop (no feedback) and saturates to $\pm V_{sat}$.

**Truth Table:**

| $V_{in}$ | $V_o$ |
|---|---|
| $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ | $-V_{sat}$ (LOW) |
| $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ | $+V_{sat}$ (HIGH) |

The output is "inverted" with respect to the input—i.e., when $V_{in}$ exceeds the reference, the output goes LOW.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **What is a zero crossing detector? Sketch its input and output waveforms when the input is a sine wave.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Zero Crossing Detector (ZCD)** is a comparator with its reference voltage set to **0 V** (grounded). It produces an output transition every time the input signal crosses the zero-volt axis.

For a sinusoidal input $V_{in} = V_p \sin(\omega t)$:
- During the **positive half cycle** ($V_{in} > 0$), the output is $+V_{sat}$ (HIGH).
- During the **negative half cycle** ($V_{in} < 0$), the output is $-V_{sat}$ (LOW).

The resulting output waveform is a **square wave** of the **same frequency** as the input, with sharp transitions occurring at the zero crossings. Application: phase measurement, frequency counters, and function generators.

---

### 5.2 PART B: Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

---

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

> **(a)** With the help of neat circuit diagrams, explain the working of **inverting** and **non-inverting comparators**. Derive the conditions under which the output saturates. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Design a **window detector** to detect whether an input voltage lies in the range **2 V to 5 V**. Use a single supply of $\pm 12$ V and explain the circuit operation with a truth table. Assume op-amp saturation at $\pm 11$ V. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) – 7 marks**

**Inverting Comparator:**

The op-amp is operated in open-loop. The input $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting (−) terminal, and a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied to the non-inverting (+) terminal.

The op-amp output is given by:
$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_+ - V_-) = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{ref} - V_{in})$$

Since $A_{OL} \to \infty$:

- If $V_{in} > V_{ref}$: $(V_+ - V_-) < 0$, hence $V_o = -V_{sat}$ (LOW)
- If $V_{in} < V_{ref}$: $(V_+ - V_-) > 0$, hence $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (HIGH)
- If $V_{in} = V_{ref}$: undefined (transition)

**Non-Inverting Comparator:**

The input $V_{in}$ is applied to the non-inverting (+) terminal, and $V_{ref}$ is applied to the inverting (−) terminal.

The op-amp output is:
$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{in} - V_{ref})$$

Hence:

- If $V_{in} > V_{ref}$: $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (HIGH)
- If $V_{in} < V_{ref}$: $V_o = -V_{sat}$ (LOW)

**Diagrams (to be drawn in exam):** Show op-amp triangle with $V_{in}$ and $V_{ref}$ at appropriate terminals, output going to $\pm V_{sat}$.

**Transfer characteristics (to be drawn in exam):** Step graph with transition at $V_{in} = V_{ref}$; falling for inverting, rising for non-inverting.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) – 7 marks**

**Design requirements:** Window detector with $V_{LTH} = 2\,\mathrm{V}$ and $V_{UTH} = 5\,\mathrm{V}$.

**Circuit configuration:**
- Use **two op-amp comparators** + **one 2-input AND gate**.
- Supply: $\pm 12$ V; saturation: $\pm 11$ V.
- **Comparator 1 (C1 – Inverting type):** $V_{in}$ to inverting terminal, $V_{UTH} = 5$ V to non-inverting terminal.
- **Comparator 2 (C2 – Non-inverting type):** $V_{in}$ to non-inverting terminal, $V_{LTH} = 2$ V to inverting terminal.
- The two outputs are fed to a 2-input AND gate (logic HIGH threshold $\approx 5.5$ V).

**Step-by-step derivation of comparator outputs:**

For C1 (inverting, $V_{ref} = 5$ V):
- If $V_{in} < 5$ V → $V_{C1} = +11$ V (HIGH)
- If $V_{in} > 5$ V → $V_{C1} = -11$ V (LOW)

For C2 (non-inverting, $V_{ref} = 2$ V):
- If $V_{in} > 2$ V → $V_{C2} = +11$ V (HIGH)
- If $V_{in} < 2$ V → $V_{C2} = -11$ V (LOW)

**Truth Table:**

| $V_{in}$ Range | $V_{C1}$ | $V_{C2}$ | AND Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{in} < 2$ V | $+11$ V (HIGH) | $-11$ V (LOW) | LOW |
| $2 \leq V_{in} \leq 5$ V | $+11$ V (HIGH) | $+11$ V (HIGH) | **HIGH** |
| $V_{in} > 5$ V | $-11$ V (LOW) | $+11$ V (HIGH) | LOW |

**Conclusion:** The AND gate output is HIGH **only** when the input voltage is within the window $[2\,\mathrm{V}, 5\,\mathrm{V}]$, exactly as required by the design specification. This forms a robust **in-band detector** circuit.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **time marker generator**. Derive the expression for the output of the differentiator stage and show that a marker pulse is generated at the **peak** of the input sinusoid. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Explain the working of a **zero crossing detector** as a square wave generator. With an input $V_{in}(t) = 5 \sin(100\pi t)$ V, determine the **time period** and **frequency** of the output square wave. Assume ideal op-amp with $V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) – 7 marks**

**Block Diagram Description:**
A time marker generator consists of two stages:
1. **Op-amp differentiator** (Stage 1): An op-amp with capacitor $C$ in the input path and resistor $R$ in the feedback path.
2. **Zero Crossing Detector** (Stage 2): An op-amp comparator with $V_{ref} = 0$ V.

**Derivation of Differentiator Output:**
Applying KCL at the inverting node (virtual ground, $V_- = 0$):

Current through the input capacitor:
$$i_C = C \cdot \frac{d(V_{in} - 0)}{dt} = C \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

Current through the feedback resistor:
$$i_R = \frac{0 - V_{o1}}{R} = -\frac{V_{o1}}{R}$$

Since no current flows into the op-amp:
$$i_C = i_R \implies C \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{o1}}{R}$$

Solving for the differentiator output:
$$V_{o1}(t) = -RC \cdot \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

**Application to a sinusoid:**
Let $V_{in}(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)$. Then:
$$V_{o1}(t) = -RC \cdot V_p \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Zero crossing condition:**
$$V_{o1}(t) = 0 \implies \cos(\omega t) = 0 \implies \omega t = \frac{\pi}{2} + n\pi$$

The ZCD produces a transition at every such instant, which corresponds to the **peaks** of the original sinusoid. Hence, a marker pulse is generated at the peak of the input waveform.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) – 7 marks**

**ZCD as a Square Wave Generator:**
A ZCD compares the input $V_{in}(t)$ with 0 V. The output is:

$$V_o(t) = \begin{cases} +V_{sat} = +13\,\mathrm{V} & \text{when } V_{in}(t) > 0 \\ -V_{sat} = -13\,\mathrm{V} & \text{when } V_{in}(t) < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Given input:** $V_{in}(t) = 5 \sin(100\pi t)$ V.

**Identification of parameters:**
- Peak voltage: $V_p = 5$ V
- Angular frequency: $\omega = 100\pi$ rad/s

**Calculation of frequency:**
$$f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{100\pi}{2\pi} = 50\,\mathrm{Hz}$$

**Calculation of time period:**
$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02\,\mathrm{s} = 20\,\mathrm{ms}$$

**Square wave characteristics of output:**
- Amplitude: $\pm V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V
- Frequency: $f = 50$ Hz
- Time period: $T = 20$ ms
- Duty cycle: 50% (symmetric square wave)

**Conclusion:** The ZCD converts the 50 Hz sine wave into a 50 Hz square wave of amplitude $\pm 13$ V, which is useful in digital systems that require a clock signal synchronized to the AC mains frequency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up terminals:** Students frequently apply $V_{in}$ and $V_{ref}$ to the wrong terminals, reversing the output logic. Always draw the circuit diagram first and label terminals clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting saturation voltage:** Many answers write just "HIGH" or "LOW" without specifying the actual value ($+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$). KTU examiners **deduct marks** for missing numerical values.
> 3. **Differentiator sign confusion:** The op-amp differentiator output is $V_{o1} = -RC \cdot dV_{in}/dt$. The negative sign is due to the inverting configuration—do **not** drop it.
> 4. **Window detector AND/OR gate confusion:** Some students use an OR gate by mistake. The correct gate is **AND** (or its equivalent combination) to produce a HIGH output only when the input is *within* the window.
> 5. **Unit mismatches:** Always express $f$ in Hz, $\omega$ in rad/s, and $T$ in seconds. Mixing units is a guaranteed mark-loss.
> 6. **No circuit diagram:** A 7-mark question without a proper circuit diagram typically loses **2 marks** under KTU valuation. Always draw a labeled schematic.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **comparator** uses an op-amp in **open-loop** with no feedback; it has a binary output ($\pm V_{sat}$).
- **Inverting comparator:** $V_{in}$ to (−) terminal, $V_{ref}$ to (+) terminal → output is the **inverted** step.
- **Non-inverting comparator:** $V_{in}$ to (+) terminal, $V_{ref}$ to (−) terminal → output is the **non-inverted** step.
- **Threshold voltage** of any comparator is **$V_{th} = V_{ref}$** (the voltage at the reference terminal).
- A **Zero Crossing Detector (ZCD)** is a comparator with $V_{ref} = 0$ V; it produces a **square wave** of the same frequency as the input.
- A **Window Detector** uses **two comparators** + an **AND gate** to detect whether $V_{in}$ lies between $V_{LTH}$ and $V_{UTH}$.
- A **Time Marker Generator** uses a **differentiator followed by a ZCD** to produce a narrow pulse at the **peaks** of the input waveform.
- The **differentiator output** is $V_{o1}(t) = -RC \cdot \dfrac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$ — derived using the **virtual ground** concept.
- For a sinusoidal input $V_p \sin(\omega t)$, the differentiator output is $-RC V_p \omega \cos(\omega t)$, which crosses zero at the **peaks** of the sinusoid.
- Standard saturation voltages: $\pm 13$ V (for $\mu$A741 with $\pm 15$ V supply); $\pm 11$ V (with $\pm 12$ V supply); $\pm 9$ V (with $\pm 10$ V supply).
- **Output frequency of ZCD** = **input frequency** (always equal).
- **Comparator ≠ Amplifier:** A comparator has *no* feedback; an amplifier has *negative* feedback. Confusing these is a classic KTU mistake.
- Real-world apps: ADC, **Schmitt trigger** (advanced comparator with hysteresis), **PLL**, **over/under-voltage alarms**, **battery management**, **time-base generators**, **CRO triggering**, **ECG peak detectors**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waveform Generators and Oscillators using Operational Amplifier: Schmitt trigger, Astable multivibrator, Monostable multivibrator, Triangular waveform generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT404 - INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Waveform Generators and Oscillators using Operational Amplifier: Schmitt trigger, Astable multivibrator, Monostable multivibrator, Triangular waveform generator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Schmitt Trigger (Regenerative Comparator)

> [!NOTE]
> **Schmitt Trigger (Inverting & Non-Inverting):** A Schmitt trigger is a **regenerative comparator** with positive feedback that converts a slowly varying or noisy input signal into a clean, sharply defined output transition. It is characterised by **two distinct threshold voltages** (Upper Threshold Voltage, $V_{UT}$, and Lower Threshold Voltage, $V_{LT}$) which form a hysteresis loop. This hysteresis makes the circuit immune to noise in the region between the two thresholds.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a Schmitt trigger like a **thermostat controlling a room heater**. You don't want the heater to switch ON/OFF rapidly when the temperature hovers near a single set-point (e.g., 25°C), as this would damage the heater. Instead, you set a "dead-band" — the heater turns ON at 23°C and turns OFF only at 27°C. The gap of 4°C is the **hysteresis**. The Schmitt trigger behaves exactly like this: noise riding on a slowly changing signal cannot flip the output unless the input crosses a *different* threshold depending on the current state.

---

### 1.2 Astable Multivibrator (Square Wave Generator)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Astable Multivibrator:** An astable multivibrator is a **free-running oscillator** that has **no stable state**. It continuously switches between its two quasi-stable states, producing a **square wave** at the output without any external triggering signal. The time spent in each state is determined by an RC time constant and the threshold voltages of the comparator (Schmitt trigger).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a **ping-pong ball on a perfectly smooth, double-inclined ramp** (like a "V" shape). The ball cannot rest at the bottom of the "V" (no stable state), so it rolls to one side, pauses momentarily, then rolls to the other side — repeating forever. The astable multivibrator is the electronic equivalent of this: a capacitor "rolls" between two threshold voltages, flipping the comparator output each time.

---

### 1.3 Monostable Multivibrator (One-Shot)

> [!NOTE]
> **Monostable Multivibrator:** A monostable multivibrator has **one stable state and one quasi-stable state**. In response to an external **trigger pulse**, the circuit switches to the quasi-stable state for a predetermined duration $T$ (set by an RC network) and then automatically returns to its stable state. It produces a single output pulse of fixed width for every trigger.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
A monostable multivibrator is like a **balloon that you squeeze once**. It is normally in its "stable" inflated shape. When you (the trigger) press it, it temporarily deforms (quasi-stable) and stays squished for a fixed time $T$ controlled by the elastic stiffness (RC), then springs back to its original shape. One trigger = one output pulse.

---

### 1.4 Triangular Waveform Generator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triangular Waveform Generator:** A triangular waveform generator integrates a square wave (produced by a Schmitt trigger / astable multivibrator) using an op-amp integrator. The integrator produces a linear ramp whose slope is determined by the input square wave amplitude and the RC time constant. When the ramp reaches the Schmitt trigger thresholds, the square wave flips polarity, generating a continuous triangular wave.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a **seesaw in a playground**. A child (the integrator) rocks the seesaw back and forth. Each time the seesaw reaches the ground on one side, the parent (the Schmitt trigger) tells the child to reverse direction. The result is a smooth triangular back-and-forth motion. The amplitude is set by how far the parent allows the seesaw to tilt, and the frequency depends on how fast the child can rock it.

---

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Assumptions in KTU Examination Analysis:**
> - **Op-amp is ideal:** $A_{OL} = \infty$ (open-loop gain), $R_{in} = \infty$, $R_{out} = 0$, $V_{sat} = \pm V_{sat}$ (assumed equal in magnitude unless specified).
> - **Threshold levels are symmetric:** $V_{UT} = +V_{sat} \cdot \beta$ and $V_{LT} = -V_{sat} \cdot \beta$ where $\beta$ is the **feedback fraction**.
> - **Output saturation voltage** is the principal DC rail that defines the entire hysteresis window. **Typically $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13\,\text{V}$** for a $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ op-amp supply.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Schmitt Trigger — Inverting Type (Inverting Schmitt / Regenerative Comparator)

**Circuit Topology:** A non-inverting terminal of the op-amp is connected to a voltage divider formed by $R_1$ (from $V_{out}$ to $V_+$) and $R_2$ (from $V_+$ to ground). The input signal is applied to the inverting terminal.

**Step-by-Step Operating Logic:**

1. The positive feedback through $R_1$ and $R_2$ creates a **feedback fraction**:
$$\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

2. The voltage at the non-inverting terminal (the reference / threshold) becomes:
$$V_+ = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$

3. When $V_{in} > V_+$: output saturates to $-V_{sat}$.
   When $V_{in} < V_+$: output saturates to $+V_{sat}$.

4. The two threshold voltages are:
   - **Upper Threshold** (transition from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$ as $V_{in}$ rises):
$$V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat}$$
   - **Lower Threshold** (transition from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$ as $V_{in}$ falls):
$$V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}$$

5. The **hysteresis voltage** (noise-rejection window):
$$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2\beta V_{sat}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hysteresis matters in engineering:** Hysteresis prevents **chattering** (false triggering) in noisy environments. Used extensively in **0/1 digitizers, line receivers, over-voltage protection circuits, and on/off controllers** (e.g., thermostat ICs like LM741 as comparator).

---

### 2.2 Schmitt Trigger — Non-Inverting Type

**Circuit Topology:** The input signal is applied to the **non-inverting terminal** through $R_1$. The feedback divider $R_1, R_2$ sets the threshold, and the inverting terminal is grounded.

**Key Result:** The thresholds are the same as the inverting type, but the transfer characteristic is **non-inverting** (output transitions occur in the same direction as input crossings).

$$V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat}, \quad V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}, \quad V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}$$

---

### 2.3 Astable Multivibrator Using Op-Amp

**Circuit Topology:** A Schmitt trigger (inverting type) whose output drives an **RC timing network** ($R$ and $C$) back to its inverting input. The non-inverting input receives the feedback fraction from the output.

**Operating Principle (Qualitative):**

1. Assume capacitor is initially uncharged and $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$.
2. The non-inverting input is at $V_+ = \beta V_{sat} = V_{UT}$.
3. Capacitor $C$ charges through $R$ towards $+V_{sat}$.
4. When $V_C$ (which equals $V_-$) just exceeds $V_+$, output flips to $-V_{sat}$.
5. Now $V_+ = -\beta V_{sat} = V_{LT}$, and capacitor charges towards $-V_{sat}$.
6. When $V_C$ crosses $V_{LT}$, output flips back to $+V_{sat}$. Cycle repeats.

**Mathematical Charging Equation:**

The capacitor voltage during charging towards a final voltage $V_f$ starting from $V_i$ for time $t$ is governed by:
$$V_C(t) = V_f + (V_i - V_f)e^{-t/RC}$$

**Half-Period Derivation:**

For the first half cycle, $V_i = -\beta V_{sat}$ and $V_f = +V_{sat}$. At $t = T/2$, $V_C$ must reach $V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat}$.

Substituting:
$$+\beta V_{sat} = +V_{sat} + (-\beta V_{sat} - V_{sat})e^{-(T/2)/RC}$$

**Total Period:**
$$T = 2RC \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$$

**Frequency:**
$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2RC \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$$

**Special Case:** If $R_1 = R_2$, then $\beta = 0.5$, and $\ln(3) \approx 1.0986$:

$$T = 2RC \ln(3) \approx 2.197 RC, \quad f \approx \frac{0.455}{RC}$$

---

### 2.4 Monostable Multivibrator Using Op-Amp

**Circuit Topology:** A Schmitt trigger with an RC timing network. A **diode** is connected across $R$ (or in parallel with the capacitor) to provide a fast discharge path. The circuit rests in the **stable state** ($V_{out} = +V_{sat}$) and is triggered by a **negative pulse** at the non-inverting input.

**Operating Principle:**

1. **Stable state:** $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$. Diode $D$ is forward biased (clamps $V_C \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$). $V_+ = \beta V_{sat} = V_{UT}$.
2. **Trigger:** A negative pulse at $V_+$ drives $V_+ < V_C$ momentarily. The comparator output flips to $-V_{sat}$.
3. **Quasi-stable state:** $V_+ = -\beta V_{sat}$. The diode is reverse-biased. Capacitor charges through $R$ towards $-V_{sat}$.
4. **Return to stable:** When $V_C$ reaches $V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}$, output returns to $+V_{sat}$, diode conducts, capacitor rapidly discharges to $0.7\,\text{V}$.

**Pulse Width:**

Starting from $V_C(0^+) = 0.7\,\text{V}$ (approx) and charging towards $V_f = -V_{sat}$, with target $V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}$:

$$-\beta V_{sat} = -V_{sat} + (0.7 + V_{sat})e^{-T/RC}$$

Assuming $V_{sat} \gg 0.7\,\text{V}$:

$$T \approx RC \ln\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\right) = RC \ln\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

---

### 2.5 Triangular Waveform Generator

**Circuit Topology:** Two op-amp stages in cascade:
- **Stage 1 (Comparator / Schmitt trigger):** Non-inverting Schmitt trigger producing a square wave.
- **Stage 2 (Integrator):** Inverting integrator ($R_{in}, C_f$) that integrates the square wave into a triangular wave.
- The output of the integrator is fed back to the **inverting input** of the comparator (closing the loop).

**Operating Principle:**

1. When $V_{o1}$ (square wave) $= +V_{sat}$, integrator output ramps **down** linearly with slope $-\frac{V_{sat}}{R_{in} C_f}$.
2. When triangular output $V_{o2}$ falls below the comparator's inverting input $V_- = 0\,\text{V}$ (ground reference via the divider), comparator output flips to $-V_{sat}$.
3. Now integrator output ramps **up** with slope $+\frac{V_{sat}}{R_{in} C_f}$.
4. When $V_{o2}$ rises above $V_-$, comparator flips back. Cycle repeats.

**Peak Amplitude of Triangular Wave:**

The feedback path in the comparator goes through a divider: $V_- = V_{o2} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}$ (or similar). At the instant of switching, $V_- = 0$ implies the triangular output peak is determined by the divider ratio from the comparator's non-inverting input (which is grounded for symmetry).

For the standard topology with $R_3 = R_4$:
$$V_{peak} = \pm \frac{R_3}{R_4} \cdot V_{sat}$$

If $R_3 = R_4$: $V_{peak} = \pm V_{sat}$ (this would saturate, so $R_3 < R_4$ is typical).

**Frequency:**

Time for one quarter period (from peak to zero crossing at slope $-V_{sat}/R_{in}C_f$):
$$t_1 = \frac{V_{peak} \cdot R_{in} C_f}{V_{sat}}$$

Full period $T = 4 t_1$:

$$T = \frac{4 R_{in} C_f \cdot V_{peak}}{V_{sat}} = \frac{4 R_{in} C_f \cdot R_3}{R_4}$$

$$f = \frac{R_4}{4 R_3 R_{in} C_f}$$

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Key Equations for KTU 2024 Exam**

| # | Circuit | Key Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | Inverting Schmitt | $\beta = R_2 / (R_1+R_2)$ | Feedback fraction |
| 2 | Inverting Schmitt | $V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat}$ | Upper threshold |
| 3 | Inverting Schmitt | $V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}$ | Lower threshold |
| 4 | Inverting Schmitt | $V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}$ | Hysteresis width |
| 5 | Astable MV | $T = 2RC \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$ | Total period |
| 6 | Astable MV ($\beta = 0.5$) | $T = 2RC \ln(3) \approx 2.197\,RC$ | Symmetric case |
| 7 | Astable MV ($\beta = 0.5$) | $f \approx 0.455 / (RC)$ | Symmetric frequency |
| 8 | Monostable MV | $T = RC \ln\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\right) = RC \ln\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | Pulse width |
| 9 | Triangular Gen | $V_{peak} = \pm \frac{R_3}{R_4} V_{sat}$ | Peak amplitude |
| 10 | Triangular Gen | $f = \frac{R_4}{4 R_3 R_{in} C_f}$ | Output frequency |
| 11 | Monostable MV | $V_C(0^+) \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$ | Diode-clamped initial condition |
| 12 | Astable MV | $V_C(0^+) = \pm \beta V_{sat}$ | Capacitor initial condition |

> **Note:** All voltages are referenced to ground. $V_{sat}$ is taken as the magnitude of the op-amp's saturation voltage (assumed symmetric: $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Schmitt Triggers:** 555 timer ICs, signal conditioning for noisy sensors, debouncing mechanical switches, **Schmitt trigger logic gates** (74HC14, 74LS14), waveform shaping in communication receivers.
- **Astable Multivibrators:** Function generators (square wave output), clock generators for digital systems, PWM modulators, tone generators in musical doorbells.
- **Monostable Multivibrators:** Missing pulse detectors, **debounce circuits** for push buttons, delay generators, digital timing circuits, frequency dividers (when triggered by repeated clock).
- **Triangular Waveform Generators:** Function generators (triangular output), **Sweep generators in CROs**, modulation in synthesizers, ADC testing, automatic test equipment (ATE).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Astable Multivibrator Time Period

**Given:** An op-amp astable multivibrator with timing components $R$ and $C$, feedback resistors $R_1$ (from output to non-inverting input) and $R_2$ (from non-inverting input to ground), and saturation voltage $\pm V_{sat}$.

**Assumption:** $V_{out}$ is initially at $+V_{sat}$ (i.e., the comparator has just flipped high).

**Step 1:** Voltage at non-inverting input due to voltage divider:
$$V_+(t) = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{out} = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$

Since $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ in this state:
$$V_+(t) = +\beta V_{sat} \equiv V_{UT}$$

**Step 2:** The capacitor is connected between the inverting input ($V_-$) and ground. The inverting input draws no current (ideal op-amp), so all the current through $R$ flows into $C$. The capacitor charges through $R$ towards $+V_{sat}$.

**Step 3:** Standard RC charging equation. With initial capacitor voltage $V_C(0) = V_i$ and final asymptotic value $V_f$:
$$V_C(t) = V_f + (V_i - V_f) e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 4:** In the first half-cycle:
- Initial voltage (immediately after the previous flip): $V_i = -\beta V_{sat}$ (this was the threshold at which the previous flip occurred, so the capacitor was at $-\beta V_{sat}$ at the moment of flip)
- Final voltage: $V_f = +V_{sat}$
- Time to reach the new threshold: $t = T/2$
- Target voltage: $V_C(T/2) = +\beta V_{sat}$ (must exceed $V_+$ to flip the output)

**Step 5:** Substitute into the charging equation:
$$+\beta V_{sat} = +V_{sat} + (-\beta V_{sat} - V_{sat}) e^{-(T/2)/RC}$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to isolate the exponential term:
$$\beta V_{sat} - V_{sat} = (-\beta V_{sat} - V_{sat}) e^{-(T/2)/RC}$$
$$-V_{sat}(1 - \beta) = -V_{sat}(1 + \beta) e^{-(T/2)/RC}$$

**Step 7:** Cancel $-V_{sat}$ from both sides:
$$(1 - \beta) = (1 + \beta) e^{-(T/2)/RC}$$

**Step 8:** Isolate the exponential:
$$e^{-(T/2)/RC} = \frac{1 - \beta}{1 + \beta}$$

**Step 9:** Take the natural logarithm on both sides:
$$-\frac{T}{2RC} = \ln\left(\frac{1 - \beta}{1 + \beta}\right)$$

**Step 10:** Apply the property $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$:
$$\frac{T}{2RC} = \ln\left(\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right)$$

**Step 11:** Final expression for the total period:
$$T = 2RC \ln\left(\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right)$$

**Step 12:** By symmetry, the second half-period is identical. Therefore the **total period** is $T$ and **frequency** is:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2RC \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$$

**Step 13:** Special case — when $R_1 = R_2$, $\beta = 0.5$:
$$T = 2RC \ln\left(\frac{1.5}{0.5}\right) = 2RC \ln(3) = 2.1972\,RC$$
$$f = \frac{1}{2.1972\,RC} = \frac{0.455}{RC}$$

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Monostable Multivibrator Pulse Width

**Given:** Op-amp monostable with timing components $R, C$, feedback resistors $R_1, R_2$, and a clamping diode $D$ across $R$ (or in parallel with $C$, with anode grounded).

**Assumption:** $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ in the stable state (diode $D$ is forward biased, clamping $V_C \approx +V_D \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$).

**Step 1:** Stable state analysis.
$$V_+ = \beta V_{sat} = +\beta V_{sat} \equiv V_{UT}$$
$$V_- = V_C = +0.7\,\text{V} \approx 0$$

Since $V_- < V_+$, output remains at $+V_{sat}$. Stable state is confirmed.

**Step 2:** Trigger event.
A negative-going trigger pulse is applied to the non-inverting input. The pulse amplitude must be sufficient to make $V_+$ momentarily drop below $V_- \approx 0$.

**Step 3:** Just after the trigger.
Output flips to $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$. The non-inverting input now sits at $V_+ = \beta(-V_{sat}) = -\beta V_{sat} = V_{LT}$. The diode $D$ becomes reverse-biased (anode at $0\,\text{V}$, cathode at $-V_{sat}$ via $R$). Capacitor begins to charge through $R$ towards $-V_{sat}$.

**Step 4:** Identify initial condition for charging.
$$V_C(0^+) = +0.7\,\text{V} \approx 0$$

(Actually, just at the moment the diode stops conducting, capacitor is at the diode forward voltage.)

**Step 5:** Apply charging equation.
$$V_C(t) = -V_{sat} + (0 - (-V_{sat})) e^{-t/RC} = -V_{sat} + V_{sat} \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 6:** Quasi-stable state ends when $V_C$ reaches $V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}$ at $t = T$:
$$-\beta V_{sat} = -V_{sat} + V_{sat} \cdot e^{-T/RC}$$

**Step 7:** Simplify:
$$-\beta V_{sat} + V_{sat} = V_{sat} \cdot e^{-T/RC}$$
$$V_{sat}(1 - \beta) = V_{sat} \cdot e^{-T/RC}$$
$$1 - \beta = e^{-T/RC}$$

**Step 8:** Solve for $T$:
$$e^{-T/RC} = 1 - \beta$$
$$-\frac{T}{RC} = \ln(1 - \beta)$$
$$T = -RC \ln(1 - \beta) = RC \ln\left(\frac{1}{1 - \beta}\right)$$

**Step 9:** Substitute $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$:
$$T = RC \ln\left(\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + R_2 - R_2}\right) = RC \ln\left(\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1}\right) = RC \ln\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$$

(Note: Some textbooks write this as $RC \ln(1/\beta)$ when using the inverted convention $\beta = R_1/(R_1+R_2)$. **Both forms are equivalent** — KTU accepts either as long as it's correctly applied to the given circuit.)

**Step 10:** Final form:
$$\boxed{T = RC \ln\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Triangular Waveform Generator Frequency

**Given:** Triangular generator with:
- **Comparator stage:** $R_3$ from integrator output to comparator's inverting input; $R_4$ from inverting input to comparator output. The non-inverting input is grounded.
- **Integrator stage:** input resistor $R_{in}$, feedback capacitor $C_f$, input is the comparator's square wave output.
- Output of comparator: $\pm V_{sat}$ (assumed equal).

**Step 1:** Establish comparator switching condition.
The voltage at the inverting input of the comparator:
$$V_- = \frac{R_4 \cdot V_{tri} + R_3 \cdot V_{sq}}{R_3 + R_4}$$

The comparator output switches when $V_- = V_+ = 0$ (since non-inverting input is grounded).

**Step 2:** Setting $V_- = 0$:
$$0 = R_4 \cdot V_{tri} + R_3 \cdot V_{sq}$$
$$V_{tri} = -\frac{R_3}{R_4} V_{sq}$$

**Step 3:** When $V_{sq} = +V_{sat}$:
$$V_{tri} = -\frac{R_3}{R_4} V_{sat} = -V_{peak}$$

When $V_{sq} = -V_{sat}$:
$$V_{tri} = +\frac{R_3}{R_4} V_{sat} = +V_{peak}$$

Therefore, the **peak amplitude of the triangular wave** is:
$$\boxed{V_{peak} = \pm \frac{R_3}{R_4} V_{sat}}$$

**Step 4:** Integrator output equation.
The output of an inverting integrator with input $V_{sq}$ is:
$$V_{tri}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_{in} C_f} \int V_{sq}\, dt + V_{tri}(0)$$

For a constant input $V_{sq} = +V_{sat}$ (negative-slope ramp):
$$V_{tri}(t) = -\frac{V_{sat}}{R_{in} C_f} t + V_{tri}(0)$$

**Step 5:** Slope of the triangular wave.
$$\frac{dV_{tri}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{sat}}{R_{in} C_f}$$

**Step 6:** Time to traverse from $+V_{peak}$ to $-V_{peak}$ (half period):
$$\frac{T}{2} = \frac{2 V_{peak} \cdot R_{in} C_f}{V_{sat}}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $V_{peak} = (R_3/R_4) V_{sat}$:
$$\frac{T}{2} = \frac{2 \cdot (R_3/R_4) V_{sat} \cdot R_{in} C_f}{V_{sat}} = \frac{2 R_3 R_{in} C_f}{R_4}$$

**Step 8:** Solve for total period and frequency:
$$T = \frac{4 R_3 R_{in} C_f}{R_4}$$
$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{R_4}{4 R_3 R_{in} C_f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duty Cycle Note:** The triangular wave generated is **symmetric** (equal rise and fall times) only when the comparator output is symmetric ($\pm V_{sat}$). The square wave output of the comparator will be **symmetric** only if the integrator is ideal (no offset).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Output Waveforms Comparison

| Circuit | Stable States | Trigger Required | Output Waveform | Typical Application |
|---------|---------------|------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Schmitt Trigger | 2 (bistable) | No (continuous monitoring) | Cleaned-up square pulses | Noise immunity, signal conditioning |
| Astable MV | 0 (free-running) | No | Square wave (continuous) | Clock generator, function generator |
| Monostable MV | 1 (one stable + one quasi-stable) | Yes (single trigger) | Single output pulse | Pulse stretching, delay, debounce |
| Triangular Gen | 0 (free-running, dual output) | No | Square + Triangular wave | Function generator, sweep generator |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set 1]**
> **Q1. Define a Schmitt trigger. Why is hysteresis important in a Schmitt trigger? Mention one application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Schmitt trigger is a regenerative comparator with positive feedback that uses **two different threshold voltages** ($V_{UT}$ and $V_{LT}$) to convert a slowly varying input into a sharply defined output. The difference between these thresholds is the **hysteresis voltage** $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2\beta V_{sat}$.

**Importance of hysteresis (1 mark):** Hysteresis provides **noise immunity**. Any noise riding on the input signal that has an amplitude less than $V_H/2$ cannot cause unwanted output transitions. This prevents chattering in noisy environments.

**Application (1 mark):** Used in **signal conditioning for noisy sensors** (e.g., debouncing mechanical switches, line receivers in digital communication, 555 timer IC input conditioning).

> **Mark distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Hysteresis importance (1 mark) + Application (1 mark).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Set 2]**
> **Q2. Distinguish between astable and monostable multivibrators using op-amps.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Astable Multivibrator | Monostable Multivibrator |
|---------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Stable states | **None** (both states are quasi-stable) | **One stable + one quasi-stable** |
| Trigger | Not required (self-starting) | **External trigger pulse** required |
| Output | **Continuous square wave** | **Single output pulse** per trigger |
| Time period | $T = 2RC \ln((1+\beta)/(1-\beta))$ | $T = RC \ln(1/\beta)$ |
| Application | Clock generator, oscillator | Delay generator, pulse stretcher |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The astable operates as a **free-running oscillator** producing a periodic square wave, while the monostable produces a **single pulse of fixed width** in response to each trigger event.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **op-amp based astable multivibrator**. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an astable multivibrator using an op-amp to generate a **1 kHz square wave** with a **50% duty cycle** and peak-to-peak amplitude of **20 V**. Assume $V_{sat} = \pm 10\,\text{V}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Explanation and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):** The op-amp astable multivibrator consists of an op-amp in **inverting Schmitt trigger configuration**. The output $V_{out}$ drives a voltage divider ($R_1$ from output to non-inverting input, $R_2$ from non-inverting input to ground) for positive feedback. The inverting input is connected to a capacitor $C$, and a timing resistor $R$ connects $V_{out}$ to the inverting input. The non-inverting input voltage is:
$$V_+ = \beta V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{out}$$

**Operating Principle (3 marks):**

1. Assume the output is at $+V_{sat}$. Then $V_+ = +\beta V_{sat}$.
2. Capacitor $C$ charges through $R$ towards $+V_{sat}$.
3. The instant $V_C$ (which equals $V_-$) just exceeds $+\beta V_{sat}$, the op-amp output switches to $-V_{sat}$.
4. Now $V_+ = -\beta V_{sat}$ and capacitor charges towards $-V_{sat}$.
5. When $V_C$ reaches $-\beta V_{sat}$, output switches back to $+V_{sat}$. The cycle is continuous — no stable state.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

The capacitor voltage during charging from $V_i = -\beta V_{sat}$ to $V_f = +V_{sat}$ over a half period $T/2$ is given by:
$$V_C(t) = V_f + (V_i - V_f)e^{-t/RC}$$

At the end of half-period, $V_C = +\beta V_{sat}$:
$$+\beta V_{sat} = +V_{sat} + (-\beta V_{sat} - V_{sat})e^{-T/(2RC)}$$

After simplification:
$$T = 2RC \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$$
$$f = \frac{1}{2RC \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$$

---

**Part (b) — Design Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f = 1\,\text{kHz}$, duty cycle = 50%, $V_{sat} = \pm 10\,\text{V}$ (so peak-to-peak = 20 V).

**Step 1 — Choose feedback fraction for 50% duty cycle (1 mark):**
Duty cycle is 50% when the time spent at $+V_{sat}$ equals time spent at $-V_{sat}$. This is automatically satisfied for **any symmetric waveform** with $\pm V_{sat}$ in the astable. So 50% duty cycle is guaranteed by the symmetric op-amp saturation.

**Step 2 — Choose $\beta$ (1 mark):** For convenience, choose $R_1 = R_2$, so $\beta = 0.5$. Then:
$$T = 2RC \ln(3) \approx 2.197\,RC$$

**Step 3 — Compute $RC$ for 1 kHz (2 marks):**
$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\,\text{ms}$$
$$RC = \frac{T}{2 \ln(3)} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 1.0986} = \frac{10^{-3}}{2.1972} \approx 4.55 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Choose component values (3 marks):** For example, $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F} = 10\,\text{nF}$:
$$R = \frac{4.55 \times 10^{-4}}{10 \times 10^{-9}} = 45.5\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Use standard value $R = 47\,\text{k}\Omega$ and adjust $C$ slightly. Alternatively, choose $C = 0.047\,\mu\text{F}$ and $R = 9.7\,\text{k}\Omega \approx 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. 

Set $R_1 = R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (standard).

**Final design:** $R = 47\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 10\,\text{nF}$, $R_1 = R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Draw the circuit of an **op-amp based monostable multivibrator** and explain its operation with relevant waveforms. Derive the expression for the **pulse width**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An op-amp monostable has $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$, $R_1 = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $V_{sat} = \pm 12\,\text{V}$. Calculate: **(i)** the feedback fraction, **(ii)** the upper and lower threshold voltages, **(iii)** the pulse width, and **(iv)** the trigger pulse minimum amplitude required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Operation and Pulse Width (7 marks):**

**Circuit (1 mark):** A non-inverting Schmitt trigger (op-amp with positive feedback via $R_1, R_2$) whose output drives a timing network $R$–$C$ to its inverting input. A **diode $D$** is connected across $R$ (cathode at op-amp output, anode at inverting input) to provide a fast charging path for $C$ during the stable state.

**Operation (4 marks):**

1. **Stable state:** Output is at $+V_{sat}$. Diode $D$ is forward biased and clamps $V_C \approx +0.7\,\text{V}$ (the diode forward drop). The non-inverting input is at $V_+ = +\beta V_{sat}$.
2. **Triggering:** A negative pulse at $V_+$ makes $V_+ < V_- = 0.7\,\text{V}$. The output flips to $-V_{sat}$.
3. **Quasi-stable state:** $V_+ = -\beta V_{sat}$. Diode is reverse biased. Capacitor charges through $R$ towards $-V_{sat}$.
4. **Recovery:** When $V_C$ reaches $V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}$, output returns to $+V_{sat}$, diode conducts, capacitor quickly charges back to $0.7\,\text{V}$, and the circuit is ready for the next trigger.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Capacitor charges from $V_C(0^+) = 0.7\,\text{V}$ to $V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}$ in time $T$:
$$V_C(t) = -V_{sat} + (0.7 + V_{sat})e^{-t/RC}$$

Setting $V_C(T) = -\beta V_{sat}$ and approximating $0.7 \ll V_{sat}$:
$$T = RC \ln\left(\frac{1}{1 - \beta}\right) = RC \ln\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$, $R_1 = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{sat} = \pm 12\,\text{V}$.

**(i) Feedback fraction (1 mark):**
$$\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{10}{20 + 10} = \frac{10}{30} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

**(ii) Threshold voltages (2 marks):**
$$V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat} = \frac{1}{3} \times 12 = +4\,\text{V}$$
$$V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat} = \frac{1}{3} \times 12 = -4\,\text{V}$$

**(iii) Pulse width (2 marks):**
$$T = RC \ln\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right) = (10 \times 10^3)(0.1 \times 10^{-6}) \ln\left(1 + \frac{20}{10}\right)$$
$$T = 10^{-3} \times \ln(3) = 10^{-3} \times 1.0986 = 1.0986\,\text{ms} \approx 1.1\,\text{ms}$$

**(iv) Minimum trigger amplitude (2 marks):**
The trigger must drop $V_+$ below $V_-$ momentarily. In the stable state, $V_+ = +4\,\text{V}$ and $V_- = 0.7\,\text{V}$. The trigger pulse must therefore:
$$\Delta V_{trigger} > V_+ - V_- = 4 - 0.7 = 3.3\,\text{V}$$

So the **minimum trigger pulse amplitude is just over 3.3 V (going negative from the resting level of 0 V)**. Conservatively, a 4 V negative-going pulse is recommended.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (Frequently Lost Marks):**
> 1. **Confusing $\beta$ notation:** Different textbooks use $\beta = R_1/(R_1+R_2)$ vs $\beta = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$. **Always derive the formula** for the specific circuit shown in the question — do not blindly copy from a memory aid. KTU examiners will check whether your $\beta$ matches the divider you drew.
> 2. **Diode drop ignored in monostable:** In the monostable derivation, students often assume $V_C(0^+) = 0$ instead of $0.7\,\text{V}$. For typical $V_{sat} > 10\,\text{V}$, the error is small but the examiner may deduct **0.5–1 mark** if no justification is given.
> 3. **Sign errors in threshold voltages:** $V_{UT}$ is **always** the threshold reached on the *rising* transition, and the formula $V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat}$ applies only when the *output* is at $+V_{sat}$. Reversing the sign is a common mistake.
> 4. **Forgetting $\ln(3)$ approximation:** For the symmetric case ($\beta = 0.5$), students often write $T = 2RC$ instead of $T = 2RC \ln(3)$. This costs full marks on the final numerical answer.
> 5. **Square wave vs triangle wave in triangular generator:** The triangular generator produces **both** a square wave (at comparator output) and a triangular wave (at integrator output). Mentioning both shows the examiner you understand the system.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Notes (for last-minute KTU exam prep):**
> - **Schmitt trigger** has **2 thresholds** ($V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat}$, $V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}$) and **hysteresis $V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}$**.
> - **Astable MV**: $T = 2RC \ln((1+\beta)/(1-\beta))$; symmetric case: $T \approx 2.197\,RC$, $f \approx 0.455/RC$.
> - **Monostable MV**: $T = RC \ln(1/\beta) = RC \ln(1 + R_1/R_2)$ — always needs a **trigger** and a **diode** for fast recovery.
> - **Triangular generator**: $V_{peak} = (R_3/R_4) V_{sat}$; $f = R_4 / (4 R_3 R_{in} C_f)$.
> - **Positive feedback** is what makes the Schmitt trigger regenerative — without it, you have a plain comparator.
> - **Negative feedback** is what makes the integrator behave as $V_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC} \int V_{in}\, dt$.
> - For **ideal op-amp**: $V_+ = V_-$ (in negative feedback) and $I_+ = I_- = 0$ (always).
> - Always **draw the circuit diagram** with proper labels of $V_{UT}$, $V_{LT}$, $V_{sat}$, and key components before starting any derivation — this earns you 1–2 marks upfront in the KTU valuation key.
> - When the question says **symmetric**, assume $R_1 = R_2$ ($\beta = 0.5$) and use $\ln(3) = 1.0986$.
> - For **monostable** in KTU, the trigger is typically a **negative-going** pulse, and you must state the **minimum trigger amplitude** that overcomes $V_+ - V_-$.
> - **Duty cycle** in astable MV is **50% by default** (symmetric saturation); if the problem mentions unequal $V_{sat}$ for +ve and -ve rails, compute duty cycle separately for each half-period.
> - **Hysteresis** prevents chattering — the **bigger the hysteresis, the more noise-immunity**, but **lower sensitivity** to small input changes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oscillator: Introduction, Block level diagram, RC phase shift oscillator, Wien Bridge Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT404 - INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Oscillator: Introduction, Block level diagram, RC phase shift oscillator, Wien Bridge Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Oscillator?

An **oscillator** is an electronic circuit that converts DC energy from a power supply into a periodic, oscillating electronic signal (typically a sine wave, square wave, or sawtooth wave) without requiring any external input signal. It is essentially a signal generator that produces a continuous, repetitive output waveform at a specific frequency determined by the circuit components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Barkhausen Criterion):**
> An oscillator is a positive feedback amplifier in which a portion of the output signal is fed back to the input in phase with the input signal. The oscillations are sustained when two conditions are simultaneously satisfied:
> 1. The **loop gain magnitude** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
> 2. The **loop phase shift** $\angle A\beta = 0°$ (or integer multiples of $360°$)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Swinging Pendulum

Imagine pushing a child on a swing:
- **First push (DC energy)**: You give the swing an initial energy impulse.
- **Synchronous push (positive feedback)**: If you keep pushing the swing at exactly the right moment in its natural motion, the swing will go higher and higher.
- **Sustained motion (sustained oscillation)**: The swing reaches a steady amplitude where the energy you supply per push exactly equals the energy lost to air resistance and friction.
- **The swing has a natural frequency** — just like an oscillator has a frequency determined by its components (L and C in LC oscillators, R and C in RC oscillators).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** An oscillator is a **self-sustaining** device. Unlike an amplifier that needs an input, an oscillator **generates** its own input through positive feedback. The noise/transient at switch-on provides the initial "kick" that gets amplified and re-fed until a stable amplitude is reached.

## 1.3 Classification of Oscillators (KTU Syllabus Context)

| Type | Frequency Range | Frequency Determining Elements |
|------|----------------|-------------------------------|
| **RC Oscillators** | Audio (20 Hz – 100 kHz) | Resistors & Capacitors |
| **LC Oscillators** | RF (100 kHz – GHz) | Inductors & Capacitors |
| **Crystal Oscillators** | Highly Stable | Piezoelectric Crystal |

The **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** and **Wien Bridge Oscillator** belong to the RC oscillator family, ideal for low-frequency audio applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Principle: Positive Feedback

Every oscillator is built on the principle of **positive (regenerative) feedback**. Consider a basic feedback block diagram where a portion of the output $V_{out}$ is returned to the input through a feedback network $\beta$.

### 2.1.1 Closed-Loop Gain Derivation

The closed-loop transfer function of a feedback amplifier is:

$$
\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}
$$

For an oscillator, there is no external input ($V_{in} = 0$), yet we need a non-zero output. This is mathematically possible only if the **denominator becomes zero**:

$$
1 + A\beta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A\beta = -1
$$

In polar form, this means $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ and the phase of $A\beta$ is $180°$. Since amplifiers typically invert (contribute $180°$ phase shift), the feedback network must contribute another $180°$ to make the total phase shift $360°$ (which is equivalent to $0°$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Barkhausen Criterion (The Two Golden Rules):**
> - **Magnitude Condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ (loop gain must equal unity)
> - **Phase Condition:** $\angle A\beta = 0°$ or $360°$ (loop phase shift must be zero or integer multiple of $2\pi$ radians)

## 2.2 Block Level Diagram of an Oscillator

The block-level diagram represents the most fundamental architecture of any sinusoidal oscillator. It consists of two essential blocks connected in a closed loop:

1. **Amplifier Block (A)**: Provides voltage gain and may introduce phase shift.
2. **Feedback Network (β)**: Returns a fraction of the output to the input with the appropriate phase.

### 2.2.1 Functional Operation

- The amplifier boosts the weak input signal.
- A fraction $\beta V_{out}$ is sampled and fed back to the input.
- For **sustained oscillations**, the feedback signal must be **in phase** with the original input and **equal in magnitude**.
- The noise/transients in the circuit act as the initial stimulus, which is amplified and re-fed repeatedly until the desired amplitude is achieved.
- **Amplitude stabilization** is achieved by non-linear gain reduction — as amplitude grows, the effective gain $A$ drops until $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$.

### 2.2.2 Classification Based on the Feedback Network

| Oscillator Type | Feedback Network | Frequency Formula |
|-----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **RC Phase Shift** | Three cascaded RC sections | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ |
| **Wien Bridge** | Series & Parallel RC (Wien Bridge) | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| **LC (Hartley)** | Tapped Inductor | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC_T}}$ |
| **LC (Colpitts)** | Capacitive Divider | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}}$ |
| **Crystal** | Piezoelectric Resonator | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Barkhausen Magnitude** | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Sustained oscillation amplitude condition |
| **Barkhausen Phase** | $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } 360°$ | Phase coherence condition |
| **RC Phase Shift Frequency** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | Frequency of RC phase shift oscillator |
| **RC Phase Shift Gain** | $\vert A \vert \geq 29$ | Minimum amplifier gain (using CE amp with $h_{fe}$) |
| **Wien Bridge Frequency** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Frequency of Wien bridge oscillator |
| **Wien Bridge Gain** | $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 3$ | Non-inverting amplifier gain requirement |
| **Closed-Loop Gain** | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$ | General feedback amplifier equation |
| **Feedback Fraction (Wien)** | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | At resonance, feedback factor is 1/3 |
| **Quality Factor (Wien)** | $Q = \dfrac{1}{3}\left(3 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$ | Selectivity of the Wien bridge network |
| **Resonant Frequency (General)** | $\omega_o = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | For generalized Wien bridge |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Audio Signal Generation**: Tone generators, musical instruments, sirens, and test equipment.
- **Carrier Generation**: Local oscillators in superheterodyne radio receivers.
- **Clock Generation**: Timing references in digital systems and microcontrollers.
- **Communication Systems**: Function generators, frequency synthesizers.
- **Industrial Control**: PWM signal sources for motor drives and power electronics.
- **Medical Electronics**: Biostimulators, ultrasound transducers, ECG reference signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** RC oscillators (Wien Bridge in particular) are preferred for **audio frequencies (20 Hz – 20 kHz)** because LC oscillators become impractically large at these low frequencies. Wien bridge is the most common laboratory audio generator due to its excellent frequency stability and low distortion.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Exhaustive Derivation

### 3.1.1 Circuit Topology

The RC phase shift oscillator consists of:
- **Three cascaded RC sections** (each producing $60°$ phase shift, total $= 180°$).
- **One inverting amplifier** (CE or op-amp based, contributing $180°$ phase shift).
- **Total loop phase shift** $= 180° + 180° = 360° = 0°$, satisfying Barkhausen phase condition.

### 3.1.2 Feedback Network Analysis

Consider the three-section RC ladder where each section has resistance $R$ and capacitance $C$. The output is taken across the last resistor and fed back to the amplifier input.

**Let the output voltage of the feedback network be $V_f$ and the current through the chain be $I$.**

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** in each mesh, we obtain three loop equations:

**Loop 1** (input to first node):
$$
I_1 R + \frac{1}{C}\int I_1 \, dt - \frac{1}{C}\int I_2 \, dt = V_{out}
$$

**Loop 2** (middle section):
$$
-\frac{1}{C}\int I_1 \, dt + I_2 R + \frac{1}{C}\int I_2 \, dt - \frac{1}{C}\int I_3 \, dt = 0
$$

**Loop 3** (output section):
$$
-\frac{1}{C}\int I_2 \, dt + I_3 R + \frac{1}{C}\int I_3 \, dt = 0
$$

Converting to **phasor (frequency domain)** form using the impedance $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$:

**Loop 1:**
$$
I_1 \left(R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}\right) - I_2 \left(\frac{1}{j\omega C}\right) = V_{out}
$$

**Loop 2:**
$$
- I_1 \left(\frac{1}{j\omega C}\right) + I_2 \left(R + \frac{2}{j\omega C}\right) - I_3 \left(\frac{1}{j\omega C}\right) = 0
$$

**Loop 3:**
$$
- I_2 \left(\frac{1}{j\omega C}\right) + I_3 \left(R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}\right) = 0
$$

### 3.1.3 Solving the Matrix System

The feedback ratio is defined as:
$$
\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_{out}} = \frac{I_3 R}{V_{out}}
$$

By solving the system of three equations, we can express $\beta$ as a complex function of $\omega$:

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{1 - 5\alpha^2 - j\left(6\alpha - \alpha^3\right)}
$$

where $\alpha = \omega RC$.

> **Logic note:** We derived this by substituting $I_3$ from Loop 3 into Loop 2, then $I_2$ into Loop 1, and finally expressing the ratio.

### 3.1.4 Applying the Phase Condition

For Barkhausen phase condition, the imaginary part of the denominator must be zero:

$$
6\alpha - \alpha^3 = 0
$$

$$
\alpha(6 - \alpha^2) = 0
$$

Since $\alpha \neq 0$, we get $\alpha^2 = 6$, hence:

$$
\alpha = \omega_o RC = \sqrt{6}
$$

Therefore, the **frequency of oscillation** is:

$$
\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}}
$$

> **Logic note:** This is the unique frequency at which the feedback network produces exactly $180°$ phase shift, complementing the amplifier's $180°$ for a total loop phase of $360°$.

### 3.1.5 Applying the Magnitude Condition

Substituting $\alpha^2 = 6$ back into the magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:

$$
\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{1 - 5(6)} = \frac{1}{1 - 30} = \frac{1}{29}
$$

Therefore, the **minimum amplifier gain** required is:

$$
\boxed{\vert A \vert \geq 29}
$$

> **Logic note:** The negative sign indicates the required phase inversion. Any gain less than 29 would result in $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$ and oscillations would die out.

### 3.1.6 Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Design an RC phase shift oscillator using a CE amplifier with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \text{ \mu F}$. Find the frequency of oscillation.

**Solution:**

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{6}}
$$

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4} \times 2.449}
$$

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{1.539 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 649.7 \text{ Hz}
$$

---

## 3.2 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Exhaustive Derivation

### 3.2.1 Circuit Topology

The Wien bridge oscillator uses:
- **A non-inverting amplifier** (op-amp based) with gain $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$.
- **A Wien bridge network** consisting of a **series RC** arm ($R_1$, $C_1$) and a **parallel RC** arm ($R_2$, $C_2$).
- Typically $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$ for a symmetric bridge.

The Wien bridge network provides **zero phase shift** at the resonant frequency, so the amplifier must be non-inverting to satisfy the loop phase condition.

### 3.2.2 Derivation of the Feedback Ratio $\beta$

The transfer function of the Wien network (assuming $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$) is:

$$
\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_{out}} = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}
$$

where $Z_s = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$ is the **series arm impedance** and $Z_p = \dfrac{R \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$ is the **parallel arm impedance**.

Computing $Z_p$:

$$
Z_p = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

Substituting into $\beta$:

$$
\beta = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C(1 + j\omega RC)$:

$$
\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{j\omega RC(1 + j\omega RC) + (1 + j\omega RC) + j\omega RC}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + 3j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + j\omega RC}
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3j\omega RC}
$$

### 3.2.3 Applying the Phase Condition

For zero phase shift in the feedback network, the imaginary part of the denominator must equal zero... actually, the **denominator must be purely real** (no imaginary part). Therefore:

$$
1 - \omega_o^2 R^2 C^2 = 0
$$

$$
\omega_o^2 R^2 C^2 = 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega_o = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}
$$

> **Logic note:** At this frequency, the Wien network becomes purely resistive (the imaginary components cancel), making the phase shift exactly zero.

### 3.2.4 Applying the Magnitude Condition

Substituting $\omega_o^2 R^2 C^2 = 1$ into the expression for $\beta$:

$$
\beta = \frac{j\omega_o RC}{0 + 3j\omega_o RC} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

For the Barkhausen magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:

$$
A \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = 3
$$

Since $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ for a non-inverting op-amp:

$$
1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2}
$$

> **Logic note:** In practice, $A$ is set slightly greater than 3 (e.g., $R_f = 2.1 R_1$) to ensure oscillations build up. Amplitude-limiting mechanisms (thermistors, diodes, or JFETs) dynamically reduce the gain to exactly 3 once the desired amplitude is reached.

### 3.2.5 Quality Factor Derivation

The Q-factor of the Wien bridge oscillator (measuring selectivity and harmonic purity) is:

$$
Q = \frac{\omega_o}{BW} = \frac{1}{3}\left(3 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)
$$

For $A = 3$, $Q = \dfrac{1}{3}(3 + 2) = \dfrac{5}{3} \approx 1.67$. Higher gain produces higher Q but also increased distortion.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | RC Phase Shift Oscillator | Wien Bridge Oscillator |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| **Frequency Formula** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| **Amplifier Type** | Inverting (Common Emitter / Inverting Op-Amp) | Non-Inverting (Op-Amp) |
| **Phase Shift by Network** | $180°$ (three $60°$ sections) | $0°$ at resonance |
| **Phase Shift by Amplifier** | $180°$ | $0°$ |
| **Total Loop Phase** | $360°$ | $0°$ |
| **Minimum Gain** | $\vert A \vert \geq 29$ | $A \geq 3$ |
| **Output Purity** | Moderate (high THD) | Excellent (low THD) |
| **Frequency Stability** | Poor | Good (with amplitude stabilization) |
| **Typical Application** | Audio tone generation | Lab audio oscillators, function generators |
| **Tunability** | Difficult (3 resistors, 3 capacitors) | Easy (single ganged pot/capacitor) |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | High (derivation & design) | High (frequency, gain, Q-factor) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Diagram of a Generalized Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[DC Power Supply Vcc] -->|Powers| nodeB[Amplifier A]
    nodeB -->|Vout| nodeC[Output Terminal]
    nodeB -->|Sample signal| nodeD[Feedback Network beta]
    nodeD -->|Phase shifted feedback Vf| nodeB
    nodeE[Initial Noise or Transient] -.->|Trigger| nodeB
    nodeF[Barkhausen Condition A*beta = 1] -.->|Governs| nodeB
```

**Interpretation:** The amplifier (A) receives power from DC supply, amplifies the input (initially noise), and sends the output to both the load and the feedback network (β). The feedback network returns a phase-shifted portion to the input, creating a self-sustaining loop.

## 4.2 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Input Node] --> nodeB[RC Section 1 60 deg]
    nodeB --> nodeC[RC Section 2 60 deg]
    nodeC --> nodeD[RC Section 3 60 deg]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Summing Node to Amplifier]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Inverting Amplifier Gain A]
    nodeF -->|Vout 180 deg shift| nodeG[Output]
    nodeG -->|Feedback to nodeA| nodeA
```

**Phase Tracking:**
- Each RC section contributes **$60°$** phase shift.
- Total network phase shift = $60° + 60° + 60° = 180°$.
- Inverting amplifier adds another $180°$.
- **Total loop phase shift = $180° + 180° = 360° = 0°$** → Barkhausen phase condition satisfied.

## 4.3 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Non Inverting Input] --> nodeB[Wien Bridge Network]
    nodeB -->|Series RC arm| nodeC[Node X]
    nodeB -->|Parallel RC arm| nodeD[Node Y]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Differential Amplifier]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE -->|Gain A = 1 + Rf by R1| nodeF[Output Vout]
    nodeF -->|Feedback to Node X| nodeC
    nodeF -->|Reference to Node Y via ground| nodeD
    nodeG[Amp Stabilization Thermistor or JFET] -.->|Controls gain| nodeE
```

**Phase Tracking:**
- At $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, the Wien network produces **$0°$** phase shift.
- Non-inverting amplifier also produces **$0°$** phase shift.
- **Total loop phase shift = $0°$** → Barkhausen phase condition satisfied.
- The amplitude stabilization block ensures $A\beta$ settles to exactly 1.

## 4.4 Signal Flow Representation of Loop Gain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input V in] --> nodeB[Amplifier Gain A]
    nodeB -->|Vout = A * V in| nodeC[Output Port]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Feedback Network beta]
    nodeD -->|Vf = beta * Vout| nodeE[Summing Junction]
    nodeE -->|Vf fed back| nodeB
    nodeF[Barkhausen Loop: A * beta = 1 at angle 0] -.->|Monitors| nodeB
    nodeF -.->|Monitors| nodeD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Diagram Note:** These block diagrams represent the **functional flow of signals and control** rather than physical circuit layouts. In a KTU board exam, a properly labeled block diagram with the loop gain equation $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ earns full credit for the introductory part of an oscillator question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State and explain the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations.**

**Model Answer:**

The Barkhausen criterion specifies the two simultaneous conditions for sustained oscillations in a feedback oscillator:

1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be exactly unity.
$$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$$

2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be zero or an integer multiple of $360°$ ($2\pi$ radians).
$$\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } 360° \text{ (or } n \times 360°, n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots)$$

When both conditions are met, any noise/transient in the circuit is amplified and re-fed in phase, leading to self-sustained oscillations at a single frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Many students write only the magnitude condition. Always state **both** conditions explicitly to earn full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Compare RC phase shift oscillator and Wien bridge oscillator based on frequency formula, amplifier type, and minimum gain.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | RC Phase Shift Oscillator | Wien Bridge Oscillator |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| **Frequency Formula** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| **Amplifier Type** | Inverting (CE / Op-Amp) | Non-Inverting (Op-Amp) |
| **Minimum Gain** | $\vert A \vert \geq 29$ | $A \geq 3$ |
| **Feedback Network** | Three cascaded RC sections | Series-Parallel RC (Wien Bridge) |
| **Phase Contribution** | $180°$ network + $180°$ amp = $360°$ | $0°$ network + $0°$ amp = $0°$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] — Set 1

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the concept of positive feedback in oscillators. Derive the condition for sustained oscillations using the closed-loop gain expression. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of Feedback Oscillator:**

[Refer to the block-level diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes]

**Closed-Loop Gain Derivation:**

Consider an amplifier with open-loop gain $A$ and a feedback network with transfer ratio $\beta$ connected in a positive feedback loop. Let the input signal be $V_{in}$.

The output of the amplifier is:
$$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in}'$$

where $V_{in}'$ is the net input voltage at the amplifier terminals:
$$V_{in}' = V_{in} + \beta \cdot V_{out}$$

Substituting:
$$V_{out} = A \cdot (V_{in} + \beta \cdot V_{out})$$

$$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in} + A\beta \cdot V_{out}$$

$$V_{out} - A\beta \cdot V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in}$$

$$V_{out} (1 - A\beta) = A \cdot V_{in}$$

Therefore, the **closed-loop gain** is:
$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}$$

> **Logic note:** Note that this is the positive feedback form (minus sign in denominator), as opposed to the negative feedback form $\dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$.

**Sustained Oscillation Condition:**

For an oscillator, there is no external input ($V_{in} = 0$), yet a non-zero output ($V_{out} \neq 0$) is desired. From the equation $V_{out} (1 - A\beta) = A \cdot V_{in}$, setting $V_{in} = 0$ gives:
$$V_{out} (1 - A\beta) = 0$$

For $V_{out} \neq 0$, we require:
$$1 - A\beta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A\beta = 1$$

Converting to polar form:
$$\vert A\beta \vert \angle (\angle A + \angle \beta) = 1 \angle 0°$$

This yields the **Barkhausen criterion**:
- Magnitude: $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
- Phase: $\angle A + \angle \beta = 0° \text{ or } 360°$

---

**(b)** Explain the working of a Wien bridge oscillator with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the condition for sustained oscillations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:**

The Wien bridge oscillator consists of:
- A non-inverting op-amp amplifier with feedback resistors $R_1$ (to ground) and $R_f$ (feedback).
- A frequency-selective Wien bridge network with two arms:
  - **Series arm:** $R$ in series with $C$ (impedance $Z_s = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$).
  - **Parallel arm:** $R$ in parallel with $C$ (impedance $Z_p = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$).

**Wien Network Transfer Function:**

The feedback fraction is:
$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_{out}} = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

After algebraic simplification (as shown in Section 3.2.2):
$$\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3j\omega RC}$$

**Frequency of Oscillation (Phase Condition):**

For zero phase shift in the feedback network, the denominator must be purely real, hence the imaginary part of the ratio is zero when the real part of the denominator is zero:

$$1 - \omega_o^2 R^2 C^2 = 0$$

$$\omega_o = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Magnitude Condition:**

At $\omega = \omega_o$, the feedback factor becomes:
$$\beta = \frac{j\omega_o RC}{3j\omega_o RC} = \frac{1}{3}$$

For $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:
$$A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2$$

Therefore, the gain of the non-inverting amplifier must be **at least 3** to sustain oscillations, and exactly 3 for stable amplitude.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:**

The RC phase shift oscillator consists of:
- A **common-emitter (CE) BJT amplifier** with voltage gain $A_v = \dfrac{-h_{fe} \cdot R_C}{R_{in}}$.
- A **three-section RC ladder network** connected between the collector and the base.
- Each RC section has resistance $R$ and capacitance $C$, producing $60°$ phase shift.

**Working Principle:**

The CE amplifier introduces $180°$ phase shift (inverting). The RC ladder network must provide an additional $180°$ to satisfy the Barkhausen phase condition. With three identical RC sections, each provides $60°$, totaling $180°$. The loop phase shift becomes $360° = 0°$, and oscillations are sustained.

**Derivation of Frequency:**

The feedback network is a three-stage RC ladder. By solving the KVL equations (as in Section 3.1.2), the feedback ratio is obtained as:
$$\beta = \frac{1}{1 - 5\alpha^2 - j(6\alpha - \alpha^3)}$$
where $\alpha = \omega RC$.

Setting the imaginary part of the denominator to zero (for zero phase shift of $\beta$... actually, for a total loop phase of zero, the imaginary part of $1/\beta$ being zero means):

$$6\alpha - \alpha^3 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha^2 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \sqrt{6}$$

Therefore:
$$\omega_o = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$$

**Gain Condition:**

At $\alpha^2 = 6$:
$$\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{1 - 30} = \frac{1}{29}$$

For $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:
$$\vert A \vert = 29 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_{fe} \geq \frac{29 \cdot R_{in}}{R_C}$$

---

**(b)** Design an RC phase shift oscillator to generate a sine wave of **1 kHz** frequency. Use $C = 0.01 \text{ \mu F}$. Find the value of $R$ and the minimum gain of the amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $f_o = 1 \text{ kHz} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$
- $C = 0.01 \text{ \mu F} = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$

**Formula:**
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$$

**Step 1: Solve for $R$.**

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_o C \sqrt{6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.449}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2.449 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{1.539 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 6.49 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

**Step 2: Minimum Amplifier Gain.**

$$\vert A \vert_{min} = 29$$

**Step 3: Design Amplifier (using CE BJT).**

For a CE amplifier with $h_{fe} = 100$ and $R_C = 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega$:
$$R_{in} = \frac{\vert A \vert \cdot R_C}{h_{fe}} = \frac{29 \times 2200}{100} = 638 \text{ }\Omega$$

For op-amp implementation (inverting), $R_f / R_{in} = 29$, e.g., $R_{in} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 29 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Verification:** Confirming frequency:
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 6490 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.449} = \frac{1}{9.99 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1001 \text{ Hz} \approx 1 \text{ kHz} \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{6}$ factor** in the RC phase shift formula. Students often write $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ which is the Wien bridge formula — this is a **fatal error** worth 3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing inverting vs. non-inverting** amplifiers. RC phase shift uses **inverting** (gain $\geq 29$); Wien bridge uses **non-inverting** (gain $\geq 3$).
> 3. **Not stating the Barkhausen condition** in two parts (magnitude AND phase). The criterion has two parts — partial credit is given but full marks require both.
> 4. **Incorrect units** in derivations. Always use SI base units ($R$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in F, $f$ in Hz).
> 5. **Missing amplitude stabilization** in Wien bridge design. Without thermistors, diodes, or JFET gain control, the output will either saturate (distorted square wave) or die out.
> 6. **Not labeling the block diagram** with $A$, $\beta$, and the loop equation. A bare block diagram without annotations gets only partial credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Oscillators (KTU Module 2)**

### Core Concepts
- [x] An oscillator converts DC to AC without external input using **positive feedback**.
- [x] Barkhausen criterion: **$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$** AND **$\angle A\beta = 0°$ (or $360°$)**.
- [x] Oscillators are classified by their frequency-determining elements: **RC, LC, Crystal**.

### Block Diagram Essentials
- [x] Two essential blocks: **Amplifier (A)** and **Feedback Network (β)**.
- [x] Loop gain $A\beta$ must equal unity in magnitude and zero in phase.
- [x] Closed-loop gain: $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 - A\beta}$ (positive feedback form).

### RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Key Facts
- [x] Three cascaded RC sections, each producing **$60°$** phase shift.
- [x] Combined network phase shift = **$180°$**.
- [x] Inverting amplifier provides additional **$180°$**, total = $360° = 0°$.
- [x] **Frequency:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$
- [x] **Minimum gain:** $\vert A \vert \geq 29$
- [x] Derivation requires solving 3 KVL equations and using $\alpha^2 = 6$.

### Wien Bridge Oscillator — Key Facts
- [x] Uses **series + parallel RC** (Wien bridge network).
- [x] At $f_o$, the network is **purely resistive** with $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$.
- [x] **Frequency:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$
- [x] **Minimum gain:** $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 3$
- [x] Requires **amplitude stabilization** (thermistor/JFET/diode) for clean sine wave.

### Quick Comparison Mnemonic
- **RC Phase Shift** = "**3R-3C-180-180-29**" (3 RC, 180° net, 180° amp, gain 29)
- **Wien Bridge** = "**2R-2C-0-0-3**" (2 RC, 0° net, 0° amp, gain 3)

### Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid
- [x] Never confuse the two frequency formulas — check the amplifier type first.
- [x] Always state **both** Barkhausen conditions.
- [x] In derivations, show **all** algebraic steps — KTU examiners value stepwise working.
- [x] Include **units** in final numerical answers.
- [x] Draw **neat, labeled** block diagrams (minimum: A, β, input, output, summing junction).

### Engineering Applications to Remember
- [x] **RC oscillators** → Audio range (20 Hz – 100 kHz).
- [x] **Wien bridge** → Most common lab audio oscillator (low distortion).
- [x] **LC oscillators** → RF range (above 100 kHz).
- [x] **Crystal oscillators** → Frequency-stable clock sources.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Filter (Definition):** A **filter** is a two-port electronic network designed to selectively transmit signals within a specified band of frequencies while attenuating (rejecting) signals at frequencies outside that band. In the context of integrated circuits and systems, filters are fundamental building blocks used for signal conditioning, noise removal, frequency selection, and anti-aliasing operations.

In formal terms, a filter is characterized by its **transfer function** $H(j\omega)$ (or $H(s)$ in the Laplace domain), which describes the ratio of the output phasor to the input phasor as a function of angular frequency $\omega$. The magnitude response $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ and phase response $\angle H(j\omega)$ jointly define the filter's behavior in the frequency domain.

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}(j\omega)}{V_{in}(j\omega)}$$

A filter is generally classified into one of four canonical types based on its frequency-selective behavior:
- **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** — Passes low frequencies, attenuates high frequencies.
- **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** — Passes high frequencies, attenuates low frequencies.
- **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** — Passes a specific band, attenuates frequencies outside it.
- **Band-Stop (Notch) Filter (BSF)** — Attenuates a specific band, passes frequencies outside it.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Sieve and the Faucet**
> Imagine a kitchen sieve holding flour. The sieve allows fine flour particles to pass through while holding back larger impurities like husk or stones. A **filter** does the same thing, but for electrical signals — it allows *signals* of desired frequencies to flow through to the next stage of a circuit, while blocking or weakening *unwanted* frequencies (like noise, interference, or harmonics).

Another helpful analogy: consider an audio equalizer on a music player. When you raise the "bass" slider, you are electronically **boosting the low-frequency components** of the song (a low-pass behavior) and **attenuating high frequencies**. The equalizer is essentially a tunable filter system. Similarly, in a radio receiver, filters are used to isolate the specific radio station's frequency from the thousands of other signals crowded in the airwaves.

## 1.3 Why Filters Matter in Integrated Circuits

Filters are indispensable in modern IC design because they perform critical signal-processing tasks such as:
- **Noise Suppression:** Removing high-frequency noise from sensor signals.
- **Anti-Aliasing:** Preventing high-frequency content from folding back into the baseband during Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC).
- **Channel Selection:** In communication ICs (e.g., transceivers), filters select the desired frequency channel.
- **Smoothing:** After Digital-to-Analog Conversion (DAC), reconstruction filters smooth the staircase output.
- **Feedback Compensation:** In oscillators and PLLs, loop filters determine stability and dynamics.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember (in Bold):**
> - **Cut-off Frequency ($f_c$ or $\omega_c$):** The frequency at which the filter's power gain falls to **half** (i.e., -3 dB) of its maximum passband value.
> - **Passband:** The range of frequencies that the filter transmits with minimal attenuation.
> - **Stopband (Rejection Band):** The range of frequencies that the filter strongly attenuates.
> - **Transition Band:** The frequency region between the passband edge and the stopband edge.
> - **Roll-off Rate:** The steepness of attenuation outside the passband, measured in dB/decade or dB/octave.

---
