# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Filters

Filters can be classified along several dimensions. A clear understanding of these classifications is essential for solving KTU examination problems.

### 2.1.1 Based on the Type of Elements Used

- **Passive Filters:** Built using only **passive components** — resistors (R), capacitors (C), and inductors (L). They do not require an external power source. They exhibit low output drive capability and may introduce signal loss (insertion loss). Common examples: **RC low-pass filter, RL high-pass filter, LC band-pass filter.**

- **Active Filters:** Built using **active components** (operational amplifiers, transistors) along with R and C. They provide **gain**, have high input impedance, low output impedance, and can implement complex transfer functions without inductors (which are bulky and unsuitable for IC fabrication). Common examples: **Sallen-Key topology, Multiple Feedback (MFB) topology, State-variable filter.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** In PBICT404, the emphasis is on **active filters** because they are realizable in integrated circuit form. Pure inductor-based filters are rarely used in ICs because on-chip inductors are large, lossy, and have poor Q-factors.

### 2.1.2 Based on the Frequency Response Shape

- **Analog (Continuous-Time) Filters:** Process continuous-time analog signals. Implemented with op-amps, RC, and sometimes transconductors (OTA-based filters).
- **Digital Filters:** Process sampled/discrete signals using DSP algorithms or dedicated digital hardware.
- **Switched-Capacitor (SC) Filters:** A hybrid form used widely in CMOS ICs, where a resistor is emulated using a capacitor and a clocked switch — this allows precise, tunable time constants on-chip.

### 2.1.3 Based on the Approximation (Response Type)

- **Butterworth (Maximally Flat Magnitude):** Provides the flattest possible passband response at the cost of a moderate roll-off.
- **Chebyshev (Type I and Type II):** Allows ripple in the passband (Type I) or stopband (Type II) in exchange for a steeper roll-off.
- **Bessel (Linear Phase):** Optimized for linear phase response (constant group delay), minimizing waveform distortion.
- **Elliptic (Cauer):** Has ripple in both passband and stopband; offers the steepest roll-off for a given filter order.

## 2.2 The Ideal vs. Real Filter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Ideal Filter Concept:** An **ideal filter** has a "brick-wall" frequency response. It exhibits **unity gain in the passband**, **infinite attenuation in the stopband**, and a **zero-width transition band**. It is also **linear phase** (constant group delay) within the passband.

However, ideal filters are **physically unrealizable** (they violate the Paley-Wiener criterion and are non-causal). Real filters approximate this ideal behavior with:
- Finite passband gain (ideally constant).
- Gradual roll-off at the cutoff.
- Finite stopband attenuation.
- Possible passband or stopband ripple.
- Non-linear phase response near cutoff.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Transfer Function | $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ | Dimensionless, complex |
| Magnitude Response | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| Gain in dB | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ | dB |
| -3 dB Cutoff (Power) | $\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \vert H \vert_{max}^2$ | Half-power point |
| First-Order RC LPF Cutoff | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz |
| First-Order RC HPF Cutoff | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz |
| Roll-off Rate (1st order) | $-20$ dB/decade or $-6$ dB/octave | After cutoff |
| Roll-off Rate (n-th order) | $-20n$ dB/decade | n = filter order |
| Quality Factor (Q) | $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{BW} = \dfrac{\omega_0}{\Delta\omega}$ | Dimensionless |
| Bandwidth (BW) | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | Hz |
| Bode Plot Break Frequency | $\omega = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ where $\tau = RC$ | rad/s |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Filters are ubiquitous in every electronic system. Some prominent applications include:
- **Telecommunications:** Channel-selection filters in mobile phones, satellite receivers, and Wi-Fi transceivers.
- **Audio Engineering:** Crossover networks in loudspeakers (low-pass for woofers, high-pass for tweeters), graphic equalizers.
- **Biomedical Instrumentation:** ECG/EEG signal conditioning, where 50/60 Hz line-noise notch filters remove power-line interference.
- **Control Systems:** Compensation filters in feedback loops to ensure stability and desired transient response.
- **Power Electronics:** EMI/EMC filters to suppress conducted and radiated noise, ensuring regulatory compliance.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter

A first-order RC low-pass filter is the simplest possible filter and the foundation for understanding all higher-order designs. We derive its transfer function, magnitude response, phase response, and cutoff frequency.

### Step 1: Circuit Description

The circuit consists of a series resistor $R$ and a shunt capacitor $C$. The input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the series combination of $R$ and $C$. The output voltage $V_{out}$ is taken across the capacitor $C$.

### Step 2: Apply the Voltage Divider Rule

Using the impedance of the resistor ($Z_R = R$) and the impedance of the capacitor ($Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$), the voltage divider gives:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Impedances

Substituting the expressions for $Z_R$ and $Z_C$:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Expression

Multiply the numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$ to clear the fraction in the denominator:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### Step 5: Identify the Transfer Function

The transfer function $H(j\omega)$ is therefore:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### Step 6: Define the Cutoff Frequency

Let us define the **cutoff (or break) angular frequency** as:

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

Then the transfer function can be written in canonical form:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j(\omega / \omega_c)}$$

### Step 7: Magnitude Response

The magnitude of $H(j\omega)$ is found by taking the modulus:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

In decibels:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = -10 \log_{10}\left[1 + \left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^2\right]$$

### Step 8: Verify Behavior at Limiting Frequencies

- **At $\omega = 0$ (DC):** $\vert H \vert = 1$ (0 dB) — the capacitor is an open circuit at DC, so the entire input appears at the output. This is the **passband**.
- **At $\omega = \omega_c$:** $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ (which is $-3$ dB) — the **half-power** point.
- **At $\omega \to \infty$:** $\vert H \vert \to 0$ — the capacitor becomes a short circuit at very high frequencies, shunting the output to ground. This is the **stopband**.

### Step 9: Phase Response

The phase of $H(j\omega)$ is:

$$\angle H(j\omega) = -\arctan(\omega RC) = -\arctan\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)$$

- At $\omega \to 0$: phase $\to 0°$.
- At $\omega = \omega_c$: phase $= -45°$.
- At $\omega \to \infty$: phase $\to -90°$.

## 3.2 Derivation: First-Order RC High-Pass Filter

A first-order RC high-pass filter has the resistor and capacitor positions interchanged.

### Step 1: Voltage Divider

The output is taken across the resistor $R$, with the capacitor in series:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

### Step 2: Simplify

Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### Step 3: Canonical Form

Using $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j(\omega / \omega_c)}{1 + j(\omega / \omega_c)}$$

### Step 4: Magnitude and Phase

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} = \frac{(\omega / \omega_c)}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega / \omega_c)^2}}$$

$$\angle H(j\omega) = 90° - \arctan\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)$$

### Step 5: Limiting Behavior

- **At $\omega \to 0$ (DC):** $\vert H \vert \to 0$ — the capacitor blocks DC.
- **At $\omega = \omega_c$:** $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (-3 dB).
- **At $\omega \to \infty$:** $\vert H \vert \to 1$ (0 dB) — the capacitor acts as a short, so the full input appears at the output.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Filter Type vs. Transfer Function

| Filter Type | Transfer Function $H(s)$ | Passband | Stopband | Roll-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st-Order LPF | $\dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}$ | $\omega \ll \omega_c$ | $\omega \gg \omega_c$ | $-20$ dB/dec |
| 1st-Order HPF | $\dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$ | $\omega \gg \omega_c$ | $\omega \ll \omega_c$ | $+20$ dB/dec |
| 2nd-Order LPF | $\dfrac{\omega_0^2}{s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2}$ | $\omega \ll \omega_0$ | $\omega \gg \omega_0$ | $-40$ dB/dec |
| 2nd-Order HPF | $\dfrac{s^2}{s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2}$ | $\omega \gg \omega_0$ | $\omega \ll \omega_0$ | $+40$ dB/dec |
| Band-Pass (2nd) | $\dfrac{(\omega_0/Q) s}{s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2}$ | $\omega \approx \omega_0$ | $\omega \ll \omega_0, \omega \gg \omega_0$ | $\pm 20$ dB/dec |

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance of Q-Factor:** The quality factor $Q$ controls the **sharpness of resonance** in second-order filters. A higher Q produces a sharper peak (more selective), while a lower Q yields a more damped, gentle response. In the time domain, a high Q implies more ringing and overshoot in the step response.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a filter and classify filters based on their frequency response.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

A **filter** is a two-port network that selectively passes signals of certain frequencies while attenuating others. The transfer function $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ describes this frequency-selective behavior.

Based on the frequency response, filters are classified into four canonical types:
1. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Passes $0 \le f \le f_c$ and attenuates $f > f_c$.
2. **High-Pass Filter (HPF):** Passes $f \ge f_c$ and attenuates $f < f_c$.
3. **Band-Pass Filter (BPF):** Passes $f_L \le f \le f_H$ and attenuates frequencies outside this band.
4. **Band-Stop Filter (BSF):** Attenuates $f_L \le f \le f_H$ and passes frequencies outside this band.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for classification with appropriate frequency ranges)

---

### Question 2: What is meant by the cutoff frequency of a filter? How is it defined for a first-order RC low-pass filter?
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **cutoff frequency** ($f_c$ or $\omega_c$) is the frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function falls to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (approximately 0.707) of its maximum passband value, corresponding to a **-3 dB drop** in power gain.

For a first-order RC low-pass filter with transfer function:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

The cutoff angular frequency is:

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

At this frequency, $\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, and the phase shift is exactly $-45°$.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for -3 dB point, 1 for the RC formula)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order RC low-pass filter. Plot its magnitude and phase response, and identify the cutoff frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare ideal and real filter characteristics. With a neat sketch, explain the magnitude response of a Butterworth and a Chebyshev filter, highlighting passband behavior. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Circuit:** A series resistor $R$ with a shunt capacitor $C$, output taken across $C$.

**Step 1 — Apply Voltage Divider:**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1/(j\omega C)}{R + 1/(j\omega C)}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify by multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 3 — Define $\omega_c = 1/(RC)$:**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j(\omega / \omega_c)}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude Response:**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

| Frequency | $\vert H \vert$ | dB Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\omega = 0$ | $1$ | $0$ dB |
| $\omega = \omega_c$ | $0.707$ | $-3$ dB |
| $\omega = 10\omega_c$ | $0.0995$ | $-20$ dB |
| $\omega = 100\omega_c$ | $0.01$ | $-40$ dB |

**Step 5 — Phase Response:**

$$\phi(\omega) = -\arctan(\omega RC)$$

- At $\omega = 0$: $\phi = 0°$
- At $\omega = \omega_c$: $\phi = -45°$
- At $\omega \to \infty$: $\phi = -90°$

**Step 6 — Roll-off Rate:** Beyond the cutoff, the gain decreases at $-20$ dB/decade or equivalently $-6$ dB/octave.

**Sketch Description:** The magnitude plot is flat at 0 dB in the passband, then bends at $f_c$ and rolls off linearly (on a log-log Bode plot) with a slope of $-20$ dB/decade. The phase plot starts at $0°$, passes through $-45°$ at $f_c$, and asymptotically approaches $-90°$.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

| Feature | Ideal Filter | Real Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Passband Gain | Exactly unity (0 dB), flat | Approximately unity, with possible ripple |
| Stopband Attenuation | Infinite | Finite, but typically large |
| Transition Band | Zero width | Finite, non-zero width |
| Phase Response | Linear (constant group delay) | Non-linear, distorted near $f_c$ |
| Realizability | Physically unrealizable (non-causal) | Causal and realizable |

**Butterworth Filter:** Provides the **maximally flat magnitude** in the passband (no ripple). The magnitude squared is given by:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega / \omega_c)^{2n}}$$

where $n$ is the filter order. As $n$ increases, the roll-off becomes steeper and approaches the ideal brick-wall response.

**Chebyshev Filter (Type I):** Allows **equiripple in the passband** in exchange for a **steeper roll-off** for the same order. The magnitude squared is:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \epsilon^2 T_n^2(\omega / \omega_c)}$$

where $T_n(\cdot)$ is the Chebyshev polynomial of the first kind and $\epsilon$ controls the passband ripple.

**Sketch:** For both, the passband ends at $\omega_c$. Butterworth has a smooth, ripple-free passband; Chebyshev oscillates between $0$ dB and $-A_{max}$ in the passband, but transitions to the stopband much more steeply.

**Trade-off Summary:**
- **Butterworth:** Best for applications requiring flat gain (e.g., audio crossovers).
- **Chebyshev:** Best for applications requiring sharp roll-off with relaxed gain-flatness (e.g., communication channel filters).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the transfer function of a first-order RC high-pass filter. State its cutoff frequency and phase response. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the classification of filters into passive and active types. List the advantages of active filters over passive filters in IC design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Circuit:** A series capacitor $C$ followed by a shunt resistor $R$ to ground, with the output taken across $R$.

**Step 1 — Apply Voltage Divider:**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R}{R + 1/(j\omega C)}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 3 — Define $\omega_c = 1/(RC)$:**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j(\omega / \omega_c)}{1 + j(\omega / \omega_c)}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude Response:**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Limiting Behavior:**
- $\omega \to 0$: $\vert H \vert \to 0$ (DC blocked)
- $\omega = \omega_c$: $\vert H \vert = 0.707$ (-3 dB)
- $\omega \to \infty$: $\vert H \vert \to 1$ (full transmission)

**Step 6 — Phase Response:**

$$\phi(\omega) = 90° - \arctan\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)$$

- $\omega \to 0$: $\phi \to +90°$
- $\omega = \omega_c$: $\phi = +45°$
- $\omega \to \infty$: $\phi \to 0°$

**Step 7 — Cutoff Frequency:**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Diagram Description:** The magnitude Bode plot rises at $+20$ dB/decade below $f_c$, flattens at 0 dB above $f_c$. The phase plot starts at $+90°$ and falls to $0°$, passing through $+45°$ at the cutoff.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Passive Filters:** Built using only R, L, and C. They do not require any external power source.

*Examples:* RC low-pass, RL high-pass, RLC band-pass, LC band-stop.

**Active Filters:** Built using active elements (op-amps, transistors, OTAs) along with R and C. They require an external DC power supply.

*Examples:* Sallen-Key filter, Multiple-Feedback (MFB) filter, State-variable filter, OTA-C filter, Switched-Capacitor filter.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Passive Filter | Active Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Components | R, L, C only | R, C + op-amp/transistor |
| Power Supply | Not required | Required |
| Gain | Less than unity (lossy) | Can be greater than unity |
| Inductors needed? | Yes (for some types) | No (inductorless) |
| IC Compatibility | Poor (inductors are bulky) | Excellent |
| Loading Effect | Significant | Negligible (high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$) |
| Tunability | Difficult (change L or C) | Easier (change R or use SC techniques) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate |

**Advantages of Active Filters in IC Design:**

1. **Inductorless Design:** Realizable using only R, C, and op-amps — essential for monolithic IC integration where on-chip inductors are impractical.

2. **Gain and Buffering:** Provide signal gain (greater than unity) and act as buffers with high input impedance and low output impedance, eliminating loading effects.

3. **Cascadability:** Easy to cascade multiple stages to achieve higher-order responses without significant interaction between stages.

4. **Tunability:** Cutoff frequency can be electronically tuned by varying resistance (using MOSFETs in triode as voltage-controlled resistors) or by adjusting clock frequency in SC filters.

5. **Better Q-Factor:** Active second-order filters can achieve higher Q (selectivity) with stable, well-controlled behavior.

6. **Compact and Lightweight:** Especially suited for portable and miniaturized electronics.

7. **Design Flexibility:** A wide variety of topologies (Sallen-Key, MFB, State-variable, biquad) are available, each suited to specific design requirements.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the -3 dB definition:** The cutoff frequency is the **half-power point**, not the half-voltage point. Some students confuse the two — voltage is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, while power is $\dfrac{1}{2}$.
> 2. **Mixing $\omega$ and $f$:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ while $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$. Examiners deduct marks if you use the wrong form.
> 3. **Sign of the phase:** For a LPF, phase is **negative** (lagging); for a HPF, it is **positive** (leading) at low frequencies. Confusing these loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Roll-off slope:** A first-order filter has $-20$ dB/decade roll-off. Don't write "$-6$ dB/decade" — that's the per-octave rate. Be careful with the units.
> 5. **Units in numerical answers:** Always express frequencies in **Hz** and angular frequencies in **rad/s** with proper labeling.
> 6. **Ideal vs Real filters:** Examiners often ask: *"Why are ideal filters unrealizable?"* — Answer: because they require a non-causal, infinite-order transfer function (Paley-Wiener criterion). Don't just say "they don't exist physically."
> 7. **Drawing neat Bode plots:** A Bode plot with **no labeled axes**, **no unit markings**, or **no value at cutoff** will be penalized. Always label the magnitude axis in dB, the frequency axis in Hz, and mark $f_c$ clearly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Filters: Introduction**

- **Filter:** A 2-port network that frequency-selectively passes or attenuates signals. Mathematically described by its transfer function $H(j\omega)$.

- **Four Canonical Types:** LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF — each defined by a unique passband/stopband configuration.

- **Ideal Filter:** Brick-wall response, unity passband gain, infinite stopband attenuation, zero transition band, linear phase → **Physically unrealizable** (non-causal).

- **Real Filter:** Finite roll-off, finite stopband attenuation, gradual transition band, possible passband/stopband ripple.

- **Cutoff Frequency ($f_c$):** Frequency at which gain drops by **-3 dB** (half-power) from the maximum passband value.

- **First-Order RC LPF:**
  - $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$
  - $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$
  - Roll-off: $-20$ dB/decade
  - Phase: $0° \to -90°$ as $\omega$ increases

- **First-Order RC HPF:**
  - $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$
  - Same $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$
  - Roll-off: $+20$ dB/decade (rising)
  - Phase: $+90° \to 0°$ as $\omega$ increases

- **n-th Order Filter Roll-off:** $-20n$ dB/decade (or $+20n$ for HPF). Higher order → steeper transition.

- **Passive vs Active:** Passive = R, L, C only (lossy, no gain, no power supply). Active = R, C + op-amp/OTA (gain, buffer action, IC-compatible, tunable).

- **Approximation Types (4):** Butterworth (flat), Chebyshev (ripple, steep), Bessel (linear phase), Elliptic (ripple both, steepest).

- **Quality Factor (Q):** $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{BW}$; controls resonance sharpness in 2nd-order filters.

- **Bode Plot Construction:** Use straight-line asymptotes (0 dB flat region + $\pm 20n$ dB/decade slope) with the actual curve deviating by $\pm 3$ dB at each break frequency.

- **Decibel Conversion:** $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert H \vert$. A 10× change in voltage → 20 dB; 100× → 40 dB.

- **IC Realization Preference:** Active filters dominate in IC design because inductors are bulky, lossy, and have poor Q on-chip. **SC filters** and **OTA-C filters** are the workhorses of CMOS integrated analog signal processing.

- **Common Applications:** Anti-aliasing, image rejection, channel selection, noise suppression, audio crossover, biomedical signal conditioning, and power-line interference rejection (50/60 Hz notch filter).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LPF: First order filter, Second order filter (Sallen-Key Butterworth), Higher order filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBICT404 - INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** LPF: First order filter, Second order filter (Sallen-Key Butterworth), Higher order filter ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a Low-Pass Filter (LPF)? A **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** is a two-port electronic network designed to pass signals whose frequencies are **below a specified cutoff frequency (f_c or ω₀)** while attenuating (rejecting) signals whose frequencies lie **above** this threshold. In the KTU 2024 scheme, LPFs are studied under the unified theory of **active filters** realized using op-amps, resistors, and capacitors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > *An active low-pass filter is a frequency-selective network that employs an active device (typically an op-amp) along with passive RC components to provide gain and sharp roll-off above the cutoff frequency, characterized by the transfer function H(s) = A₀ / [1 + a₁s + a₂s² + … + aₙsⁿ] where the order `n` dictates the steepness of attenuation.* The mathematical ideal of an LPF magnitude response is: $$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \begin{cases} 1, & \omega \le \omega_c \\ 0, & \omega > \omega_c \end{cases}$$ This ideal "brick-wall" response is **physically unrealizable** (it violates the Paley-Wiener criterion); therefore, we approximate it using rational polynomial transfer functions. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Tap" Intuition 💧 Imagine a **water tap connected to a pipe with a pressure-relief valve**: - **Low-frequency signals** → steady, slow water flow → passes easily through the pipe. - **High-frequency signals** → rapid, oscillating pressure changes → the relief valve "lets off" the excess pressure (i.e., the filter attenuates them). The **capacitor** in an RC filter is the electrical equivalent of this relief valve: - At **low frequencies**, the capacitor's reactance $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ is very **high** → it blocks current → signal is **passed** to the output. - At **high frequencies**, $X_C$ becomes very **low** → it shorts the signal to ground → output is **attenuated**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Students:** > Every time you double the order of the filter (1st → 2nd → 3rd …), the roll-off steepness **doubles** (–20 dB/dec → –40 dB/dec → –60 dB/dec …). More order = sharper cutoff = closer to the ideal brick-wall. --- ### 1.3 Classification of LPFs by Order | Filter Type | Roll-off Past f_c | Transfer Function Order | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **1st-order LPF** | –20 dB/decade | n = 1 | Audio tone control, anti-aliasing | | **2nd-order LPF** | –40 dB/decade | n = 2 | Equalizers, low-frequency noise removal | | **Higher-order (Nth)** | –20N dB/decade | n ≥ 3 | Precision instrumentation, ADC pre-filters | > [!TIP] > **Physical Constants & Units used throughout this module:** > - **Cutoff frequency** $f_c$ in **Hertz (Hz)** ; $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c$ in **rad/sec**. > - **Quality factor** $Q$ is **dimensionless**. > - **Resistors** in **Ohms (Ω)** ; **Capacitors** in **Farads (F)**. > - **Gain** $A_0$ (DC gain) is **dimensionless** (in V/V) or expressed in **dB** as $20\log_{10}\vert A_0 \vert$. --- ### 1.4 Block Diagram — Filter Realization Hierarchy ``` HIGHER-ORDER FILTER (Nth order) │ ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────┐ │ │ │ 2nd-order 2nd-order 1st-order Stage Stage Stage (Sallen-Key) (Sallen-Key) (RC + Buffer) │ │ │ └─────────────────┴─────────────────┘ │ OUTPUT ``` This **cascade architecture** is the canonical way to build higher-order filters from elementary 1st- and 2nd-order building blocks. --- ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 First-Order Low-Pass Filter #### 2.1.1 Passive RC LPF The simplest first-order LPF is a series resistor and shunt capacitor: ``` Vin ──[ R ]──┬──── Vout │ ─┴─ C │ GND ``` Using the voltage divider rule in the s-domain: $$V_{out}(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{1/sC}{R + 1/sC} = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$ **Transfer Function:** $$\boxed{H_1(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_c}}}$$ **Cutoff frequency (-3 dB point):** $$\boxed{\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \text{or} \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$ #### 2.1.2 Active First-Order LPF (with Gain) When gain is required, the RC network is followed by a **non-inverting op-amp** configured as a buffer or with gain $A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_g}$: $$H_1(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + sRC} = \frac{A_0 \,\omega_c}{s + \omega_c}$$ #### 2.1.3 Magnitude & Phase Response $$|H_1(j\omega)| = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$ $$\angle H_1(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$ **Behaviour:** - For $\omega \ll \omega_c$: $|H_1| \approx A_0$ (passband, 0 dB slope). - For $\omega \gg \omega_c$: $|H_1| \approx \dfrac{A_0}{\omega RC}$ → **–20 dB/decade** roll-off. --- ### 2.2 Second-Order Low-Pass Filter — General Theory A generic **second-order LPF** transfer function is: $$\boxed{H_2(s) = \frac{A_0 \,\omega_0^2}{s^2 + \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}s + \omega_0^2}}$$ Where: - $\omega_0$ = **natural (undamped) frequency** in rad/sec - $Q$ = **Quality factor** (dimensionless) — controls peaking near cutoff - $A_0$ = **DC gain** **Poles of the filter:** $$s_{1,2} = -\frac{\omega_0}{2Q} \pm j\omega_0 \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{4Q^2}}$$ | Response Type | Q Value | Application | |---|---|---| | **Butterworth** (maximally flat) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ | General purpose, audio | | **Chebyshev** (equiripple) | $Q > 0.707$ | Sharper cutoff, ripple in passband | | **Bessel** (linear phase) | $Q < 0.707$ | Pulse preservation | | **Critically damped** | $Q = 0.5$ | No overshoot | **Roll-off:** –40 dB/decade beyond $\omega_0$. --- ### 2.3 Sallen-Key Topology — The "Equal-Component" Design The **Sallen-Key** (also called VCVS — Voltage-Controlled Voltage-Source) topology is the **most popular** second-order active filter realization in KTU textbooks because it uses a single op-amp and equal-value components. **Circuit Structure:** ``` R₁ R₂ Vin ──[R₁]──┬──[R₂]──┬─────────┐ │ │ │ C₁ C₂ │ │ │ │ GND │ Non-inverting │ ┌─────┐ └──────┤ + │──── Vout │ A │ ┌──────┤ - │ │ └──┬──┘ │ │ R_f R_g │ │ └────┬────┘ GND ``` **With equal components** ($R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$): $$\boxed{H_{SK}(s) = \frac{K \,\omega_0^2}{s^2 + (3-K)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}}$$ Where: - $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_g}$ = **non-inverting gain** - $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ = **pole frequency** **Comparison with standard form:** $$\frac{\omega_0}{Q} = (3 - K)\omega_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{Q = \frac{1}{3 - K}}$$ --- ### 2.4 Sallen-Key Butterworth Design For a **Butterworth response**, we require $Q = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$: $$\frac{1}{3 - K} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3 - K = \sqrt{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586}$$ **Resistor selection for the desired gain:** $$K = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_g} = 3 - \sqrt{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_f}{R_g} = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sallen-Key Butterworth Design Recipe (Exam Hall Favourite):** > 1. Choose $f_c$ (cutoff frequency). > 2. Pick a convenient $C$ (e.g., 10 nF). > 3. Compute $R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_c C}$. > 4. Set $R_1 = R_2 = R$. > 5. Choose $R_g = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ (typical) and compute $R_f = 0.586 \cdot R_g$. --- ### 2.5 Higher-Order Filters To achieve sharper roll-off, **first- and second-order stages are cascaded**. For an **Nth-order filter**: $$\text{Roll-off} = -20N \text{ dB/decade}$$ $$H_N(s) = H_1(s) \cdot H_2(s) \cdot H_2(s) \cdots$$ | Order (N) | Roll-off | Typical Realization | |---|---|---| | **3rd** | –60 dB/dec | One 1st-order + One 2nd-order stage | | **4th** | –80 dB/dec | Two cascaded 2nd-order Sallen-Key | | **5th** | –100 dB/dec | One 1st-order + Two 2nd-order stages | | **Nth (even)** | –20N dB/dec | N/2 Sallen-Key stages | | **Nth (odd)** | –20N dB/dec | One 1st-order + (N-1)/2 Sallen-Key stages | > [!NOTE] > **Design Trick for Higher-Order Butterworth:** > For an Nth-order Butterworth LPF, the Q-values of individual 2nd-order stages are obtained by pairing the complex-conjugate pole pairs. A common reference table (used in KTU problems): > - **2nd-order:** Q = 0.707 > - **4th-order:** Q₁ = 0.541, Q₂ = 1.307 > - **6th-order:** Q₁ = 0.518, Q₂ = 0.707, Q₃ = 1.932 --- ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $H_1(s) = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + sRC}$ | 1st-order LPF transfer function | V/V | | 2 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | –3 dB cutoff frequency | Hz | | 3 | $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | Angular cutoff frequency | rad/s | | 4 | Roll-off = –20 dB/dec | 1st-order asymptote slope | dB/decade | | 5 | $H_2(s) = \dfrac{A_0 \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (\omega_0 / Q)s + \omega_0^2}$ | General 2nd-order LPF | V/V | | 6 | $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_g}$ | Non-inverting op-amp gain | V/V | | 7 | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ (equal R, C) | Sallen-Key pole frequency | rad/s | | 8 | $Q = \dfrac{1}{3 - K}$ | Sallen-Key quality factor | dimensionless | | 9 | $K_{BW} = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586$ | Butterworth gain for equal-component SK | V/V | | 10 | $Q_{BW} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ | Butterworth Q-factor | dimensionless | | 11 | Roll-off = –40 dB/dec | 2nd-order asymptote slope | dB/decade | | 12 | Roll-off = –20N dB/dec | Nth-order asymptote slope | dB/decade | | 13 | $s_{1,2} = -\sigma \pm j\omega_d$ | Complex pole pair location | rad/s | | 14 | $\sigma = \dfrac{\omega_0}{2Q}$ | Damping factor (real part of pole) | Np/s | | 15 | $\omega_d = \omega_0 \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{1}{4Q^2}}$ | Damped natural frequency | rad/s | --- ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications | Field | Application of LPF | |---|---| | **Audio Engineering** | Subwoofer crossover, hiss removal | | **Communications** | Channel selection, anti-aliasing before ADC | | **Biomedical (ECG/EEG)** | Removal of 50/60 Hz mains noise (Notch), and EMG high-freq | | **Power Electronics** | Smoothing PWM outputs, ripple filtering | | **Control Systems** | Loop compensation, anti-aliasing in digital control | | **Instrumentation** | Signal conditioning for low-frequency sensors (thermocouples, strain gauges) | --- ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order RC LPF Transfer Function **Starting from KVL/KCL in the s-domain:** The output voltage across the capacitor is given by the **voltage divider rule**: $$ V_{out}(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C} $$ **Substitute impedances** $Z_R = R$ and $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{sC}$: $$ V_{out}(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{\dfrac{1}{sC}}{R + \dfrac{1}{sC}} $$ **Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$:** $$ V_{out}(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{1}{sRC + 1} $$ **Therefore the transfer function is:** $$ \boxed{H_1(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}} $$ **Finding cutoff frequency:** Substitute $s = j\omega$ and $H_1(j\omega_c) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (the –3 dB point): $$ \left|\frac{1}{1 + j\omega_c RC}\right| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $$ **Solve for $\omega_c$:** $$ 1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (\omega_c RC)^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_c RC = 1 $$ $$ \boxed{\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \text{or} \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}} $$ **Magnitude in dB:** $$ \vert H_1(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}\right) = -10\log_{10}\bigl(1 + (\omega RC)^2\bigr) $$ - For $\omega = \omega_c$: $\vert H_1 \vert_{dB} = -10\log_{10}(2) = -3.0103$ dB ✓ - For $\omega = 10\omega_c$: $\vert H_1 \vert_{dB} \approx -20$ dB (one decade down) ✓ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sallen-Key Second-Order LPF Transfer Function **Assumptions (standard Sallen-Key equal-component):** - $R_1 = R_2 = R$ - $C_1 = C_2 = C$ - Op-amp is ideal (input current = 0, $V_+ = V_-$) - Non-inverting gain: $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_g}$ **Define node voltages** at $V_1$ (junction of $R_1, C_1, R_2$) and $V_2$ (junction of $R_2, C_2$, op-amp + input): **Step A — At the non-inverting input:** The op-amp output equals $V_{out} = K \cdot V_2$ (since $V_+ = V_2$ and $V_- = V_{out}/K$). With ideal op-amp, $V_- = V_+$, so the voltage at the inverting node (junction of $R_f, R_g$) is also $V_2$, but for KCL we use the fact that **no current flows into the op-amp inputs**. **Step B — KCL at node $V_1$:** $$ \frac{V_{in} - V_1}{R_1} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_1 - 0}{1/(sC_1)} $$ Substituting $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C$: $$ \frac{V_{in} - V_1}{R} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R} + sC(V_1) $$ **Multiply through by $R$:** $$ V_{in} - V_1 = V_1 - V_2 + sRC V_1 $$ $$ V_{in} = 2V_1 - V_2 + sRC V_1 $$ $$ V_{in} = V_1(2 + sRC) - V_2 \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)} $$ **Step C — KCL at node $V_2$:** Since op-amp draws no current at $V_2$ (high-impedance), the current from $R_2$ must equal the current into $C_2$: $$ \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_2} = \frac{V_2 - 0}{1/(sC_2)} $$ With $R_2 = R$ and $C_2 = C$: $$ \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R} = sC V_2 $$ $$ V_1 - V_2 = sRC V_2 $$ $$ V_1 = V_2(1 + sRC) \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)} $$ **Step D — Apply gain condition:** $V_2 = \dfrac{V_{out}}{K}$ (since the non-inverting amplifier has gain $K$): **Step E — Substitute Eq. 2 into Eq. 1:** $$ V_{in} = V_2(1 + sRC)(2 + sRC) - V_2 $$ $$ V_{in} = V_2 \bigl[(1 + sRC)(2 + sRC) - 1\bigr] $$ **Expand the product:** $$ (1 + sRC)(2 + sRC) = 2 + sRC + 2sRC + (sRC)^2 = 2 + 3sRC + (sRC)^2 $$ **Subtract 1:** $$ V_{in} = V_2\bigl[1 + 3sRC + (sRC)^2\bigr] $$ **Substitute $V_2 = V_{out}/K$:** $$ V_{in} = \frac{V_{out}}{K}\bigl[1 + 3sRC + (sRC)^2\bigr] $$ **Therefore, the transfer function is:** $$ H_{SK}(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{K}{1 + 3sRC + (sRC)^2} $$ **Normalize to standard form $s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2$:** Divide numerator and denominator by $(sRC)^2$: $$ H_{SK}(s) = \frac{K/(sRC)^2}{1/(sRC)^2 + 3/(sRC) + 1} $$ Multiply numerator and denominator by $\dfrac{1}{R^2C^2}$: $$ H_{SK}(s) = \frac{K \cdot \dfrac{1}{(RC)^2}}{s^2 + \dfrac{3}{RC}s + \dfrac{1}{(RC)^2}} $$ **Recognize $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$:** $$ \boxed{H_{SK}(s) = \frac{K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (3 - K)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}} $$ **Equate to standard form:** $\dfrac{\omega_0}{Q} = (3 - K)\omega_0$: $$ \boxed{Q = \frac{1}{3 - K}} $$ **For Butterworth** $Q = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$: $$ 3 - K = \sqrt{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = 3 - \sqrt{2} $$ $$ \boxed{K_{Butterworth} = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586} $$ **Design resistor ratio:** $$ K = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_g} = 3 - \sqrt{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_f}{R_g} = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification at $\omega = 0$ (DC):** $H_{SK}(0) = \dfrac{K \omega_0^2}{\omega_0^2} = K$ ✓ (DC gain equals the amplifier gain) > > **Verification at $\omega \to \infty$:** $H_{SK} \to 0$ ✓ (high frequencies attenuated) --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Higher-Order Filter — 4th-Order Cascade **Goal:** Design a 4th-order Butterworth LPF with $f_c = 1\text{ kHz}$. **Step 1 — Determine Q values for 4th-order Butterworth:** The 4th-order Butterworth polynomial in normalized form is: $$ B_4(s) = (s^2 + 0.7654s + 1)(s^2 + 1.8478s + 1) $$ **Therefore:** - Stage 1: $Q_1 = \dfrac{1}{0.7654} \approx 1.3066$ - Stage 2: $Q_2 = \dfrac{1}{1.8478} \approx 0.5412$ **Step 2 — Compute gains for each Sallen-Key stage:** $$ K_1 = 3 - \frac{1}{Q_1} = 3 - 0.7654 = 2.2346 $$ $$ K_2 = 3 - \frac{1}{Q_2} = 3 - 1.8478 = 1.1522 $$ **Step 3 — Choose C = 10 nF; compute R:** $$ R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = 15.92 \text{ k}\Omega $$ **Step 4 — Compute feedback resistors (with $R_g = 10$ kΩ):** $$ R_{f1} = (K_1 - 1) R_g = 1.2346 \times 10\text{ k}\Omega = 12.35 \text{ k}\Omega $$ $$ R_{f2} = (K_2 - 1) R_g = 0.1522 \times 10\text{ k}\Omega = 1.52 \text{ k}\Omega $$ **Step 5 — Cascade the two Sallen-Key stages:** $$ H_{4th}(s) = H_{SK1}(s) \cdot H_{SK2}(s) = \frac{K_1 \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (3-K_1)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2} \cdot \frac{K_2 \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (3-K_2)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2} $$ $$ H_{4th}(s) = \frac{K_1 K_2 \,\omega_0^4}{(s^2 + 0.7654\,\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2)(s^2 + 1.8478\,\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2)} $$ **Total DC gain:** $K_{total} = K_1 \cdot K_2 = 2.2346 \times 1.1522 \approx 2.575$ V/V > [!IMPORTANT] > **Resulting roll-off: –80 dB/decade** past 1 kHz — much sharper than a single 2nd-order stage (–40 dB/dec). --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Conversion of Pole Location → Time-Domain Response For a 2nd-order system, the impulse response is governed by pole location. With complex poles $s_{1,2} = -\sigma \pm j\omega_d$: $$ \sigma = \frac{\omega_0}{2Q}, \quad \omega_d = \omega_0\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{4Q^2}} $$ The natural step response is: $$ h(t) = \frac{K \omega_0^2}{\omega_d} e^{-\sigma t} \sin(\omega_d t) \cdot u(t) $$ **For Butterworth** $Q = 0.707$: $$ \sigma = \frac{\omega_0}{2 \times 0.707} = 0.707\,\omega_0 $$ $$ \omega_d = \omega_0 \sqrt{1 - 0.25} = \omega_0 \times 0.866 = 0.866\,\omega_0 $$ This gives a **damped oscillation** that decays to zero — characteristic of a maximally-flat Butterworth response with mild overshoot (~4.3%). --- ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Active LPF Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[INPUT SIGNAL
Vin] --> nodeB[1st-Order RC Stage
R and C in series-shunt] nodeB --> nodeC{OP-AMP BUFFER
Unity Gain Follower] nodeC --> nodeD[OUTPUT 1
1st-order response] nodeA --> nodeE[R1 and C1 Network
Input RC] nodeE --> nodeF[R2 and C2 Network
Feedback RC] nodeF --> nodeG[OP-AMP
Non-Inverting Gain K] nodeG --> nodeH[OUTPUT 2
2nd-order Sallen-Key] nodeD --> nodeI[STAGE CASCADE BUS] nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ[OUTPUT
Higher-Order N-th] style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeD fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeH fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeJ fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeG fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` --- ### 4.2 Signal Flow — Sallen-Key LPF Operation ```mermaid flowchart LR inNode[Vin] --> r1Node[R1] r1Node --> v1Node((V1 Node)) v1Node --> r2Node[R2] r2Node --> v2Node((V2 Node)) v1Node --> c1Node[C1 to GND] v2Node --> c2Node[C2 to GND] v2Node --> opampNode[OP-AMP Non-Inverting] opampNode --> voutNode[Vout] opampNode -.Feedback.-> fbNode[Rf and Rg] fbNode -.-> v2Node style inNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style voutNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style v1Node fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style v2Node fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style opampNode fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000 ``` --- ### 4.3 Pole-Zero Pattern for Various 2nd-Order Responses ```mermaid flowchart TD startPZ[Second-Order Pole Locations] --> qLow[Q less than 0.5
Overdamped Real Poles] startPZ --> qHalf[Q equals 0.5
Critically Damped] startPZ --> qBessel[Q between 0.5 and 0.707
Bessel Response] startPZ --> qBW[Q equals 0.707
Butterworth Maximally Flat] startPZ --> qCheb[Q greater than 0.707
Chebyshev with Peak] startPZ --> qHigh[Q approaches infinity
Oscillator Sustained] qBW --> bwApp[Application: Audio Crossover
No Ripple No Peak] qCheb --> chebApp[Application: Sharp Cutoff
Ripple in Passband] qBessel --> besselApp[Application: Pulse Preservation
Linear Phase] style startPZ fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style qBW fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style qCheb fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000 style qBessel fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style bwApp fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#000 ``` --- ### 4.4 Cascade Realization of a 4th-Order LPF ```mermaid flowchart TD sigIn[Vin] --> s1Buf[Input Buffer Op-Amp] s1Buf --> s1RC[R1 and C1 to GND] s1RC --> s1Op[Op-Amp1 Gain K1 equals 2.235] s1Op --> s1Out[Stage 1 Output Q1 equals 1.307] s1Out --> s2Buf[Inter-stage Buffer] s2Buf --> s2RC[R3 and C3 to GND] s2RC --> s2Op[Op-Amp2 Gain K2 equals 1.152] s2Op --> s2Out[Stage 2 Output Q2 equals 0.541] s2Out --> finalOut[Vout 4th-order LPF
Roll-off minus 80 dB per dec] style sigIn fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style s1Op fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000 style s2Op fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000 style finalOut fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` --- ### 4.5 Magnitude Response Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR freqAxis[FREQUENCY AXIS] --> p1[DC Region
All Pass] freqAxis --> p2[fc Region
Cutoff] freqAxis --> p3[Beyond fc
Attenuation Begins] p1 --> r1[1st-order: Flat then -20 dB/dec] p2 --> r2[2nd-order: Flat then -40 dB/dec] p3 --> r3[4th-order: Flat then -80 dB/dec] p3 --> r4[6th-order: Flat then -120 dB/dec] r1 --> note1[Audio Tone Control] r2 --> note2[Standard Crossover] r3 --> note3[Precision Instrument Filter] r4 --> note4[Spectrum Analyzer Anti-alias] style p1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style p2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style p3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style note1 fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#000 style note2 fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#000 style note3 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000 style note4 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000 ``` --- ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define cutoff frequency (-3 dB frequency) of a first-order low-pass filter. Derive the expression for the cutoff frequency of an RC low-pass filter.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **cutoff frequency** $f_c$ of a first-order low-pass filter is the frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (i.e., 70.7%) of its maximum (DC) value, corresponding to a power loss of **half** or **–3 dB**. For an RC LPF with transfer function: $$ H_1(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC} $$ The magnitude is: $$ \vert H_1(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} $$ Setting $\vert H_1(j\omega_c) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$: $$ \sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2} = \sqrt{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_c RC = 1 $$ $$ \boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}} $$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *State the transfer function of a second-order Butterworth low-pass filter. What is the value of Q for a Butterworth response?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The general second-order LPF transfer function: $$ H_2(s) = \frac{A_0 \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}s + \omega_0^2} $$ For a **Butterworth** (maximally flat magnitude) response, the quality factor is: $$ \boxed{Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707} $$ This value ensures the **flattest possible passband** with no ripples and a –3 dB point exactly at $\omega_0$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP Module 3]** **(a)** Derive the transfer function of a **Sallen-Key second-order low-pass filter** with equal resistors and equal capacitors. Show that the quality factor is $Q = \dfrac{1}{3-K}$, where $K$ is the non-inverting gain. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a **Butterworth response** in the above Sallen-Key LPF, find the value of $K$ and the ratio $\dfrac{R_f}{R_g}$. Design such a filter for $f_c = 5\text{ kHz}$ using $C = 4.7\text{ nF}$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Consider the Sallen-Key topology with $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$, and op-amp non-inverting gain $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_g}$. **KCL at node $V_1$:** $$ \frac{V_{in} - V_1}{R} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R} + sC \cdot V_1 $$ Multiplying by $R$: $$ V_{in} = 2V_1 - V_2 + sRC V_1 \quad \text{...(1)} $$ **KCL at node $V_2$** (no current into op-amp): $$ \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R} = sC \cdot V_2 $$ $$ V_1 = V_2(1 + sRC) \quad \text{...(2)} $$ **Op-amp gain condition:** $V_2 = \dfrac{V_{out}}{K}$. **Substituting (2) into (1):** $$ V_{in} = V_2(1 + sRC)(2 + sRC) - V_2 $$ Expanding: $$ V_{in} = V_2 \bigl[1 + 3sRC + (sRC)^2\bigr] $$ Substituting $V_2 = \dfrac{V_{out}}{K}$: $$ H_{SK}(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{K}{s^2(RC)^2 + 3sRC + 1} $$ **In standard form** with $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$: $$ \boxed{H_{SK}(s) = \frac{K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (3-K)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}} $$ **Comparing with** $H_2(s) = \dfrac{A_0 \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2}$: $$ \frac{\omega_0}{Q} = (3-K)\omega_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{Q = \frac{1}{3-K}} $$ **Part (b) — Butterworth design (7 marks):** For Butterworth, $Q = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$: $$ \frac{1}{3-K} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3 - K = \sqrt{2} $$ $$ \boxed{K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586} $$ **Resistor ratio:** $$ K = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_f}{R_g} = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586 $$ **Compute R for $f_c = 5$ kHz, $C = 4.7$ nF:** $$ R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5000 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-9}} $$ $$ R = \frac{1}{1.476 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 6.77 \text{ k}\Omega $$ $$ \boxed{R_1 = R_2 = 6.77 \text{ k}\Omega} $$ **Choose $R_g = 10$ kΩ:** $$ R_f = 0.586 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 5.86 \text{ k}\Omega $$ $$ \boxed{R_f = 5.86 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad R_g = 10 \text{ k}\Omega} $$ **Verification of $\omega_0$:** $$ \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} = \frac{1}{6.77 \times 10^3 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-9}} = 2\pi \times 5000 \text{ rad/s} \checkmark $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **first-order active low-pass filter**. Derive its transfer function and obtain the cutoff frequency expression. State the magnitude and phase response. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **need for higher-order filters**. Design a **3rd-order Butterworth low-pass filter** with $f_c = 2$ kHz using one 1st-order and one 2nd-order Sallen-Key stage with equal components. List the Q and gain values used. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — First-order active LPF (7 marks):** **Circuit:** It consists of a passive RC low-pass network followed by a non-inverting op-amp (gain $A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_g}$). **Derivation:** The RC network provides: $$ H_{RC}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC} $$ The op-amp multiplies this by $A_0$ (voltage follower or non-inverting amplifier): $$ \boxed{H_1(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + sRC} = \frac{A_0 \,\omega_c}{s + \omega_c}} $$ **Cutoff frequency:** $$ \boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}} $$ **Magnitude:** $$ \vert H_1(j\omega) \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} $$ **Phase:** $$ \angle H_1(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC) $$ **Behaviour:** DC gain = $A_0$; –20 dB/decade roll-off beyond $f_c$; phase shifts from 0° to –90°. **Part (b) — 3rd-order Butterworth (7 marks):** A 3rd-order Butterworth LPF can be realized as **one 1st-order section** cascaded with **one 2nd-order Sallen-Key section**. **Step 1 — Choose C = 10 nF (typical), compute R:** $$ R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = 7.958 \text{ k}\Omega $$ **Step 2 — 1st-order stage:** - $R_1 = 7.958$ kΩ - $C_1 = 10$ nF - Unity gain follower ($A_0 = 1$) **Step 3 — 2nd-order Sallen-Key Butterworth stage:** - $R_2 = R_3 = 7.958$ kΩ - $C_2 = C_3 = 10$ nF - $Q = 0.707$ - $K = 3 - \sqrt{2} = 1.586$ **Choose $R_g = 10$ kΩ:** $$ R_f = 0.586 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 5.86 \text{ k}\Omega $$ **Step 4 — Cascade:** $$ H_{3rd}(s) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{1 + sRC}}_{\text{1st-order}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1.586 \,\omega_0^2}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}}_{\text{2nd-order SK}} $$ **Final Specifications:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | $f_c$ | 2 kHz | | Roll-off | –60 dB/decade | | 1st-stage Q | N/A (real pole) | | 2nd-stage Q | 0.707 | | Stage gain (SK) | 1.586 | | Total DC gain | 1.586 | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing ω and f:** Many students write $f_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ instead of $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ — this loses 1 mark. Remember: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ and $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$. > > 2. **Forgetting the gain K in SK derivation:** A frequent error is to derive $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + 3sRC + (sRC)^2}$ without accounting for the op-amp gain. The full transfer function **must** include $K$ in the numerator. > > 3. **Wrong K for Butterworth:** Students often write $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_g} = 0.586$ (the resistor ratio) instead of $K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586$ (the gain). Re-read the question. > > 4. **Mixing up Q with K:** The Q-factor is $Q = \dfrac{1}{3-K}$, NOT $Q = 3-K$. This is the **most common derivation error**. > > 5. **Forgetting to mention Q in higher-order designs:** When designing a 4th-order Butterworth, you **must** specify $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ separately — using $Q = 0.707$ for both stages is **wrong** (it gives a Chebyshev-like response, not Butterworth). > > 6. **Wrong sign in transfer function:** The standard form is $s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2$ in the denominator (not $s^2 - (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2$). The negative sign would make the system unstable. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 - [x] **1st-order LPF** transfer function: $H_1(s) = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + sRC}$ ; roll-off = **–20 dB/decade**. - [x] **2nd-order LPF** general form: $H_2(s) = \dfrac{A_0 \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2}$. - [x] **Cutoff frequency** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — at this point, magnitude is **–3 dB** of passband. - [x] **Sallen-Key equal-component design** uses $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$. - [x] **Sallen-Key transfer function:** $H_{SK}(s) = \dfrac{K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (3-K)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}$. - [x] **Quality factor for SK:** $Q = \dfrac{1}{3-K}$. - [x] **Butterworth Q-value:** $Q_{BW} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$. - [x] **Butterworth gain (equal-component SK):** $K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586$. - [x] **Resistor ratio for Butterworth:** $\dfrac{R_f}{R_g} = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586$. - [x] **Pole frequency** for equal components: $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$. - [x] **Higher-order filters** are realized by cascading 1st- and 2nd-order stages. - [x] **Nth-order roll-off** = **–20N dB/decade**. - [x] **4th-order Butterworth** uses two Sallen-Key stages with $Q_1 = 1.307$ and $Q_2 = 0.541$. - [x] **Real-world applications:** audio crossovers, anti-aliasing filters, biomedical signal conditioning, ADC pre-filters, control loop compensation. - [x] **Magnitude asymptote** for 1st-order beyond cutoff: $|H_1|_{dB} \approx -20\log_{10}(\omega/\omega_c)$ dB. - [x] **Phase response** for 1st-order: 0° at DC, –45° at $f_c$, –90° at infinity. - [x] **Damped frequency** for 2nd-order: $\omega_d = \omega_0\sqrt{1 - 1/(4Q^2)}$. > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for Sallen-Key Butterworth (MUST remember for exams):** > *"**Three minus root two** is the K you need for Butterworth Q."* → $K = 3 - \sqrt{2} = 1.586$, $Q = 0.707$. > > *"**Two minus root two** is the resistor ratio."* → $R_f/R_g = 0.586$. ---

topics.title: HPF: First order filter, Second order filter (Sallen-Key Butterworth), Higher order filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT404 - INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** HPF: First order filter, Second order filter (Sallen-Key Butterworth), Higher order filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a High-Pass Filter (HPF)

A **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** is a two-port linear time-invariant electronic network that attenuates signal components whose frequencies are **below** a designated cutoff frequency ($\mathbf{f_c}$) and transmits (passes) signal components whose frequencies are **above** $f_c$ with minimal attenuation. Mathematically, its ideal magnitude response is:

$$
H_{ideal}(j\omega) = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert \omega \vert < \omega_c \\ 1, & \vert \omega \vert > \omega_c \end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Active high-pass filters using op-amps (Sallen-Key topology) are the primary focus of this module. Passive RC high-pass filters serve as the building block upon which the active second-order Sallen-Key and higher-order filters are constructed.

The **order** of the filter (denoted **N**) determines the steepness of the transition from the stopband to the passband:
- **1st order HPF:** Roll-off of **-20 dB/decade**
- **2nd order HPF:** Roll-off of **-40 dB/decade**
- **N-th order HPF:** Roll-off of **-20N dB/decade**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Treble Boost" of Audio

Imagine a music equalizer on a home theater system. When you turn up the **treble knob** and turn down the **bass knob**, you are essentially using a high-pass filter. The low-frequency rumbles (subwoofer-like bass below 100 Hz) are blocked, while the high-frequency details (cymbal crashes, vocal sibilance, violin harmonics) are passed through cleanly.

In the same way, an electronic HPF:
- **Blocks** low-frequency noise (e.g., **DC offsets**, **50 Hz power-line hum**, **thermal drift**)
- **Passes** high-frequency information (audio signals above cutoff, communication carriers, sharp digital edges)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Example:** In biomedical instrumentation, an ECG signal contains the heartbeat (≈ 1 Hz to 40 Hz) corrupted by baseline wander and electrode DC offset. A first-order active HPF with $f_c \approx 0.05$ Hz removes the wander while preserving the QRS complex. Higher-order filters are used in **anti-aliasing** applications before an ADC.

## 1.3 Key Performance Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cutoff Frequency | $f_c$ | **Hz** | Frequency where magnitude is $-3$ dB below passband |
| Passband Gain | $K$ | dimensionless (V/V) | DC gain in passband region |
| Quality Factor | $Q$ | dimensionless | Selectivity near cutoff; Butterworth: $Q = 0.707$ |
| Roll-off Rate | - | **dB/decade** | Slope of attenuation in stopband |
| Stopband Attenuation | $A_s$ | **dB** | Minimum attenuation in stopband |

> [!TIP]
> The cutoff frequency is **NOT** the 3 dB point for every filter type. For Chebyshev and Bessel filters, the relationship between $f_c$ and the design frequency differs. However, for **Butterworth** (our focus), the $-3$ dB frequency **is** the cutoff.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 First-Order Active High-Pass Filter

The simplest active HPF is built by cascading a **passive RC high-pass network** with a **voltage follower** (or non-inverting amplifier) for buffering and gain control.

### Circuit Topology Reasoning
- The series capacitor $C$ **blocks DC** and offers impedance $Z_C = 1/(j\omega C)$ that **decreases** as frequency increases.
- The resistor $R$ to ground carries the return current.
- At very low $\omega$: $Z_C \rightarrow \infty$, so input is blocked.
- At very high $\omega$: $Z_C \rightarrow 0$, so the signal passes directly to the op-amp.

### Operational Analysis (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Voltage Divider Stage:** The RC network forms a frequency-dependent voltage divider.
2. **Transfer Function Formation:** Output of the RC network feeds the op-amp non-inverting input.
3. **Buffering:** The unity-gain follower provides infinite input impedance (no loading on RC) and zero output impedance (drives any load).
4. **Gain Control:** By replacing the follower with a non-inverting amplifier, the passband gain becomes $K = 1 + R_f/R_g$.

## 2.2 Second-Order Sallen-Key Butterworth HPF

This is the **most important** active filter topology from the KTU 2024 syllabus. It uses a single op-amp with **positive feedback** to synthesize a high-Q second-order response from two capacitors and two resistors.

### Why Sallen-Key?
- **Single op-amp** (low cost, low power)
- **Equal-component design** possible (eases fabrication)
- **Tunable** gain $K$ controls the damping (and hence $Q$)
- **Butterworth response** (maximally flat passband) is achieved when $Q = 1/\sqrt{2}$

### Step-by-Step Operational Concept
1. The input signal passes through $C_1$ into node $A$ (the non-inverting input of the op-amp).
2. From node $A$, $C_2$ leads to the output, and $R_1$ provides positive feedback from output to node $A$.
3. $R_2$ (and the gain-setting network) form the inverting feedback path that determines closed-loop gain $K$.
4. The op-amp output drives the load; the **positive feedback** through $C_2$ and $R_1$ generates the second-order pole pair.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every formula** you will need for KTU 2024 exam problems on HPF. All expressions use $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_c$ as the natural (pole) frequency.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First-order HPF cutoff | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz |
| 2 | First-order magnitude response | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{K \cdot (f/f_c)}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$ | V/V |
| 3 | First-order roll-off | $-20$ | dB/decade |
| 4 | General 2nd-order denominator | $s^2 + \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}s + \omega_0^2$ | — |
| 5 | Sallen-Key HPF (equal R, C) | $H(s) = \dfrac{K \cdot s^2}{s^2 + (3-K)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}$ | $\omega_0 = 1/RC$ |
| 6 | Quality factor (Sallen-Key) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{3-K}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Butterworth condition | $Q = 1/\sqrt{2}$ | $\Rightarrow K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586$ |
| 8 | Sallen-Key closed-loop gain | $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_b}{R_a}$ | V/V |
| 9 | Butterworth gain resistor ratio | $\dfrac{R_b}{R_a} = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Higher-order magnitude (asymptotic) | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert \approx \dfrac{K \cdot (f/f_c)^N}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^{2N}}}$ | $f \gg f_c$ |
| 11 | Total roll-off for order N | $-20N$ | dB/decade |
| 12 | Phase of Nth-order HPF | $\angle H = (N \cdot 90^\circ) - \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{(f/f_c)^N \sin\theta}{\cos\theta}\right)$ | degrees |
| 13 | Stopband attenuation | $A_s = 20 \log_{10}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{f_c}{f_s}\right)^{\!N}\right]$ | dB |
| 14 | Design: filter order from specs | $N \geq \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left[(10^{A_s/10} - 1)\right]}{2 \log_{10}(f_s/f_c)}$ | integer |
| 15 | Gain-BW product constraint | $f_{GBW} \geq 100 \cdot f_c$ | Hz (rule of thumb) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard form reminder:** The general second-order transfer function is $H(s) = N(s)/[s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2]$. The **Butterworth** approximation is a special case with $Q = 1/\sqrt{2}$ giving a **maximally flat** magnitude response in the passband (no ripples).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Active HPF |
|---|---|
| **Audio Engineering** | Removes subsonic rumble and DC offset from microphones |
| **Communication Systems** | AC-couples baseband signals, blocks DC bias from mixers |
| **Biomedical (ECG/EEG)** | Eliminates baseline wander and electrode polarization |
| **Data Acquisition** | Anti-aliasing filter (combined with LPF) before ADC sampling |
| **Control Systems** | Blocks integrator wind-up, isolates high-frequency control loop |
| **Power Electronics** | Coupling stages in switched-mode power supplies (SMPS) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order Active HPF Transfer Function

### Circuit Description
A passive RC high-pass network is buffered by a non-inverting op-amp with gain $K = 1 + R_f/R_g$.

### Step 1: Apply the Voltage Divider Rule

The capacitor $C$ is in series with resistor $R$ (which goes to ground). The voltage at the op-amp's non-inverting input is:

$$
V_+(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{R}{R + \dfrac{1}{sC}}
$$

**Logic:** As frequency rises, capacitor impedance $1/(sC)$ shrinks, so a larger fraction of $V_{in}$ appears across $R$ and reaches the op-amp input.

### Step 2: Simplify the Voltage Divider Expression

Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$:

$$
V_+(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Op-Amp Gain

The non-inverting amplifier produces:

$$
V_{out}(s) = K \cdot V_+(s) = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_g}\right) \cdot V_+(s)
$$

### Step 4: Combine to Get the Transfer Function

$$
H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = K \cdot \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

### Step 5: Express in Standard First-Order Form

Define the pole frequency $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$, so the transfer function is:

$$
H(s) = \frac{K \cdot (s/\omega_0)}{1 + (s/\omega_0)} = \frac{K \cdot s}{s + \omega_0}
$$

### Step 6: Magnitude and Phase Response

Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$
H(j\omega) = \frac{K \cdot j\omega}{j\omega + \omega_0} = \frac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_0/\omega)^2}} \angle \left(90^\circ - \tan^{-1}\!\frac{\omega}{\omega_0}\right)
$$

At $\omega = \omega_0$:

$$
\vert H(j\omega_0) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707K \quad \text{(which is the -3 dB point)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The first-order HPF has a **single pole** at $s = -\omega_0$ and a **single zero** at $s = 0$. The high-frequency gain equals $K$ (the non-inverting amplifier gain), and the low-frequency gain rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Second-Order Sallen-Key Butterworth HPF

### Circuit Setup
The Sallen-Key HPF consists of:
- $C_1$ from $V_{in}$ to node $A$ (non-inverting input)
- $C_2$ from node $A$ to $V_{out}$
- $R_1$ from $V_{out}$ to node $A$ (positive feedback)
- $R_2$ from node $A$ to ground
- Op-amp with closed-loop gain $K = 1 + R_b/R_a$ (set by feedback network)

> [!NOTE]
> We will first derive the **general equal-component** case (R1 = R2 = R, C1 = C2 = C). The unequal-component extension is provided afterwards.

### Step 1: Write KCL at Node A

Let the op-amp output be $V_{out} = K \cdot V_A$ (where $V_A$ is the voltage at the non-inverting input). Summing currents leaving node $A$:

$$
(sC_1)(V_A - V_{in}) + (sC_2)(V_A - V_{out}) + \frac{V_A - 0}{R_1} = 0
$$

**Logic:** The current through $C_1$ flows from node $A$ to $V_{in}$. The current through $C_2$ flows from node $A$ to $V_{out}$. The current through $R_1$ flows from node $A$ to ground.

### Step 2: Substitute $V_{out} = K V_A$ and Simplify

Group terms in $V_A$:

$$
sC_1 V_A - sC_1 V_{in} + sC_2 V_A - sC_2 K V_A + \frac{V_A}{R_1} = 0
$$

$$
V_A \left[ sC_1 + sC_2(1-K) + \frac{1}{R_1} \right] = sC_1 V_{in}
$$

Solve for $V_A$:

$$
V_A = \frac{sC_1 V_{in}}{sC_1 + sC_2(1-K) + \dfrac{1}{R_1}}
$$

### Step 3: Compute $V_{out} = K V_A$

$$
V_{out} = \frac{sKC_1 V_{in}}{sC_1 + sC_2(1-K) + \dfrac{1}{R_1}}
$$

### Step 4: Form the Transfer Function

$$
H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{sKC_1}{sC_1 + sC_2(1-K) + \dfrac{1}{R_1}}
$$

### Step 5: Apply the Equal-Component Simplification

Set $C_1 = C_2 = C$ and $R_1 = R_2 = R$:

$$
H(s) = \frac{sKC}{sC + sC(1-K) + \dfrac{1}{R}} = \frac{sKC}{sC[1 + (1-K)] + \dfrac{1}{R}}
$$

Since $1 + (1 - K) = 2 - K$:

$$
H(s) = \frac{sKC}{sC(2-K) + \dfrac{1}{R}}
$$

### Step 6: Normalize to Standard Second-Order Form

Divide numerator and denominator by $C(2-K)$:

$$
H(s) = \frac{\dfrac{sK}{2-K}}{\dfrac{s}{1} + \dfrac{1}{RC(2-K)}}
$$

Define the natural frequency:

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

Then we have:

$$
H(s) = \frac{K \cdot s^2 / (2-K)}{s^2 + \dfrac{1}{RC(2-K)}s}
$$

To get the standard form with $\omega_0^2$ in the denominator, multiply top and bottom appropriately. Recognizing the Butterworth form is:

$$
H(s) = \frac{K s^2}{s^2 + (3-K)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}
$$

### Step 7: Derive the General (Unequal Component) Form

If $R_1, R_2, C_1, C_2$ are not all equal, the transfer function becomes:

$$
H(s) = \dfrac{K \cdot s^2}{s^2 + s \left[\dfrac{1}{R_1 C_2} + \dfrac{1}{R_2 C_2} + \dfrac{1-K}{R_1 C_1}\right] + \dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}
$$

From this we identify:

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}{R_2 C_1 + R_1 C_1 + R_1 C_2(1-K)}
$$

**Logic of Q derivation:** The denominator's $s$ coefficient normalized by $\omega_0$ gives $1/Q$.

### Step 8: Apply the Butterworth Condition

For maximally flat response:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

With equal $R$ and equal $C$, the $Q$ expression simplifies to $Q = 1/(3-K)$. Setting this equal to $1/\sqrt{2}$:

$$
\frac{1}{3 - K} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

$$
3 - K = \sqrt{2}
$$

$$
K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586
$$

And since $K = 1 + R_b/R_a$:

$$
\frac{R_b}{R_a} = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586
$$

### Step 9: Final Butterworth HPF Transfer Function

$$
H_{BW}(s) = \frac{(3-\sqrt{2}) \, s^2}{s^2 + \sqrt{2} \, \omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** Verify the DC response by setting $s = 0$. The numerator is $0$, so the gain is exactly $0$ — which is correct for an HPF (it blocks DC). At very high $\omega$, the $s^2$ terms dominate and the gain approaches $K = 1.586$ (the passband gain).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Higher-Order Filter via Cascading

### Concept
A higher-order filter (order $N > 2$) is constructed by **cascading** first-order and second-order sections. The total transfer function is the **product** of the individual sections.

### Step 1: Cascade Equation

$$
H_{total}(s) = \prod_{i=1}^{M} H_i(s)
$$

where $M$ is the number of sections. For a 4th order filter, $M = 2$ (two second-order sections); for a 5th order filter, $M = 3$ (two second-order + one first-order), and so on.

### Step 2: Example — 4th-Order Butterworth HPF

Using two cascaded 2nd-order Sallen-Key sections with **staggered** cutoff frequencies, the overall response approximates the Butterworth polynomial:

$$
B_4(s) = s^4 + 2.6131 \, s^3 + 3.4142 \, s^2 + 2.6131 \, s + 1
$$

Each section is tuned to one of two different $Q$ values:
- **Section 1:** $Q_1 = 0.5412$, $K_1 = 1$ (or related design value)
- **Section 2:** $Q_2 = 1.3066$, $K_2 = 1$ (or related design value)

### Step 3: Roll-Off for Higher Orders

At $f \gg f_c$, the asymptotic magnitude is:

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert \approx K_{total} \cdot \left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^N \quad \text{(for } f \gg f_c \text{)}
$$

In dB:

$$
A_{dB} \approx 20 \log_{10}(K_{total}) + 20N \log_{10}(f/f_c)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each doubling of order doubles the slope. A 2nd order filter rolls off at $-40$ dB/dec (or $-12$ dB/octave). A 4th order rolls off at $-80$ dB/dec. The downside is increased component count, op-amp noise, and sensitivity to component tolerances.
