# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Define a high-pass filter. State the order of the filter required to achieve an attenuation of 60 dB/decade.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A high-pass filter (HPF) is a frequency-selective circuit that passes frequencies above the cutoff frequency $f_c$ and attenuates frequencies below $f_c$.

Roll-off rate is $-20N$ dB/decade where $N$ is the filter order. For 60 dB/decade:

$$
20N = 60 \Rightarrow N = 3
$$

**A 3rd order HPF** is required. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** For a Sallen-Key second-order Butterworth high-pass filter, state the values of the quality factor $Q$ and the closed-loop gain $K$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a Butterworth response (maximally flat passband):

$$
Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707
$$

For a Sallen-Key HPF with equal R and C: $Q = 1/(3-K)$. Setting $Q = 1/\sqrt{2}$:

$$
3 - K = \sqrt{2} \Rightarrow K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586
$$

This requires the gain-setting resistor ratio $R_b/R_a = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586$. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B carries 14 marks and offers an **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model]** 

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order active high-pass filter. Sketch its frequency response and explain the significance of the cutoff frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a first-order Butterworth high-pass filter with a cutoff frequency of 5 kHz and a passband gain of 2. Choose $R = 10$ k$\Omega$ and find $C$. Also compute the gain at 1 kHz and 50 kHz. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Circuit: $C$ in series with $R$ (to ground) feeds the non-inverting input of an op-amp with gain $K = 1 + R_f/R_g$.

**Step 2:** Voltage divider at the RC network:

$$
V_+(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{R}{R + 1/(sC)} = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC} V_{in}(s)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply op-amp gain:

$$
V_{out}(s) = K \cdot V_+(s) = \frac{K sRC}{1 + sRC} V_{in}(s)
$$

**Step 4:** Transfer function:

$$
H(s) = \frac{K sRC}{1 + sRC} = \frac{K \cdot s}{s + \omega_0}, \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

**Step 5:** Magnitude response (substituting $s = j\omega$):

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K \cdot (f/f_c)}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}
$$

**Step 6:** Frequency response sketch:
- DC gain = 0 (the capacitor blocks DC)
- At $f = f_c$: gain = $K/\sqrt{2}$ (i.e., $-3$ dB below passband)
- For $f \gg f_c$: gain $\approx K$ (flat passband)
- Slope below $f_c$: $-20$ dB/decade

**Significance of $f_c$:** The cutoff frequency $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ is the $-3$ dB point that marks the boundary between passband and stopband. It is the design parameter that determines the lowest frequency the filter will pass. **(7 marks distributed: 1+1+1+1+1+2)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Given: $f_c = 5$ kHz, $K = 2$, $R = 10$ k$\Omega$.

**Step 2:** Compute $C$:

$$
C = \frac{1}{2\pi R f_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 5 \times 10^3}
$$

$$
C = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 10^6} = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 3.183 \text{ nF}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute gain at $f = 1$ kHz (below cutoff):

$$
\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{1 \text{ kHz}}{5 \text{ kHz}} = 0.2
$$

$$
\vert H \vert = \frac{2 \times 0.2}{\sqrt{1 + 0.2^2}} = \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{1.04}} = \frac{0.4}{1.0198} \approx 0.392
$$

**Step 4:** Compute gain at $f = 50$ kHz (above cutoff):

$$
\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{50 \text{ kHz}}{5 \text{ kHz}} = 10
$$

$$
\vert H \vert = \frac{2 \times 10}{\sqrt{1 + 10^2}} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{101}} = \frac{20}{10.05} \approx 1.990
$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation:
- At 1 kHz, gain ≈ 0.39 V/V (heavily attenuated)
- At 50 kHz, gain ≈ 1.99 V/V ≈ $K$ (passband gain reached)
- This confirms the HPF behavior. **(7 marks distributed: 1+1+2+1+2)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model]**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a second-order Sallen-Key high-pass filter with equal components ($R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$). Show that the quality factor is $Q = 1/(3-K)$ and determine $K$ for a Butterworth response. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a second-order Sallen-Key Butterworth high-pass filter with cutoff frequency 10 kHz. Choose $C = 10$ nF and find $R$. Also determine the gain-setting resistors $R_a$ and $R_b$ such that Butterworth response is achieved. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Write KCL at the non-inverting input node $A$ with $V_{out} = K V_A$:

$$
(sC)(V_A - V_{in}) + (sC)(V_A - V_{out}) + \frac{V_A - 0}{R} = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $V_{out} = K V_A$:

$$
sC V_A - sC V_{in} + sC V_A - sC K V_A + \frac{V_A}{R} = 0
$$

$$
V_A [sC(2 - K) + 1/R] = sC V_{in}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $V_{out} = K V_A$:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{K sC V_{in}}{sC(2-K) + 1/R}
$$

**Step 4:** Divide numerator and denominator by $C(2-K)$:

$$
H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{Ks}{s(2-K) + 1/[RC(2-K)]}
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by an additional factor to obtain the standard form. Recognize the pole frequency $\omega_0 = 1/RC$:

$$
H(s) = \frac{K s^2}{s^2 + (3-K)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}
$$

**Step 6:** Compare with standard form $H(s) = K s^2 / (s^2 + \omega_0 s/Q + \omega_0^2)$:

$$
\frac{1}{Q} = 3 - K \Rightarrow \boxed{Q = \frac{1}{3-K}}
$$

**Step 7:** Butterworth condition: $Q = 1/\sqrt{2}$:

$$
\frac{1}{3-K} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow 3 - K = \sqrt{2} \Rightarrow \boxed{K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586}
$$

**(7 marks distributed: 1+1+1+1+1+1+1)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Given: $f_c = 10$ kHz, $C = 10$ nF, Butterworth response ($K = 1.586$).

**Step 2:** Compute $R$:

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi}
$$

$$
\boxed{R \approx 1.5915 \text{ k}\Omega}
$$

**Step 3:** Choose $R_a$ (a standard value), then compute $R_b$:

For convenience, let $R_a = 10$ k$\Omega$:

$$
K = 1 + \frac{R_b}{R_a} \Rightarrow \frac{R_b}{R_a} = K - 1 = 0.586
$$

$$
R_b = 0.586 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 5.86 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

Use standard value $R_b \approx 5.6$ k$\Omega$ or $5.9$ k$\Omega$.

**Step 4:** Verify the gain:

$$
K = 1 + \frac{5.86}{10} = 1.586 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5:** Design summary:
- $R_1 = R_2 = R \approx 1.59$ k$\Omega$
- $C_1 = C_2 = C = 10$ nF
- $R_a = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_b = 5.86$ k$\Omega$
- Passband gain $K = 1.586$
- $Q = 0.707$ (Butterworth)
- Roll-off: $-40$ dB/decade above $f_c$

**Step 6:** Op-amp selection constraint: $f_{GBW} \geq 100 \times f_c = 1$ MHz (a standard op-amp like $\mu$A741 may not suffice; use TL081 or better). **(7 marks distributed: 1+1+1+1+1+2)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $f_c$ with the 3 dB frequency for non-Butterworth filters** — for Chebyshev/Bessel, the design $f_c$ and $-3$ dB frequency differ. KTU primarily tests Butterworth; stay focused on $Q = 1/\sqrt{2}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the gain factor $K$** — students often write $H(s) = s^2/(s^2 + \sqrt{2}\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2)$ and miss the numerator $K s^2$. The passband gain at high frequency is $K$, not 1.
> 3. **Wrong $Q$ formula for unequal components** — the formula $Q = 1/(3-K)$ is valid **only for equal R and equal C**. For general components, use the full expression with $R_1, R_2, C_1, C_2$.
> 4. **Not verifying Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** — at high frequencies, the op-amp's finite GBW attenuates the signal. Always check $f_{GBW} \geq 100 f_c$ as a rule of thumb.
> 5. **Mixing up Sallen-Key LPF and HPF forms** — the LPF has $K \omega_0^2$ in the numerator; the HPF has $K s^2$ in the numerator. Don't transpose them!
> 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units (e.g., writing "C = 3.18" without "nF").

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- A **High-Pass Filter** passes frequencies above $f_c$ and attenuates those below it. The cutoff is the $-3$ dB point.
- **First-order active HPF** transfer function: $H(s) = \dfrac{K s}{s + \omega_0}$ with $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$ and roll-off of $-20$ dB/decade.
- **Second-order Sallen-Key HPF** with equal R and C: $H(s) = \dfrac{K s^2}{s^2 + (3-K)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}$.
- **Quality factor** (Sallen-Key equal-component): $Q = 1/(3-K)$.
- **Butterworth condition**: $Q = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$, giving $K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586$.
- **Gain-setting resistor ratio** for Butterworth: $R_b / R_a = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586$.
- **Roll-off** for $N$-th order filter: $-20N$ dB/decade (or equivalently $-6N$ dB/octave).
- **Higher-order filters** are built by **cascading** first-order and second-order sections.
- For a 4th order Butterworth, use **two second-order sections** with $Q$ values of **0.5412** and **1.3066**.
- The **order $N$** required for given specs is found from: $N \geq \dfrac{\log_{10}(10^{A_s/10} - 1)}{2\log_{10}(f_s/f_c)}$.
- **General Sallen-Key HPF** pole frequency: $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}$.
- Always check the **op-amp Gain-Bandwidth Product** satisfies $f_{GBW} \geq 100 f_c$ to avoid high-frequency attenuation.
- The Sallen-Key topology is **sensitive to component tolerances** at high $Q$; use 1% resistors and NP0/C0G capacitors for precision applications.
- **Practical cutoff range**: First-order HPF is suitable for $f_c > 1$ Hz if $C \geq 1$ $\mu$F; for sub-Hz cutoffs, use very large $C$ or switched-capacitor techniques.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BPF: Wide BPF, Narrow band pass filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBICT404 - INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** BPF: Wide BPF, Narrow band pass filter # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Band-Pass Filter (BPF)? A **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** is a second-order (or higher) frequency-selective active network that allows signals within a specified **passband** (between a lower cutoff frequency $f_L$ and an upper cutoff frequency $f_H$) to pass through with minimal attenuation, while rejecting signals outside this band. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** > An active band-pass filter is realized by cascading a high-pass filter (HPF) and a low-pass filter (LPF), or by using the multiple-feedback (MFB) topology with a single operational amplifier. The filter is classified as **Wide BPF** or **Narrow BPF** based on the ratio of the upper-to-lower cutoff frequencies. ## 1.2 Classification of Band-Pass Filters BPFs are classified based on the **quality factor (Q)** or the ratio $f_H / f_L$: | Classification | Condition | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Wide Band-Pass Filter (Wide BPF)** | $f_H / f_L \geq 2$ (i.e., $Q \leq \sqrt{2}/2 \approx 0.707$) | Audio equalizers, broadband communication | | **Narrow Band-Pass Filter (Narrow BPF)** | $f_H / f_L \approx 1$ (i.e., $Q \gg 1$) | Radio tuning, IF stages, telemetry | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Classification Rule:** > - **Wide BPF** → Realized as a **cascade of LPF and HPF** (two separate stages). > - **Narrow BPF** → Realized using the **Multiple-Feedback (MFB) topology** with a **single op-amp**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tuning Fork" of Electronics Imagine a **musical tuning fork** that vibrates strongly only at one specific frequency. Now imagine a **window screen** that lets through any frequency of sound equally. A band-pass filter is like a **selective window** — it only lets through a specific range of "frequencies" (sounds) and blocks both the low rumbles and the high-pitched noise. - A **wide BPF** is like a **large window** that admits a broad range of sound (e.g., the entire vocal range). - A **narrow BPF** is like a **pinhole** that admits only a very specific pitch (e.g., a single musical note). ## 1.4 Quality Factor (Q) — The "Selectivity" Metric The **Quality Factor Q** is the most critical parameter that distinguishes a wide BPF from a narrow BPF. $$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{f_0}{f_H - f_L}$$ Where: - $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$ is the **geometric center frequency** (in **Hz**). - $BW = f_H - f_L$ is the **bandwidth** (in **Hz**). - $f_L$ and $f_H$ are the **lower and upper $-3\text{ dB}$ cutoff frequencies** (in **Hz**). > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid:** A high Q means a **narrow, selective** filter (sharp peak). A low Q means a **wide, broadband** filter (gentle peak). ## 1.5 Standard Response of a BPF The ideal second-order band-pass transfer function is: $$H(s) = \frac{H_0 \cdot \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q} \cdot s}{s^2 + \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q} \cdot s + \omega_0^2}$$ Where: - $H_0$ is the **center-frequency gain** (dimensionless). - $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ is the **angular center frequency** (in **rad/s**). - $Q$ is the **quality factor** (dimensionless). - $s = j\omega$ is the **Laplace variable**. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** The center frequency $f_0$ is **NOT** the arithmetic mean $(f_L + f_H)/2$. It is the **geometric mean** $\sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$. This is a frequent trap in KTU numerical problems. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — Wide BPF ### 2.1.1 Topology A wide band-pass filter is constructed by **cascading two first-order (or second-order) stages**: - A **first-order High-Pass Filter (HPF)** stage at the front end. - A **first-order Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** stage at the back end. The HPF removes the low-frequency components (sets $f_L$), and the LPF removes the high-frequency components (sets $f_H$). The overall passband is the **intersection** of the two individual passbands. ### 2.1.2 Design Logic 1. **HPF Stage:** Choose $R_1$ and $C_1$ such that $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$. 2. **LPF Stage:** Choose $R_2$ and $C_2$ such that $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$. 3. **Verify:** Ensure $f_H > f_L$ by a sufficient margin (typically $f_H \geq 2 f_L$). ### 2.1.3 Loading Effect Consideration When cascading, the input impedance of the LPF stage acts as a load on the HPF stage. To minimize this: - The op-amp output impedance of the HPF stage must be **very low**. - Use **buffer (voltage follower) stages** between HPF and LPF for isolation. - The passband gain is the **product** of individual stage gains. ## 2.2 Operational Concept — Narrow BPF (Multiple Feedback Topology) ### 2.2.1 Why a Single Op-Amp? In a narrow BPF, the lower and upper cutoff frequencies are very close together. The cascade approach would require extremely precise component matching, which is impractical. Instead, a **single op-amp Multiple-Feedback (MFB) topology** is used, where the HPF and LPF functions are **interleaved** in the same feedback network. ### 2.2.2 Component Roles in MFB BPF - $R_1, R_2$ — Set the input resistance and influence $Q$. - $C_1, C_2$ — Set the center frequency $f_0$. - The op-amp's **negative feedback** through $C_1$ and $C_2$ provides the dual HPF + LPF behavior. ### 2.2.3 Design Logic 1. Choose a **standard capacitor value** for $C_1 = C_2 = C$. 2. Compute $R_1$ and $R_2$ from the desired $f_0$ and $Q$. 3. Use a **non-inverting gain stage** (voltage follower with controlled gain) if $Q$ enhancement is needed. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains the **must-know formulas** for KTU 2024 Scheme exams on BPF. ### **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Sheet for BPF** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |:---:|---|---|:---:|---| | 1 | Lower cutoff frequency (Wide BPF) | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ | **Hz** | HPF stage component values | | 2 | Upper cutoff frequency (Wide BPF) | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$ | **Hz** | LPF stage component values | | 3 | Center frequency (Wide BPF) | $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$ | **Hz** | Geometric mean | | 4 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | **Hz** | For any BPF | | 5 | Quality factor | $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{BW}$ | dimensionless | High Q → Narrow BPF | | 6 | Center freq. (MFB Narrow BPF) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi C}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}}$ | **Hz** | When $C_1 = C_2 = C$ | | 7 | Quality factor (MFB BPF) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}}$ | dimensionless | When $C_1 = C_2 = C$ | | 8 | Center-freq. gain (MFB BPF) | $\vert A_0 \vert = \dfrac{R_2}{2 R_1}$ | dimensionless | Inverting gain | | 9 | Resonance condition | $f_0^2 = f_L \cdot f_H$ | **Hz²** | Always true for any BPF | | 10 | Passband gain (Wide BPF) | $A_{mid} = A_{HPF} \cdot A_{LPF}$ | dimensionless | Product of stages | > [!CAUTION] > **LaTeX Note:** The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ in formulas uses `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown tables. The pipe `|` is a reserved table delimiter. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility | Application Domain | Filter Type Used | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **FM Radio Receivers** (88–108 MHz) | Narrow BPF (MFB) | Select one station from many | | **Audio Graphic Equalizers** | Multiple Wide BPFs | Boost/cut specific frequency bands | | **Biomedical Instrumentation** (ECG/EEG) | Wide BPF | Remove baseline drift + high-freq. noise | | **Telecommunication IF Stages** | Narrow BPF (455 kHz, 10.7 MHz) | Channel selection | | **Radar Signal Processing** | Narrow BPF | Matched filtering of pulse returns | | **Oscilloscope Trigger Circuits** | BPF | Trigger on specific frequency components | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wide BPF: Cascade of LPF and HPF ### **Step 1 — Define the transfer function of the first-order HPF stage** A first-order active high-pass filter using an inverting op-amp configuration has the transfer function: $$H_{HPF}(s) = -\frac{sR_1 C_1}{1 + sR_1 C_1}$$ The $-3\text{ dB}$ cutoff frequency of the HPF is obtained by setting $\vert H_{HPF}(j\omega_L) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \vert H_{HPF}(\infty) \vert$: $$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$$ ### **Step 2 — Define the transfer function of the first-order LPF stage** A first-order active low-pass filter has the transfer function: $$H_{LPF}(s) = -\frac{R_2/R_{in}}{1 + sR_2 C_2}$$ The $-3\text{ dB}$ cutoff frequency of the LPF is: $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$$ ### **Step 3 — Cascade transfer function** Since the stages are isolated by op-amp buffers (input impedance $\to \infty$, output impedance $\to 0$), the overall transfer function is the **product**: $$H_{BPF}(s) = H_{HPF}(s) \cdot H_{LPF}(s)$$ $$\boxed{H_{BPF}(s) = \frac{sR_1 C_1 \cdot R_2/R_{in}}{(1 + sR_1 C_1)(1 + sR_2 C_2)}}$$ ### **Step 4 — Identify passband and stopbands** - For $\omega \ll \omega_L$: $|H| \to 0$ (low-frequency rejection). - For $\omega_L < \omega < \omega_H$: $|H| \approx$ constant (passband). - For $\omega \gg \omega_H$: $|H| \to 0$ (high-frequency rejection). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Narrow BPF: Multiple-Feedback Topology ### **Step 1 — Circuit Description** Consider the MFB band-pass filter with: - $R_1$ from input $v_{in}$ to the inverting node (node A). - $R_2$ from node A to ground. - $C_1$ from input $v_{in}$ to the output $v_{out}$. - $C_2$ from node A to the output $v_{out}$. - The non-inverting input is grounded (simplest case). ### **Step 2 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (virtual ground)** Since the op-amp input is ideal, the inverting node voltage is **0 V** (virtual ground). Summing currents leaving node A: $$\frac{v_{in} - 0}{R_1} + \frac{0}{R_2} + (0 - v_{out}) s C_2 = 0$$ Simplifying: $$\frac{v_{in}}{R_1} = v_{out} \cdot s C_2$$ ### **Step 3 — Write the KCL at the input node (considering the $C_1$ branch)** The current flowing through $C_1$ from $v_{in}$ to $v_{out}$: $$i_{C_1} = (v_{in} - v_{out}) \cdot s C_1$$ This current must be supplied by the source plus flow through $R_1$ into the virtual ground. Using the source-current balance at the $v_{in}$ node and the result from Step 2, after algebraic manipulation (using $i_{C_1} = i_{R_1} - i_{C_2}$ from the virtual-ground constraint), we obtain: $$v_{in} = v_{out} \left[ s^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 + s R_1 (C_1 + C_2) + 1 \right] \cdot \frac{C_2}{C_1}$$ ### **Step 4 — Solve for the transfer function** $$\boxed{H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\frac{s R_2 C_1}{1 + s R_1 (C_1 + C_2) + s^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$ ### **Step 5 — Normalize to the standard second-order BPF form** Comparing with $H(s) = \dfrac{H_0 \cdot (\omega_0/Q) \cdot s}{s^2 + (\omega_0/Q) s + \omega_0^2}$, we identify: **Center frequency:** $$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$ **Bandwidth (and Q):** $$\frac{\omega_0}{Q} = \frac{C_1 + C_2}{R_2 C_1 C_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = \frac{\sqrt{R_2 / R_1} \cdot \sqrt{C_1 / C_2}}{1 + C_2/C_1}$$ **Center-frequency gain:** $$H_0 = -\frac{R_2 C_1}{R_1 (C_1 + C_2)}$$ ### **Step 6 — Special Case: $C_1 = C_2 = C$ (Equal Capacitor Design)** When $C_1 = C_2 = C$, the formulas simplify significantly: $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi C} \sqrt{\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}}$$ $$Q = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{R_2}{R_1}}$$ $$\vert H_0 \vert = \frac{R_2}{2 R_1}$$ ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example:** Design a narrow band-pass filter with $f_0 = 1\text{ kHz}$ and $Q = 5$. Use the MFB topology with $C_1 = C_2 = 10\text{ nF}$. ### **Step 1 — Compute the center frequency parameter** $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi C \sqrt{R_1 R_2}} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$ $$\sqrt{R_1 R_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (10 \times 10^{-9})(1000)} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$\sqrt{R_1 R_2} = 15915.5 \text{ }\Omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 R_2 = 2.533 \times 10^8 \text{ }\Omega^2$$ ### **Step 2 — Use the Q constraint** $$Q = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{R_2}{R_1}} = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{\frac{R_2}{R_1}} = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_2}{R_1} = 100$$ ### **Step 3 — Solve the simultaneous equations** From Step 2: $R_2 = 100 R_1$. Substituting into Step 1: $$100 R_1^2 = 2.533 \times 10^8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1^2 = 2.533 \times 10^6$$ $$R_1 = 1591.5 \text{ }\Omega \quad (\text{use } R_1 = 1.6 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ standard value})$$ $$R_2 = 100 \times 1591.5 = 159154 \text{ }\Omega \quad (\text{use } R_2 = 160 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ standard value})$$ ### **Step 4 — Compute the center-frequency gain** $$\vert H_0 \vert = \frac{R_2}{2 R_1} = \frac{159154}{2 \times 1591.5} = 50$$ ### **Step 5 — Compute the bandwidth** $$BW = \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{1000}{5} = 200 \text{ Hz}$$ $$f_L = f_0 - \frac{BW}{2} = 900 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_H = f_0 + \frac{BW}{2} = 1100 \text{ Hz}$$ ## 3.4 Summary Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Wide BPF | Narrow BPF | |---|---|---| | Topology | Cascade LPF + HPF | MFB (single op-amp) | | Number of Op-amps | $\geq 2$ | 1 | | $Q$ value | $Q \leq 0.707$ | $Q \gg 1$ | | Component sensitivity | Low | High (precise values needed) | | Roll-off rate | $\pm 20 \text{ dB/decade}$ (1st order stages) | Up to $\pm 40 \text{ dB/decade}$ (2nd order) | | Best for | Broadband applications | High-selectivity applications | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Wide BPF Realization ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[INPUT SIGNAL
Vin] --> nodeB[HPF STAGE
First Order High Pass
Cuts below fL] nodeB --> nodeC[BUFFER
Voltage Follower
Isolation] nodeC --> nodeD[LPF STAGE
First Order Low Pass
Cuts above fH] nodeD --> nodeE[OUTPUT SIGNAL
Vout] style nodeA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style nodeB fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style nodeC fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style nodeD fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#008800 style nodeE fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Narrow BPF Realization (MFB) ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[INPUT
Vin] --> nodeB[R1] nodeB --> nodeC[VIRTUAL GROUND
Inverting Input] nodeA --> nodeD[C1] nodeD --> nodeE[OUTPUT
Vout] nodeC --> nodeF[C2] nodeF --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeG[R2] nodeG --> nodeH[GROUND] nodeE --> nodeI[NON INVERTING
Input Ground] style nodeA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style nodeC fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style nodeE fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#008800 ``` ## 4.3 Frequency Response Comparison ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "WIDE BPF RESPONSE" W1[Gain dB] --> W2[Flat Top Region
fL to fH wide] W2 --> W3[Roll off at fL
and fH] end subgraph "NARROW BPF RESPONSE" N1[Gain dB] --> N2[Sharp Peak at f0] N2 --> N3[Rapid Roll off
High Q] end style W2 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style N2 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 ``` ## 4.4 Signal Flow — MFB Narrow BPF Detailed ```mermaid graph LR input[Vin] --> r1[R1] r1 --> inv[Inverting Node A
Virtual Ground] input --> c1[C1] c1 --> output[Vout] inv --> c2[C2] c2 --> output inv --> r2[R2] r2 --> gnd[GND] output --> noninv[Non Inverting Input] noninv --> gnd r1 -.->|"Sets Input
Impedance"| note1[Design Note] c1 -.->|"Forward Path
Capacitor"| note2[Design Note] c2 -.->|"Feedback
Capacitor"| note3[Design Note] r2 -.->|"Sets Q
Factor"| note4[Design Note] style input fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style inv fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style output fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#008800 style gnd fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333333 ``` ## 4.5 Comparison Flow Chart — Wide vs Narrow BPF Design Choice ```mermaid graph TD start[Design BPF] --> q1{What is the
Required Q?} q1 -->|"Q less than 1"| wide[Choose WIDE BPF] q1 -->|"Q greater than 1"| narrow[Choose NARROW BPF] wide --> wide1[Cascade HPF and LPF] wide1 --> wide2[Use Op amp Buffers] wide2 --> wide3[Calculate fL and fH] wide3 --> wide4[Verify Cascade Gain] narrow --> narrow1[Use MFB Topology] narrow1 --> narrow2[Select C1 equals C2 equals C] narrow2 --> narrow3[Calculate R1 and R2] narrow3 --> narrow4[Verify Q and f0] style start fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style wide fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#008800 style narrow fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a band-pass filter. Distinguish between a wide band-pass filter and a narrow band-pass filter based on the quality factor Q.** **Model Answer:** A **band-pass filter (BPF)** is a frequency-selective network that allows signals within a specific frequency range (between $f_L$ and $f_H$) to pass through while attenuating frequencies outside this band. **Distinction based on Q:** | Parameter | Wide BPF | Narrow BPF | |---|---|---| | Quality Factor Q | $Q \leq \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ | $Q \gg 1$ | | Bandwidth | Large (BW $\geq f_0$) | Small (BW $\ll f_0$) | | Topology | Cascade of LPF and HPF | Multiple-Feedback (MFB) with single op-amp | | Realization | Two or more op-amps | One op-amp | The relationship is governed by: $$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{f_0}{f_H - f_L}$$ A high Q produces a narrow, sharply-peaked response; a low Q produces a wide, gentle response. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **With a neat block diagram, explain the realization of a wide band-pass filter using first-order low-pass and high-pass filter stages.** **Model Answer:** A **wide band-pass filter** is realized by **cascading** a first-order high-pass filter (HPF) and a first-order low-pass filter (LPF), with an optional op-amp buffer between them for isolation. **Block Diagram:** ``` ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ Vin ────►│ HPF │────►│ BUFFER │────►│ LPF │────► Vout │ (fL) │ │ (Follow) │ │ (fH) │ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ ``` - The **HPF stage** determines the **lower cutoff frequency**: $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ - The **LPF stage** determines the **upper cutoff frequency**: $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$ - The passband extends from $f_L$ to $f_H$ (with $f_H \gg f_L$ for a wide BPF). - The buffer prevents **loading effect** between the two stages. --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) > **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### **(a) Derive the transfer function of a multiple-feedback (MFB) narrow band-pass filter. From the transfer function, identify the expressions for the center frequency $f_0$, the quality factor $Q$, and the center-frequency gain $H_0$. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Circuit Description:** The MFB BPF consists of an op-amp with: - $R_1$ from $v_{in}$ to the inverting node (A). - $C_1$ from $v_{in}$ to $v_{out}$. - $R_2$ from node A to ground. - $C_2$ from node A to $v_{out}$. - Non-inverting input grounded. **Step 2 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (virtual ground = 0 V):** $$\frac{v_{in}}{R_1} + v_{out} \cdot sC_2 = 0$$ This gives: $v_{in} = -v_{out} \cdot s R_1 C_2$ ... (i) **Step 3 — Apply KCL at the $v_{in}$ node** (currents supplied by source = sum of currents through $R_1$ and $C_1$): Using the current through $C_1$ from $v_{in}$ to $v_{out}$: $$i_{C_1} = (v_{in} - v_{out}) sC_1$$ Combining with (i) and eliminating $v_{in}$, we obtain after algebraic manipulation: $$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{s R_2 C_1}{1 + s R_1 (C_1 + C_2) + s^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}$$ **Step 4 — Comparison with standard form:** $$H(s) = \frac{H_0 \cdot (\omega_0/Q) \cdot s}{s^2 + (\omega_0/Q) s + \omega_0^2}$$ By comparison: $$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}}$$ $$\boxed{Q = \frac{\sqrt{R_2/R_1} \cdot \sqrt{C_1/C_2}}{1 + C_2/C_1}}$$ $$\boxed{|H_0| = \frac{R_2 C_1}{R_1 (C_1 + C_2)}}$$ **For the special case $C_1 = C_2 = C$:** $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi C \sqrt{R_1 R_2}}, \quad Q = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{R_2}{R_1}}, \quad |H_0| = \frac{R_2}{2R_1}$$ --- #### **(b) Design a narrow band-pass filter using the MFB topology with center frequency $f_0 = 2 \text{ kHz}$ and quality factor $Q = 8$. Choose $C_1 = C_2 = 10 \text{ nF}$. Determine $R_1$, $R_2$, the bandwidth, and the $-3\text{ dB}$ cutoff frequencies. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Use the Q equation:** $$Q = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{R_2}{R_1}} = 8$$ $$\sqrt{\frac{R_2}{R_1}} = 16 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_2}{R_1} = 256 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_2 = 256 R_1$$ **Step 2 — Use the $f_0$ equation:** $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi C \sqrt{R_1 R_2}} = 2000 \text{ Hz}$$ $$\sqrt{R_1 R_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (10 \times 10^{-9})(2000)} = \frac{1}{4\pi \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$\sqrt{R_1 R_2} = 7957.7 \text{ }\Omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 R_2 = 6.333 \times 10^7 \text{ }\Omega^2$$ **Step 3 — Solve simultaneously:** Substituting $R_2 = 256 R_1$: $$256 R_1^2 = 6.333 \times 10^7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1^2 = 2.474 \times 10^5$$ $$R_1 = 497.4 \text{ }\Omega \quad (\text{use standard } R_1 = 499 \text{ }\Omega)$$ $$R_2 = 256 \times 497.4 = 127334 \text{ }\Omega \quad (\text{use standard } R_2 = 127 \text{ k}\Omega)$$ **Step 4 — Compute the bandwidth:** $$BW = \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{2000}{8} = 250 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 5 — Compute cutoff frequencies:** $$f_L = f_0 \left( -\frac{1}{2Q} + \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{4Q^2}} \right) = 2000 \left( -\frac{1}{16} + \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{256}} \right)$$ $$f_L = 2000 ( -0.0625 + 1.00195 ) = 2000 \times 0.93945 = 1878.9 \text{ Hz}$$ $$f_H = f_0 \left( \frac{1}{2Q} + \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{4Q^2}} \right) = 2000 (0.0625 + 1.00195) = 2128.9 \text{ Hz}$$ **Verification:** $f_L \cdot f_H = 1878.9 \times 2128.9 \approx 4 \times 10^6 = f_0^2$ ✓ **Center-frequency gain:** $$|H_0| = \frac{R_2}{2R_1} = \frac{127334}{2 \times 497.4} = 128$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### **(a) With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a wide band-pass filter realized by cascading a first-order high-pass filter and a first-order low-pass filter. Derive the overall transfer function. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Circuit Description:** ``` C1 R2 Vin─||──┬──/\/\/──┐ │ │ ┌┴┐ R1 ┌┴┐ R3 │-│\ │-│\ │ │ \──┬───┤ │ \ └┬┘ │ ┌─└┬┘ │ │ │ │────Vout GND GND │ GND C2 ``` **Step 1 — HPF Stage Transfer Function:** The first-order active HPF (inverting configuration) has: $$H_{HPF}(s) = -\frac{sR_1 C_1}{1 + sR_1 C_1}$$ **Step 2 — LPF Stage Transfer Function:** $$H_{LPF}(s) = -\frac{R_2/R_3}{1 + sR_2 C_2}$$ **Step 3 — Cascaded Transfer Function (with buffer isolation):** $$H_{BPF}(s) = H_{HPF}(s) \cdot H_{LPF}(s) = \frac{sR_1 C_1 \cdot (R_2/R_3)}{(1 + sR_1 C_1)(1 + sR_2 C_2)}$$ **Step 4 — Frequency Interpretation:** - At $\omega \to 0$: $H \to 0$ (low-frequency rejection). - At $\omega = \omega_L$: $|H| = |H_{max}|/\sqrt{2}$ (lower cutoff). - At $\omega \to \infty$: $H \to 0$ (high-frequency rejection). - For $\omega_L \ll \omega \ll \omega_H$: $|H| \approx R_2/R_3$ (passband). **Cutoff frequencies:** $$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}, \quad f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$$ --- #### **(b) A wide band-pass filter is to be designed with $f_L = 200 \text{ Hz}$ and $f_H = 2 \text{ kHz}$. If the HPF stage uses $C_1 = 0.1 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and the LPF stage uses $C_2 = 1 \text{ nF}$, determine the resistor values $R_1$ and $R_2$. Calculate the center frequency, bandwidth, and Q factor. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute $R_1$ for the HPF stage:** $$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi f_L C_1}$$ $$R_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 200 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{4\pi \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$R_1 = 7957.7 \text{ }\Omega \quad (\text{use standard } R_1 = 7.96 \text{ k}\Omega)$$ **Step 2 — Compute $R_2$ for the LPF stage:** $$R_2 = \frac{1}{2\pi f_H C_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{4\pi \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R_2 = 79577.5 \text{ }\Omega \quad (\text{use standard } R_2 = 79.6 \text{ k}\Omega)$$ **Step 3 — Compute the center frequency:** $$f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H} = \sqrt{200 \times 2000} = \sqrt{400000} = 632.46 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the bandwidth:** $$BW = f_H - f_L = 2000 - 200 = 1800 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the Q factor:** $$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{632.46}{1800} = 0.3514$$ **Step 6 — Verify wide BPF condition:** $$\frac{f_H}{f_L} = \frac{2000}{200} = 10 \gg 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Confirmed as Wide BPF} \checkmark$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU BPF Problems:** > > 1. **Confusing arithmetic and geometric mean:** The center frequency is $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L f_H}$ (geometric), NOT $(f_L + f_H)/2$. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this mistake. > > 2. **Forgetting the buffer/isolation stage:** In a wide BPF cascade, students often forget to mention that the HPF and LPF stages must be isolated using a voltage follower. This omission costs **1–2 marks** in 7-mark sub-questions. > > 3. **Sign convention error:** The MFB BPF gives an **inverting** output. Students sometimes write $|H_0| = R_2/R_1$ (without the factor of 2 for $C_1 = C_2$ case) or forget the negative sign. > > 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Always express $f$ in Hz, $\omega$ in rad/s, and $C$ in Farads (or nF/pF with proper conversion: $1 \text{ nF} = 10^{-9} \text{ F}$). Mixing units is a **favourite deduction point** for KTU evaluators. > > 5. **Wide vs Narrow confusion:** Students often design an MFB narrow BPF when a wide BPF is asked (or vice versa). Always check the Q value first! --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for BPF:** ### **Core Concepts:** - A **BPF** passes frequencies between $f_L$ and $f_H$, with center $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L f_H}$ and bandwidth $BW = f_H - f_L$. - **Quality factor** $Q = f_0 / BW$ — high Q means narrow band. - **Wide BPF** ($Q \leq 0.707$): cascade of LPF and HPF. - **Narrow BPF** ($Q \gg 1$): Multiple-Feedback (MFB) topology with single op-amp. ### **Key Formulas to Memorize:** | Formula | Use | |---|---| | $f_L = 1/(2\pi R_1 C_1)$ | HPF stage cutoff (Wide BPF) | | $f_H = 1/(2\pi R_2 C_2)$ | LPF stage cutoff (Wide BPF) | | $f_0 = 1/(2\pi C \sqrt{R_1 R_2})$ | Center freq. of MFB narrow BPF | | $Q = (1/2)\sqrt{R_2/R_1}$ | Quality factor for $C_1 = C_2$ case | | $\|H_0\| = R_2/(2R_1)$ | Center-freq. gain for $C_1 = C_2$ case | | $BW = f_0/Q$ | Bandwidth from Q | ### **Design Procedure — Quick Steps:** 1. Identify if Wide or Narrow BPF based on Q. 2. For Wide BPF: choose $C$, compute $R$ from $f_L$ and $f_H$ separately. 3. For Narrow BPF: choose $C_1 = C_2 = C$, solve for $R_1$ and $R_2$ from $f_0$ and $Q$ simultaneously. ### **Common Exam Traps:** - Center frequency is **geometric mean**, not arithmetic. - MFB BPF is **inverting** (negative gain). - Always isolate cascade stages with **voltage followers**. - For $C_1 = C_2$ case, gain formula has a **factor of 2** in the denominator. ### **Real-World Tie-Ins (for viva/short answers):** - Wide BPF → Audio equalizers, ECG/EEG filters. - Narrow BPF → FM radio IF stages, channel selectors in TV tuners. - High Q → Sharper selectivity, used in spectrum analyzers. - Low Q → Broader bandwidth, used in broadband amplifiers.

topics.title: BRF: Wide BRF, Narrow BRF (Twin T Notch design)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBICT404 - INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** BRF: Wide BRF, Narrow BRF (Twin T Notch design) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Band Rejection Filter (BRF) — Formal Definition A **Band Rejection Filter (BRF)**, also called a **Band Stop Filter (BSF)** or **Notch Filter**, is a two-port frequency-selective network that **attenuates (rejects) a specific band of frequencies** while allowing frequencies **below** and **above** that band to pass through with minimal attenuation. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A Band Rejection Filter is a filter whose magnitude response $|H(j\omega)|$ is approximately unity for $\omega \rightarrow 0$ and $\omega \rightarrow \infty$, but falls to a deep minimum (ideally zero) at a designated **notch frequency** $\omega_0$. The classification is based on the **bandwidth** of the rejected region relative to the geometric center frequency: - **Wide BRF** — The stop-band is broad (high fractional bandwidth, BW $\geq$ 0.5 $f_0$). - **Narrow BRF** — The stop-band is very sharp and tightly localized (BW $\ll f_0$). The classic realization is the **Twin-T (or Parallel-T) Notch Network**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Intuition — "The Noisy Neighbor" Analogy** > Imagine your room window faces a street. You want to hear quiet conversations (low frequencies like 100 Hz hum) and the high-pitched chirping of birds (high frequencies like 5 kHz). However, there is a single, persistent nuisance — a **whistling kettle at exactly 1 kHz** that ruins everything. A *Wide BRF* would be like closing all the windows between 500 Hz and 2 kHz (a broad blockage), accidentally silencing some birds and conversations. A *Narrow BRF* (Twin-T) is like a **precision surgical eraser** that removes *only* the 1 kHz whistle while leaving everything else untouched. **Physical Constants / Key Metrics (must be memorized):** - Notch angular frequency: $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ rad/s - Notch frequency: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ Hz - Standard Twin-T Quality Factor: $Q = \dfrac{1}{4}$ --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Wide Band Rejection Filter (Wide BRF) A wide BRF is realized by the **parallel (summing) combination** of a **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** and a **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** using an inverting summing amplifier. **Operational Logic:** 1. The HPF has a cutoff $\omega_1$ (lower edge of stop band). Frequencies *below* $\omega_1$ are blocked by HPF and *passed* toward output. 2. The LPF has a cutoff $\omega_2$ (upper edge of stop band). Frequencies *above* $\omega_2$ are blocked by LPF and *passed* toward output. 3. Both filters are **summed (with proper sign)** at the inverting summer. The result: only the band **$\omega_1$ to $\omega_2$** is suppressed; the rest passes through. **Key Design Constraint:** $\omega_1 < \omega_2$ (HPF cutoff < LPF cutoff) — this ensures there is a finite overlapping stop-band. > [!NOTE] > The *wide* designation comes from the fact that the transition bands of practical 1st-order LPF/HPF are gradual, producing a **broad, smooth stop-band** (no sharp null). For deeper nulls, the filters must be cascaded to higher orders. ### 2.2 Narrow BRF — The Twin-T Notch Network The **Twin-T (or Parallel-T) Notch Filter** is the canonical narrow BRF. It is a passive RC network constructed from two T-sections connected in **parallel** between the same input and output nodes: - **Top T-section (Low-Pass T):** Two series resistors $R$ – $R$ with a shunt capacitor $2C$ to ground. - **Bottom T-section (High-Pass T):** Two series capacitors $C$ – $C$ with a shunt resistor $R/2$ to ground. **Operational Logic (Why it works — the "Why" and "How"):** 1. At **low frequencies** ($\omega \to 0$): The series capacitors in the HP-T act as *open circuits*; signal flows unimpeded through the LPF-T (where the series Rs are pass-through) → **Output $\approx$ Input**. 2. At **high frequencies** ($\omega \to \infty$): The series capacitors in HP-T short the signal; in the LPF-T, the shunt $2C$ shorts the output to ground, but the signal still reaches the output through the HP-T (series Cs pass high frequencies) → **Output $\approx$ Input**. 3. At the **notch frequency** $\omega_0 = 1/RC$: The two parallel paths produce **equal and opposite currents** into the output node, causing **complete cancellation** → **Output = 0** (the notch). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Twin-T" name** comes from the two T-shaped sections (one with R-R-C, the other with C-C-R). The element ratio $2:1$ between the series and shunt components is the *critical balance* that produces the deep null. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Wide BRF (Parallel LPF+HPF) | Narrow BRF (Twin-T Notch) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Lower edge frequency** | $\omega_1 = \dfrac{1}{R_{HPF} C_{HPF}}$ | $\omega_1 = \omega_0 \cdot \sqrt{9 - 4\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.236\,\omega_0$ | | **Upper edge frequency** | $\omega_2 = \dfrac{1}{R_{LPF} C_{LPF}}$ | $\omega_2 = \omega_0 \cdot \sqrt{9 + 4\sqrt{5}} \approx 4.236\,\omega_0$ | | **Center / Notch frequency** | $\omega_c = \sqrt{\omega_1 \omega_2}$ | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | | **Stop-band Bandwidth** | $BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1$ (wide) | $BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1 \approx 4\,\omega_0$ | | **Quality Factor** | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_c}{BW}$ (low) | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0}{BW} = \dfrac{1}{4}$ (symmetric) | | **Transfer Function** | $H(s) = -\left[\dfrac{R_f/R_1}{1+s/\omega_2} + \dfrac{R_f/R_2 \cdot s/\omega_1}{1+s/\omega_1}\right]$ | $H(s) = \dfrac{1 + s^2 R^2 C^2}{1 + 4sRC + s^2 R^2 C^2}$ | | **Magnitude at $\omega_0$** | Finite attenuation (gradual) | Theoretically **0** (perfect null) | | **Passband Gain** | $G_{LPF} + G_{HPF}$ (sum of individual gains) | Unity (0 dB) at $\omega \to 0$ and $\omega \to \infty$ | > **Engineering Utility:** The Twin-T notch is heavily used in **biomedical instrumentation** (notably the **50 Hz/60 Hz power-line interference** rejection in ECG/EEG amplifiers), **audio equalization** (anti-feedback notch for stage microphones), and **communication receivers** (single-tone jammer cancellation). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Transfer Function of the Twin-T Notch Filter **Network Definition:** - Let $V_{in}$ = input node, $V_{out}$ = output node (high-impedance load assumed). - Let $V_1$ = voltage at the midpoint of the LP-T section. - Let $V_2$ = voltage at the midpoint of the HP-T section. **Step 1: KCL at the LP-T midpoint $V_1$** Currents leaving node $V_1$ must sum to zero. The three branches connected to $V_1$ are: $R$ to $V_{in}$, $R$ to $V_{out}$, and shunt $2C$ to ground. $$ \frac{V_1 - V_{in}}{R} + \frac{V_1 - V_{out}}{R} + V_1 \cdot (2Cs) = 0 $$ Multiplying by $R$: $$ (V_1 - V_{in}) + (V_1 - V_{out}) + 2RCs \cdot V_1 = 0 $$ $$ 2V_1(1 + RCs) = V_{in} + V_{out} $$ $$ V_1 = \frac{V_{in} + V_{out}}{2(1 + RCs)} \quad \cdots (1) $$ **Step 2: KCL at the HP-T midpoint $V_2$** The three branches at $V_2$ are: capacitor $C$ to $V_{in}$, capacitor $C$ to $V_{out}$, and shunt $R/2$ to ground. $$ (V_2 - V_{in})Cs + (V_2 - V_{out})Cs + \frac{V_2}{R/2} = 0 $$ $$ (V_2 - V_{in})Cs + (V_2 - V_{out})Cs + \frac{2V_2}{R} = 0 $$ Multiplying by $\dfrac{1}{Cs}$: $$ (V_2 - V_{in}) + (V_2 - V_{out}) + \frac{2V_2}{RCs} = 0 $$ $$ 2V_2\left(1 + \frac{1}{RCs}\right) = V_{in} + V_{out} $$ $$ V_2 = \frac{(V_{in} + V_{out}) \cdot RCs}{2(1 + RCs)} \quad \cdots (2) $$ **Step 3: KCL at the output node $V_{out}$ (no load current)** Current into $V_{out}$ from the LP-T branch + current into $V_{out}$ from the HP-T branch = 0. $$ \frac{V_1 - V_{out}}{R} + (V_2 - V_{out})Cs = 0 $$ Multiplying the second term by $R/R$ to get a common denominator, and dividing the whole equation by $Cs$: $$ \frac{V_1 - V_{out}}{RCs} + (V_2 - V_{out}) = 0 $$ $$ V_1 - V_{out} = (V_{out} - V_2)RCs $$ $$ V_1 + V_2 \cdot RCs = V_{out}(1 + RCs) \quad \cdots (3) $$ **Step 4: Substitute (1) and (2) into (3)** First, compute $V_2 \cdot RCs$: $$ V_2 \cdot RCs = \frac{(V_{in} + V_{out}) \cdot RCs}{2(1 + RCs)} \cdot RCs = \frac{(V_{in} + V_{out}) \cdot (RCs)^2}{2(1 + RCs)} $$ Adding $V_1 + V_2 \cdot RCs$: $$ V_1 + V_2 \cdot RCs = \frac{V_{in} + V_{out}}{2(1 + RCs)} + \frac{(V_{in} + V_{out})(RCs)^2}{2(1 + RCs)} $$ $$ = \frac{V_{in} + V_{out}}{2(1 + RCs)} \left[1 + (RCs)^2\right] $$ Substituting into (3): $$ \frac{(V_{in} + V_{out})\left[1 + (RCs)^2\right]}{2(1 + RCs)} = V_{out}(1 + RCs) $$ **Step 5: Algebraic simplification** Cross-multiplying: $$ (V_{in} + V_{out})\left[1 + (RCs)^2\right] = 2V_{out}(1 + RCs)^2 $$ Expand both sides: $$ V_{in}\left[1 + s^2R^2C^2\right] + V_{out}\left[1 + s^2R^2C^2\right] = 2V_{out}\left[1 + 2RCs + s^2R^2C^2\right] $$ Isolating $V_{in}$: $$ V_{in}\left[1 + s^2R^2C^2\right] = 2V_{out}\left[1 + 2RCs + s^2R^2C^2\right] - V_{out}\left[1 + s^2R^2C^2\right] $$ $$ V_{in}\left[1 + s^2R^2C^2\right] = V_{out}\left[2 + 4RCs + 2s^2R^2C^2 - 1 - s^2R^2C^2\right] $$ $$ V_{in}\left[1 + s^2R^2C^2\right] = V_{out}\left[1 + 4RCs + s^2R^2C^2\right] $$ **Final Transfer Function:** $$ \boxed{H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1 + s^2R^2C^2}{1 + 4sRC + s^2R^2C^2}} $$ --- ### 3.2 Frequency Response Analysis (Magnitude) Substitute $s = j\omega$, and let $x = \omega RC$: $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{1 - x^2}{1 - x^2 + j4x} $$ $$ |H(j\omega)| = \frac{\vert 1 - x^2 \vert}{\sqrt{(1 - x^2)^2 + 16x^2}} $$ **Verification of the Notch:** At $x = 1$ (i.e., $\omega = \omega_0 = 1/RC$): $$ |H(j\omega_0)| = \frac{|1 - 1|}{\sqrt{0 + 16}} = \frac{0}{4} = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Perfect null)} $$ **Verification of Unity Pass-band:** At $x = 0$ ($\omega \to 0$): $$ |H(0)| = \frac{1}{1} = 1 \quad \checkmark $$ At $x \to \infty$ ($\omega \to \infty$): $$ |H(\infty)| = \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{x^2}{x^2} = 1 \quad \checkmark $$ **Derivation of -3 dB Bandwidth (Bode Approach):** Set $|H(j\omega)|^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}$: $$ \frac{(1 - x^2)^2}{(1 - x^2)^2 + 16x^2} = \frac{1}{2} $$ $$ 2(1 - x^2)^2 = (1 - x^2)^2 + 16x^2 $$ $$ (1 - x^2)^2 = 16x^2 $$ Expanding: $$ 1 - 2x^2 + x^4 = 16x^2 $$ $$ x^4 - 18x^2 + 1 = 0 $$ Using the quadratic formula with $u = x^2$: $$ u = \frac{18 \pm \sqrt{324 - 4}}{2} = 9 \pm 4\sqrt{5} $$ $$ u_1 = 9 - 4\sqrt{5} \approx 0.0557, \quad u_2 = 9 + 4\sqrt{5} \approx 17.944 $$ $$ x_1 = \sqrt{0.0557} \approx 0.236, \quad x_2 = \sqrt{17.944} \approx 4.236 $$ Therefore: $$ \omega_1 \approx 0.236\,\omega_0, \quad \omega_2 \approx 4.236\,\omega_0 $$ $$ BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1 \approx 4\,\omega_0 $$ $$ \boxed{Q = \frac{\omega_0}{BW} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25} $$ --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Wide BRF | Narrow BRF (Twin-T) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Topology** | Active: LPF ‖ HPF → Summing Amp | Passive: Two parallel T-sections | | **Stop-band Shape** | Smooth, gradual, broad | Sharp, narrow, deep null | | **Notch Depth** | Finite (depends on filter order) | **Theoretically infinite** (perfect cancellation) | | **Q-factor** | Low ($\sim$ 0.5 to 1) | **0.25** (symmetric); can be boosted via active modification | | **Tunability** | Easy (adjust $R$, $C$ of each filter) | Moderate (matched $R, C$ critical) | | **Sensitivity** | Low | High (component mismatch degrades null depth) | | **Typical Use** | Crossover networks, sub-band removal | Power-line hum rejection, single-tone cancellation | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Wide BRF Realization (LPF + HPF Summer) ```mermaid graph LR nodeVin["V_in (Source)"] --> nodeLPF["LPF Stage
Cutoff f2"] nodeVin --> nodeHPF["HPF Stage
Cutoff f1"] nodeLPF --> nodeSum["Summing Amplifier
Inverting Adder"] nodeHPF --> nodeSum nodeSum --> nodeVout["V_out (BRF Response)"] nodeNote["Condition: f1 less than f2
Stop band: f1 to f2"] -.-> nodeSum ``` ### 4.2 Twin-T Notch Network — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TB nodeA["Input Node V_in"] --> nodeR1a["Series R"] nodeR1a --> nodeMid1["Midpoint M1
LP-T section"] nodeMid1 --> nodeR1b["Series R"] nodeR1b --> nodeB["Output Node V_out"] nodeMid1 --> nodeCshunt1["Shunt 2C to GND"] nodeA --> nodeC1a["Series C"] nodeC1a --> nodeMid2["Midpoint M2
HP-T section"] nodeMid2 --> nodeC1b["Series C"] nodeC1b --> nodeB nodeMid2 --> nodeRshunt1["Shunt R by 2 to GND"] nodeB --> nodeLoad["High Z Load"] nodeNote["Balanced condition:
Top T: R R 2C
Bottom T: C C R by 2"] -.-> nodeB ``` ### 4.3 Frequency Response Comparison — Wide vs. Narrow BRF ```mermaid graph LR nodeFreq["Frequency axis log scale"] --> nodeLow["Low Freq Region
Both filters pass"] nodeLow --> nodeStopWide["Wide BRF
Broad smooth dip
from f1 to f2"] nodeStopWide --> nodeStopNarrow["Narrow BRF Twin T
Sharp deep null at f0"] nodeStopNarrow --> nodeHigh["High Freq Region
Both filters pass"] style nodeStopWide fill:#fff4cc style nodeStopNarrow fill:#ffcccc ``` ### 4.4 Standard Component Selection Chart (Twin-T) | Notch Frequency $f_0$ | Choose $C$ | Then $R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_0 C}$ | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **50 Hz** (power line) | $1\,\mu F$ | $R \approx 3.18\,k\Omega$ | | **60 Hz** (US power line) | $1\,\mu F$ | $R \approx 2.65\,k\Omega$ | | **1 kHz** (audio anti-feedback) | $10\,nF$ | $R \approx 15.9\,k\Omega$ | | **10 kHz** | $1\,nF$ | $R \approx 15.9\,k\Omega$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Note:** In real designs, the shunt elements are chosen as $2C$ and $R/2$ from *standard* E12 series values closest to the calculated $C$ and $R$. Component tolerance must be **≤ 1%** for deep nulls — otherwise, mismatch causes the notch depth to degrade from "infinite" to perhaps only 20–30 dB. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define a Band Rejection Filter. With a neat sketch, distinguish between a wide BRF and a narrow BRF.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Band Rejection Filter (BRF) is a two-port network that **attenuates a band of frequencies** while passing all others with negligible loss. Mathematically, $|H(j\omega)| \to 1$ as $\omega \to 0$ and $\omega \to \infty$, with a deep minimum at the **notch frequency** $\omega_0$. - **Wide BRF:** Stop-bandwidth is broad (BW ≥ 0.5 $f_0$). Realized by parallel summing of an LPF and HPF; produces a smooth, gradual dip in $|H(j\omega)|$. - **Narrow BRF:** Stop-bandwidth is very small relative to $f_0$ (BW $\ll f_0$). Realized classically by the **Twin-T notch network**, which gives a *sharp, theoretically infinite-depth null* at $\omega_0 = 1/RC$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Write the transfer function of a standard Twin-T notch filter. What is its notch frequency and quality factor?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Transfer function: $$H(s) = \frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{1 + s^2R^2C^2}{1 + 4sRC + s^2R^2C^2}$$ - **Notch frequency:** $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ rad/s → $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ Hz - **Quality factor:** $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0}{BW} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25$ - At $\omega = \omega_0$, the numerator becomes zero, producing a **perfect null** in the output. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]* **(a)** Derive the transfer function $H(s) = V_o/V_i$ of the Twin-T notch filter shown below, identifying each node equation step. State the notch frequency and the Q-factor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a Twin-T notch filter to reject **50 Hz** power-line interference in an ECG amplifier. Choose $C = 100\,nF$. Calculate $R$, the shunt $2C$ and $R/2$ values, and verify the -3 dB frequencies. **(7 marks)** ##### **Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** *Full step-by-step nodal analysis as detailed in Section 3.1 of these notes.* - Apply KCL at the LP-T midpoint → obtain $V_1 = \dfrac{V_{in} + V_o}{2(1+RCs)}$ ... (1) - Apply KCL at the HP-T midpoint → obtain $V_2 = \dfrac{(V_{in} + V_o)RCs}{2(1+RCs)}$ ... (2) - Apply KCL at the output node (no-load) → $V_1 + V_2 RCs = V_o(1+RCs)$ ... (3) - Substitute (1) and (2) into (3), simplify algebraically → final result: $$ H(s) = \frac{1 + s^2R^2C^2}{1 + 4sRC + s^2R^2C^2} $$ - **Notch frequency:** $\omega_0 = 1/RC$ - **Q-factor:** $Q = 1/4$ - **-3 dB edges:** $\omega_1 = 0.236\,\omega_0$, $\omega_2 = 4.236\,\omega_0$ **(b) Design Calculation (7 marks):** Given: $f_0 = 50$ Hz, $C = 100\,nF = 100 \times 10^{-9}$ F Calculate $R$: $$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$ R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 31{,}831\,\Omega \approx 31.83\,k\Omega $$ - Series resistors in LP-T: $R_1 = R_2 = R = 31.83\,k\Omega$ - Shunt capacitor: $2C = 200\,nF$ - Series capacitors in HP-T: $C_1 = C_2 = C = 100\,nF$ - Shunt resistor: $R/2 = 15.915\,k\Omega$ Verify -3 dB edges: $$ f_1 = 0.236 \times 50 = 11.8\,Hz $$ $$ f_2 = 4.236 \times 50 = 211.8\,Hz $$ $$ BW = f_2 - f_1 = 200\,Hz, \quad Q = 50/200 = 0.25 \quad \checkmark $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]* **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the realization of a **wide band rejection filter** using a parallel combination of a 1st-order LPF and HPF. Derive the expression for its transfer function. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the wide BRF and narrow BRF (Twin-T) on the basis of: (i) stop-band shape, (ii) notch depth, (iii) Q-factor, (iv) sensitivity to component tolerance, and (v) typical applications. State one real-world scenario where you would prefer a Twin-T over a wide BRF. **(7 marks)** ##### **Solution:** **(a) Wide BRF Realization (7 marks):** *Block diagram as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes.* A wide BRF uses a **summing amplifier** with two filter inputs: - **HPF branch** (cutoff $\omega_1$): allows $\omega > \omega_1$ to pass. - **LPF branch** (cutoff $\omega_2$): allows $\omega < \omega_2$ to pass. Both outputs are summed at the inverting input of an op-amp. Result: only the band $\omega_1 < \omega < \omega_2$ is *suppressed* (because each filter individually blocks that band). Transfer function: $$ H(s) = -\left[\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + s/\omega_2} + \frac{R_f}{R_2} \cdot \frac{s/\omega_1}{1 + s/\omega_1}\right] $$ For unity pass-band gains, choose $R_1 = R_2 = R_f$. The pass-band (low & high) gain is unity; the stop-band gain is the smooth sum of two decreasing/increasing functions, producing a broad dip centered at $\omega_c = \sqrt{\omega_1 \omega_2}$. **(b) Comparative Analysis (7 marks):** | Parameter | Wide BRF | Narrow BRF (Twin-T) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | (i) Stop-band shape | Smooth, gradual dip | Sharp, narrow null | | (ii) Notch depth | Finite (e.g., -20 dB) | Theoretically infinite (∞ dB) | | (iii) Q-factor | Low (0.5 – 1) | 0.25 (symmetric); boostable actively | | (iv) Component sensitivity | Low (LPF/HPF independent) | High (mismatch in R, C ratio degrades null) | | (v) Applications | Crossover networks, broadband noise removal | 50/60 Hz hum rejection in ECG/EEG, audio anti-feedback | **Preferred Scenario (1 mark):** A Twin-T is preferred in an **ECG amplifier** to remove **50 Hz mains hum** without affecting the diagnostic QRS complex (0.5 Hz – 40 Hz) and P/T waves, where a *narrow, sharp* null is essential to preserve nearby cardiac signal content. A wide BRF would erase large portions of the actual ECG signal. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):** > 1. **Sign Error in KCL:** Many students forget the direction of current through the shunt element at the midpoints. Always write KCL as *"sum of currents leaving the node = 0"* — not "entering". A sign slip in $V_1$ or $V_2$ will *propagate* and wreck the entire transfer function. **(-3 to -5 marks)** > 2. **Forgetting the "No-Load" Assumption at the Output Node:** The KCL equation $\frac{V_1 - V_o}{R} + (V_2 - V_o)Cs = 0$ is valid **only** for a high-impedance load. If a finite $R_L$ is connected, the analysis changes drastically. **(-2 marks)** > 3. **Confusing the Notch Frequency with the -3 dB Frequency:** Students often write $f_{-3dB} = f_0$ which is *wrong*. The notch is at $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$; the -3 dB points are at $0.236\,f_0$ and $4.236\,f_0$. **(-2 marks)** > 4. **Component Ratio Mistake:** The shunt elements are **2C** and **R/2**, *not* 2R and C/2. Mixing up the placement breaks the balance and ruins the null. **(-3 marks)** > 5. **Omitting the Q-Factor in the Final Answer:** Even if derivation is perfect, failing to explicitly state $Q = 1/4$ loses a mark. Examiners expect a quantitative summary. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **BRF definition:** Rejects one band, passes two (low and high). - **Wide BRF** = LPF + HPF in parallel-summer; broad smooth stop-band; $Q < 1$. - **Narrow BRF** = Twin-T notch; sharp deep null; standard $Q = 1/4$. - **Twin-T components:** Top T = $R$–$R$–$2C$; Bottom T = $C$–$C$–$R/2$. - **Standard Transfer Function:** $H(s) = \dfrac{1 + s^2R^2C^2}{1 + 4sRC + s^2R^2C^2}$. - **Notch frequency:** $\omega_0 = 1/RC$, i.e., $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$. - **Verification identity:** At $s = j\omega_0$, numerator = 0, output = 0 (perfect null). - **-3 dB edges:** $\omega_{1,2} = \omega_0 \sqrt{9 \mp 4\sqrt{5}}$. - **Quality factor:** $Q = 1/4$ (symmetric, passive); can be boosted by active feedback or by deviating element ratios (modified Twin-T with parameter $k$, where $Q = 1/(4(1-k))$). - **Engineering Applications:** 50/60 Hz hum rejection (ECG, EEG), anti-Larsen audio feedback, single-tone jammer cancellation, biomedical instrumentation front-ends. - **Sensitivity caveat:** Twin-T requires precision components (≤ 1% tolerance) — otherwise the null depth degrades severely. - **Design shortcut:** Pick $C$ first, compute $R = 1/(2\pi f_0 C)$, then $2C$ and $R/2$ for shunts.

topics.title: All pass filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT404 - INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** All pass filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# All-Pass Filter (APF) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

An **All-Pass Filter (APF)** is a special class of active filter whose **magnitude response is constant (unity) over the entire frequency spectrum**, while its **phase response is a function of frequency**. In other words, the filter passes signals of all frequencies with equal gain, but selectively delays or advances the phase of different frequency components.

Mathematically, for a continuous-time all-pass filter:

$$|H(j\omega)| = 1 \quad \forall \omega \in [0, \infty)$$

while $\angle H(j\omega) = \phi(\omega)$ is non-linear with frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The All-Pass Filter is studied under *Active Filter Design using Op-Amps*. It is fundamentally a **phase-shaping network** — not a frequency-selective amplitude filter. Students must remember that APFs do NOT attenuate any frequency; they ONLY alter the phase.

The most general transfer function forms studied in KTU 2024 scheme are:

- **First-Order APF:** $H(s) = \dfrac{s - a}{s + a}$
- **Second-Order APF:** $H(s) = \dfrac{s^2 - \frac{\omega_0}{Q}s + \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \frac{\omega_0}{Q}s + \omega_0^2}$

where $a, \omega_0, Q > 0$.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a long hallway with multiple sound sources playing different musical notes simultaneously:

- A **normal filter** (low-pass, high-pass) is like a wall that lets some notes through loudly and blocks others — it changes the **volume** of each note.
- An **All-Pass Filter** is like an *invisible glass wall* — every note reaches your ear at the **same volume**, BUT some notes arrive a tiny bit **earlier or later** than they should. The wall distorts the **timing (phase)**, not the loudness.

### Everyday Engineering Analogy: Traffic Signal Synchronization
Consider a green-wave traffic system. Each car (frequency component) moves at the same speed (same magnitude/gain), but reaches the next signal (output) with a different **time delay** (phase shift). The APF acts like the traffic engineer — adjusting phase relationships without changing the "speed" of any individual signal component.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** An All-Pass Filter is a **"phase equalizer"** or **"phase shifter"**. Its sole purpose is to alter phase relationships, leaving amplitude untouched. This makes it indispensable in **phase equalization**, **delay equalization**, and **Q-enhancement of oscillators**.

---

## Why All-Pass Filters Matter in Engineering

| Application | Role of APF |
|---|---|
| **Phase Equalization** | Compensates for unwanted phase distortion introduced by other filters |
| **Oscillator Design** | Combined with another APF to form quadrature oscillators (sine-cosine generators) |
| **Audio Processing** | Used in reverberation and stereo image enhancement (Schroeder all-pass) |
| **Communication Systems** | Group delay equalization in transmission channels |
| **Closed-Loop Control** | Stabilization by phase lead/lag compensation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. First-Order All-Pass Filter

### 1.1 Circuit Topology (Op-Amp Realization)

The classic first-order APF uses a single op-amp with a **resistor–capacitor network** that produces a transfer function whose numerator is the *mirror image* of its denominator (i.e., coefficients are sign-reversed except for the constant term).

**Standard Form of the Transfer Function:**

$$H(s) = \frac{1 - sRC}{1 + sRC}$$

Equivalently, normalized to pole-zero form:

$$H(s) = \frac{s - \frac{1}{RC}}{s + \frac{1}{RC}}$$

### 1.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

- The **pole** of the system lies at $s = -\dfrac{1}{RC}$ in the **Left Half Plane (LHP)** → guarantees stability.
- The **zero** lies at $s = +\dfrac{1}{RC}$ in the **Right Half Plane (RHP)** → this is what creates the phase shift.
- The pole and zero are **mirror images** about the $j\omega$-axis.
- Because they are symmetric about the imaginary axis, their distance from any point on the $j\omega$-axis is **equal in magnitude**, giving $|H(j\omega)| = 1$.

### 1.3 Frequency Response of First-Order APF

Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1 - j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Magnitude Response:**

$$|H(j\omega)| = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} = 1 \quad \text{(for all } \omega \text{)}$$

**Phase Response:**

$$\phi(\omega) = -2 \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** At $\omega = 0$, $\phi(0) = 0°$. At $\omega \to \infty$, $\phi(\infty) = -180°$. At $\omega = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ (the pole-zero frequency), $\phi = -90°$ — the **half-power phase point**.

### 1.4 Group Delay of First-Order APF

$$\tau_g(\omega) = -\frac{d\phi(\omega)}{d\omega} = \frac{2RC}{1 + (\omega RC)^2}$$

- Maximum group delay occurs at **DC** ($\omega = 0$): $\tau_g(0) = 2RC$.
- As $\omega \to \infty$, $\tau_g \to 0$.

---

## 2. Second-Order All-Pass Filter

### 2.1 Standard Transfer Function

The second-order APF is more versatile and is built around a **Sallen-Key** or **Delyiannis-Friend** topology. Its canonical form is:

$$H(s) = \frac{s^2 - \left(\dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}\right)s + \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \left(\dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}\right)s + \omega_0^2}$$

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

- The **denominator** defines a standard second-order low-pass-like pole pair: $s^2 + \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}s + \omega_0^2 = 0$ gives two LHP poles (for $Q > 0$), guaranteeing stability.
- The **numerator** has the same constant term $\omega_0^2$ and the same linear coefficient magnitude, but with **opposite sign**. This creates two **Right-Half-Plane zeros** that mirror the poles about the $j\omega$-axis.
- The mirror symmetry forces $|H(j\omega)| = 1$ at every frequency.

### 2.3 Magnitude and Phase Response

$$|H(j\omega)| = 1 \quad \text{(always)}$$

$$\phi(\omega) = -2 \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{\omega \cdot \left(\dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}\right)}{\omega_0^2 - \omega^2}\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Property:** The phase shift at $\omega = \omega_0$ (resonance) is **exactly** $-180°$ only if $Q$ is moderate. For $Q = \infty$, the phase at $\omega_0$ is undefined (jumps from $-180°$ to $+180°$ instantaneously). For finite $Q$, the phase passes continuously through $-180°$ at resonance.

### 2.4 Group Delay of Second-Order APF

$$\tau_g(\omega) = \frac{2Q\left(1 + \dfrac{\omega^2}{\omega_0^2}\right)}{Q \cdot \text{(denominator derivative term)}}$$

The maximum group delay at $\omega = \omega_0$ is:

$$\tau_g(\omega_0) = \frac{2Q}{\omega_0} = \frac{4Q}{\omega_{BW}}$$

where $\omega_{BW} = \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}$ is the **bandwidth**.

---

## 3. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

| Parameter | First-Order APF | Second-Order APF |
|---|---|---|
| **Transfer Function** | $H(s) = \dfrac{1 - sRC}{1 + sRC}$ | $H(s) = \dfrac{s^2 - \frac{\omega_0}{Q}s + \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \frac{\omega_0}{Q}s + \omega_0^2}$ |
| **Pole Location** | $s = -\dfrac{1}{RC}$ (LHP) | $s = -\dfrac{\omega_0}{2Q} \pm j\omega_0\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{1}{4Q^2}}$ (LHP) |
| **Zero Location** | $s = +\dfrac{1}{RC}$ (RHP) | $s = +\dfrac{\omega_0}{2Q} \pm j\omega_0\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{1}{4Q^2}}$ (RHP) |
| **Magnitude** $\|H(j\omega)\vert$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| **Phase** $\phi(\omega)$ | $-2\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ | $-2\tan^{-1}\!\left[\dfrac{\omega \omega_0/Q}{\omega_0^2 - \omega^2}\right]$ |
| **Phase at DC** | $0°$ | $0°$ |
| **Phase at $\omega \to \infty$** | $-180°$ | $-360°$ |
| **Phase at $\omega_0$** | N/A | $-180°$ (passes through) |
| **Max Group Delay** | $2RC$ at $\omega=0$ | $\dfrac{2Q}{\omega_0}$ at $\omega=\omega_0$ |
| **Stability** | Always stable (LHP pole) | Stable for $Q > 0$ |
| **Active Elements** | 1 Op-Amp | 1–2 Op-Amps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Second-order APFs are the workhorse of **quadrature oscillator design** (two APFs in cascade produce a $90°$ phase split → sine-cosine generation). They are also the basis of **Schroeder's reverberator** in digital audio.

---

## 4. Practical Op-Amp Implementation Notes

- Use **precision metal-film resistors** ($\pm 1\%$) and **NPO/C0G ceramic capacitors** or **film capacitors** to achieve predictable $RC$ products.
- For second-order APFs, the **gain-bandwidth product (GBW)** of the op-amp must be at least **$10 \times$ the maximum frequency of operation** to avoid phase error.
- Common op-amp choices: **µA741** (low-frequency teaching labs), **TL082** (general purpose), **OP27** (precision audio), **LM318** (high-speed).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: First-Order All-Pass Filter Transfer Function

### Circuit Description

Consider the following op-amp configuration:
- A resistor $R_1$ connects the input $V_{in}$ to the **inverting input** (node A).
- A capacitor $C$ connects node A to the **output** $V_{out}$.
- The **non-inverting input** is connected to the junction of a voltage divider formed by $R_2$ and $R_3$ between $V_{in}$ and $V_{out}$.

For symmetry, choose $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and let $R_3$ be a variable to set gain.

### Step 1: Apply KCL at the Inverting Node (Virtual Short Assumption)

Using the ideal op-amp rule $V^+ = V^-$ and zero current into op-amp inputs:

$$\frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1} = C \cdot \frac{d(V^- - V_{out})}{dt}$$

In the s-domain:

$$\frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R} = sC(V^- - V_{out}) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

### Step 2: Voltage at the Non-Inverting Input

By voltage divider:

$$V^+ = V^- = \frac{R_3 \cdot V_{in} + R_2 \cdot V_{out}}{R_2 + R_3}$$

With $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and choosing $R_3 = R$ (for unity configuration):

$$V^- = \frac{R \cdot V_{in} + R \cdot V_{out}}{2R} = \frac{V_{in} + V_{out}}{2} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify

Substitute (ii) into (i):

$$\frac{V_{in} - \frac{V_{in} + V_{out}}{2}}{R} = sC\left(\frac{V_{in} + V_{out}}{2} - V_{out}\right)$$

Left side:

$$\frac{\frac{2V_{in} - V_{in} - V_{out}}{2}}{R} = \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{2R}$$

Right side:

$$sC \cdot \frac{V_{in} + V_{out} - 2V_{out}}{2} = sC \cdot \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{2}$$

### Step 4: Equate Both Sides

$$\frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{2R} = sC \cdot \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{2}$$

Cancel the common factor $\dfrac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{2}$ (non-trivial case):

$$\frac{1}{R} = sC \quad \text{(steady-state identity — not useful)}$$

**Reconsider:** The above shows that this simple node equation alone doesn't give APF. We need the **non-inverting input's signal path** through the divider to provide the all-pass behavior. Let us write the proper KCL at the inverting node considering $V^+$ properly.

### Corrected Derivation (Standard Topology)

For the canonical first-order APF:

> Let the **impedance** from $V_{in}$ to inverting input be $Z_1$ and the **feedback impedance** from output to inverting input be $Z_2$. The non-inverting input receives $V_{in}$ through a divider and the output directly.

Using the **superposition-based non-inverting amplifier** approach:

$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{Z_2}{Z_1}\right) V^+ - \frac{Z_2}{Z_1} V_{in}$$

For the all-pass topology: $Z_1 = R$ and $Z_2 = \dfrac{1}{sC}$, and $V^+ = V_{in}$ (input fed directly to non-inverting terminal through a unity buffer consideration).

Substituting:

$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{1/(sC)}{R}\right) V_{in} - \frac{1/(sC)}{R} V_{in}$$

$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{1}{sRC}\right) V_{in} - \frac{1}{sRC} V_{in}$$

$$V_{out} = V_{in} + \frac{V_{in}}{sRC} - \frac{V_{in}}{sRC}$$

$$V_{out} = V_{in}$$

This yields a unity-gain buffer — **not the APF**. The classical APF requires a more careful topology, presented next.

---

## Derivation 2: Classical First-Order All-Pass (Moodley–Sedra Topology)

### Circuit Configuration

- $R_1$ from $V_{in}$ to **non-inverting input** (+) of op-amp.
- $C_1$ from non-inverting input to ground.
- $R_2$ from $V_{in}$ to inverting input (−).
- $R_3$ (feedback) from output $V_{out}$ to inverting input.

For balance: $R_1 = R_2 = R$, and $C_1 = C$.

### Step 1: Voltage at Non-Inverting Input

The non-inverting input sees $R$ in series with $C$ to ground (with $V_{in}$ at the top of $R$):

$$V^+ = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1/(sC)}{R + 1/(sC)} = \frac{V_{in}}{1 + sRC}$$

### Step 2: Voltage at Inverting Input (Virtual Short)

$$V^- = V^+ = \frac{V_{in}}{1 + sRC}$$

### Step 3: KCL at Inverting Node

$$\frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_2} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_3}$$

With $R_2 = R_3$:

$$V_{in} - V^- = V^- - V_{out}$$

$$V_{out} = 2V^- - V_{in}$$

### Step 4: Substitute $V^-$

$$V_{out} = \frac{2V_{in}}{1 + sRC} - V_{in}$$

$$V_{out} = V_{in}\left(\frac{2 - (1 + sRC)}{1 + sRC}\right)$$

$$V_{out} = V_{in}\left(\frac{1 - sRC}{1 + sRC}\right)$$

### Step 5: Final Transfer Function

$$\boxed{H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1 - sRC}{1 + sRC}}$$

### Step 6: Magnitude Verification

Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1 - j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

$$|H(j\omega)| = \frac{\sqrt{1^2 + (\omega RC)^2}}{\sqrt{1^2 + (\omega RC)^2}} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 7: Phase Response

$$\phi(\omega) = \angle(1 - j\omega RC) - \angle(1 + j\omega RC) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC) - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

$$\boxed{\phi(\omega) = -2\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanatory Note on Step 7:** The numerator $(1 - j\omega RC)$ lies in the **fourth quadrant** (positive real, negative imaginary), so its phase is $-\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$. The denominator $(1 + j\omega RC)$ lies in the **first quadrant**, with phase $+\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$. Subtracting gives $-2\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$.

---

## Derivation 3: Second-Order All-Pass Filter — Pole-Zero Mirror Property

### Starting from the Canonical Form

$$H(s) = \frac{s^2 - bs + c}{s^2 + bs + c}, \quad b = \frac{\omega_0}{Q}, \quad c = \omega_0^2$$

### Step 1: Find the Poles

Solve $s^2 + bs + c = 0$:

$$s = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4c}}{2}$$

For $b^2 < 4c$ (underdamped, typical case):

$$s_{p_{1,2}} = -\frac{b}{2} \pm j\frac{\sqrt{4c - b^2}}{2}$$

Both poles have **negative real part** → LHP → stable.

### Step 2: Find the Zeros

Solve $s^2 - bs + c = 0$:

$$s = \frac{+b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4c}}{2}$$

$$s_{z_{1,2}} = +\frac{b}{2} \pm j\frac{\sqrt{4c - b^2}}{2}$$

### Step 3: Show Mirror Symmetry

Compare pole-zero pairs:

$$s_{p_k} = -a_k, \quad s_{z_k} = +a_k$$

where $a_k$ is a complex number. Therefore:

$$s_{z_k} = -s_{p_k}$$

The zeros are **exact mirror images** of the poles about the $j\omega$-axis. This guarantees:

$$|s - s_{z_k}| = |s - (-s_{p_k})| = |s + s_{p_k}| \quad \text{at } s = j\omega$$

Hence on the $j\omega$-axis: $\vert s - s_{z_k}\vert = \vert s - s_{p_k}\vert$, and:

$$|H(j\omega)| = \prod_k \frac{|j\omega - s_{z_k}|}{|j\omega - s_{p_k}|} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 4: Phase Response from Poles and Zeros

The total phase is:

$$\phi(\omega) = \sum_k \angle(j\omega - s_{z_k}) - \sum_k \angle(j\omega - s_{p_k})$$

For the second-order case, this reduces to:

$$\phi(\omega) = -2\tan^{-1}\left[\frac{\omega \cdot b}{c - \omega^2}\right] = -2\tan^{-1}\left[\frac{\omega \omega_0 / Q}{\omega_0^2 - \omega^2}\right]$$

### Step 5: Group Delay at Resonance ($\omega = \omega_0$)

Using the identity $\tau_g = -\dfrac{d\phi}{d\omega}$ and applying the derivative:

$$\tau_g(\omega_0) = \frac{2Q}{\omega_0} = \frac{4Q}{\omega_{BW}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Higher $Q$ in a second-order APF produces **larger group delay at resonance** — this is exactly why second-order APFs are used in **delay lines, phase shift networks, and oscillator loops** to fine-tune frequency.

---

## Derivation 4: Numerical Worked Example (First-Order APF)

**Given:** $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 10 \text{ nF}$

**Find:** (a) Pole frequency, (b) Phase at $\omega = 10^4$ rad/s, (c) Group delay at $\omega = 0$.

### Solution

**Step (a):** Pole frequency

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^4 \cdot 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi}$$

$$f_p \approx 1591.55 \text{ Hz} \quad ; \quad \omega_p = 10^4 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step (b):** Phase at $\omega = 10^4$ rad/s

$$\omega RC = 10^4 \cdot 10^4 \cdot 10^{-8} = 1$$

$$\phi = -2\tan^{-1}(1) = -2 \cdot 45° = -90°$$

**Step (c):** Group delay at $\omega = 0$

$$\tau_g(0) = 2RC = 2 \cdot 10^4 \cdot 10^{-8} = 2 \times 10^{-4} = 200 \text{ μs}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture — First-Order All-Pass Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin AC Source] --> B1[Resistor R1 to Non Inverting Input]
    B1 --> C1[Capacitor C1 to Ground]
    C1 --> D[Non Inverting Input Plus of Op Amp]
    A --> B2[Resistor R2 to Inverting Input Minus]
    B2 --> E[Inverting Input Minus of Op Amp]
    F[Output Vout] --> B3[Feedback Resistor R3]
    B3 --> E
    D --> G[Op Amp Ideal]
    E --> G
    G --> F
    G --> H[Output Node Vout]
    F --> I[Load Resistor RL]
    I --> J[Ground Reference]
    H --> K[Phase Shifted Output equals Magnitude Same as Vin]
```

## Diagram 2: Pole-Zero Plot of a First-Order All-Pass Filter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Complex s plane] --> B[j omega axis vertical imaginary]
    A --> C[Real sigma axis horizontal]
    B --> D[Point at plus 1 by RC on RHP labeled Zero]
    C --> E[Point at minus 1 by RC on LHP labeled Pole]
    F[Mirror symmetry about j omega axis] --> D
    F --> E
    G[Distance from any point on j omega to pole equals distance to zero] --> H[Magnitude equals 1 for all omega]
```

## Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Second-Order All-Pass Filter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Vin] --> B[First Op Amp Stage]
    B --> C[Inverting Integrator with Capacitor C1 and Resistor R1]
    C --> D[Second Op Amp Stage]
    D --> E[Inverting Integrator with Capacitor C2 and Resistor R2]
    E --> F[Summing Junction with Resistor R3 and R4]
    F --> G[Final Op Amp Inverting Summer]
    G --> H[Vout Phase Shifted 360 degrees max]
    H --> I[Feedback Path to First Stage]
    I --> B
    J[Transfer Function H of s equals s squared minus omega0 by Q times s plus omega0 squared divided by s squared plus omega0 by Q times s plus omega0 squared] --> H
```

## Diagram 4: Frequency Response Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Frequency axis omega in rad per sec] --> B[DC point at omega equals 0]
    A --> C[Resonance point at omega equals omega0]
    A --> D[High frequency at omega tends to infinity]
    B --> E[Magnitude equals 1 at all points]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    B --> F[Phase equals 0 degrees]
    C --> G[Phase equals minus 180 degrees for second order or minus 90 for first order]
    D --> H[Phase equals minus 360 degrees for second order or minus 180 for first order]
    E --> I[Constant unity magnitude characteristic of All Pass Filter]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## Diagram 5: Application Flow — APF in Quadrature Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sine Wave Input at Frequency f0] --> B[First All Pass Filter H1]
    B --> C[Sine Output with minus 90 degree phase shift]
    A --> D[Second All Pass Filter H2 cascaded]
    D --> E[Cosine Output with minus 180 degree phase shift]
    C --> F[Summing Amplifier with Gain]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Regenerative Feedback to Input]
    G --> A
    H[Barkhausen Criterion satisfied] --> F
    I[Quadrature Oscillator Output Sine and Cosine] --> A
```

> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Mermaid block diagrams above represent **functional signal flow** rather than literal physical schematics. They map the input-output relationships, the op-amp stages, the feedback paths, and the phase behavior of the all-pass filter topologies studied in KTU 2024 scheme Module 3.
