# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Explain the concept of resolution of a DAC. A 12-bit DAC has a reference voltage of 10 V. Determine its resolution in volts and as a percentage.**

**Model Answer:**

Resolution is the smallest change in analog output corresponding to a 1-LSB change in the digital input. It is the step size of the DAC transfer function.

For a 12-bit DAC with $V_{REF} = 10$ V:

Resolution (in volts):
$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.44 \text{ mV}
$$

Resolution (as percentage):
$$
\% \text{ Res} = \frac{1}{2^{12}} \times 100 = \frac{100}{4096} = 0.0244\%
$$

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**List any four specifications of an ADC. Compare Flash and SAR ADCs in terms of speed and hardware complexity.**

**Model Answer:**

Four specifications of an ADC:
1. **Resolution** — smallest input change detectable (in bits or volts).
2. **Conversion time** — time required for one complete A/D conversion.
3. **Accuracy** — closeness of output to true value (in ± LSB).
4. **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** — ratio of signal power to noise power (in dB).

Comparison:

| Parameter | Flash ADC | SAR ADC |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Very High (one clock cycle) | Medium (n clock cycles) |
| Hardware | $2^n - 1$ comparators (very complex) | One comparator + SAR (simple) |
| Cost | Very High | Low |
| Resolution | Typically ≤ 8 bits | 12–16 bits commonly |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 4-bit binary-weighted resistor DAC. Derive the expression for the output voltage. What are its disadvantages?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an R-2R ladder DAC. Show that the output voltage expression is the same as the binary-weighted DAC.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a successive approximation ADC with a neat block diagram. An 8-bit SAR ADC uses a clock of 1 MHz. Find the conversion time and conversion rate.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram and integrator waveform, explain the working of a dual slope ADC. Why is it preferred in digital voltmeters?

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solution — Question A (Part a)

**Solution for Question A, Part (a):**

**Circuit Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above for the Mermaid block diagram).**

**Operation:**
The 4-bit binary-weighted resistor DAC consists of:
- An op-amp configured as an inverting summer with feedback resistor $R_f = R$
- Four input resistors with values $R, 2R, 4R, 8R$ corresponding to bits $b_3$ (MSB), $b_2, b_1, b_0$ (LSB)
- Four electronically controlled switches (one per bit) that connect each resistor to either $V_{REF}$ (bit = 1) or ground (bit = 0)

When a bit is "1", the corresponding switch connects the resistor to $V_{REF}$, and a current proportional to $V_{REF}/(2^{3-k} R)$ flows into the virtual ground. When the bit is "0", the switch connects the resistor to ground and no current flows (since the inverting input is at 0 V, both ends are at 0 V).

**Derivation:**

The total current entering the inverting node is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_T &= \frac{V_{REF}}{R} \left( b_3 \cdot \frac{1}{1} + b_2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + b_1 \cdot \frac{1}{4} + b_0 \cdot \frac{1}{8} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The op-amp output voltage is $V_o = -I_T \cdot R_f$. With $R_f = R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= -V_{REF} \left( b_3 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_1}{4} + \frac{b_0}{8} \right) \\
V_o &= -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^4} \sum_{k=0}^{3} b_k \cdot 2^k
\end{aligned}
$$

Defining $D = b_3 2^3 + b_2 2^2 + b_1 2^1 + b_0 2^0$ (decimal value):

$$
\boxed{V_o = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \cdot D}
$$

**Disadvantages:**

1. **Wide resistance spread:** The MSB resistor is $R$ and the LSB resistor is $2^{n-1} R$. For a 12-bit DAC, the ratio is 2048:1 — extremely difficult to fabricate accurately in ICs.
2. **Switch resistance variation:** Different currents pass through different switches, making switch-resistance errors a major source of non-linearity.
3. **Limited to low resolution (≤ 5 bits in practice).**

---

## 5.4 Complete Model Solution — Question A (Part b)

**Solution for Question A, Part (b):**

**Circuit Diagram (refer to Section 4.2).**

**Operation:**
The R-2R ladder uses only two resistor values, R and 2R, regardless of the number of bits. The switches connect each 2R branch to either $V_{REF}$ (bit = 1) or ground (bit = 0). At each ladder node, the current is divided into two equal halves due to the symmetric R/2R structure.

**Current Analysis:**
At the rightmost node (LSB), the current through the 2R resistor (when ON) is $V_{REF}/(2R+2R \parallel 2R) = V_{REF}/(3R)$ initially, but the **Thevenin equivalent** at the node yields an effective driving voltage of $V_{REF}/2$ into a $2R$ load, giving $V_{REF}/(4R)$. This binary halving continues at each node.

For the LSB branch current: $I_0 = \frac{V_{REF}}{2 \cdot 2R} \cdot \frac{1}{2^{n-1}}$
For the MSB branch current: $I_{n-1} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2 \cdot 2R}$

Summing all currents at the output of the ladder and converting through the op-amp:

$$
V_o = -V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}
$$

**Advantages over weighted resistor DAC:**
1. Only two resistor values — easy to fabricate and match.
2. Excellent for IC implementation.
3. Suitable for high resolution (12, 14, 16 bits).
4. Switch resistance has minimal effect (all switches see same current).

---

## 5.5 Complete Model Solution — Question B (Part a)

**Solution for Question B, Part (a):**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.4).**

**Working Principle:**
The SAR ADC performs a binary search using:
- **SAR (Successive Approximation Register):** Holds the trial digital word
- **DAC:** Converts the trial word to an analog voltage $V_{DAC}$
- **Comparator:** Compares $V_a$ (input) with $V_{DAC}$
- **Control Logic:** Implements the binary search algorithm

**Algorithm (for 8-bit, trial starts with $10000000$):**
1. **Cycle 1:** Set MSB $b_7 = 1$, all others 0. Compare $V_a$ with $V_{DAC} = V_{REF}/2$.
   - If $V_a > V_{DAC}$: keep $b_7 = 1$.
   - If $V_a < V_{DAC}$: reset $b_7 = 0$.
2. **Cycle 2:** Set $b_6 = 1$, compare $V_a$ with $V_{DAC}$ (now includes $b_7$ decision). Keep or reset $b_6$.
3. **Continue** until LSB $b_0$ is tested.
4. **After 8 cycles:** SAR holds the final digital output.

**Calculation:**

Clock frequency $f_{clk} = 1$ MHz → $T_{clk} = 1 \mu s$.

Conversion time:
$$
T_c = n \cdot T_{clk} = 8 \times 1 \mu s = 8 \mu s
$$

Conversion rate:
$$
\text{Rate} = \frac{1}{T_c} = \frac{1}{8 \mu s} = 125,000 \text{ conversions/sec} = 125 \text{ kSPS}
$$

**Advantages:** Fixed conversion time, medium-high speed, moderate hardware.
**Disadvantages:** Requires a precision DAC internally.

---

## 5.6 Complete Model Solution — Question B (Part b)

**Solution for Question B, Part (b):**

**Block Diagram:** Integrator → Comparator → Counter. The integrator output waveform is shown in Section 4.6.

**Working:**
The dual slope ADC operates in two phases:

**Phase 1 — Run-Up Phase (Duration $T_1$):**
- Switch $S_1$ connects the analog input $V_a$ to the integrator.
- The integrator output ramps downward (assuming $V_a > 0$):

$$
V_{INT}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_0^{t} V_a \, d\tau = -\frac{V_a \cdot t}{RC}
$$

- After fixed time $T_1 = 2^n \cdot T_{clk}$, the output is:

$$
V_A = -\frac{V_a \cdot T_1}{RC}
$$

- At this instant, the control logic switches $S_1$ to the reference $-V_{REF}$.

**Phase 2 — Run-Down Phase (Duration $T_2$):**
- The integrator input is now $-V_{REF}$.
- The integrator output ramps upward:

$$
V_{INT}(t) = V_A - \frac{1}{RC} \int_0^{t} (-V_{REF}) \, d\tau = V_A + \frac{V_{REF} \cdot t}{RC}
$$

- A counter counts clock pulses from the start of Phase 2.
- When the integrator output reaches 0 V, the comparator flips and stops the counter.

Setting $V_{INT}(T_2) = 0$:

$$
0 = V_A + \frac{V_{REF} \cdot T_2}{RC} \Rightarrow T_2 = -\frac{V_A \cdot RC}{V_{REF}} = \frac{V_a \cdot T_1}{V_{REF}}
$$

**Digital Output:**
$$
D = \frac{T_2}{T_{clk}} = \frac{V_a \cdot T_1}{V_{REF} \cdot T_{clk}} = \frac{V_a \cdot 2^n \cdot T_{clk}}{V_{REF} \cdot T_{clk}} = \frac{V_a}{V_{REF}} \cdot 2^n
$$

**Why Preferred in Digital Voltmeters:**

1. **Clock independence:** Both $T_1$ and the count use the same clock, so clock frequency variations cancel out.
2. **R and C independence:** RC constant cancels in the ratio $T_2/T_1$.
3. **Excellent noise rejection:** The integration acts as a low-pass filter, averaging out noise.
4. **High accuracy (16–24 bits)** achievable.
5. **Excellent linearity** due to the natural ramp characteristic.

---

## 5.7 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Pattern)**
> 
> 1. **Sign Error in DAC Output:** Students often forget the **negative sign** in $V_o = -V_{REF} \cdot D / 2^n$. This is a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 
> 2. **Confusion Between Resolution and Step Size:** Resolution is sometimes given as $V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$ and sometimes as $V_{FS}/2^n$. The KTU accepted form is $V_{REF}/2^n$ (LSB weight). Use the form specified in your problem.
> 
> 3. **ADC Conversion Time Confusion:** For counter-type ADC, students write $T_c = 2^n \cdot T_{clk}$ instead of $(2^n - 1) \cdot T_{clk}$. The "$2^n - 1$" is critical for full-scale.
> 
> 4. **SAR Binary Search Order:** Students must explain that SAR starts from the **MSB and works downward**, not the reverse. Writing "start from LSB" loses 2 marks.
> 
> 5. **Dual Slope Phase Confusion:** Students mix up the two phases. Remember: **Run-Up** = fixed time $T_1$ with input $V_a$, **Run-Down** = variable time $T_2$ with reference $-V_{REF}$.
> 
> 6. **Missing Block Diagrams:** Every 14-mark question in KTU 2024 scheme requires a **neat labeled block diagram**. Missing diagrams = 2–3 marks automatic deduction.
> 
> 7. **Units:** Always specify units in final answers (V, mV, µs, etc.). Bare numbers may lose 0.5 mark per occurrence.

---

## 5.8 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This 5 Minutes Before Exam**

- [ ] **DAC Master Formula:** $V_o = -V_{REF} \cdot D / 2^n$ (memorize for both DAC types)
- [ ] **DAC Resolution:** $V_{REF}/2^n$ in volts; $100/2^n$ in percent
- [ ] **Weighted DAC Resistor Spread:** $R, 2R, 4R, \ldots, 2^{n-1}R$ — limited to ≤ 5 bits in practice
- [ ] **R-2R DAC:** Only R and 2R values, suitable for 8–16+ bits, industry standard
- [ ] **Flash ADC:** $2^n - 1$ comparators, conversion in one clock cycle, ≤ 8 bits practical
- [ ] **Counter ADC:** $T_c = (2^n - 1) \cdot T_{clk}$, very slow, simple
- [ ] **Servo/Tracking ADC:** Uses up/down counter, good for slowly varying signals
- [ ] **SAR ADC:** $T_c = n \cdot T_{clk}$, binary search, fixed conversion time, most popular
- [ ] **Dual Slope ADC:** $D = (V_a / V_{REF}) \cdot 2^n$, immune to clock and RC drift, used in DVMs
- [ ] **SNR Formula:** $6.02n + 1.76$ dB (theoretical)
- [ ] **ENOB Formula:** $(\text{SNR} - 1.76)/6.02$
- [ ] **Key Specifications:** Resolution, Accuracy, Linearity, Settling time, Conversion time, SNR, ENOB, Monotonicity
- [ ] **Block Diagrams:** Always draw neat, labeled diagrams for full marks
- [ ] **Signs:** DAC output is negative for inverting configuration; use $V_{REF} = \pm V$ if asked for bipolar
- [ ] **Order of Magnitude:** 10-bit DAC = ~10 mV LSB (with 10V ref); 12-bit = ~2.4 mV; 16-bit = ~0.15 mV
- [ ] **Conversion Rate:** Always $1/T_c$ in samples per second (SPS) or conversions per second
- [ ] **Master Comparison:** Flash > SAR > Pipeline > Integrating (in terms of speed, reverse for resolution)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Regulator: Introduction, Series op amp regulator, IC regulators, 78XX and 79XX characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT404 - INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Voltage Regulator: Introduction, Series op amp regulator, IC regulators, 78XX and 79XX characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Voltage Regulators

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Voltage Regulator** is an electronic circuit or integrated device designed to automatically maintain a constant output voltage level despite variations in input voltage, load current, or ambient temperature. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, voltage regulators form the final stage of any DC power supply, converting rectified and filtered unregulated DC into a precise, stable DC reference suitable for analog and digital subsystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4:** Voltage regulators are classified under **Linear Voltage Regulators**, which dissipate excess voltage as heat across a series pass element. Switching regulators are explicitly out of scope for this module.

### Why Are Voltage Regulators Required?
Every practical DC power supply has three inherent problems that must be solved before the supply can power sensitive electronics:

1. **Input Voltage Variation:** The unregulated DC after rectification varies with AC mains fluctuations (typically ±10% in India as per IS 2026).
2. **Load Current Variation:** When load current changes, the voltage drop across the transformer's source impedance and the rectifier's dynamic resistance changes, altering the output.
3. **Ripple:** The filtered DC still contains a residual AC component (ripple) at 100 Hz (full-wave rectified) that must be suppressed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Water Tap:** Imagine a water tap connected to a high-pressure overhead tank. No matter how the water level in the tank changes (input variation) or how many people open their taps downstream (load variation), a good pressure regulator at the outlet ensures a constant, smooth water flow. A voltage regulator performs the *electrical equivalent* of this pressure regulation.

### Performance Metrics of a Voltage Regulator

| Metric | Definition | Ideal Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Line Regulation (LR)** | Change in $V_{out}$ per unit change in $V_{in}$, with load held constant | **0 %/V** |
| **Load Regulation (LDR)** | Change in $V_{out}$ from no-load to full-load | **0 %** |
| **Ripple Rejection (PSRR)** | Ability to reject AC ripple at the input | **∞ dB** |
| **Temperature Coefficient** | Drift of $V_{out}$ per °C | **0 mV/°C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant Used Throughout:** The thermal voltage $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85\ mV}$ at **300 K** (room temperature). This is the foundational constant for all BJT-based regulator analysis in this module.

## 1.2 Types of Voltage Regulators (KTU Classification)

The KTU 2024 module specifically covers **linear series regulators**, which are classified as:

- **Discrete Series Op-Amp Regulator** — uses an op-amp as the error amplifier with a discrete series pass transistor.
- **Three-Terminal Fixed IC Regulator (78XX / 79XX)** — fully integrated, fixed positive/negative output.
- **Three-Terminal Adjustable IC Regulator (LM317 / LM337)** — integrated but with user-programmable output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Boundary:** Switching regulators (SMPS, buck, boost), shunt regulators using only Zener diodes, and ICs like LT3080 are **not** part of this module. Focus strictly on **linear series regulators**.

## 1.3 The 78XX and 79XX Family — First Look

The **78XX** series provides **fixed positive** output voltages, while the **79XX** series provides **fixed negative** output voltages. The "XX" denotes the output voltage magnitude.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Lego Block:** Think of 78XX and 79XX as ready-made Lego blocks. A 7805 always gives +5 V, a 7812 always gives +12 V, a 7905 always gives -5 V, and so on. The designer does not need to design any feedback network — the IC is a complete, self-contained regulator in a 3-pin package.

Common family members you must memorize for KTU exams:
- **7805 / 7905** → ±5 V
- **7806 / 7906** → ±6 V
- **7808 / 7908** → ±8 V
- **7812 / 7912** → ±12 V
- **7815 / 7915** → ±15 V
- **7824 / 7924** → ±24 V
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Diagram of a Series Voltage Regulator

Every series voltage regulator — whether discrete or IC — contains **four fundamental functional blocks** arranged in a negative-feedback loop.

### Functional Block Architecture

1. **Series Pass Element (Q1):** A power BJT or Darlington pair placed in *series* between the unregulated input and the regulated output. It operates in the **active (linear) region** and behaves as a variable resistor controlled by the error amplifier.
2. **Sampling Network (R1, R2):** A resistor divider that senses the output voltage and produces a feedback voltage $V_f$ proportional to $V_{out}$.
3. **Reference Source (Vref / Zener):** A temperature-compensated Zener diode or bandgap reference that provides a stable internal voltage $V_{ref}$ against which the sampled output is compared.
4. **Error Amplifier (Op-Amp):** A high-gain differential amplifier that compares $V_f$ with $V_{ref}$ and drives the base of the series pass transistor. Any error is amplified and corrects the pass transistor's conduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Negative Feedback Loop:** The op-amp's high open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ combined with negative feedback forces the inverting input voltage to equal the non-inverting input voltage. This is the **virtual short principle** and it is the heart of every op-amp regulator.

## 2.2 Series Op-Amp Regulator — Detailed Analysis

### Circuit Topology
The op-amp's **non-inverting input** is tied to a precision Zener reference $V_Z$. The **inverting input** receives the feedback voltage $V_f$ from the output sampling network. The op-amp output drives the **base** of the series pass transistor Q1 (typically a 2N3055 or TIP31).

### Operating Principle — Step by Step
- If $V_{out}$ **rises** (due to input increase or load decrease), $V_f$ rises, making the inverting input higher than the non-inverting input. The op-amp output **falls**, reducing Q1's base drive, increasing $V_{CE}$ of Q1, and pulling $V_{out}$ back down.
- If $V_{out}$ **falls**, the inverse sequence occurs — op-amp output rises, Q1 conducts harder, and $V_{out}$ is restored.

### The Governing Equation
Applying the **virtual short** principle at the op-amp inputs:

$$V_f = V_{ref} = V_Z$$

The feedback voltage is:

$$V_f = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Equating the two:

$$V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = V_Z$$

Solving for $V_{out}$:

$$V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Equation Matters:** The output voltage is set entirely by the **Zener voltage** and the **resistor ratio**, *independent* of load current, input voltage, or transistor beta. This is the engineering elegance of negative feedback.

## 2.3 Limitations of the Discrete Op-Amp Regulator

- **Load current is limited** by the $\beta$ of the pass transistor and the op-amp's output current rating (typically $\pm 20$ mA). For higher currents, a **Darlington pair** is used.
- **No built-in short-circuit protection.** A separate current-sense resistor and transistor must be added.
- **No thermal shutdown.** The pass transistor can over-dissipate and destroy itself.
- **Zener drift** with temperature introduces output voltage drift.

These limitations motivated the development of **monolithic three-terminal regulators** (78XX/79XX).

## 2.4 The 78XX and 79XX Three-Terminal IC Regulators

### Internal Architecture (What's Inside the IC)
A modern 78XX/79XX IC integrates the following on a single silicon die:
1. A **bandgap reference** (typically 1.25 V) — superior to a Zener because of its low temperature coefficient.
2. A **high-gain error amplifier**.
3. A **Darlington pass transistor** for high current handling.
4. **Current limiting** circuitry (short-circuit protection).
5. **Thermal shutdown** (typically at 150–175 °C junction temperature).
6. **Safe Operating Area (SOA) protection.**

### Pin Configuration (TO-220 Package, viewed from the front)

| Pin | 78XX Function | 79XX Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Pin 1** | Input ($V_{in}$) | Ground (GND) |
| **Pin 2** | Ground (GND) | Input ($V_{in}$) |
| **Pin 3** | Output ($V_{out}$) | Output ($V_{out}$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Confused:** The ground and input pins are **swapped** between 78XX and 79XX. This is one of the most common mistakes students make in PCB design. Memorize the asymmetry.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat-Sheet — Print This Table.** All formulas below are fair game for KTU 2024 Part-A (3-mark) and Part-B (14-mark) questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$ | Op-amp series regulator output | Volts |
| 2 | $V_f = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Feedback voltage from divider | Volts |
| 3 | $V_{CE}(Q1) = V_{in} - V_{out}$ | Voltage drop across pass transistor | Volts |
| 4 | $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$ | Power dissipation in pass transistor | Watts |
| 5 | $P_D(\text{IC}) = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L + V_{in} \cdot I_Q$ | Power dissipation in 78XX/79XX | Watts |
| 6 | $LR = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \Big\vert_{I_L = const}$ | Line regulation | V/V or %/V |
| 7 | $LDR = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$ | Load regulation | % |
| 8 | $\text{Efficiency } \eta = \frac{V_{out} \cdot I_L}{V_{in} \cdot (I_L + I_Q)} \times 100\%$ | Regulator efficiency | % |
| 9 | $\theta_{JA} = \frac{T_J(\max) - T_A}{P_D}$ | Thermal resistance relation | °C/W |
| 10 | $V_{out}(\text{LM317}) = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} \cdot R_2$ | Adjustable regulator output | Volts |
| 11 | $V_{in}(\min) = V_{out} + V_{dropout}$ | Minimum headroom (dropout = 2–3 V for 78XX) | Volts |
| 12 | $I_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE}}{R_{sense}}$ | Short-circuit current limit | Amps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition for Linear Regulators:** $V_{in}$ **must always be greater than** $V_{out} + V_{dropout}$. Otherwise, the pass transistor enters saturation and regulation is lost. This is called **dropout**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **78XX/79XX in 5 V logic supplies** for Arduino, Raspberry Pi, and microcontroller boards.
- **Op-amp series regulators** in lab-bench power supplies where the output must be tunable over a wide range.
- **LM317/LM337** in battery chargers and adjustable bench supplies.
- **High-current regulators** (LT1083, LT1084) using 78XX with a **current-boost PNP transistor** in parallel for currents up to 10 A.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard:** The 7805 has been the **default 5 V regulator** in commercial electronics for over four decades. Its datasheet parameters are memorized by every practicing hardware engineer worldwide.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Voltage of the Op-Amp Series Regulator

We will derive $V_{out}$ from first principles using the virtual-short principle.

### Given
- Zener reference voltage $V_Z$ at the non-inverting input.
- Sampling resistors $R_1$ (from output to inverting input) and $R_2$ (from inverting input to ground).
- Op-amp is assumed ideal: $A_{OL} = \infty$, input bias current = 0, $V_{os} = 0$.

### Step 1 — Apply the Virtual Short
Because the op-amp is in a negative feedback loop and $A_{OL} \to \infty$, the differential input voltage must be zero for finite output. Therefore:

$$V_+ = V_-$$

Since $V_+ = V_Z$ and $V_- = V_f$:

$$V_f = V_Z$$

### Step 2 — Express the Feedback Voltage
The resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a voltage divider from $V_{out}$ to ground. By the voltage divider rule:

$$V_f = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

### Step 3 — Equate the Two Expressions for $V_f$
From Step 1 and Step 2:

$$V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_Z$$

### Step 4 — Solve Algebraically for $V_{out}$

$$V_{out} = V_Z \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Convention:** The formula uses $R_1$ as the upper resistor and $R_2$ as the lower resistor. Some textbooks (including Roy Choudhury) reverse this. Always check the schematic.

### Step 5 — Sanity Check
If $R_1 = 0$ (no upper resistor), then $V_{out} = V_Z$. This is correct because the op-amp output simply buffers the Zener voltage through the pass transistor. If $R_1$ is large, $V_{out}$ rises above $V_Z$, confirming the gain behavior of the non-inverting topology.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Dissipation in the Pass Transistor

### Given
- $V_{in}$ = unregulated DC input to the regulator.
- $V_{out}$ = regulated output.
- $I_L$ = load current.
- $I_Q$ = quiescent current drawn by the op-amp / IC.

### Step 1 — Voltage Across the Pass Element
By KVL across the input-output loop:

$$V_{CE}(Q1) = V_{in} - V_{out}$$

### Step 2 — Current Through the Pass Element
Assuming $I_B \ll I_C$ (high $\beta$):

$$I_C \approx I_E \approx I_L + I_Q$$

### Step 3 — Power Dissipated
Power = Voltage × Current:

$$P_D = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$$

$$\boxed{P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L}$$

For an IC regulator (78XX/79XX), the quiescent current is also present:

$$P_D(\text{IC}) = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L + V_{in} \cdot I_Q$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example
**Problem:** A 7805 supplies 1 A at 5 V. The unregulated input is 12 V. Find the power dissipated in the IC.

**Solution:**

$$V_{in} - V_{out} = 12 - 5 = 7\ \text{V}$$

$$P_D = 7 \times 1 = 7\ \text{W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Heat-Sink Alert:** 7 W is *substantial*. Without a heat sink, the 7805 will thermally shutdown in seconds. The KTU 2024 examiner loves asking "Why is a heat sink necessary for a 7805?" — your answer must reference $P_D$ and $\theta_{JA}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Load Regulation from First Principles

### Definition
Load regulation quantifies how much $V_{out}$ droops as load current increases from no-load (NL) to full-load (FL).

### Step 1 — Output Impedance Model
A practical regulator can be modeled as an ideal source $V_{ideal}$ in series with an output impedance $Z_{out}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ideal} - I_L \cdot Z_{out}$$

### Step 2 — No-Load and Full-Load Expressions

$$V_{NL} = V_{ideal}\quad (\text{since } I_L = 0)$$

$$V_{FL} = V_{ideal} - I_{FL} \cdot Z_{out}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the Load Regulation Definition

$$LDR = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

$$LDR = \frac{V_{ideal} - (V_{ideal} - I_{FL} Z_{out})}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

$$LDR = \frac{I_{FL} \cdot Z_{out}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

### Step 4 — Equivalent Output Resistance
We can equivalently write:

$$Z_{out} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_L} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{FL}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A well-designed op-amp regulator with high loop gain has $Z_{out}$ in the **milliohm** range, even though the op-amp's intrinsic output impedance is tens of ohms. This is the **impedance multiplication** property of negative feedback: $Z_{out,closed} = \frac{Z_{out,open}}{1 + A\beta}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Output Voltage of LM317 Adjustable Regulator

The LM317 is a classic adjustable positive regulator. Its reference voltage $V_{ref} = 1.25$ V appears between the **Output** pin and the **Adjust** pin.

### Step 1 — Current Through R1
The R1 resistor is connected between the Output and Adjust pins. The current through it is:

$$I_{R_1} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_1} = \frac{1.25}{R_1}$$

### Step 2 — Current Through R2
Assuming $I_{ADJ}$ (typically 50 µA) is small:

$$I_{R_2} \approx I_{R_1}$$

### Step 3 — Voltage Across R2

$$V_{R_2} = I_{R_1} \cdot R_2 = \frac{1.25 \cdot R_2}{R_1}$$

### Step 4 — Total Output Voltage

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} + V_{R_2} = 1.25 + \frac{1.25 \cdot R_2}{R_1}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 1.25 \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} \cdot R_2}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example
**Design a 9 V supply using LM317.** Choose $R_1 = 240\ \Omega$ (the recommended minimum).

$$9 = 1.25 \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{240}\right)$$

$$\frac{R_2}{240} = \frac{9}{1.25} - 1 = 7.2 - 1 = 6.2$$

$$R_2 = 240 \times 6.2 = 1488\ \Omega \approx 1.5\ \text{k}\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The 240 Ω minimum for R1 ensures a minimum load current of 5 mA for proper regulation. This is documented in the LM317 datasheet.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Regulator Types

| Parameter | Discrete Op-Amp Regulator | 78XX / 79XX | LM317 / LM337 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Output voltage** | User-defined (any value) | Fixed (XX value) | Adjustable (1.25 V to 40 V) |
| **Components external** | 5–10 | 2 (input/output caps) | 2 resistors + 2 caps |
| **Short-circuit protection** | No (must add) | **Yes (built-in)** | **Yes (built-in)** |
| **Thermal shutdown** | No | **Yes (built-in)** | **Yes (built-in)** |
| **Current limit** | No | **Yes (foldback)** | **Yes (constant)** |
| **Quiescent current** | Low (~1 mA) | 5–8 mA | 5–10 mA |
| **Dropout voltage** | ~$V_{CE(sat)}$ + $V_Z$ margin | **2 V typical** | **3 V typical** |
| **Accuracy** | Set by Zener tolerance | ±2% to ±4% | ±1% to ±2% |
| **PSRR @ 120 Hz** | High (depends on op-amp) | **60–80 dB** | **65–80 dB** |
| **Cost (approx.)** | ₹40 (discrete BOM) | **₹15–25** | **₹20–30** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Series Voltage Regulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VinUnreg["Unregulated DC Input Vin"] -->|enters| PassTrans["Series Pass Transistor Q1"]
    PassTrans -->|Vout| VoutNode["Regulated DC Output Vout"]
    SampleNet["Sampling Network R1 R2"] -->|Vf| ErrorAmp["Error Amplifier Op Amp"]
    VoutNode -->|sensed| SampleNet
    Vref["Reference Voltage Vref Zener"] -->|Vz| ErrorAmp
    ErrorAmp -->|base drive| PassTrans
    LoadBranch["Load RL"] --> VoutNode
    VoutNode --> LoadBranch
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows show *signal flow*, not current flow. The negative feedback loop is: $V_{out} \rightarrow$ SampleNet $\rightarrow$ $V_f \rightarrow$ ErrorAmp $\rightarrow$ base of Q1 $\rightarrow$ correction of $V_{out}$.

## 4.2 Op-Amp Series Regulator — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputStage["Unregulated DC Stage"]
        Vi["Vin 12 to 18 V DC"]
    end
    subgraph RegCore["Regulator Core"]
        Q1["Pass Transistor Q1 2N3055 Tip31"]
        Op["Op Amp Error Amplifier 741"]
        Z["Zener Vz 5.6 V"]
        R1["R1 Sampling Upper"]
        R2["R2 Sampling Lower"]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage["Regulated Output"]
        Vo["Vout Regulated 9 V"]
        RL["Load RL"]
    end
    Vi -->|DC rail| Q1
    Q1 -->|emitter| Vo
    Z -->|non inverting| Op
    Vo -->|tapped| R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 -->|ground| GND1["GND"]
    R1 -->|feedback Vf| Op
    Op -->|base drive| Q1
    Vo --> RL
    RL --> GND2["GND"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Reading:** The op-amp continuously compares $V_f$ (derived from $V_{out}$ via R1, R2) against $V_Z$ at the Zener. Any error is amplified and applied to Q1's base, which adjusts the pass transistor's conduction to restore $V_{out}$ to the designed value.

## 4.3 Internal Block Diagram of 78XX / 79XX IC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Pin1["Pin 1 Input Vin"] -->|into| Bandgap["Bandgap Reference 1.25 V"]
    Bandgap --> VrefInternal["Internal Vref 1.25 V"]
    VrefInternal --> ErrorAmp1["Error Amplifier"]
    Sampler1["Internal Divider R1 R2"] -->|Vf to error amp| ErrorAmp1
    ErrorAmp1 -->|drives| Darlington["Darlington Q1 Q2 Pass Pair"]
    Darlington -->|Vout| Pin3["Pin 3 Output Vout"]
    Pin3 --> Sampler1
    Pin1 -->|Vin| Darlington
    Pin2["Pin 2 Ground"] -->|reference| GNDInternal["IC Ground Bus"]
    Overcurrent["Overcurrent Sense"] -->|limits| Darlington
    Thermal["Thermal Shutdown 150 C"] -->|protects| Darlington
    SOA["SOA Protection"] -->|clamps| Darlington
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The internal divider ratio inside a 7805 is set so that the output sits at 5 V when $V_{ref} = 1.25$ V. For example, in 7805 the divider is designed such that $V_{out} = V_{ref}(1 + R_a/R_b)$ with $R_a/R_b = 3$, giving 5 V. Different 78XX variants use different internal resistor ratios to produce 6 V, 8 V, 12 V, etc.

## 4.4 Pin Configuration — TO-220 Package (Front View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TO220["TO 220 Package Top View"]
        P1Left["Pin 1"]
        P2Mid["Pin 2"]
        P3Right["Pin 3"]
        Tab["Heat Sink Tab on Back"]
    end
    P1Left --- P2Mid
    P2Mid --- P3Right
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin mapping for 78XX (left to right looking at the front):** Pin 1 = **Input**, Pin 2 = **Ground**, Pin 3 = **Output**.
> **Pin mapping for 79XX (left to right):** Pin 1 = **Ground**, Pin 2 = **Input**, Pin 3 = **Output**.
> The metal tab is electrically connected to the same potential as Pin 2 (Ground for 78XX, Ground for 79XX) and is used for heat-sink mounting.

## 4.5 Current Boost Configuration Using 78XX + External PNP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VinBoost["Vin 12 V"] --> Qext["External PNP Pass MJ2955"]
    Qext -->|boosted| VoutBoost["Vout 5 V at 5 A"]
    InBoost["78XX internal Q1"] -->|drives base| Qext
    Pin1Boost["Pin 1 of 78XX"] --> InBoost
    Pin3Boost["Pin 3 of 78XX"] --> Qext
    VoutBoost --> Pin1Boost
    VoutBoost --> Pin3Boost
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The internal pass transistor of the 78XX provides the base current for the external PNP MJ2955, which carries the bulk of the load current. The 78XX itself only carries a fraction of the total current (typically 0.5–1 A), keeping its internal dissipation low. This is a classic KTU design question.

## 4.6 Complete Power Supply Block Diagram (Putting It All Together)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC["AC Mains 230 V 50 Hz"] --> Transformer["Step Down Transformer 230 to 12 V AC"]
    Transformer --> Rectifier["Bridge Rectifier 4 Diodes"]
    Rectifier --> Filter["Capacitor Filter C 1000 uF"]
    Filter -->|Unregulated DC 15 V approx| Regulator["Three Terminal Regulator 7812 or 7805"]
    Regulator -->|Regulated DC 12 V or 5 V| LoadFinal["Electronic Load"]
    Regulator -->|bypass caps| CinCout["Cin 0.33 uF Cout 0.1 uF"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Picture:** A complete DC regulated power supply has four stages: **Transformer → Rectifier → Filter → Regulator**. The KTU 2024 module 4 focuses exclusively on the last stage, but you should know its place in the full system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define line regulation and load regulation. Why are they important in a voltage regulator?
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

**Line Regulation (LR):** It is defined as the change in regulated output voltage per unit change in unregulated input voltage, with the load current held constant.

$$LR = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \bigg\vert_{I_L = \text{const}}$$

It is expressed in %/V or mV/V. A low LR means the regulator is insensitive to input voltage variations.

**Load Regulation (LDR):** It is defined as the change in output voltage when the load current is varied from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of the full-load output.

$$LDR = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

A low LDR means the regulator maintains a constant output regardless of load current changes.

**Importance:** Both metrics quantify the *quality* of regulation. In sensitive analog and digital systems (ADC references, op-amp supplies, microcontroller rails), any variation in supply voltage can cause errors, noise, or malfunction. A good regulator must therefore minimize both.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between 78XX and 79XX voltage regulators. Draw the pin configuration of each.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 78XX | 79XX |
|---|---|---|
| **Output polarity** | Positive (+5 V, +12 V, etc.) | Negative (-5 V, -12 V, etc.) |
| **Pin 1** | Input ($V_{in}$) | Ground (GND) |
| **Pin 2** | Ground (GND) | Input ($V_{in}$) |
| **Pin 3** | Output ($V_{out}$) | Output ($V_{out}$) |
| **Quiescent current flow** | From input to ground | From ground to input (reversed) |
| **Common applications** | Logic ICs, op-amp positive rail | Op-amp negative rail, RS-232 driver supplies |

**Pin Diagram (TO-220, front view):**

```
   78XX Pinout          79XX Pinout
  ___________          ___________
 | 1  2  3 |          | 1  2  3 |
 | I  G  O |          | G  I  O |
  ___|___|_             ___|___|_
     |   |                 |   |
   tab=tab=GND           tab=tab=GND
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often assume 78XX and 79XX have the same pinout. They don't! Pin 1 and Pin 2 are swapped. In the KTU 2024 exam, drawing the wrong pinout in a circuit diagram will cost you full marks for that sub-part.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B questions in the End-Semester Examination carry **14 marks** and have **internal choice** (i.e., the student attempts *either* Question A *or* Question B). Each question is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a series voltage regulator using an op-amp as the error amplifier. Derive the expression for the output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (See SECTION 4.1 — refer to the block-level architecture diagram).

**Operation:**
The series op-amp regulator consists of four blocks: a **reference source** (Zener diode $D_Z$), a **sampling network** (resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$), an **error amplifier** (op-amp), and a **series pass element** (BJT Q1). The op-amp non-inverting input is tied to $V_Z$. The inverting input receives $V_f$ from the sampling network connected across $V_{out}$.

The op-amp continuously compares $V_f$ with $V_Z$. Any difference is amplified and applied to the base of Q1, modulating its collector-emitter voltage drop and thereby regulating $V_{out}$.

**Derivation:**
Applying the virtual short principle: $V_f = V_Z$.

Using the voltage divider: $V_f = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.

Equating:

$$V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_Z$$

Solving:

$$V_{out} = V_Z \left(\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}\right) = V_Z \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

---

**Q. (b)** Design a series op-amp regulator to provide $V_{out} = 9$ V. The Zener reference is 5.6 V. Determine the values of $R_1$ and $R_2$ if the feedback network current is to be 1 mA. Also calculate the power dissipation in the pass transistor if the load current is 500 mA and $V_{in} = 15$ V. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Choose R1 and R2 for Vout = 9 V:**

$$9 = 5.6 \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{9}{5.6} - 1 = 1.607 - 1 = 0.607$$

$$R_1 = 0.607 \cdot R_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Current Constraint:**
The current through the feedback network is to be 1 mA. This current flows from $V_{out}$ to ground through $R_1 + R_2$:

$$I_f = \frac{V_{out}}{R_1 + R_2} = 1\ \text{mA}$$

$$R_1 + R_2 = \frac{9}{1\ \text{mA}} = 9\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Two Equations:**

$$R_1 + R_2 = 9\ \text{k}\Omega$$
$$R_1 = 0.607 \cdot R_2$$

Substituting:

$$0.607 R_2 + R_2 = 9\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$1.607 R_2 = 9\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_2 = \frac{9000}{1.607} = 5600\ \Omega \approx 5.6\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_1 = 0.607 \times 5600 = 3400\ \Omega \approx 3.4\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Power Dissipation in the Pass Transistor:**

$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L = (15 - 9) \times 0.5 = 6 \times 0.5 = 3\ \text{W}$$

**Answer:** $R_1 = 3.4\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5.6\ \text{k}\Omega$, $P_D = 3\ \text{W}$.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Alternative]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the internal block diagram of the 78XX series three-terminal voltage regulator. List its key features and explain how short-circuit protection and thermal shutdown are implemented internally. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Internal Blocks:** (See SECTION 4.3 — refer to the internal block diagram of 78XX/79XX IC).

The 78XX IC integrates the following:

1. **Bandgap Reference (1.25 V):** Provides a temperature-stable reference voltage, far superior to a Zener diode.
2. **Error Amplifier:** A high-gain differential amplifier that compares the feedback voltage (from an internal divider) with the reference.
3. **Darlington Pass Transistor:** A composite Q1-Q2 configuration that provides high current gain ($\beta_{total} = \beta_1 \cdot \beta_2$), allowing the IC to deliver 1–1.5 A with a small base drive.
4. **Current Limiting Circuit:** A sense resistor and a transistor that reduce the base drive when the output current exceeds the safe limit (typically 1.5 A for 7805).
5. **Thermal Shutdown Circuit:** A temperature-sensing transistor turns off the Darlington when the junction temperature exceeds ~150–175 °C.
6. **Safe Operating Area (SOA) Protection:** Limits the V×I product to prevent second breakdown.

**Key Features of 78XX:**
- Fixed output voltage (no external components except input/output capacitors).
- Internal short-circuit protection.
- Internal thermal overload protection.
- Output current up to 1 A (with proper heat sinking).
- Dropout voltage of 2 V typical.
- Line regulation of 0.01 %/V typical.
- Load regulation of 0.1 % typical.
- Available in TO-220, TO-3, and surface-mount packages.

**Short-Circuit Protection:** A small resistor $R_{sense}$ is placed in series with the output. The voltage across it is proportional to $I_L$. When $I_L$ exceeds the limit, an internal transistor $Q_{SC}$ turns on, shunting base drive away from the Darlington, thus limiting the current to a safe value.

**Thermal Shutdown:** A temperature-sensing circuit (a bandgap-referenced comparator with a voltage proportional to absolute temperature, or VPTAT) monitors the die temperature. When $T_J$ exceeds the threshold, it forces the Darlington off, reducing dissipation. The IC cools, re-enables, and the cycle repeats — this is called **thermal cycling**.

---

**Q. (b)** For a 7805 regulator supplying a load of 0.8 A at 5 V with an unregulated input of 12 V, calculate: **(7 Marks)**
   (i) The power dissipated in the IC.
   (ii) The minimum heat-sink thermal resistance required if the maximum junction temperature is 150 °C and the ambient temperature is 50 °C.
   (iii) The efficiency of the regulator.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Power Dissipation:**

$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L = (12 - 5) \times 0.8 = 5.6\ \text{W}$$

(Adding the quiescent current term: $V_{in} \cdot I_Q = 12 \times 0.008 = 0.096$ W, giving $P_D \approx 5.7$ W. For a KTU exam, the simple formula without $I_Q$ is usually sufficient.)

**(ii) Thermal Resistance Calculation:**

The total thermal resistance budget from junction to ambient is:

$$\theta_{JA} = \frac{T_J(\max) - T_A}{P_D} = \frac{150 - 50}{5.6} = \frac{100}{5.6} \approx 17.86\ ^\circ\text{C/W}$$

The total thermal resistance is the sum of three components:

$$\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$$

For a TO-220 package with a mica insulator and thermal compound, typical values are:
- $\theta_{JC}$ (junction to case) = 5 °C/W
- $\theta_{CS}$ (case to heat sink, with insulator) = 1 °C/W
- $\theta_{SA}$ (heat sink to ambient) = to be determined

Solving for the heat sink:

$$\theta_{SA} = \theta_{JA} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS} = 17.86 - 5 - 1 = 11.86\ ^\circ\text{C/W}$$

**Required heat sink thermal resistance: $\theta_{SA} \le 11.86\ ^\circ\text{C/W}$.**

**(iii) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{V_{out} \cdot I_L}{V_{in} \cdot I_L} \times 100\% = \frac{5 \times 0.8}{12 \times 0.8} \times 100\% = 41.67\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Low Efficiency Trap:** Linear regulators are inherently inefficient. The remaining 58.3% of the input power is dissipated as heat. For high-current applications, this is why **switching regulators (SMPS)** are preferred in industry. The KTU 2024 examiner may award bonus marks for this observation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mark-Deduction Areas in Voltage Regulator Questions:**
>
> 1. **Drawing the wrong pinout for 78XX vs 79XX.** Pin 1 and Pin 2 are swapped. (-2 to -3 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the heat-sink requirement** when the calculated $P_D$ exceeds ~1 W. Always compute $P_D$ and state whether a heat sink is needed. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 3. **Confusing the position of $R_1$ and $R_2$** in the formula $V_{out} = V_Z(1 + R_1/R_2)$. Always define which resistor is upper (tied to $V_{out}$) and which is lower (tied to GND) before writing the equation. (-2 marks)
> 4. **Ignoring the dropout voltage** in IC regulator design. A 7805 requires $V_{in} \ge 7$ V typically. A 7805 supplied with 6 V will *not* regulate. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 5. **Not drawing bypass capacitors** ($C_{in} = 0.33\ \mu F$, $C_{out} = 0.1\ \mu F$) at the input and output of IC regulators. The 78XX datasheet explicitly requires them. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Confusing "line regulation" with "load regulation"** in definitions. Memorize: line = input variation, load = output current variation. (-2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Voltage Regulators (Module 4)**

- [ ] A **voltage regulator** maintains a constant output despite variations in input voltage and load current.
- [ ] **Line regulation** quantifies $V_{out}$ change with $V_{in}$; **load regulation** quantifies $V_{out}$ change with $I_L$.
- [ ] The **series pass transistor** operates in the **active region** and drops the excess voltage as heat.
- [ ] The op-amp series regulator uses the **virtual short principle**: $V_f = V_Z$.
- [ ] **Governing equation:** $V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ — independent of $\beta$, $I_L$, and $V_{in}$.
- [ ] The **78XX** family provides **fixed positive** output (5 V, 6 V, 8 V, 12 V, 15 V, 24 V).
- [ ] The **79XX** family provides **fixed negative** output of the same magnitudes.
- [ ] **78XX pinout:** Pin 1 = Input, Pin 2 = Ground, Pin 3 = Output.
- [ ] **79XX pinout:** Pin 1 = Ground, Pin 2 = Input, Pin 3 = Output. (Reversed!)
- [ ] The 78XX/79XX ICs include **short-circuit protection**, **thermal shutdown**, and **SOA protection** internally.
- [ ] **Dropout voltage** for 78XX/79XX is typically 2 V. Minimum $V_{in} = V_{out} + 2$ V.
- [ ] **Power dissipation:** $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$. Always check if a **heat sink** is required.
- [ ] **Thermal resistance equation:** $\theta_{JA} = \frac{T_J(\max) - T_A}{P_D}$.
- [ ] **LM317** output: $V_{out} = 1.25 \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} \cdot R_2$. $R_1$ minimum = 240 Ω.
- [ ] **Current boosting** uses an external PNP transistor whose base is driven by the 78XX's internal Darlington.
- [ ] **Bypass capacitors:** $C_{in} = 0.33\ \mu F$, $C_{out} = 0.1\ \mu F$ (mandatory per datasheet).
- [ ] **Efficiency** of a linear regulator = $V_{out}/V_{in}$ × 100% (ignoring $I_Q$). Always low (~40–70%).
- [ ] **KTU 2024 Module Boundary:** Only **linear series regulators** — *no* switching regulators in this module.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 — Voltage Regulators.** You are now equipped to tackle both 3-mark and 14-mark KTU questions on this topic. Practice the derivations in Section 3.3 and 3.4 by hand, memorize the pinouts, and remember: in KTU exams, the **schematic diagram** and the **formula derivation** together carry 60% of the marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 555 Timer: Introduction, Functional diagram, Monostable operation and its applications, Astable operation and its applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT404 - INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** 555 Timer: Introduction, Functional diagram, Monostable operation and its applications, Astable operation and its applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 555 Timer — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **IC 555 Timer** is a highly stable, monolithic integrated circuit that functions as a precise timing, pulse-generation, delay, and oscillator device. It was originally designed by **Signetics Corporation in 1971** by Hans Camenzind and is popularly known as the *“workhorse of analog/digital ICs”*. The device can operate in three primary modes:

1. **Monostable (one-shot) mode** — produces a single fixed-duration output pulse when triggered.
2. **Astable (free-running oscillator) mode** — produces a continuous train of rectangular pulses without any external triggering.
3. **Bistable (flip-flop) mode** — acts as a basic SR latch with two stable states.

The chip is typically an **8-pin DIP package** containing approximately **25 transistors, 2 diodes, and 15 resistors** on a single silicon die.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 555 timer is a hybrid analog–digital IC because it uses **analog comparators** to set thresholds and a **digital SR flip-flop** to store the state. This dual nature is the key to its versatility.

## 1.2 Pin Configuration (8-Pin DIP)

| Pin No. | Pin Name | Function |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **GND** | Ground reference (**0 V**) |
| 2 | **TRIG (Trigger)** | Starts the timing cycle when voltage falls below **1/3 Vcc** |
| 3 | **OUT (Output)** | Drives the load; can sink/source up to **200 mA** |
| 4 | **RESET** | Active-low reset; forces output LOW when pulled below **0.4 V** |
| 5 | **CONT (Control Voltage)** | Modulates threshold/trigger levels; usually bypassed with **0.01 μF** to GND |
| 6 | **THR (Threshold)** | Ends timing cycle when voltage rises above **2/3 Vcc** |
| 7 | **DIS (Discharge)** | Open-collector transistor used to discharge the timing capacitor |
| 8 | **Vcc** | Supply voltage (**+4.5 V to +15 V**, nominally **+5 V** or **+12 V**) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Staircase Light Timer:**  
> Think of the 555 in monostable mode as the **staircase light timer** in your apartment building. When you press the switch (trigger), the light turns ON. After a fixed time (say, 60 seconds), the light automatically turns OFF, regardless of what you do. You cannot extend the ON-time by pressing the button again mid-way. The 555 behaves the same way — **one trigger, one timed pulse, automatic reset**.  
> In **astable** mode, imagine a **flashing railway crossing signal** that turns ON and OFF continuously at fixed intervals with no human intervention — the 555 free-runs forever.

## 1.4 Key Features at a Glance

- **Supply voltage range:** **+4.5 V to +15 V** (some variants up to **+18 V**).
- **Maximum output current:** **±200 mA** (can directly drive a relay, speaker, or LED).
- **Timing range:** From **microseconds (μs)** to **hours** — practically **10 μs to several minutes**.
- **Temperature stability:** Typically **0.005 % per °C** of timing drift.
- **Duty cycle (astable):** Adjustable from **~50 % to ~100 %** (cannot go below 50 % in standard astable configuration).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Functional Block Diagram — Component-by-Component Breakdown

The 555 timer consists of **five functional blocks** connected internally. Understanding each block is essential for KTU exam answers.

### Block 1: Three-Resistor Voltage Divider
- Consists of **three equal resistors of 5 kΩ each** (this is the origin of the name “**555**”).
- Forms a reference network between **Vcc** and **GND**.
- Sets two precise reference voltages:
  - **Lower reference = (1/3) × Vcc** → fed to the **inverting (–) input of the Lower Comparator**.
  - **Upper reference = (2/3) × Vcc** → fed to the **non-inverting (+) input of the Upper Comparator**.

> [!NOTE]
> If a control voltage is applied to pin 5, the upper reference becomes **Vcontrol** and the lower reference becomes **(1/2) × Vcontrol**.

### Block 2: Two Analog Comparators
- **Upper Comparator (C1):** Compares **Threshold (pin 6)** with **(2/3) Vcc**. Output goes HIGH when Vthreshold > (2/3) Vcc.
- **Lower Comparator (C2):** Compares **Trigger (pin 2)** with **(1/3) Vcc**. Output goes HIGH when Vtrigger < (1/3) Vcc.

### Block 3: SR Flip-Flop (Set-Reset Latch)
- Receives inputs from the two comparators.
- **S input ← Output of Lower Comparator** (sets Q = 1, OUT = HIGH).
- **R input ← Output of Upper Comparator** (resets Q = 0, OUT = LOW).
- Pin 4 (RESET) is an **asynchronous active-low override** that forces Q = 0 directly.

### Block 4: Discharge Transistor (Open-Collector NPN)
- Connected between **pin 7 (DIS)** and **GND**.
- Its base is driven by the **Q̄ (complement) output** of the SR flip-flop.
- When Q = 0 → Q̄ = 1 → transistor ON → capacitor discharges through pin 7.
- When Q = 1 → Q̄ = 0 → transistor OFF → capacitor charges through external resistor.

### Block 5: Output Buffer (Totem-Pole Push-Pull)
- A high-current inverting amplifier providing **±200 mA** drive capability.
- Inverts the flip-flop output: **OUT = Q̄**.

## 2.2 Functional Logic Table

| Reset (Pin 4) | Trigger (Pin 2) | Threshold (Pin 6) | Output (Pin 3) | Discharge (Pin 7) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | × | × | LOW (0) | ON (shorted to GND) |
| 1 | < (1/3) Vcc | < (2/3) Vcc | HIGH (1) | OFF (open) |
| 1 | > (1/3) Vcc | > (2/3) Vcc | LOW (0) | ON (shorted to GND) |
| 1 | > (1/3) Vcc | < (2/3) Vcc | Previous state | Previous state |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following formulas. They are the most-asked derivations in KTU board exams for Module 4.

| Mode | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Monostable** | Pulse width | $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ | seconds | R in Ω, C in F |
| **Monostable** | Threshold voltage | $V_{TH} = (2/3) \cdot V_{cc}$ | volts | Upper comparator reference |
| **Monostable** | Trigger voltage | $V_{TR} = (1/3) \cdot V_{cc}$ | volts | Lower comparator reference |
| **Astable** | Charging time (HIGH) | $t_{H} = 0.693 \cdot (R_{1}+R_{2}) \cdot C$ | seconds | Capacitor charges from (1/3)Vcc to (2/3)Vcc |
| **Astable** | Discharging time (LOW) | $t_{L} = 0.693 \cdot R_{2} \cdot C$ | seconds | Capacitor discharges from (2/3)Vcc to (1/3)Vcc |
| **Astable** | Total time period | $T = t_{H} + t_{L} = 0.693 \cdot (R_{1}+2R_{2}) \cdot C$ | seconds | Sum of charge + discharge |
| **Astable** | Frequency of oscillation | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_{1}+2R_{2}) \cdot C}$ | Hz | Reciprocal of T |
| **Astable** | Duty cycle (fraction) | $D = \dfrac{t_{H}}{T} = \dfrac{R_{1}+R_{2}}{R_{1}+2R_{2}}$ | dimensionless | Multiplied by 100 for percentage |
| **Astable** | Duty cycle (minimum) | $D_{min} = 50\,\%$ (when $R_{1} \ll R_{2}$) | % | Cannot be less than 50 % in basic astable |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Monostable applications:** Missing-pulse detector, bounce-free switches, digital debouncing circuits, frequency dividers, ramp generators.
- **Astable applications:** Square-wave clock generators for digital logic, pulse-width modulation (PWM), tone generation in alarms, LED flashers, FSK (Frequency-Shift Keying) modems.
- The 555 has powered everything from **toy sound generators** to **precision instrumentation**, and is still manufactured in **billions of units per year** due to its simplicity, robustness, and low cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are written explicitly for KTU 14-mark answers. **Every algebraic step is shown** so you never lose marks for skipping transitions.

## 3.1 Derivation of Monostable Pulse Width: $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$

### Circuit Setup
A 555 in monostable mode is wired with:
- **External resistor R** between **Vcc (pin 8)** and **pin 7 (Discharge)**.
- **External capacitor C** between **pin 7 (Discharge)** and **GND**.
- **Trigger input** applied to **pin 2**.

### Step-by-Step Charging Analysis

**Step 1 — Initial state before trigger:**  
The flip-flop is in RESET (Q = 0), so Q̄ = 1. The discharge transistor is **ON**, clamping pin 7 to GND. Therefore, the capacitor voltage is:

$$V_{C}(0) = 0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Trigger event:**  
A negative-going trigger pulse (< (1/3) Vcc) arrives at pin 2. The lower comparator fires, SETTING the flip-flop (Q = 1, Q̄ = 0). The discharge transistor turns **OFF**, and the output goes **HIGH**.

**Step 3 — Capacitor charging phase begins:**  
With the transistor OFF, the capacitor C charges through resistor R from Vcc according to the standard RC charging equation:

$$V_{C}(t) = V_{cc} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right) + V_{C}(0) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

Substituting $V_{C}(0) = 0$:

$$V_{C}(t) = V_{cc} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Determine the time to reach threshold (2/3) Vcc:**  
The upper comparator will reset the flip-flop the instant $V_{C}(t)$ reaches $(2/3) \cdot V_{cc}$. Substituting:

$$\frac{2}{3} \cdot V_{cc} = V_{cc} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-T/RC}\right)$$

Divide both sides by $V_{cc}$:

$$\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T/RC}$$

Rearrange to isolate the exponential term:

$$e^{-T/RC} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$- \frac{T}{RC} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = - \ln(3)$$

$$\frac{T}{RC} = \ln(3)$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the constant:**  
We know $\ln(3) \approx 1.0986 \approx 1.1$. Therefore:

$$T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The numerical factor **1.1** comes directly from $\ln(3)$. This is the **most important formula** in monostable mode and a guaranteed 3-mark derivation in KTU exams.

## 3.2 Derivation of Astable Charging Time: $t_{H} = 0.693 \cdot (R_{1}+R_{2}) \cdot C$

### Circuit Setup
In astable mode, two resistors $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ are in series between Vcc and pin 7, with the capacitor connected from the junction of $R_{1}$–$R_{2}$ to GND. The threshold (pin 6) and trigger (pin 2) are tied together and connected to the capacitor.

### Charging Phase Analysis

**Step 1 — Charging path:**  
When the discharge transistor is OFF, the capacitor charges through **both $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ in series** from Vcc. The effective charging resistance is:

$$R_{charge} = R_{1} + R_{2}$$

**Step 2 — Initial and final capacitor voltages:**  
At the start of charging, the capacitor is at the **lower threshold**:

$$V_{C}(0) = \frac{1}{3} \cdot V_{cc}$$

At the end of charging (upper threshold):

$$V_{C}(t_{H}) = \frac{2}{3} \cdot V_{cc}$$

**Step 3 — General RC charging equation from a non-zero initial voltage toward $V_{cc}$:**

$$V_{C}(t) = V_{cc} - \left(V_{cc} - V_{C}(0)\right) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the boundary values:**

$$\frac{2}{3} \cdot V_{cc} = V_{cc} - \left(V_{cc} - \frac{1}{3} \cdot V_{cc}\right) \cdot e^{-t_{H}/RC}$$

$$\frac{2}{3} \cdot V_{cc} = V_{cc} - \frac{2}{3} \cdot V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t_{H}/RC}$$

**Step 5 — Isolate the exponential:**

$$\frac{2}{3} \cdot V_{cc} - V_{cc} = -\frac{2}{3} \cdot V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t_{H}/RC}$$

$$-\frac{1}{3} \cdot V_{cc} = -\frac{2}{3} \cdot V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t_{H}/RC}$$

$$e^{-t_{H}/RC} = \frac{1/3}{2/3} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Take the natural logarithm:**

$$- \frac{t_{H}}{R_{charge} \cdot C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = - \ln(2)$$

$$t_{H} = \ln(2) \cdot (R_{1}+R_{2}) \cdot C$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the constant:**  
$\ln(2) \approx 0.6931 \approx 0.693$. Therefore:

$$t_{H} = 0.693 \cdot (R_{1}+R_{2}) \cdot C$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Astable Discharging Time: $t_{L} = 0.693 \cdot R_{2} \cdot C$

### Discharging Phase Analysis

**Step 1 — Discharging path:**  
When the discharge transistor is ON, pin 7 is shorted to GND. The capacitor discharges **only through $R_{2}$** (since $R_{1}$ is now connected from Vcc to GND via the saturated transistor and does not carry the discharge current of C). The effective resistance is:

$$R_{discharge} = R_{2}$$

**Step 2 — Discharge equation:**  
The capacitor voltage decays from $(2/3) \cdot V_{cc}$ to $(1/3) \cdot V_{cc}$. Since the discharge transistor shorts pin 7 to GND, the equation becomes a simple exponential decay through $R_{2}$ toward 0 V:

$$V_{C}(t) = V_{C}(start) \cdot e^{-t/R_{2}C}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute boundary values:**

$$\frac{1}{3} \cdot V_{cc} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t_{L}/(R_{2}C)}$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the exponential:**

$$e^{-t_{L}/(R_{2}C)} = \frac{1/3}{2/3} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm:**

$$t_{L} = \ln(2) \cdot R_{2} \cdot C = 0.693 \cdot R_{2} \cdot C$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Astable Frequency and Duty Cycle

**Step 1 — Total time period:**

$$T = t_{H} + t_{L} = 0.693 \cdot (R_{1}+R_{2}) \cdot C + 0.693 \cdot R_{2} \cdot C$$

$$T = 0.693 \cdot C \cdot \left[(R_{1}+R_{2}) + R_{2}\right]$$

$$\boxed{T = 0.693 \cdot (R_{1} + 2R_{2}) \cdot C}$$

**Step 2 — Frequency of oscillation:**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.693 \cdot (R_{1}+2R_{2}) \cdot C}$$

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1.44}{(R_{1}+2R_{2}) \cdot C}}$$

**Step 3 — Duty cycle (fraction of time output is HIGH):**

$$D = \frac{t_{H}}{T} = \frac{0.693 \cdot (R_{1}+R_{2}) \cdot C}{0.693 \cdot (R_{1}+2R_{2}) \cdot C}$$

$$\boxed{D = \frac{R_{1}+R_{2}}{R_{1}+2R_{2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Duty cycle in percentage:**

$$\boxed{D\,\% = \frac{R_{1}+R_{2}}{R_{1}+2R_{2}} \times 100\,\%}$$

## 3.5 Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 555 timer is configured in **astable mode** with $R_{1} = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{2} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $C = 0.1 \text{ μF}$. Calculate the frequency, time period, and duty cycle.

**Solution:**

$$R_{1} + 2R_{2} = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega + 2 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 24.7 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$f = \frac{1.44}{24.7 \times 10^{3} \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.44}{2.47 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 583 \text{ Hz}$$

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{583} \approx 1.715 \text{ ms}$$

$$D = \frac{4.7 + 10}{4.7 + 20} = \frac{14.7}{24.7} = 0.595 = 59.5\,\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Functional Block Diagram of the 555 Timer

The following Mermaid diagram maps the internal architecture block-by-block, showing the signal flow from the voltage divider → comparators → SR flip-flop → output buffer and discharge transistor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VCC Supply Pin 8] --> B[Resistor Divider 5k 5k 5k]
    B --> C1[Upper Ref 2 by 3 Vcc]
    B --> C2[Lower Ref 1 by 3 Vcc]
    C1 --> D1[Upper Comparator Inverting Input]
    D1 --> D2[Threshold Pin 6]
    D1 --> E[Output to SR FlipFlop R Input]
    C2 --> F1[Lower Comparator NonInverting Input]
    F1 --> F2[Trigger Pin 2]
    F1 --> G[Output to SR FlipFlop S Input]
    H[Reset Pin 4 Active Low] --> I[SR FlipFlop Qbar Output]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    I --> J[Output Buffer Inverting]
    J --> K[Output Pin 3]
    I --> L[Discharge NPN Transistor Base]
    L --> M[Discharge Pin 7 Open Collector]
    N[Control Voltage Pin 5] -.Modulates.-> C1
    O[GND Pin 1] --> B
```

## 4.2 Monostable Mode Circuit Connection

This block-level schematic shows how the 555 is wired in monostable configuration. Note that pin 7 is the discharge path, and the R–C network at pins 6/7/2 sets the timing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VCC] --> R1[External Resistor R]
    R1 --> P7[Pin 7 Discharge]
    P7 --> C1[External Capacitor C]
    C1 --> GND1[Ground]
    P7 -.Connection.-> P6[Pin 6 Threshold]
    P6 -.Connection.-> P2[Pin 2 Trigger]
    P2 --> TRIG[External Negative Trigger Pulse]
    P3[Pin 3 Output] --> OUTLOAD[Load LED Relay etc]
    P4[Pin 4 Reset] --> VCC2[Tied to VCC for normal operation]
    P5[Pin 5 Control] --> CAP[0.01 uF Decoupling Cap]
    CAP --> GND2[Ground]
    P1[Pin 1 GND] --> GND3[Ground]
```

## 4.3 Astable Mode Circuit Connection

The astable configuration uses two timing resistors. Pin 2 and pin 6 are tied together to the capacitor node, creating a self-triggering free-running oscillator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VCC] --> R1A[Resistor R1]
    R1A --> R2A[Resistor R2]
    R2A --> P7A[Pin 7 Discharge Node]
    P7A --> CA[Capacitor C]
    CA --> GNDA[Ground]
    P7A -.Junction.-> P6A[Pin 6 Threshold]
    P7A -.Junction.-> P2A[Pin 2 Trigger]
    P3A[Pin 3 Output] --> OUTA[Square Wave Output]
    P4A[Pin 4 Reset] --> VCCA[Tied to VCC]
    P5A[Pin 5 Control] --> CAPA[0.01 uF Decoupling Cap]
    CAPA --> GNDB[Ground]
    P1A[Pin 1 GND] --> GNDC[Ground]
```

## 4.4 Monostable Timing Waveform (Block-Level Functional Sequence)

This block diagram shows the **chronological state machine** of the 555 in monostable mode — from idle → trigger → timing → auto-reset.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[STABLE State Output LOW C discharged] --> S2{Trigger Pulse Arrives Pin 2 less than 1 by 3 Vcc}
    S2 --> S3[FlipFlop SET Output goes HIGH]
    S3 --> S4[Discharge Transistor OFF C charges through R]
    S4 --> S5{C reaches 2 by 3 Vcc}
    S5 --> S6[Upper Comparator Resets FlipFlop]
    S6 --> S7[Output goes LOW Discharge Transistor ON C rapidly discharges]
    S7 --> S1
```

## 4.5 Astable Timing Waveform (Block-Level Free-Running Sequence)

This block diagram shows the **continuous oscillation cycle** of the 555 in astable mode — it never stops on its own.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[C starts charging from 1 by 3 Vcc through R1 plus R2] --> A2{Voltage reaches 2 by 3 Vcc}
    A2 --> A3[Upper Comparator Resets FlipFlop]
    A3 --> A4[Output goes LOW Discharge Transistor ON]
    A4 --> A5[C discharges through R2 only from 2 by 3 Vcc]
    A5 --> A6{Voltage reaches 1 by 3 Vcc}
    A6 --> A7[Lower Comparator Sets FlipFlop]
    A7 --> A8[Output goes HIGH Discharge Transistor OFF]
    A8 --> A1
```
