# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
List the **eight pins** of the 555 timer and state the function of any **four** of them.

**Model Answer:**

The 555 timer is housed in an 8-pin DIP package. The pin functions are:

1. **Pin 1 — GND:** Common ground reference (0 V).
2. **Pin 2 — TRIG:** Trigger input; a voltage below (1/3) Vcc starts the timing cycle.
3. **Pin 3 — OUT:** Output pin; provides up to ±200 mA drive.
4. **Pin 4 — RESET:** Active-low asynchronous reset; forces output LOW.
5. **Pin 5 — CONT:** Control voltage input; modulates threshold/trigger levels.
6. **Pin 6 — THR:** Threshold input; ends the timing cycle above (2/3) Vcc.
7. **Pin 7 — DIS:** Open-collector discharge pin for the timing capacitor.
8. **Pin 8 — Vcc:** Positive supply voltage (+4.5 V to +15 V).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
State the **two reference voltages** used inside the 555 timer and explain how they are generated.

**Model Answer:**

The 555 timer uses an internal voltage divider made of **three equal 5 kΩ resistors** connected between Vcc and GND. This produces two reference voltages:

- **Upper reference** = (2/3) × Vcc — fed to the **inverting input of the upper comparator** (used to compare the threshold pin).
- **Lower reference** = (1/3) × Vcc — fed to the **non-inverting input of the lower comparator** (used to compare the trigger pin).

The name “**555**” is derived from the value of these three resistors (**5 kΩ each**).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU allows **internal choice** in 14-mark questions. We provide TWO complete alternative questions. Answer **ANY ONE**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**  
**(a)** With the help of a **functional block diagram**, explain the operation of the **IC 555 timer** in **monostable mode**. Derive the expression for the output pulse width. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a **monostable multivibrator** using 555 timer to produce an output pulse of **duration 5 seconds**. Assume C = 10 μF. List two applications of the monostable 555 circuit. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram and Operation:**

In monostable mode, the 555 has **one stable state** (output LOW) and **one quasi-stable state** (output HIGH). Connections:

- An external resistor **R** is connected between **Vcc and pin 7**.
- An external capacitor **C** is connected between **pin 7 and GND**.
- **Pins 6 and 2 are tied together** to the capacitor node.
- **Pin 4 is tied to Vcc** (no reset).
- **Pin 5** is bypassed to GND via **0.01 μF**.

**Stable State (Idle):** The flip-flop is in RESET → Q = 0, Q̄ = 1 → discharge transistor ON → pin 7 shorted to GND → $V_C = 0$ V → output LOW. The circuit remains here indefinitely.

**Triggered State:** When an external negative trigger pulse (< (1/3) Vcc) arrives at pin 2, the lower comparator SETS the flip-flop → Q = 1, Q̄ = 0 → discharge transistor OFF → output goes HIGH. The capacitor now begins charging through R toward Vcc.

**Return to Stable State:** As the capacitor charges, when $V_C$ reaches **(2/3) Vcc**, the upper comparator RESETS the flip-flop → Q = 0, Q̄ = 1 → output goes LOW and the discharge transistor turns ON, rapidly discharging C to 0 V. The circuit returns to the stable state, awaiting the next trigger.

**Derivation of Pulse Width:**

The capacitor charges from 0 V toward Vcc through R with the equation:

$$V_C(t) = V_{cc} \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

Setting $V_C(t) = (2/3) V_{cc}$ at $t = T$:

$$\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T/RC} \implies e^{-T/RC} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$T = RC \cdot \ln(3) = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Design for T = 5 seconds, C = 10 μF:**

Using $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$:

$$R = \frac{T}{1.1 \cdot C} = \frac{5}{1.1 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5}{1.1 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$R = \frac{5}{1.1 \times 10^{-5}} = 454{,}545 \, \Omega \approx 454.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

A standard value of **470 kΩ** can be used.

**Two Applications of Monostable 555:**

1. **Missing Pulse Detector:** A retriggerable monostable can detect when a periodic input pulse train is interrupted.
2. **Bounce-free Switch / Pulse Generator:** Generates a clean, debounced digital pulse from a mechanical switch closure.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**  
**(a)** With the help of a **functional block diagram**, explain the operation of the **IC 555 timer** in **astable mode**. Derive the expression for the **frequency of oscillation** and the **duty cycle**. (7 marks)

**(b)** An astable 555 timer has $R_1 = 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 68 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $C = 1 \text{ μF}$. Calculate the **(i) time period, (ii) frequency, (iii) duty cycle**, and **(iv) HIGH and LOW time durations** of the output waveform. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram and Operation:**

In astable mode, the 555 has **no stable state** — it oscillates continuously. Connections:

- Two resistors **R1 and R2** in series between Vcc and pin 7.
- Capacitor **C** between the **junction of R1–R2** and GND.
- **Pins 2 and 6 tied together** to the capacitor node.
- **Pin 4 tied to Vcc**, **Pin 5 bypassed to GND**.

**Cycle Begins (Charging Phase):** Initially, the flip-flop is SET → discharge transistor OFF → capacitor charges through **R1 + R2 in series** from (1/3) Vcc to (2/3) Vcc. Output is **HIGH**.

**End of Charging (Switch to LOW):** When $V_C$ reaches (2/3) Vcc, the upper comparator RESETS the flip-flop → discharge transistor turns ON → output goes **LOW**. The capacitor now discharges through **R2 only** (pin 7 is shorted to GND) from (2/3) Vcc down to (1/3) Vcc.

**End of Discharging (Switch to HIGH):** When $V_C$ reaches (1/3) Vcc, the lower comparator SETS the flip-flop → discharge transistor OFF → output goes **HIGH** again. The cycle repeats forever.

**Derivation of Frequency and Duty Cycle:**

Charging time (HIGH):

$$t_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C$$

Discharging time (LOW):

$$t_L = 0.693 \cdot R_2 \cdot C$$

Total period:

$$T = t_H + t_L = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C$$

Frequency:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C}$$

Duty cycle:

$$D = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $R_1 = 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 68 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 1 \text{ μF}$

**(i) Time Period T:**

$$R_1 + 2R_2 = 2.2 + 2(68) = 2.2 + 136 = 138.2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$T = 0.693 \times 138.2 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T = 0.693 \times 138.2 \times 10^{-3} = 95.77 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Frequency f:**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{95.77 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 10.44 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Duty Cycle D:**

$$D = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} = \frac{2.2 + 68}{2.2 + 136} = \frac{70.2}{138.2} = 0.508 = 50.8\,\%$$

**(iv) HIGH and LOW time durations:**

$$t_H = 0.693 \times (R_1 + R_2) \times C = 0.693 \times 70.2 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_H = 0.693 \times 70.2 \times 10^{-3} = 48.65 \text{ ms}$$

$$t_L = 0.693 \times R_2 \times C = 0.693 \times 68 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_L = 0.693 \times 68 \times 10^{-3} = 47.12 \text{ ms}$$

**Verification:** $t_H + t_L = 48.65 + 47.12 = 95.77$ ms ✓ (matches T)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Spots**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.1 and 0.693 constants:** These are NOT random — they come from $\ln(3)$ and $\ln(2)$. You must **derive** them; just writing the final formula without derivation loses 3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the charging vs. discharging resistance in astable mode:** Charging uses $R_1 + R_2$, but discharging uses **only $R_2$**. Mixing these up gives a wrong duty cycle.
> 3. **Writing the duty cycle formula upside down:** It is $D = t_H / T$, not $t_L / T$. The output is HIGH during $t_H$.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units:** If R is in kΩ and C is in μF, then $R \cdot C$ is in **milliseconds**, not seconds. Always do the unit check.
> 5. **Not stating the control voltage pin bypass:** Examiners often give a bonus mark for mentioning the **0.01 μF** decoupling cap on pin 5.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most-Important Things to Remember for the KTU Exam**

- **Five internal blocks** of the 555: Voltage divider (3 × 5 kΩ), two comparators, SR flip-flop, discharge transistor, output buffer.
- **Two reference voltages:** $(1/3) \cdot V_{cc}$ and $(2/3) \cdot V_{cc}$, generated by the internal 5 kΩ resistor string.
- **Name origin:** The "555" comes from the **three 5 kΩ resistors** inside the chip.
- **Supply range:** +4.5 V to +15 V. **Output drive:** up to **±200 mA**.
- **Monostable pulse width formula:** $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ — derived from $\ln(3) \approx 1.0986$.
- **Astable charging time:** $t_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C$ — through both resistors.
- **Astable discharging time:** $t_L = 0.693 \cdot R_2 \cdot C$ — through R2 only.
- **Astable frequency formula:** $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C}$.
- **Astable duty cycle formula:** $D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2}$ — **always ≥ 50 %** in standard configuration.
- **Pin 4 (RESET)** is active-low — must be tied to Vcc to enable normal operation.
- **Pin 5 (CONTROL)** should be bypassed to GND via a 0.01 μF cap to prevent noise coupling.
- **Monostable** is a **one-shot** — it has one stable state and produces ONE pulse per trigger.
- **Astable** is a **free-running oscillator** — it has NO stable state and produces a continuous square wave.
- **Applications (Mono):** Missing-pulse detector, pulse stretcher, bounce-free switch, frequency divider.
- **Applications (Astable):** Square-wave clock, LED flasher, tone generator, PWM, FSK modulator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Controlled Oscillator and PLL (basic principle only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBICT404 - INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Voltage Controlled Oscillator and PLL (basic principle only) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) ### Formal Definition A **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** is an electronic oscillator circuit whose instantaneous output frequency $\omega_{out}$ (or $f_{out}$) is a linear (or piece-wise linear) function of an externally applied control voltage $V_C$. In the context of integrated circuit design under the KTU 2024 syllabus, the VCO is treated as a **frequency-to-voltage transducer** in the reverse sense, where the input voltage dictates the oscillation frequency. The general transfer characteristic is given by: $$f_{out} = f_0 + K_V \cdot (V_C - V_{ref})$$ where $f_0$ is the **free-running (center) frequency**, $K_V$ is the **tuning sensitivity (gain)** expressed in **Hz/V** (or **rad/s/V** for angular frequency), and $V_{ref}$ is a reference bias voltage (often $V_{DD}/2$ for CMOS circuits). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A VCO is the heart of any Phase Locked Loop (PLL) and is also used in **frequency synthesizers, FSK modulators, function generators, and clock recovery circuits** in modern communication ICs. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **musical instrument like a guitar with a foot-pedal (wah-wah pedal)**: - The **string's natural vibration** is analogous to the **free-running frequency $f_0$** of the VCO. - The **foot-pedal position** is the **control voltage $V_C$**. Press harder, the pitch goes up; release, the pitch returns to baseline. - The **rate at which the pitch changes per unit pedal pressure** is the **tuning sensitivity $K_V$**. Just as the guitarist can smoothly slide from one note to another by adjusting pedal pressure, the VCO sweeps across a band of frequencies as $V_C$ is varied. This "smooth glide" is exactly what a PLL exploits to lock onto an incoming reference signal. ### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - The tuning sensitivity $K_V$ for typical IC VCOs ranges from **a few kHz/V to several hundred MHz/V**. - The center frequency $f_0$ is generally in the range of **1 MHz to 10 GHz** for communication ICs. - The phase noise specification is usually expressed in **dBc/Hz** at a given offset frequency. --- ## 1.2 Phase Locked Loop (PLL) ### Formal Definition A **Phase Locked Loop (PLL)** is a **closed-loop negative feedback control system** that automatically adjusts the phase (and hence frequency) of a locally generated oscillator signal to match the phase of an incoming reference signal. The locally generated signal is produced by a **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)**. When the PLL is in the "locked" state, the following condition holds: - The **output frequency equals the input reference frequency** ($f_{out} = f_{in}$). - The **phase difference** between the two signals is **constant** (but not necessarily zero). > [!NOTE] > **Core Distinction:** A PLL locks on **phase**, not directly on frequency. Since frequency is the time-derivative of phase, locking the phase implicitly forces the frequencies to match as well. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **child on a swing being pushed by a parent**: - The **child's swinging motion** is the **VCO output** (locally generated signal). - The **parent's pushing rhythm** is the **incoming reference signal** ($f_{in}$). - The **parent's eyes and brain** act as the **phase detector** — they constantly compare the child's motion with the desired rhythm. - The **arm muscles applying force** are the **loop filter + amplifier** — they convert the perceived mismatch into a corrective push. - Over time, the child's swinging **locks in synchronism** with the parent's pushing — this is the **locked state**. If the parent pushes faster, the child must swing faster to keep up. If the child drifts, the parent adjusts the push strength and timing. This continuous error-correction is the essence of PLL operation. ### Block Diagram (Intuitive) A PLL consists of **three fundamental blocks** in a negative feedback configuration: $$\boxed{\text{PD} \rightarrow \text{LPF} \rightarrow \text{VCO} \rightarrow \text{(feedback to PD)}}$$ - **Phase Detector (PD):** Multiplies the two input signals, producing an output proportional to their phase difference. - **Loop Filter (Low Pass Filter, LPF):** Extracts the low-frequency error component and rejects high-frequency noise. - **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** Generates an output whose frequency is steered by the filtered error voltage. - **(Optional) Frequency Divider:** In frequency synthesizers, a $\div N$ counter in the feedback path multiplies the effective input frequency by $N$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Note:** The syllabus specifically requires the **basic principle** of PLL. Do not get lost in detailed transfer function derivations of higher-order loops — focus on the **first-order conceptual model** with clear block-level understanding. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Voltage Controlled Oscillator — Detailed Theory ### 2.1.1 Classification of VCOs VCOs are classified into two broad categories based on the waveform they generate: | **Type** | **Waveform** | **Mechanism** | **KTU Relevance** | |---|---|---|---| | **Harmonic Oscillator VCO** | Sinusoidal | LC tank or crystal whose effective L/C is voltage-tunable (varactor diode) | RF/mmWave ICs | | **Relaxation Oscillator VCO** | Square / Triangular | Current charges a capacitor; threshold detector resets | Monolithic CMOS ICs (most common in KTU syllabus) | > [!NOTE] > For the **KTU Integrated Circuits course**, the relaxation-type VCO (built around OTAs, op-amps, or Schmitt triggers) is the primary focus because it is fully compatible with monolithic CMOS/BiCMOS technology. ### 2.1.2 Ideal VCO Transfer Characteristic The ideal VCO output is modeled as: $$v_{out}(t) = V_m \cos\left(\omega_0 t + K_{VCO} \int_{-\infty}^{t} V_C(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$$ The **instantaneous angular frequency** is therefore: $$\omega_{inst}(t) = \omega_0 + K_{VCO} \cdot V_C(t)$$ where $K_{VCO}$ is the VCO gain in **rad/s/V** and $V_C(t)$ is the control voltage. ### 2.1.3 Key Performance Parameters of a VCO 1. **Center Frequency ($f_0$):** The free-running frequency when $V_C = V_{ref}$. 2. **Tuning Range:** $\Delta f = f_{max} - f_{min}$, the span over which the VCO can be tuned. 3. **Tuning Sensitivity ($K_V$ or $K_{VCO}$):** Slope of the $f_{out}$ vs. $V_C$ curve, in **Hz/V**. 4. **Linearity:** Deviation of the actual transfer curve from the ideal straight line (expressed as a percentage). 5. **Phase Noise:** Short-term random frequency fluctuation, measured in **dBc/Hz** at a given offset. 6. **Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR):** Sensitivity of $f_0$ to supply noise. 7. **Frequency Pushing:** Sensitivity of $f_0$ to changes in supply voltage. 8. **Frequency Pulling:** Sensitivity of $f_0$ to changes in load impedance. ### 2.1.4 VCO Implementation Using OTA (Operational Transconductance Amplifier) A widely-used IC VCO topology uses an OTA whose transconductance $g_m$ is controlled by a bias current $I_{abc}$, which in turn is set by the control voltage $V_C$. The output frequency becomes: $$f_{out} = \frac{g_m}{2C} = \frac{I_{abc}}{2 C \cdot V_T} \quad \text{(for BJT)}$$ $$f_{out} = \frac{g_m}{2C} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_C - V_{TH})}{2C} \quad \text{(for CMOS)}$$ This gives a **linear voltage-to-frequency conversion**, which is the essence of a VCO. --- ## 2.2 Phase Locked Loop — Detailed Theory ### 2.2.1 The Three Building Blocks #### **Block 1: Phase Detector (PD)** The phase detector compares the phase of the incoming reference $\phi_{in}(t)$ with the phase of the VCO output $\phi_{out}(t)$ and produces an output whose **average (DC) value** is proportional to the phase error $\phi_e(t) = \phi_{in}(t) - \phi_{out}(t)$. **Multiplier-Type PD (XOR gate):** If both inputs are square waves, an XOR gate acts as a phase detector: $$\overline{v_d} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{2\Delta\phi}{\pi} \quad \text{(for } 0 \leq \Delta\phi \leq \pi\text{)}$$ The output is a **triangular waveform** with average value proportional to $\Delta\phi$, modulated by a $2f$ ripple. #### **Block 2: Loop Filter (LPF)** A simple **first-order RC low-pass filter** with transfer function: $$F(s) = \frac{1}{1 + s\tau_1}, \quad \tau_1 = R_1 C$$ A more common **proportional-integral (PI) filter** is: $$F(s) = \frac{1 + s\tau_2}{1 + s(\tau_1 + \tau_2)}$$ The filter removes the high-frequency ripple from the PD output and shapes the loop dynamics. #### **Block 3: Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** The VCO is a **pure integrator** in the phase domain (since frequency is the derivative of phase): $$\frac{d\phi_{out}}{dt} = K_{VCO} \cdot V_C(t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi_{out}(s) = \frac{K_{VCO}}{s} \cdot V_C(s)$$ ### 2.2.2 Operation Modes of a PLL | **Mode** | **Description** | **Mathematical Condition** | |---|---|---| | **Free Running** | PLL is open or not yet locked; VCO oscillates at $f_0$ | $f_{out} = f_0$, no feedback action | | **Capture (Acquisition)** | PLL is searching for lock; $f_{out}$ sweeps toward $f_{in}$ | Transient, non-linear | | **Tracking (Locked)** | PLL is locked; $f_{out} = f_{in}$, phase error is constant | $\Delta\phi = \text{const}$ | | **Out of Lock** | Reference drifts beyond the hold range; loop unlocks | $\Delta f > \text{Lock Range}$ | ### 2.2.3 Lock Range and Capture Range - **Lock Range ($\Delta\omega_L$):** The range of input frequencies over which a PLL **can maintain** lock once it has acquired lock. For a first-order loop: $$\Delta\omega_L = K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO} \cdot \vert F(0) \vert = K \cdot K_{VCO}$$ - **Capture Range ($\Delta\omega_C$):** The range of input frequencies over which a PLL **can acquire** lock from a free-running state. It is **always smaller than** the lock range. $$\Delta\omega_C \approx \Delta\omega_L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\tau_2}{\tau_1 + \tau_2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Memorize the **inequality**: **Capture Range $\leq$ Lock Range**. This is one of the most commonly tested concepts in KTU examinations. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Description** | |---|---|---|---| | VCO Output Frequency | $f_{out} = f_0 + K_V (V_C - V_{ref})$ | Hz | Linear tuning equation | | VCO Phase Equation | $\phi_{out}(s) = \dfrac{K_{VCO}}{s} V_C(s)$ | rad | VCO as an integrator in phase | | VCO Instantaneous Frequency | $\omega_{inst} = \omega_0 + K_{VCO} V_C(t)$ | rad/s | Time-domain definition | | Lock Condition | $f_{out} = f_{in}, \quad \Delta\phi = \text{const}$ | Hz, rad | Steady-state locked PLL | | Lock Range (1st order) | $\Delta\omega_L = K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}$ | rad/s | Symmetric around $f_0$ | | Capture Range | $\Delta\omega_C \approx \Delta\omega_L \sqrt{\tau_2/(\tau_1+\tau_2)}$ | rad/s | Always $\leq \Delta\omega_L$ | | Loop Bandwidth | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/\tau_1}$ | rad/s | Natural frequency of 2nd-order loop | | Damping Factor | $\zeta = \dfrac{\tau_2}{2}\sqrt{K/\tau_1}$ | dimensionless | Stability indicator | | OTA-VCO Frequency | $f_0 = g_m / (2C) = I_{abc}/(2 C V_T)$ | Hz | CMOS/BJT relaxation VCO | | PD Average Output | $\overline{v_d} = K_{PD} \cdot \Delta\phi$ | V | Linear PD model | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications | **Application** | **Function of PLL** | |---|---| | **FM Demodulation** | The control voltage $V_C(t)$ is the recovered FM modulating signal | | **Frequency Synthesizer** | A $\div N$ divider in feedback multiplies $f_{in}$ by $N$ to generate precise channel frequencies | | **Clock Recovery** | Extracts clock from serial data stream in digital communication | | **FSK Demodulation** | FSK signal drives the PLL; the VCO control voltage carries the binary data | | **AM Detection** | A PLL locked to the carrier detects AM by measuring amplitude changes | | **Motor Speed Control** | Frequency of an optical encoder is locked to a reference, controlling motor speed | | **GPS Receivers** | PLLs track the carrier frequency of satellite signals | | **Microprocessor Clock Generation** | On-chip PLLs multiply a low-frequency crystal reference to GHz CPU clocks | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Output Frequency of an OTA-Based Relaxation VCO Consider a current-starved CMOS inverter-based VCO (also called a **ring oscillator VCO**). The delay of each inverter stage is set by a current $I$ charging a parasitic capacitance $C$. The propagation delay per stage is: $$\tau_d = \frac{C \cdot V_{DD}}{2 I}$$ For a **3-stage ring oscillator**, the total period is $T = 6\tau_d$ (each stage transitions twice per period), giving: $$f_{out} = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{6\tau_d} = \frac{I}{3 C V_{DD}}$$ Now, the bias current $I$ is set by a control voltage $V_C$ applied across an NMOS transistor in saturation: $$I = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right) (V_C - V_{TH})^2$$ Substituting back: $$f_{out} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) (V_C - V_{TH})^2}{6 C V_{DD}}$$ To make $f_{out}$ **linear** in $V_C$, the transistor should operate in the **triode (linear) region**, where: $$I \approx \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right) (V_C - V_{TH}) V_{DS}$$ which yields: $$\boxed{f_{out} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{DS}}{6 C V_{DD}} (V_C - V_{TH})}$$ This confirms the **linear voltage-to-frequency** conversion, with tuning sensitivity: $$K_V = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{DS}}{6 C V_{DD}} \quad [\text{Hz/V}]$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Locked-Loop Phase Error in a First-Order PLL Let the input signal be $v_{in}(t) = V_s \sin(\omega_{in} t + \phi_{in})$ and the VCO output be $v_{o}(t) = V_o \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi_{out})$. The multiplier (phase detector) output is: $$v_d(t) = v_{in} \cdot v_{o} = V_s V_o \sin(\omega_{in} t + \phi_{in}) \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi_{out})$$ Using the trigonometric identity $\sin A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)]$: $$v_d(t) = \frac{V_s V_o}{2} \left[ \sin((\omega_{in}+\omega_0)t + \phi_{in} + \phi_{out}) + \sin((\omega_{in}-\omega_0)t + \phi_{in} - \phi_{out}) \right]$$ After low-pass filtering, the high-frequency term $(\omega_{in}+\omega_0)$ is removed, leaving: $$v_{LPF}(t) = \frac{V_s V_o}{2} \sin((\omega_{in}-\omega_0)t + \phi_{in} - \phi_{out})$$ Define the **phase error**: $\phi_e(t) = (\omega_{in}-\omega_0)t + \phi_{in} - \phi_{out}(t)$. In the **locked state**, $\phi_e$ is constant, so $\frac{d\phi_e}{dt} = 0$: $$\frac{d\phi_e}{dt} = (\omega_{in}-\omega_0) - \frac{d\phi_{out}}{dt} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\phi_{out}}{dt} = \omega_{in} - \omega_0$$ Since VCO frequency is $\omega_0 + K_{VCO} \cdot V_C$: $$\omega_0 + K_{VCO} \cdot V_C = \omega_{in} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_C = \frac{\omega_{in} - \omega_0}{K_{VCO}}$$ The **steady-state phase error** in the locked loop is: $$\phi_{e,ss} = \arcsin\left(\frac{2 V_C}{V_s V_o}\right)$$ For a sinusoidal phase detector operating in its **linear range** (small $\phi_e$): $$\phi_{e,ss} \approx \frac{\omega_{in} - \omega_0}{K_{PD} K_{VCO}} = \frac{\Delta\omega}{K}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The steady-state phase error is **finite** only if there is a **frequency offset** $\Delta\omega = \omega_{in} - \omega_0$. If $\omega_{in} = \omega_0$, the PLL locks with **zero phase error**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Lock Range of a First-Order PLL In a first-order PLL (no integrator in the loop filter), the closed-loop equation in the phase domain is: $$\frac{d\phi_e}{dt} = \Delta\omega - K \sin(\phi_e)$$ At steady state, $\frac{d\phi_e}{dt} = 0$: $$\sin(\phi_{e,ss}) = \frac{\Delta\omega}{K}$$ A real solution for $\phi_{e,ss}$ exists only when $\vert \sin(\phi_{e,ss}) \vert \leq 1$, which gives: $$\boxed{\vert \Delta\omega \vert \leq K \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta\omega_L = \pm K = \pm K_{PD} K_{VCO}}$$ This is the **lock range** of a first-order PLL. The total lock range is: $$\Delta f_L = \frac{K_{PD} K_{VCO}}{2\pi} \quad [\text{Hz}]$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: VCO vs. PLL Summary | **Parameter** | **VCO (Standalone)** | **PLL (VCO in Loop)** | |---|---|---| | **Input** | Control voltage $V_C$ | Reference frequency $f_{in}$ | | **Output** | Frequency $f_{out}$ | Frequency $f_{out} = f_{in}$ (in lock) | | **Stability** | Open-loop, drifts with temperature | Closed-loop, tracks reference | | **Phase Noise** | High | Reduced (especially within loop bandwidth) | | **Linearity** | Important for distortion | Less critical; loop corrects non-linearities | | **KTU Exam Focus** | Transfer characteristic, $K_V$ | Block diagram, lock/capture range | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Phase Locked Loop ```mermaid graph LR A[REF IN
fin phin] --> B[PHASE
DETECTOR PD] C[VCO OUT
fout phiout] --> B B --> D[LOOP FILTER
LPF Fs] D --> E[VCO
KVCO per s] E --> C style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Description of flow:** The reference input $f_{in}$ and the VCO output $f_{out}$ are fed to the Phase Detector. The PD generates an error signal proportional to the phase difference. The Loop Filter (LPF) smooths this error and removes high-frequency ripple. The filtered control voltage steers the VCO frequency, closing the negative-feedback loop. In the locked state, $f_{out} = f_{in}$ with a constant phase offset. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram of a VCO Frequency Synthesizer (PLL Application) ```mermaid graph LR A[CRYSTAL
REFERENCE fref] --> B[PHASE
DETECTOR PD] H[DIVIDE BY N
COUNTER] --> B B --> C[LOOP FILTER
LPF] C --> D[VCO] D --> E[CHANNEL OUTPUT
fout = N x fref] E --> H style A fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Description of flow:** A stable crystal reference $f_{ref}$ is compared with a divided-down version of the VCO output. The loop forces the VCO output to be exactly $N$ times the reference frequency. By varying $N$ (the division ratio), the synthesizer generates many precise channel frequencies from a single crystal — the principle behind every mobile phone's RF transceiver. --- ## 4.3 VCO Transfer Characteristic Curve ```mermaid graph TD A[VCO Transfer Characteristic] --> B[Slope = KV in Hz per V] A --> C[Intercept f0 at Vc = Vref] A --> D[Linear Region approx for small VC swing] A --> E[Saturation at Vc near supply rails] style A fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Key points:** The VCO's $f_{out}$ vs. $V_C$ curve is approximately linear over a limited range around $V_{ref}$, with slope equal to the tuning sensitivity $K_V$. Beyond this range, the curve bends and saturates as $V_C$ approaches the supply rails. The non-linear regions introduce distortion and should be avoided in precision applications. --- ## 4.4 Functional Flow of PLL Lock Acquisition ```mermaid graph TD A[PLL Powered ON] --> B{VCO free running at f0} B --> C[PD compares fin and fout] C --> D{Phase and frequency equal} D -- No --> E[PD generates error voltage] E --> F[LPF filters and amplifies] F --> G[VCO frequency adjusted] G --> C D -- Yes --> H[PLL LOCKED] H --> I[Continuous phase tracking] style A fill:#e8f4f8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Description:** This state diagram illustrates the lock-acquisition process. The loop iteratively adjusts the VCO frequency until the phase and frequency match the reference. Once locked, the PLL continuously tracks small variations in the reference, maintaining the locked condition through negative feedback. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Define a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO). Derive the expression for its output frequency in terms of the control voltage.** **Model Answer:** A Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) is an electronic oscillator whose instantaneous output frequency $f_{out}$ is a linear function of an externally applied control voltage $V_C$. The transfer characteristic is given by: $$f_{out} = f_0 + K_V (V_C - V_{ref})$$ where: - $f_0$ is the free-running (center) frequency when $V_C = V_{ref}$ - $K_V$ is the tuning sensitivity in **Hz/V** - $V_{ref}$ is the reference bias voltage The corresponding angular frequency is: $$\omega_{out} = \omega_0 + K_{VCO} \cdot V_C(t)$$ The VCO is the core building block of any Phase Locked Loop and is also used in frequency synthesizers, FSK modulators, and clock generation circuits. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **List the three basic building blocks of a Phase Locked Loop and state the condition for the PLL to be in the locked state.** **Model Answer:** The three basic building blocks of a PLL are: 1. **Phase Detector (PD):** Compares the phases of the input reference and VCO output, producing an error voltage. 2. **Loop Filter (Low Pass Filter, LPF):** Filters out the high-frequency components of the PD output and provides the control voltage to the VCO. 3. **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** Generates an output signal whose frequency is controlled by the filtered error voltage. **Locked-State Condition:** The PLL is said to be in the locked state when the output frequency exactly equals the input reference frequency, and the phase difference between the two signals is constant (but not necessarily zero). Mathematically: $$f_{out} = f_{in} \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta\phi = \text{constant}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern ### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** #### **Q.A.(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]** **With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the basic principle of operation of a Phase Locked Loop (PLL). Discuss the role of each block.** **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram:** ``` f_in, φ_in ─────┐ ▼ ┌─────────┐ v_d ┌────────┐ V_C ┌──────────┐ │ PHASE │──────────▶│ LOOP │──────────▶│ VCO │──┐ │DETECTOR │ │ FILTER │ │ │ │ └─────────┘ └────────┘ └──────────┘ │ ▲ │ │ │ │ f_out = f_in (in lock) │ └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ f_out, φ_out ``` **Principle of Operation:** A PLL is a **negative feedback control system** that locks the phase of a locally generated VCO signal to that of an incoming reference signal. 1. **Phase Detector (PD):** It multiplies the reference input $v_{in}(t) = V_s \sin(\omega_{in} t + \phi_{in})$ with the VCO output $v_o(t) = V_o \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi_{out})$. The output contains a sum-frequency term and a difference-frequency term. The average (DC) value of the PD output is proportional to the phase error $\Delta\phi = \phi_{in} - \phi_{out}$. Mathematically: $$\overline{v_d} = K_{PD} \cdot \sin(\Delta\phi) \approx K_{PD} \cdot \Delta\phi \quad \text{(for small } \Delta\phi\text{)}$$ 2. **Loop Filter (LPF):** It removes the high-frequency components (sum frequency and noise) from the PD output and generates a smooth DC control voltage $V_C(t)$. A simple first-order RC filter has transfer function $F(s) = 1/(1+sRC)$. A proportional-integral filter provides better tracking and is commonly used in second-order PLLs. 3. **VCO:** The control voltage $V_C$ adjusts the VCO's output frequency according to $\omega_{out} = \omega_0 + K_{VCO} V_C$. The VCO output is fed back to the PD, closing the loop. **Locked-State Operation:** When the loop is locked, $\omega_{out} = \omega_{in}$ and $\Delta\phi = $ constant. Any drift in $f_{in}$ or $f_0$ produces a phase error, which the PD converts into a corrective $V_C$, which in turn shifts the VCO frequency to re-establish lock. This continuous correction is what gives the PLL its ability to track frequency variations and reject noise. #### **Q.A.(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** **Define lock range and capture range of a PLL. Derive the expression for the lock range of a first-order PLL.** **Model Solution:** **Lock Range ($\Delta\omega_L$):** The lock range is the range of input frequencies over which the PLL can maintain lock once lock has been acquired. It is a **static (steady-state)** parameter. **Capture Range ($\Delta\omega_C$):** The capture range is the range of input frequencies over which the PLL can acquire lock from an initially unlocked (free-running) state. It is a **dynamic (transient)** parameter and is **always smaller than the lock range**. **Mathematical Derivation of Lock Range:** For a first-order PLL, the closed-loop equation in the phase domain is: $$\frac{d\phi_e}{dt} = \Delta\omega - K \sin(\phi_e)$$ where $K = K_{PD} K_{VCO}$ is the loop gain and $\Delta\omega = \omega_{in} - \omega_0$ is the frequency offset. At steady state, $\frac{d\phi_e}{dt} = 0$: $$K \sin(\phi_{e,ss}) = \Delta\omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin(\phi_{e,ss}) = \frac{\Delta\omega}{K}$$ A real solution for $\phi_{e,ss}$ exists only if $\vert \sin(\phi_{e,ss}) \vert \leq 1$: $$\vert \Delta\omega \vert \leq K$$ Therefore, the lock range is: $$\boxed{\Delta\omega_L = \pm K = \pm K_{PD} K_{VCO}}$$ In terms of frequency (Hz): $$\Delta f_L = \pm \frac{K_{PD} K_{VCO}}{2\pi}$$ **Conclusion:** The lock range depends on the **product** of the PD gain and the VCO gain. To increase the lock range, we can increase either $K_{PD}$ (by using a higher-amplitude comparator) or $K_{VCO}$ (by using a more sensitive VCO). However, this also increases the loop bandwidth and may degrade noise performance. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** #### **Q.B.(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **Explain the working of a Voltage Controlled Oscillator using an OTA with a suitable circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the output frequency.** **Model Solution:** **Circuit Concept:** An OTA-based VCO uses the transconductance $g_m$ of an OTA to convert a control voltage into a current, which charges and discharges a capacitor to generate the oscillation. A Schmitt trigger (or comparator) detects the threshold crossings and resets the cycle. **Working Principle:** 1. A control voltage $V_C$ is applied to the input of the OTA, producing an output current $I_{out} = g_m \cdot V_C$. 2. This current charges a capacitor $C$ linearly until the voltage across $C$ reaches the upper threshold $V_{TH+}$ of the Schmitt trigger. 3. The Schmitt trigger output switches state, reversing the direction of the current (through an H-bridge or by changing the sign of the OTA input), discharging $C$. 4. When $V_C$ reaches the lower threshold $V_{TH-}$, the cycle repeats. The period of oscillation is: $$T = \frac{2C (V_{TH+} - V_{TH-})}{I_{out}} = \frac{2C \Delta V_{TH}}{g_m V_C}$$ The output frequency is: $$f_0 = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{g_m V_C}{2C \Delta V_{TH}}$$ For a BJT OTA, $g_m = I_{abc} / V_T$, so: $$f_0 = \frac{I_{abc} V_C}{2C V_T \Delta V_{TH}}$$ where $I_{abc}$ is the OTA bias current and $V_T = kT/q \approx 26$ mV at room temperature. For a CMOS OTA in saturation, $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$: $$f_0 = \frac{V_C}{2C \Delta V_{TH}} \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$$ **Key Feature:** The output frequency is **linearly proportional** to the control voltage $V_C$, which is the defining property of a VCO. #### **Q.B.(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **List any four applications of PLL. Explain how a PLL is used as a frequency multiplier.** **Model Solution:** **Applications of PLL:** 1. **FM Demodulation:** The control voltage $V_C(t)$ of the VCO is the recovered FM modulating signal. 2. **Frequency Synthesizer:** Generates many precise channel frequencies from a single reference. 3. **Clock Recovery:** Extracts the clock from a serial data stream in digital communication. 4. **FSK Demodulation:** Decodes binary FSK signals by measuring VCO control voltage. 5. **AM Detection:** Detects AM by locking onto the carrier and measuring amplitude changes. 6. **Motor Speed Control:** Locks to a reference frequency to maintain constant motor RPM. **PLL as a Frequency Multiplier:** A PLL can multiply an input frequency by an integer $N$ by inserting a **frequency divider ($\div N$ counter)** in the feedback path. **Block Diagram (Conceptual):** ``` f_in ──────▶ PD ──────▶ LPF ──────▶ VCO ──────▶ f_out = N × f_in ▲ │ │ │ └───────── ÷N DIVIDER ──────────┘ ``` **Working:** 1. The reference $f_{in}$ is applied to the PD along with the divided-down VCO output $f_{out}/N$. 2. In the locked state, the two PD inputs must be equal: $$f_{in} = \frac{f_{out}}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{out} = N \cdot f_{in}$$ 3. Therefore, the PLL output frequency is exactly $N$ times the input frequency, with the same long-term stability as the reference. **Example:** If $f_{in} = 1$ kHz and $N = 1000$, then $f_{out} = 1$ MHz. By changing $N$, we can generate a wide range of frequencies from a single stable reference — the principle used in every **radio tuner, mobile phone, and signal generator**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing Lock Range with Capture Range:** Students often write the two terms interchangeably. The **lock range** is **static** (steady-state tracking), while the **capture range** is **dynamic** (acquisition). The capture range is **always smaller** than the lock range. > > 2. **Forgetting the Units:** $K_V$ must be expressed in **Hz/V** (or rad/s/V for $K_{VCO}$). Writing it in dB or as a dimensionless number is incorrect. > > 3. **Missing the Phase Detector's Nonlinearity:** The PD output is proportional to $\sin(\phi_e)$, not $\phi_e$. The linear approximation $\sin(\phi_e) \approx \phi_e$ is valid **only for small phase errors** (less than about 30°). > > 4. **Drawing Block Diagrams Without Arrows:** The KTU examiner expects **labeled arrows** indicating signal flow direction. A block diagram with boxes but no connecting arrows loses at least 1-2 marks. > > 5. **Confusing the VCO's Role in PLL:** The VCO is **not** a frequency detector — it is a **frequency generator** controlled by voltage. The Phase Detector is what compares the frequencies. > > 6. **Writing "PLL is a frequency-locked loop":** A PLL locks on **phase**, not directly on frequency. The frequency lock is a **consequence** of the phase lock. > > 7. **Missing the Free-Running Frequency:** When asked about PLL operation, always mention the initial state where the VCO is free-running at $f_0$ before lock is acquired. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) — Key Takeaways** - A VCO produces an output frequency $f_{out}$ that is a **linear function** of an input control voltage $V_C$: $f_{out} = f_0 + K_V(V_C - V_{ref})$. - Two main types: **harmonic (sinusoidal)** and **relaxation (square/triangular)**. CMOS ICs predominantly use relaxation-type VCOs. - Key performance metrics: **tuning range, tuning sensitivity $K_V$, linearity, phase noise**. - OTA-based VCO is the most common monolithic implementation: $f_0 = g_m / (2C)$. ### **Phase Locked Loop (PLL) — Key Takeaways** - A PLL is a **closed-loop negative feedback** system that locks the **phase** (and hence frequency) of a VCO to a reference signal. - Three core blocks: **Phase Detector (PD) → Loop Filter (LPF) → Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)**. - **Locked condition:** $f_{out} = f_{in}$ and $\Delta\phi = $ constant. - **Lock range** (steady-state tracking) $\geq$ **Capture range** (initial acquisition). This inequality is a **favourite KTU question topic**. - For a first-order PLL: $\Delta\omega_L = \pm K_{PD} K_{VCO}$. - PD output: $v_d = K_{PD} \sin(\phi_e) \approx K_{PD} \phi_e$ (small signal). - VCO is an **integrator in the phase domain**: $\phi_{out}(s) = K_{VCO} V_C(s) / s$. ### **Rapid Revision Checklist (Board Exam Ready)** - ✅ Definition and transfer equation of VCO - ✅ OTA-based VCO circuit and frequency formula - ✅ Block diagram of PLL with proper arrows and labels - ✅ Role of each PLL block (PD, LPF, VCO) - ✅ Locked-state conditions (frequency equality + constant phase) - ✅ Lock range formula: $\Delta\omega_L = K_{PD} K_{VCO}$ - ✅ Capture range formula and inequality with lock range - ✅ PLL applications: FM demod, frequency synth, clock recovery, FSK demod - ✅ PLL as frequency multiplier using $\div N$ divider - ✅ Linear model: PD gain $K_{PD}$, VCO gain $K_{VCO}$, total loop gain $K = K_{PD} K_{VCO}$ - ✅ Difference between first-order and second-order PLL behavior - ✅ Always mention the **free-running state** before the **locked state** when explaining PLL operation ### **Memory Mnemonics for KTU Viva** - **"P-F-V"** = **P**hase Detector → **F**ilter (LPF) → **V**CO (the order of PLL blocks). - **"L is bigger than C"** = **L**ock range $\geq$ **C**apture range. - **"VCO = Voltage-Controlled Oscillator = Variable-Frequency Source"** — remember, the VCO is the only source of the output signal in the PLL. - **"PLL locks Phase, but Frequency follows automatically"** — the gold rule of PLL understanding. ### **Common Numerical Problem Templates for KTU** 1. **Given $K_{PD}$, $K_{VCO}$, and $f_0$:** Find lock range and capture range. 2. **Given input frequency and division ratio $N$:** Find output frequency of a PLL frequency multiplier. 3. **Given VCO transfer equation and control voltage:** Find the output frequency. 4. **Given loop filter components and loop gain:** Find natural frequency and damping factor of a second-order PLL.
